topics.title: Basic Principles of Fluid Mechanics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles of Fluid Mechanics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Fluid Mechanics?

**Fluid Mechanics** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the behavior of fluids (liquids and gases) at rest (**Fluid Statics / Hydrostatics**) and in motion (**Fluid Dynamics / Hydrodynamics**), together with the study of the forces and energy interactions involved.

For a **Pumping Engineer**, fluid mechanics is the absolute foundation — every pump selection, every head calculation, every pipe sizing decision, and every NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head) analysis rests upon these principles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (PEAGT522 – Module 1):**
> Students must master fluid properties, pressure concepts, continuity, Bernoulli's theorem, momentum principles, and flow measurement as a direct prerequisite for studying centrifugal and positive displacement pumps in Modules 2 and 3.

---

## 1.2 Properties of Fluids — The Engineering Fingerprint

Every fluid is uniquely described by a set of **intensive and extensive properties**. The following properties form the "identity card" of the fluid in any pump or hydraulic system.

### A. Density ($\rho$)

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad \text{[SI Unit: kg/m}^3\text{]}$$

- **Mass density** is the mass per unit volume of the fluid.
- For water at **4 °C**: $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (this is a standard reference).
- For mercury: $\rho_{Hg} \approx 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

### B. Specific Weight ($\gamma$)

$$\gamma = \rho \, g \quad \text{[SI Unit: N/m}^3\text{]}$$

- Specific weight is the **weight per unit volume**.
- Standard gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- For water: $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$.

### C. Specific Gravity (SG)

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water @ 4°C}} \quad \text{[Dimensionless]}$$

- A **dimensionless ratio** that compares fluid density to water at its densest state.
- SG of water = 1; SG of mercury = 13.6.

### D. Viscosity (Dynamic $\mu$ and Kinematic $\nu$)

Viscosity is the fluid's **internal resistance to flow** — its "stickiness" or "thickness."

$$\mu \;\text{[Pa·s or N·s/m}^2\text{]} \quad ; \quad \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \text{[m}^2\text{/s]}$$

- **Dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)**: resistance to shearing deformation.
- **Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$)**: ratio of dynamic viscosity to density; appears naturally in Reynolds number calculations.

### E. Bulk Modulus of Elasticity ($K$)

$$K = -\frac{dP}{(dV/V)} \quad \text{[SI Unit: Pa or N/m}^2\text{]}$$

- Measures the fluid's resistance to **uniform compression**.
- For water: $K \approx 2.1 \times 10^9$ Pa.
- High $K \Rightarrow$ fluid is nearly incompressible (water); low $K \Rightarrow$ gas (compressible).

### F. Surface Tension ($\sigma$)

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{L} \quad \text{[SI Unit: N/m]}$$

- The force per unit length acting along a free surface.
- Important in capillary rise, droplet formation, and small-scale pump flows.

### G. Vapor Pressure ($P_v$)

- The pressure at which a liquid **boils** at a given temperature.
- **Critical in NPSH calculations** — cavitation occurs when local pressure drops below $P_v$.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Traffic" View of Fluid Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Imagine a Highway:**
>
> - **Density** = how many cars per kilometer of road (mass per volume).
> - **Specific Weight** = the actual weight the bridge must support (force per volume).
> - **Viscosity** = friction between cars and road, and car-to-car drag (internal resistance).
> - **Pressure** = the "push" the cars exert on each other when traffic jams form.
> - **Flow Rate** = how many cars pass a toll booth per minute.
> - **Continuity** = what goes in must come out (no cars vanish on the highway).
> - **Bernoulli's Principle** = when traffic squeezes into a narrow tunnel, speed increases and "side pressure" decreases — the Venturi effect.
>
> Fluid mechanics, then, is simply the **physics of how the "cars" (fluid particles) move, push, and lose energy** along their journey through pipes and pumps.

---

## 1.4 Pressure — The "Push" Inside a Fluid

**Pressure** is the normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area:

$$P = \frac{F}{A} \quad \text{[SI Unit: Pa = N/m}^2\text{]}$$

Two reference conventions are critical for KTU problems:

| Reference Type | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Absolute Pressure** | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Measured from a perfect vacuum (true zero) |
| **Gauge Pressure** | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | Measured above atmospheric pressure |
| **Vacuum Pressure** | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Measured below atmospheric (always negative gauge) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 10.33 \text{ m of water column}$.
> KTU problems often use $P_{atm} = 101.3$ kPa. Always state your reference!

---

## 1.5 Pascal's Law — The Heart of Hydraulic Machines

> **Pascal's Law (1653):** *"The pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in every direction throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the walls of the containing vessel."*

This is the **physical basis of all hydraulic presses, hydraulic jacks, and hydraulic actuators** — and the fundamental working principle behind positive displacement pumps.

$$\Delta P_1 = \Delta P_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pump Engineering Connection:** Reciprocating pumps and hydraulic actuators exploit Pascal's law to multiply force. A small force on a small piston creates a large force on a large piston — mechanical advantage of $A_2 / A_1$.

---

## 1.6 Types of Fluid Flow — Classification for KTU

| Flow Type | Description | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady vs Unsteady** | Properties at a point do/don't change with time | Most pump flows assumed steady at design point |
| **Uniform vs Non-uniform** | Velocity constant/changing along the flow direction | Pipe flow ≈ uniform; around bends ≈ non-uniform |
| **Laminar vs Turbulent** | Smooth layers / chaotic mixing | Determined by **Reynolds number** |
| **Compressible vs Incompressible** | Density changes with pressure are negligible/significant | Water ≈ incompressible; air/gas = compressible |
| **Rotational vs Irrotational** | Fluid particles rotate/don't rotate about their own axis | Ideal flow theory assumption |
| **1-D, 2-D, 3-D** | Flow variation in 1, 2, or 3 spatial directions | Pipe flow often modeled as 1-D |

---

## 1.7 Reynolds Number — The "Flow Regime Judge"

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho \, V \, D}{\mu} = \frac{V \, D}{\nu}}$$

Where:
- $V$ = mean velocity [m/s]
- $D$ = pipe diameter [m]
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity [m²/s]

| Regime | Reynolds Number | Physical Character |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** | $Re < 2000$ | Smooth, parallel streamlines; viscous forces dominate |
| **Transitional** | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ | Unstable; intermittent bursts of turbulence |
| **Turbulent** | $Re > 4000$ | Chaotic eddies; inertia dominates over viscosity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The Reynolds number is the single most important dimensionless number in pump engineering. It dictates friction factor selection, the validity of Bernoulli's equation (with or without losses), and the type of pump suited to the application.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Laws Governing Fluid Flow

Fluid mechanics is built upon three conservation laws. Every pump equation, every head loss formula, and every flow measurement device is a direct application of one of these.

### Law 1: Conservation of Mass → **Continuity Equation**

For a **steady, incompressible flow** through a pipe of varying cross-section:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \quad \text{(constant)}$$

- $A$ = cross-sectional area [m²]
- $V$ = average velocity [m/s]
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate [m³/s]

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** Incompressibility means $\rho_1 = \rho_2$, and steady flow means no mass accumulation in the control volume. What flows in must flow out.

**Generalized form (compressible flow):**

$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m} \quad \text{(constant mass flow rate)}$$

### Law 2: Conservation of Energy → **Bernoulli's Equation**

For a **steady, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless) flow** along a streamline:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = \text{constant}$$

| Term | Name | Represents |
|---|---|---|
| $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | **Pressure head** [m] | Flow energy due to pressure |
| $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | **Velocity head / Kinetic head** [m] | Flow energy due to motion |
| $z$ | **Elevation head** [m] | Flow energy due to gravity/position |

**Extended (Real) Bernoulli Equation with losses and pump/turbine work:**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_{pump} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L + H_{turbine}$$

Where:
- $H_{pump}$ = head added by pump [m]
- $H_{turbine}$ = head extracted by turbine [m]
- $h_L$ = total head loss (friction + minor losses) [m]

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important equation in the entire PEAGT522 course.** It is used to determine pump head, draft tube analysis, and turbine power calculations.

### Law 3: Conservation of Momentum → **Momentum Equation**

$$\sum F_x = \dot{m} (V_{2x} - V_{1x}) = \rho Q (V_{2x} - V_{1x})$$

- Used to calculate the **force exerted by a fluid jet on a vane**, a pipe bend, or a Pelton wheel bucket.
- Foundation of pump and turbine **force and torque analysis**.

---

## 2.2 Pressure–Depth Relationship (Hydrostatic Law)

For a static fluid, pressure increases linearly with depth:

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

- $P_0$ = pressure at the free surface [Pa]
- $h$ = depth below the free surface [m]

For a fluid column of height $H$ in a tank/pipe:

$$P = \rho g H$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Manometer Equation (KTU Favourite):** The differential manometer equation is a direct application of this principle — pressures are summed as $\rho g h$ segments going through the manometer fluid.

**U-tube Manometer:**

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$$

where $\rho_m$ = manometer fluid density, $\rho_f$ = flowing fluid density, $h$ = manometer deflection.

---

## 2.3 Head Loss in Pipe Flow (Darcy–Weisbach Equation)

The energy lost due to friction in a pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ carrying flow at velocity $V$:

$$\boxed{h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}}$$

Where $f$ is the **Darcy friction factor** (4× the Fanning friction factor):

| Regime | Friction Factor Formula |
|---|---|
| **Laminar** ($Re < 2000$) | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ |
| **Turbulent — smooth pipe** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2.0 \log(Re \sqrt{f}) - 0.8$ (Prandtl) |
| **Turbulent — rough pipe** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2.0 \log\left(\dfrac{D}{k_s}\right) + 1.14$ (Nikuradse–Colebrook) |
| **Fully turbulent (rough)** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2.0 \log\left(\dfrac{D}{k_s}\right) + 1.14$ |

$k_s$ = equivalent sand-grain roughness [m] of the pipe material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The minor losses (entrance, exit, bends, valves, contractions, enlargements) are NOT included in $h_f$ — they are added separately:
> $$h_m = K \frac{V^2}{2g}$$
> Total head loss: $h_L = h_f + \sum h_m$.

---

## 2.4 Velocity Distribution in Pipes

| Flow Regime | Velocity Profile | Maximum Velocity ($V_{max}$) vs. Mean ($V$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** | Parabolic (Poiseuille) | $V_{max} = 2 V$ |
| **Turbulent** | Flatter, blunt profile (1/7th power law) | $V_{max} \approx 1.22 V$ |

For laminar flow in a circular pipe:

$$V(r) = V_{max} \left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right]$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Print This!

| # | Concept | Formula | SI Units | Key Points |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density | $\rho = m/V$ | kg/m³ | Water at 4 °C → 1000 kg/m³ |
| 2 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | N/m³ | $\gamma_{water}$ = 9810 N/m³ |
| 3 | Specific Gravity | $SG = \rho_{f} / \rho_{water}$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | Pa·s | Newton's law: $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ |
| 5 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s | Used in Reynolds number |
| 6 | Pressure | $P = F/A$ | Pa = N/m² | 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa |
| 7 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | Pa | Increases linearly with depth |
| 8 | Absolute Pressure | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Pa | Always positive |
| 9 | Pascal's Law | $\Delta P$ transmitted equally | Pa | Basis of hydraulic systems |
| 10 | Continuity (incompressible) | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$ | m³/s | Mass conservation |
| 11 | Bernoulli (ideal) | $P/\rho g + V^2/2g + z = C$ | m | Energy per unit weight |
| 12 | Bernoulli (real) | includes $H_{pump}$ and $h_L$ | m | Use this for pump problems |
| 13 | Reynolds Number | $Re = V D / \nu$ | — | < 2000 laminar, > 4000 turbulent |
| 14 | Darcy–Weisbach | $h_f = f (L/D) (V^2/2g)$ | m | Pipe friction loss |
| 15 | Laminar friction factor | $f = 64 / Re$ | — | Valid only for $Re < 2000$ |
| 16 | Minor loss | $h_m = K (V^2/2g)$ | m | $K$ depends on fitting |
| 17 | Momentum equation | $\sum F = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$ | N | Jet force on vane |
| 18 | Bulk Modulus | $K = -dP / (dV/V)$ | Pa | Compressibility measure |
| 19 | Surface Tension | $\sigma = F / L$ | N/m | Capillary effects |
| 20 | Vapor Pressure | $P_v(T)$ | Pa | Critical for NPSH / cavitation |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application | Governing Principle |
|---|---|
| **Pump head calculation** | Extended Bernoulli equation |
| **Pipe sizing for a flow rate** | Continuity + Darcy–Weisbach |
| **Cavitation prevention in pumps** | Vapor pressure + NPSH analysis |
| **Hydraulic press / jack** | Pascal's law |
| **Pelton / Francis turbine** | Momentum + Bernoulli |
| **Venturi / orifice flow meters** | Bernoulli + continuity |
| **Pitot-static tube (aircraft)** | Bernoulli (stagnation pressure) |
| **Sediment transport in pipes** | Critical velocity + Reynolds number |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (Euler's Approach)

**Starting Assumptions:**
1. Steady flow ($\partial / \partial t = 0$)
2. Incompressible fluid ($\rho = $ const)
3. Inviscid (frictionless)
4. Flow along a streamline
5. Body force = gravity only

### Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law to a Fluid Element

Consider a fluid element of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline, moving from point 1 to point 2.

The net pressure force on the element in the direction of motion:

$$F_{net} = P \, dA - \left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds\right) dA = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds \, dA$$

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$)

Mass of element: $dm = \rho \, dA \, ds$
Acceleration along streamline: $a = V \, \dfrac{dV}{ds}$

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds \, dA = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot V \frac{dV}{ds}$$

The $ds \, dA$ terms cancel:

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} = \rho V \frac{dV}{ds}$$

### Step 3 — Include Gravity in the Streamwise Direction

Gravity acts in the $-z$ direction. Its component along the streamline (which makes an angle $\theta$ with horizontal) is $-g \sin\theta = -g \dfrac{dz}{ds}$.

The full Euler equation along a streamline becomes:

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} - \rho g \frac{dz}{ds} = \rho V \frac{dV}{ds}$$

Rearranging:

$$-\frac{1}{\rho} \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} - g \frac{dz}{ds} = V \frac{dV}{ds}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by $ds$ to Make It an Exact Differential

$$-\frac{1}{\rho} \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds - g \, dz = V \, dV$$

### Step 5 — Integrate from Point 1 to Point 2

For an incompressible fluid, $\rho$ is constant, and the integration is straightforward:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} -\frac{1}{\rho} dP \;-\; \int_{z_1}^{z_2} g \, dz \;=\; \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V \, dV$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate the Three Integrals

$$-\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho} \;-\; g(z_2 - z_1) \;=\; \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$

### Step 7 — Rearrange into the Classical Form

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$

Dividing throughout by $g$ gives the **head form** (most common in KTU):

$$\boxed{\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2}$$

**Note:** Each term has units of meters [m] — energy per unit weight, called "head."

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Continuity Equation

### Step 1 — Control Volume Definition

Consider a pipe with cross-sections $A_1$ and $A_2$ at sections 1 and 2. Fluid enters at 1 and leaves at 2.

### Step 2 — Conservation of Mass (Steady State)

For **steady flow**, the mass entering per unit time equals the mass leaving per unit time:

$$\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2$$

### Step 3 — Express Mass Flow Rate

$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$$

### Step 4 — Apply Incompressibility ($\rho_1 = \rho_2$)

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \quad \text{(volumetric flow rate)}$$

For a circular pipe: $A = \pi D^2/4$, so:

$$D_1^2 V_1 = D_2^2 V_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** If the pipe narrows ($A$ decreases), the velocity must increase proportionally to maintain the same flow rate — a fundamental result used in Venturi meters and nozzles.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Darcy–Weisbach Equation (Dimensional Analysis Approach)

The head loss $h_f$ in a pipe depends on:
- Pipe length $L$
- Pipe diameter $D$
- Average velocity $V$
- Fluid density $\rho$
- Fluid viscosity $\mu$
- Pipe wall roughness $k_s$

By dimensional analysis (Buckingham $\Pi$ theorem), $h_f$ can be expressed as:

$$h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

where $f$ is the **friction factor** (a function of $Re$ and $k_s/D$).

**Physical interpretation:**

1. $h_f$ is proportional to $L$ — longer pipe, more loss.
2. $h_f$ is inversely proportional to $D$ — smaller pipe, more loss.
3. $h_f \propto V^2$ — doubling velocity quadruples friction loss.
4. $f$ captures both viscous and roughness effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "derive Darcy–Weisbach from first principles," the examiner expects either a dimensional-analysis approach or an integration of the shear-stress distribution over the pipe wall for laminar flow.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Application of Bernoulli + Continuity

**Problem (KTU-style):**
Water flows steadily through a horizontal pipe that narrows from a diameter of $D_1 = 0.20$ m to $D_2 = 0.10$ m. The pressure at section 1 is $P_1 = 200$ kPa, and the velocity at section 1 is $V_1 = 1.5$ m/s. Find the velocity and pressure at section 2. Assume ideal (frictionless) flow, $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply Continuity to Find $V_2$**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \Rightarrow V_2 = V_1 \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2$$

$$V_2 = 1.5 \times \left(\frac{0.20}{0.10}\right)^2 = 1.5 \times 4 = 6.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Horizontal: $z_1 = z_2$)**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P_2$**

$$P_2 = P_1 + \frac{\rho}{2}(V_1^2 - V_2^2)$$

$$P_2 = 200{,}000 + \frac{1000}{2}(1.5^2 - 6.0^2)$$

$$P_2 = 200{,}000 + 500 \times (2.25 - 36.0)$$

$$P_2 = 200{,}000 + 500 \times (-33.75) = 200{,}000 - 16{,}875$$

$$\boxed{P_2 = 183{,}125 \text{ Pa} = 183.125 \text{ kPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** As the pipe narrows, velocity rises 4× and pressure DROPS by 16.875 kPa. This is the Venturi effect — the same principle that lets an aircraft fly and lets a carburetor meter fuel.

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fluid Mechanics — Conceptual Map

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete logical structure of the topic, showing how the foundational principles feed into the engineering applications studied later in the course.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID MECHANICS FUNDAMENTALS] --> B[FLUID PROPERTIES]
    A --> C[FLUID STATICS]
    A --> D[FLUID KINEMATICS]
    A --> E[FLUID DYNAMICS]

    B --> B1[Density and Specific Weight]
    B --> B2[Viscosity Dynamic and Kinematic]
    B --> B3[Compressibility and Bulk Modulus]
    B --> B4[Surface Tension and Vapor Pressure]

    C --> C1[Pressure Definition and Units]
    C --> C2[Hydrostatic Pressure P equals rho g h]
    C --> C3[Pascals Law]
    C --> C4[Manometry]

    D --> D1[Flow Types Steady Unsteady]
    D --> D2[Laminar vs Turbulent]
    D --> D3[Reynolds Number]
    D --> D4[Continuity Equation]

    E --> E1[Bernoulli Equation Ideal Form]
    E --> E2[Bernoulli Equation Real Form with Losses]
    E --> E3[Momentum Equation]
    E --> E4[Darcy Weisbach Friction Loss]
    E --> E5[Minor Losses in Fittings]

    E1 --> F[PUMP HEAD CALCULATION]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> G[TURBINE AND JET FORCE ANALYSIS]
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    C2 --> H[NPSH AND CAVITATION ANALYSIS]
    B4 --> H
    D3 --> F
    D3 --> E4
```

---

## 4.2 Reynolds Number Regime Flowchart

A decision-tree schematic for identifying flow regime in a pipe — essential for friction factor selection in KTU problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Given V D nu] --> B[Compute Reynolds Number Re equals V D over nu]
    B --> C{Re less than 2000}
    C -->|YES| D[LAMINAR FLOW]
    D --> D1[Use f equals 64 over Re]
    D --> D2[Parabolic velocity profile]
    D --> D3[h_f proportional to V]
    C -->|NO| E{Re between 2000 and 4000}
    E -->|YES| F[TRANSITIONAL FLOW]
    F --> F1[Unstable intermittent turbulence]
    F --> F2[Avoid in design if possible]
    E -->|NO| G[Re greater than 4000]
    G -->|YES| H[TURBULENT FLOW]
    H --> H1[Use Moody Chart or Colebrook equation]
    H --> H2[Blunt velocity profile]
    H --> H3[h_f proportional to V squared]
    H --> H4{Pipe roughness dominant}
    H4 -->|YES| H5[Fully rough regime f depends only on k_s over D]
    H4 -->|NO| H6[Smooth pipe regime f depends only on Re]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bernoulli's Real-Flow Application

The following schematic shows how the **Extended Bernoulli Equation** integrates with the **Continuity Equation** and **Head Losses** to form the complete pump-system analysis framework used throughout the PEAGT522 course.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Inlet Reservoir Section 1] --> CP[Continuity Equation A1V1 equals A2V2]
    CP --> VB[Velocity Head Computation V squared over 2g]
    IN --> PH[Pressure Head P over rho g]
    IN --> EH[Elevation Head z1]

    PH --> SUM1[Total Energy at Section 1]
    VB --> SUM1
    EH --> SUM1

    SUM1 --> PUMP[PUMP HEAD ADDITION H_pump]
    PUMP --> SUM2[Total Energy at Section 2]

    VB2[Velocity Head V2 squared over 2g] --> SUM2
    PH2[Pressure Head P2 over rho g] --> SUM2
    EH2[Elevation Head z2] --> SUM2

    SUM2 --> DWF[Darcy Weisbach Friction Loss h_f]
    DWF --> FINAL[FINAL ENERGY BALANCE]
    VB2 --> FINAL
    PH2 --> FINAL
    EH2 --> FINAL

    MIN[Minor Losses in Fittings Bends Valves] --> DWF
    ROUGH[Pipe Roughness k_s] --> DWF
    REV[Reynolds Number Re] --> DWF
```

---

## 4.4 Pipe Flow Components — System Block Diagram

This block diagram identifies the elements of a typical pump–pipeline system, showing where each fluid-mechanics principle is applied.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUC[SUCTION TANK] --> SUC1[Strainer]
    SUC1 --> SUC2[Suction Pipe]
    SUC2 --> SUC3[Foot Valve and Strainer]
    SUC3 --> P1[PUMP]
    P1 --> D1[Delivery Pipe]
    D1 --> D2[Gate Valve]
    D2 --> D3[Elbows and Bends]
    D3 --> D4[Non Return Valve]
    D4 --> D5[Delivery Tank]
    D5 --> OV[Overflow]

    SUC2 -.Pressure drop h_f suction.-> P1
    P1 -.H_pump added.-> D1
    D1 -.Friction h_f.-> D2
    D2 -.Minor loss K V2 over 2g.-> D3
    D3 -.Minor loss K V2 over 2g.-> D4
    D4 -.Minor loss K V2 over 2g.-> D5
```

---

## 4.5 Table of Engineering Symbols Used in Fluid Mechanics

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $P$ | Pressure | Pa = N/m² |
| $\rho$ | Density | kg/m³ |
| $\gamma$ | Specific Weight | N/m³ |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic Viscosity | Pa·s |
| $\nu$ | Kinematic Viscosity | m²/s |
| $V$ | Velocity | m/s |
| $Q$ | Volumetric Flow Rate | m³/s |
| $\dot{m}$ | Mass Flow Rate | kg/s |
| $g$ | Gravitational Acceleration | m/s² |
| $z$ | Elevation | m |
| $D$ | Pipe Diameter | m |
| $L$ | Pipe Length | m |
| $f$ | Darcy Friction Factor | dimensionless |
| $k_s$ | Roughness Height | m |
| $Re$ | Reynolds Number | dimensionless |
| $H$ | Head | m |
| $h_L$ | Head Loss | m |
| $K$ | Minor Loss Coefficient | dimensionless |
| $F$ | Force | N |
| $T$ | Torque | N·m |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the deformation (shearing) of the fluid. According to Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\tau$ is the shear stress [Pa] and $du/dy$ is the velocity gradient [s⁻¹].

**SI Unit:** Pa·s (or N·s/m²).

**Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**SI Unit:** m²/s.

**Physical Difference:** $\mu$ represents the fluid's internal friction; $\nu$ represents the ratio of momentum diffusivity to mass diffusivity — it naturally appears in Reynolds number and is used in pipe-flow calculations.

---

### Question 2: State Bernoulli's equation for a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline. Explain the meaning of each term. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = \text{constant}$$

**Meaning of Each Term:**

| Term | Name | Physical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Pressure Head | Energy per unit weight stored as pressure |
| $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Velocity (Kinetic) Head | Energy per unit weight stored as motion |
| $z$ | Elevation (Potential) Head | Energy per unit weight stored as position |

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady flow
2. Incompressible fluid ($\rho = $ const)
3. Inviscid (frictionless)
4. Flow along a streamline

The equation expresses **conservation of mechanical energy** per unit weight of the fluid.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive Bernoulli's equation for a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow from Euler's equation of motion along a streamline. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Water flows through a horizontal Venturi meter. The pipe diameter at the inlet is 20 cm and at the throat is 10 cm. The differential manometer (mercury) reads 15 cm. Calculate: **(i)** the discharge through the meter, **(ii)** the pressure at the throat if the inlet pressure is 150 kPa. Take $C_d = 0.98$ for the meter. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady flow ($\partial/\partial t = 0$)
2. Incompressible fluid ($\rho$ = constant)
3. Inviscid (frictionless, $\mu = 0$)
4. Flow along a streamline
5. Only body force is gravity

**Step 1:** Consider a fluid element of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline. The net pressure force in the direction of motion is:

$$F_{net} = -dA \, ds \frac{dP}{ds}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Newton's second law. Mass = $\rho \, dA \, ds$; acceleration along streamline = $V \, dV/ds$:

$$-dA \, ds \frac{dP}{ds} = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot V \frac{dV}{ds}$$

Cancel $dA \, ds$:

$$-\frac{dP}{ds} = \rho V \frac{dV}{ds}$$

**Step 3:** Include gravity component along streamline: $-g \, dz/ds$

$$-\frac{dP}{ds} - \rho g \frac{dz}{ds} = \rho V \frac{dV}{ds}$$

Rearrange:

$$-\frac{1}{\rho} dP - g \, dz = V \, dV$$

**Step 4:** Integrate between sections 1 and 2:

$$-\frac{1}{\rho}(P_2 - P_1) - g(z_2 - z_1) = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to get Bernoulli's equation (head form, dividing by $g$):

$$\boxed{\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Venturi Meter Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 0.20$ m, $D_2 = 0.10$ m
- Manometer reading $h = 0.15$ m (Hg)
- $P_1 = 150$ kPa, $C_d = 0.98$
- $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Discharge Formula for a Venturi Meter:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h_{eff}}$$

where $h_{eff} = \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} - 1\right) h$

**Step 2 — Calculate $h_{eff}$:**

$$h_{eff} = \left(\frac{13600}{1000} - 1\right) \times 0.15 = 12.6 \times 0.15 = 1.89 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2, \quad A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 0.00785 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Area Ratio:**

$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.10}{0.20}\right)^2 = 0.25$$

**Step 5 — Discharge:**

$$Q = 0.98 \times \frac{0.00785}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0625}} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.89}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \times \frac{0.00785}{0.9682} \times \sqrt{37.08}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \times 0.00811 \times 6.090$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.0484 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 48.4 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step 6 — Pressure at Throat:**

Apply Bernoulli's equation (horizontal Venturi, $z_1 = z_2$):

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

$V_1 = Q/A_1 = 0.0484 / 0.0314 = 1.541$ m/s
$V_2 = Q/A_2 = 0.0484 / 0.00785 = 6.166$ m/s

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho}{2}(V_2^2 - V_1^2) = \frac{1000}{2}(38.02 - 2.37) = 500 \times 35.65 = 17{,}825 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_2 = 150{,}000 - 17{,}825 = 132{,}175 \text{ Pa} \approx 132.2 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_2 \approx 132.2 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Define the following fluid properties and state their SI units: **(i)** Specific Weight, **(ii)** Bulk Modulus of Elasticity, **(iii)** Kinematic Viscosity, **(iv)** Surface Tension. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Oil of specific gravity 0.85 and dynamic viscosity $0.105$ Pa·s is pumped through a 100 mm diameter horizontal pipe at a velocity of 2 m/s. Calculate: **(i)** Reynolds number and the flow regime, **(ii)** Friction factor (assuming smooth pipe / Blasius equation), **(iii)** Head loss due to friction over a 500 m length of pipe. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Fluid Properties Definitions (7 Marks)

**(i) Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** The weight per unit volume of a fluid.

$$\gamma = \rho g \quad \text{[SI Unit: N/m}^3\text{]}$$

**(ii) Bulk Modulus of Elasticity ($K$):** The ratio of change in pressure to the volumetric strain.

$$K = -\frac{dP}{dV/V} \quad \text{[SI Unit: Pa or N/m}^2\text{]}$$

High $K$ indicates a nearly incompressible fluid (e.g., water). Low $K$ indicates high compressibility (e.g., air).

**(iii) Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** The ratio of dynamic viscosity to density.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \text{[SI Unit: m}^2\text{/s]}$$

It represents momentum diffusivity per unit mass — used in Reynolds number and gravity-driven flow analysis.

**(iv) Surface Tension ($\sigma$):** The force per unit length acting along a free liquid surface, arising from molecular cohesion.

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{L} \quad \text{[SI Unit: N/m]}$$

Important in capillary rise, droplet formation, and small-scale flow phenomena.

---

#### Part (b) — Pipe Friction Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $SG = 0.85$, $\mu = 0.105$ Pa·s
- $D = 0.100$ m, $V = 2$ m/s, $L = 500$ m
- $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Oil Density and Kinematic Viscosity:**

$$\rho_{oil} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.105}{850} = 1.235 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds Number:**

$$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{2 \times 0.10}{1.235 \times 10^{-4}} = 1619.4$$

$$\boxed{Re \approx 1619 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{ LAMINAR flow } (Re < 2000)}$$

**Step 3 — Friction Factor (Laminar):**

$$f = \frac{64}{Re} = \frac{64}{1619} = 0.0395$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.0395}$$

(If turbulent, Blasius equation: $f = 0.316 / Re^{0.25}$.)

**Step 4 — Head Loss (Darcy–Weisbach):**

$$h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.0395 \times \frac{500}{0.10} \times \frac{2^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.0395 \times 5000 \times \frac{4}{19.62} = 0.0395 \times 5000 \times 0.2039$$

$$h_f = 197.5 \times 0.2039 \approx 40.27 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_f \approx 40.27 \text{ m of oil}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention for pressure:** In Bernoulli, NEVER mix absolute and gauge pressures. Pick one reference and stick to it throughout the problem.
> 2. **Reynolds number threshold:** Many students write $Re < 2000$ is laminar, $Re > 2000$ is turbulent — INCORRECT. The transitional zone is $2000 \le Re \le 4000$. Both answers can be challenged.
> 3. **Manometer density difference:** For differential manometer, use $(\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$, NOT $\rho_m g h$. The fluid-column correction is the most-missed step.
> 4. **Units in Darcy–Weisbach:** $V^2 / (2g)$ has units of meters — your final $h_f$ will be in meters. Do NOT leave it in J/kg.
> 5. **Continuity equation diameter squared:** $A \propto D^2$, so $V \propto 1/D^2$, not $1/D$. A frequent algebra mistake.
> 6. **NPSH and vapor pressure:** If a problem mentions "boiling" or "cavitation," you MUST involve vapor pressure in the analysis. Marks are reserved for this.
> 7. **Assumptions before derivation:** Always write the assumptions of Bernoulli's equation BEFORE the derivation. Examiners allot 1–2 marks for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Basic Principles of Fluid Mechanics**

### Core Properties
- [ ] Density $\rho$ = mass/volume; water at 4 °C → **1000 kg/m³**.
- [ ] Specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$; water → **9810 N/m³**.
- [ ] Specific gravity = dimensionless ratio to water.
- [ ] Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ [Pa·s] and kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu/\rho$ [m²/s].
- [ ] Bulk modulus $K$ → high for liquids, low for gases.
- [ ] Vapor pressure $P_v$ → key to cavitation analysis.

### Pressure Concepts
- [ ] $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$.
- [ ] $P = \rho g h$ (hydrostatic).
- [ ] Pascal's law → pressure transmitted equally in enclosed incompressible fluid.
- [ ] Differential manometer: $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$.

### Continuity Equation
- [ ] Incompressible: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$.
- [ ] Compressible: $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m}$.

### Bernoulli's Equation
- [ ] Ideal form: $P/\rho g + V^2/2g + z = $ const.
- [ ] Real form: includes $H_{pump}$ (added) and $h_L$ (lost).
- [ ] Each term has units of **meters** (head form).

### Reynolds Number & Friction
- [ ] $Re = \rho V D / \mu = V D / \nu$.
- [ ] **Laminar** $Re < 2000$; **Transitional** $2000 \le Re \le 4000$; **Turbulent** $Re > 4000$.
- [ ] Laminar: $f = 64/Re$.
- [ ] Darcy–Weisbach: $h_f = f (L/D) (V^2/2g)$.
- [ ] Minor losses: $h_m = K (V^2/2g)$.

### Momentum Principle
- [ ] $\sum F = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$ — for jet force on vanes.

### High-Yield Engineering Connections
- [ ] Venturi meter → Bernoulli + continuity.
- [ ] Pump head → extended Bernoulli with $H_{pump}$.
- [ ] Cavitation → vapor pressure + NPSH.
- [ ] Pipe sizing → continuity + Darcy–Weisbach.
- [ ] Jet on vane (Pelton) → momentum equation.

### Numerical Problem Solving Strategy
1. **List given data** with units.
2. **Identify the governing equation** (Bernoulli, continuity, momentum, Darcy–Weisbach).
3. **Apply assumptions** (steady, incompressible, etc.).
4. **Show all algebra steps** — no skipping.
5. **State final answer with units.**
6. **Sanity check**: Does the answer make physical sense? (e.g., higher velocity → lower pressure in a Venturi.)

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Basic Principles of Fluid Mechanics**
>
> Mastery of this module is the absolute prerequisite for Module 2 (Centrifugal Pumps) and Module 3 (Positive Displacement Pumps). Every pump performance equation — head, power, efficiency, NPSH — is built upon Bernoulli's principle, continuity, and momentum conservation as covered above.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Fluid Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Fluid Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fluid? — The KTU Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **fluid** is a substance that **continuously deforms (flows)** under the application of a **shear stress** (tangential force), no matter how small that shear stress may be. Unlike a solid which deforms by a finite, fixed amount, a fluid offers no resistance to shape change — it can only resist **normal stresses** (compression).

Mathematically, the relationship is expressed by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (N·s/m²)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (rate of shear strain, in 1/s)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Spreading Honey" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Honey vs. a Steel Block**
> Imagine you place a thin layer of **honey** on a tilted plate. The honey will slowly *creep* downward. That "creeping" is fluid behavior — even the tiniest gravity component along the surface creates motion. Now place a **steel block** on the same tilted plate. The block will not move until the gravity component exceeds the static friction threshold. **That threshold-free flow** is the defining feature of a fluid.
>
> In the context of a **centrifugal pump** (the heart of this course), the fluid (water/oil/coolant) must continuously deform as it travels through the impeller — this is exactly why understanding fluid properties is the foundation of pump engineering.

## 1.3 The Two Major Branches of Fluid Mechanics

| Branch | Assumption | Key Equation | Real Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fluid Statics** | Fluid is at rest ($\Sigma F = 0$) | $\frac{dp}{dy} = -\rho g$ | Pressure gauges, manometers, tank design |
| **Fluid Kinematics** | Studies motion *without* forces | $a = \frac{dV}{dt}$ | Flow visualization, velocity fields |
| **Fluid Dynamics** | Studies motion *with* forces | Euler's & Bernoulli's equations | Pump design, pipe networks |

## 1.4 The Properties Classification — The "Big Five" of Fluid Properties

Every property studied in Module 1 falls into one of these categories:

1. **Intensive Properties** — Independent of system mass (e.g., pressure, temperature, density at a point)
2. **Extensive Properties** — Dependent on system mass (e.g., total volume, total mass, total energy)
3. **Specific Properties** — Extensive per unit mass (e.g., specific volume, specific enthalpy)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners often ask: *"Is density intensive or extensive?"* The answer is **intensive** — splitting a tank of water in half gives the *same* density in each half, but *half* the mass.

## 1.5 Why This Matters for Pump Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A pump is fundamentally a device that **adds energy to a fluid to overcome**:
> 1. **Friction losses** (governed by **viscosity** $\mu$)
> 2. **Height differences** (governed by **specific weight** $\gamma = \rho g$)
> 3. **Pressure differences** (governed by **bulk modulus** $K$ for compressible fluids)
> 4. **Surface effects at small scales** (governed by **surface tension** $\sigma$)
>
> Without mastering these properties, you cannot design, select, or troubleshoot any pump.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Density ($\rho$) — The "Heaviness Per Unit Volume"

**Definition:** Mass contained per unit volume of a fluid.

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad \text{[kg/m}^3\text{]}$$

**Why it matters:** Determines the **inertial behavior** of fluid in a pump impeller. Higher density = more momentum transfer = higher pump head requirement.

**Standard Reference:** **$\rho_{\text{water at } 4°C} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$**

> [!NOTE]
> Density of water *decreases* with temperature above 4°C and *increases* slightly under pressure. This is critical for hot-water pump applications.

## 2.2 Specific Weight ($\gamma$) — The "Weight Per Unit Volume"

**Definition:** Weight of fluid per unit volume — the *force* equivalent of density.

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g \quad \text{[N/m}^3\text{]}$$

Where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard gravity on Earth).

**Why it matters:** In **static head** calculations for a pump, we use $\gamma$, not $\rho$:

$$H_{\text{static}} = \frac{\Delta p}{\gamma}$$

## 2.3 Specific Gravity ($S$ or $SG$) — The "Comparison Number"

**Definition:** A *dimensionless* ratio comparing fluid density to water density at a standard reference temperature (typically 4°C).

$$S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water at } 4°C}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **seawater**, $S \approx 1.025$. For **mercury**, $S \approx 13.6$. For **petroleum oils**, $S$ ranges from **0.85 to 0.95**.

## 2.4 Specific Volume ($v$) — The "Space Per Unit Mass"

**Definition:** Volume occupied by unit mass — the mathematical reciprocal of density.

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{V}{m} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/kg]}$$

Used extensively in **thermodynamics** (Rankine cycle, refrigeration) — less common in basic pump hydraulics, but KTU loves to test it.

## 2.5 Viscosity — The "Internal Friction" of Fluids

This is the **single most important property** for pump selection. It comes in two forms:

### 2.5.1 Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity ($\mu$)

**Definition:** The fluid's resistance to deformation under shear stress.

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\frac{du}{dy}} \quad \text{[N·s/m}^2\text{] or [Pa·s]}$$

### 2.5.2 Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$)

**Definition:** The ratio of dynamic viscosity to density — emerges naturally in momentum equations.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \text{[m}^2\text{/s]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Newton's vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids**
> - **Newtonian** (water, air, oil): $\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$ linearly (straight line through origin on a $\tau$ vs. $\frac{du}{dy}$ graph)
> - **Non-Newtonian** (blood, paint, mud, polymer solutions): Viscosity *changes* with shear rate

### 2.5.3 Viscosity Variation with Temperature

| Fluid | Viscosity Behavior with $\uparrow$ Temperature |
|---|---|
| **Liquids** (water, oil) | Viscosity **decreases** (molecules slide past each other more easily) |
| **Gases** (air, steam) | Viscosity **increases** (more molecular collisions) |

**Engineer's Rule of Thumb (Crane, 1988 — KTU accepted):**

$$\frac{1}{\mu} = A T + B$$

Where $T$ is in Rankine and $A, B$ are fluid-specific constants.

## 2.6 Bulk Modulus of Elasticity ($K$ or $E_v$) — The "Compressibility Indicator"

**Definition:** The ratio of change in pressure to the **volumetric strain** (relative change in volume). Higher $K$ = less compressible.

$$K = -\frac{dp}{\frac{dV}{V}} = -V\frac{dp}{dV} \quad \text{[Pa or N/m}^2\text{]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water at standard conditions:** $K \approx 2.2 \text{ GPa}$ — practically incompressible for most pump calculations. **Air at atmospheric pressure:** $K \approx 101 \text{ kPa}$ — highly compressible, requires **compressible flow** analysis.

**Compressibility** $\beta$ is the reciprocal of bulk modulus:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{K} \quad \text{[Pa}^{-1}\text{]}$$

## 2.7 Surface Tension ($\sigma$) — The "Skin Effect" of Liquids

**Definition:** The force per unit length acting along the surface of a liquid, caused by **cohesive forces** between molecules. It is what makes water droplets form spheres and allows insects to walk on water.

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{L} \quad \text{[N/m]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface tension decreases with temperature** and becomes zero at the **critical point**. For water at 20°C: $\sigma \approx 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$. For mercury: $\sigma \approx 0.485 \text{ N/m}$.

### 2.7.1 Capillarity

The rise or fall of a liquid in a thin tube due to surface tension is described by **Jurin's Law**:

$$h = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$$

Where:
- $h$ = Capillary rise/fall (m)
- $\theta$ = Contact angle between liquid and tube wall
- $d$ = Tube diameter (m)

## 2.8 Vapor Pressure ($p_v$) — The "Boiling Threshold"

**Definition:** The pressure at which a liquid **boils** at a given temperature. If the local absolute pressure drops below $p_v$, **cavitation** occurs — the killer of centrifugal pumps.

> [!WARNING]
> **Cavitation Warning:** When local pressure $\leq p_v$, vapor bubbles form and collapse violently on the impeller surface, causing **pitting, noise, vibration, and efficiency loss**. The KTU 2024 syllabus links this directly to **NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)** calculations in Module 3.

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Property | Formula | SI Unit | Dimension | Typical Value (Water, 4°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density | $\rho = \frac{m}{V}$ | kg/m³ | $ML^{-3}$ | 1000 kg/m³ |
| 2 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | N/m³ | $ML^{-2}T^{-2}$ | 9810 N/m³ |
| 3 | Specific Gravity | $S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water,4°C}}}$ | — | Dimensionless | 1.0 |
| 4 | Specific Volume | $v = \frac{1}{\rho}$ | m³/kg | $M^{-1}L^{3}$ | 0.001 m³/kg |
| 5 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$ | Pa·s or N·s/m² | $ML^{-1}T^{-1}$ | $1.00 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s |
| 6 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s | $L^{2}T^{-1}$ | $1.00 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s |
| 7 | Bulk Modulus | $K = -V \frac{dp}{dV}$ | Pa | $ML^{-1}T^{-2}$ | 2.2 × 10⁹ Pa |
| 8 | Compressibility | $\beta = 1/K$ | Pa⁻¹ | $M^{-1}LT^{2}$ | 4.55 × 10⁻¹⁰ Pa⁻¹ |
| 9 | Surface Tension | $\sigma = F/L$ | N/m | $MT^{-2}$ | 0.0728 N/m (at 20°C) |
| 10 | Capillary Rise | $h = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$ | m | $L$ | — |
| 11 | Vapor Pressure | $p_v$ (function of $T$) | Pa | $ML^{-1}T^{-2}$ | 2.34 kPa (at 20°C) |
| 12 | Newton's Law | $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ | Pa | $ML^{-1}T^{-2}$ | — |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** Memorize the dimensional formulas. Marks are often awarded *just* for correctly writing the dimensional symbol of a property in derivation questions.

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Critical Fluid Property | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Pumps** (e.g., construction equipment) | Bulk Modulus + Viscosity | Power density, response time |
| **Centrifugal Pumps** (water supply) | Density + Vapor Pressure | NPSH margin, head calculation |
| **Petroleum Pipelines** | Viscosity (Sayeed) | Friction factor, pumping power |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Surface Tension | Droplet size in atomizers |
| **Hydraulic Accumulators** | Bulk Modulus | Energy storage capacity |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Newton's Law of Viscosity from First Principles

We consider a fluid element between two parallel plates. The bottom plate is fixed; the top plate moves with velocity $U$ under applied force $F$.

**Step 1 — Identify the experimental setup:**
- Plate area: $A$
- Distance between plates: $dy$
- Top plate velocity: $U$
- Force applied: $F$

**Step 2 — Define shear stress:**
Shear stress is force per unit area:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the linear velocity profile assumption (Newton's postulate):**
For thin fluid films, the velocity varies linearly from 0 (bottom) to $U$ (top):

$$u(y) = \frac{U}{dy} \cdot y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Formulate the proportionality:**
Newton hypothesized that shear stress is proportional to the velocity gradient:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the constant of proportionality (dynamic viscosity $\mu$):**

$$\boxed{\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}}$$

**Step 6 — Identify kinematic viscosity:**
Dividing both sides by density:

$$\frac{\tau}{\rho} = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \cdot \frac{du}{dy} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)

Consider a liquid rising in a vertical capillary tube of diameter $d$ immersed in a reservoir.

**Step 1 — Identify forces acting on the liquid column:**
- Upward force due to surface tension acting along the perimeter: $F_{\uparrow} = \sigma \cdot \pi d \cos\theta$
- Downward force due to gravity on the liquid column of height $h$: $F_{\downarrow} = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot h$

**Step 2 — Apply force equilibrium (column is in static equilibrium):**
At equilibrium, the net vertical force is zero:

$$\sigma \cdot \pi d \cos\theta = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the common factor $\pi d$ from both sides:**

$$\sigma \cos\theta = \rho g \cdot \frac{d}{4} \cdot h$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $h$:**

$$h = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**
- $\theta < 90°$ (e.g., water in glass): $\cos\theta > 0$ → liquid **rises** ($h > 0$)
- $\theta > 90°$ (e.g., mercury in glass): $\cos\theta < 0$ → liquid **depresses** ($h < 0$)
- Smaller tube diameter $d$ → higher capillary rise

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between Bulk Modulus, Density, and Pressure

Consider a fluid element of volume $V$ and density $\rho$ subjected to a pressure change $dp$.

**Step 1 — Express volume in terms of mass and density:**

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to pressure (mass is constant):**

$$\frac{dV}{dp} = m \cdot \frac{d(1/\rho)}{dp} = -\frac{m}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{d\rho}{dp}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the bulk modulus definition:**
The bulk modulus is defined as:

$$K = -V \frac{dp}{dV} = -V \cdot \left(\frac{dV}{dp}\right)^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Replace $V$ and $dV/dp$:**

$$K = -\left(\frac{m}{\rho}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{m}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{d\rho}{dp}\right)^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the expression:**

$$K = -\frac{m}{\rho} \cdot \left(-\frac{\rho^2}{m} \cdot \frac{dp}{d\rho}\right)$$

$$K = \frac{\rho^2}{m} \cdot \frac{m}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{dp}{d\rho} \cdot \rho$$

Wait — let me redo this carefully:

$$K = -\left(\frac{m}{\rho}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{\rho^2}{m \frac{d\rho}{dp}}\right) = -\left(\frac{m}{\rho}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{\rho^2 dp}{m \, d\rho}\right) = \frac{\rho \, dp}{d\rho}$$

**Step 6 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{K = \frac{\rho \, dp}{d\rho} = \rho \frac{dp}{d\rho}}$$

This shows that as density increases (fluid compressed), the relationship between pressure and density is governed by the bulk modulus. For an **isothermal** ideal gas: $K = p$ (pressure equals its own bulk modulus). For an **isentropic** ideal gas: $K = \gamma p$, where $\gamma$ is the specific heat ratio.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Specific Weight from Density

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of weight:**
Weight of a fluid element is its mass times gravitational acceleration:

$$W = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by volume $V$:**

$$\frac{W}{V} = \frac{m}{V} \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the left side as specific weight and the right side's first term as density:**

$$\boxed{\gamma = \rho g}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check:**
For water at 4°C: $\gamma = 1000 \times 9.81 = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: How Fluid Properties Link to Pump Phenomena

| Pump Phenomenon | Governing Property | Governing Equation | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Friction head loss** | Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ | $h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Determines pump power |
| **Static head** | Specific weight $\gamma$ | $h_{static} = \Delta z$ | Suction lift limitations |
| **Cavitation** | Vapor pressure $p_v$ | $\text{NPSH}_a > \text{NPSH}_r$ | Maximum allowable suction lift |
| **Water hammer** | Bulk modulus $K$ | $\Delta p = \rho a \Delta V$ | Surge protection design |
| **Pipe flow regime** | Kinematic viscosity $\nu$ | $\text{Re} = \frac{VD}{\nu}$ | Laminar vs. turbulent |
| **Atomization** | Surface tension $\sigma$ | $\text{We} = \frac{\rho V^2 L}{\sigma}$ | Spray nozzle design |

## 3.6 Numerical Demonstration: Viscosity Conversion

**Problem:** A fluid has a dynamic viscosity of 0.5 Pa·s and a density of 850 kg/m³. Calculate the kinematic viscosity in (a) m²/s and (b) Stokes (cgs unit, 1 Stoke = 10⁻⁴ m²/s).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the kinematic viscosity formula:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.5 \text{ Pa·s}}{850 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 5.882 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

Step 2 — Convert to Stokes:

$$\nu = 5.882 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \times \frac{1 \text{ St}}{10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}} = 5.882 \text{ cSt}$$

Step 3 — Verify dimensional consistency:
$\frac{[\text{Pa·s}]}{[\text{kg/m}^3]} = \frac{[\text{N/m}^2 \cdot \text{s}]}{[\text{kg/m}^3]} = \frac{[\text{kg/(m·s)}]}{[\text{kg/m}^3]} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **cP** (centipoise, for dynamic viscosity) with **cSt** (centistokes, for kinematic). Remember: **1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s**, **1 cSt = 0.000001 m²/s**. They differ by a factor of **1000×** when applied to water at 20°C.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification of Fluid Properties
