<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) — Short Answer

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Define the following fluid properties and state their SI units:**
(a) Dynamic viscosity
(b) Kinematic viscosity
(c) Bulk modulus of elasticity

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the deformation of the fluid under the action of an external shear force. It is defined as the ratio of shear stress to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient).

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\frac{du}{dy}}$$

**SI Unit:** N·s/m² or Pa·s

**(b) Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**SI Unit:** m²/s

**(c) Bulk Modulus of Elasticity ($K$):** It is defined as the ratio of change in pressure to the corresponding volumetric strain produced in the fluid. It measures the compressibility of the fluid.

$$K = -\frac{dp}{\frac{dV}{V}}$$

**SI Unit:** N/m² or Pa

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Explain the term "specific gravity" of a fluid. How is it related to density? What is its importance in pump selection?**

**Model Answer:**

**Specific Gravity ($S$):** It is defined as the ratio of the density of a given fluid to the density of a standard fluid (usually water at 4°C, where $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$).

$$S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water at } 4°C}}$$

**Relationship to Density:**

$$\rho_{\text{fluid}} = S \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Importance in Pump Selection:**
1. It is a **dimensionless** quantity that simplifies fluid comparison across different units.
2. It is used in calculating the **specific weight** ($\gamma = S \times 9810$ N/m³), which directly enters the **static head** equation.
3. For **seawater pumps** ($S \approx 1.025$) and **chemical pumps** (e.g., sulfuric acid $S \approx 1.84$), the specific gravity significantly alters the head and power requirements.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Past Year Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the capillary rise of a liquid in a vertical tube, clearly stating the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid has a dynamic viscosity of 0.045 Pa·s and a specific gravity of 0.85. Calculate:
   (i) Density in kg/m³
   (ii) Specific weight in N/m³
   (iii) Kinematic viscosity in m²/s and centistokes
   (iv) State one application where this fluid is commonly used in hydraulic systems. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. The capillary tube is of uniform circular cross-section.
2. The liquid wets the tube wall with a constant contact angle $\theta$.
3. The meniscus is spherical in shape.
4. The temperature is uniform and the process is in equilibrium (steady state).

**Derivation:**

Consider a capillary tube of diameter $d$ immersed in a liquid reservoir. The liquid rises to a height $h$ inside the tube due to surface tension.

**Step 1:** Identify the upward force due to surface tension acting along the perimeter of the contact line:

$$F_{\text{up}} = \sigma \times \text{wetted perimeter} \times \cos\theta = \sigma \cdot \pi d \cos\theta$$

**Step 2:** Identify the downward gravitational force on the liquid column of height $h$:

$$F_{\text{down}} = \text{mass} \times g = (\text{volume} \times \rho) \times g = \left(\frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot h\right) \cdot \rho \cdot g$$

**Step 3:** At static equilibrium, $F_{\text{up}} = F_{\text{down}}$:

$$\sigma \cdot \pi d \cos\theta = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot h \cdot \rho g$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $\pi d$ and solve for $h$:

$$\boxed{h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}}$$

**Step 5:** Discussion:
- If $\theta < 90°$ (water-glass), liquid rises.
- If $\theta > 90°$ (mercury-glass), liquid falls.
- Capillary rise is inversely proportional to tube diameter $d$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $\mu = 0.045$ Pa·s, $S = 0.85$

**(i) Density:**

$$\rho = S \times 1000 = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(ii) Specific Weight:**

$$\gamma = \rho g = 850 \times 9.81 = 8338.5 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**(iii) Kinematic Viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.045}{850} = 5.294 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

Converting to centistokes (1 m²/s = 10⁶ cSt):

$$\nu = 5.294 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{6} = 52.94 \text{ cSt}$$

**(iv) Application:** This fluid is consistent with a **hydraulic oil (ISO VG 46–68 grade)**, commonly used in **hydraulic presses, machine tool actuators, and industrial hydraulic circuits** where moderate viscosity provides good lubrication and sealing.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Define the bulk modulus of elasticity of a fluid. Two fluids A and B have bulk moduli of $2.2 \times 10^9$ Pa and $1.4 \times 10^5$ Pa respectively. Identify which fluid is more compressible and justify. What implications does this have for pump selection? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An oil with dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.1$ Pa·s flows between two parallel horizontal plates separated by a distance of 5 mm. The upper plate moves at 1.5 m/s, while the lower plate is stationary. Assuming a linear velocity profile, calculate:
   (i) The shear stress on the upper plate
   (ii) The shear stress on the lower plate
   (iii) The velocity at a point 2 mm above the lower plate
   (iv) The drag force on a plate area of 0.5 m² **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** The bulk modulus of elasticity ($K$) of a fluid is defined as the ratio of the change in pressure (compressive stress) to the corresponding volumetric strain (relative change in volume). A higher $K$ indicates lower compressibility.

$$K = -\frac{dp}{\frac{dV}{V}}$$

**Identification of More Compressible Fluid:**

Compressibility $\beta = \frac{1}{K}$

For Fluid A: $\beta_A = \frac{1}{2.2 \times 10^9} = 4.55 \times 10^{-10}$ Pa⁻¹

For Fluid B: $\beta_B = \frac{1}{1.4 \times 10^5} = 7.14 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa⁻¹

Since $\beta_B \gg \beta_A$, **Fluid B is more compressible** (Fluid B is likely a gas; Fluid A is likely a liquid like water).

**Implications for Pump Selection:**
1. **Liquid pumps (Fluid A):** Can be treated as **incompressible** in design — use simple Bernoulli-based head equations.
2. **Gas/steam pumps (Fluid B):** Must use **compressible flow** equations; volumetric efficiency drops significantly at high pressure ratios.
3. **Water hammer protection:** Required for Fluid A; gas systems have built-in "cushioning" and are less prone to destructive pressure surges.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $\mu = 0.1$ Pa·s, $dy = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m, $U = 1.5$ m/s, $A = 0.5$ m²

**Velocity gradient:**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{dy} = \frac{1.5}{0.005} = 300 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(i) Shear stress on the upper plate:**

By Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.1 \times 300 = 30 \text{ Pa (N/m}^2\text{)}$$

**(ii) Shear stress on the lower plate:**
For a linear velocity profile, the velocity gradient is constant throughout the gap:

$$\tau_{\text{lower}} = \tau = 30 \text{ Pa (N/m}^2\text{)}$$

**(iii) Velocity at 2 mm above the lower plate:**

$$u(y=0.002) = \frac{U}{dy} \cdot y = \frac{1.5}{0.005} \times 0.002 = 0.6 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iv) Drag force on plate area of 0.5 m²:**

$$F = \tau \times A = 30 \times 0.5 = 15 \text{ N}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in Fluid Properties (As per KTU 2024 Valuation Key)**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Viscosity:** Writing dynamic viscosity in m²/s or kinematic viscosity in Pa·s. **Always** use Pa·s for $\mu$ and m²/s for $\nu$.
>
> 2. **Sign Error in Bulk Modulus:** The negative sign in $K = -V \frac{dp}{dV}$ is **critical**. Removing it gives a negative $K$ (meaningless for incompressible fluids).
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Cosine Term in Capillarity:** Students often write $h = \frac{4\sigma}{\rho g d}$, forgetting $\cos\theta$. For water-glass, this works (since $\cos 0° = 1$), but **for mercury-glass, this is catastrophically wrong** (mercury depresses, not rises).
>
> 4. **Confusing Specific Weight with Density:** Density is in **kg/m³**; specific weight is in **N/m³**. Mixing up these units gives 9.81× errors in head calculations.
>
> 5. **Not Stating "At 4°C" for Specific Gravity:** The standard reference for water density is **1000 kg/m³ at 4°C**, not at room temperature. Failing to specify this loses 0.5 marks in KTU valuation.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Bullet Points for Fluid Properties**
>
> ✅ **Density $\rho$** = mass/volume [kg/m³] — water at 4°C = 1000
> ✅ **Specific weight $\gamma$** = $\rho g$ [N/m³] — *not* kg/m³!
> ✅ **Specific gravity $S$** = ratio, dimensionless, water = 1.0
> ✅ **Specific volume $v$** = $1/\rho$ [m³/kg]
> ✅ **Dynamic viscosity $\mu$** = $\tau / (du/dy)$ [Pa·s] — decreases with $T$ for liquids
> ✅ **Kinematic viscosity $\nu$** = $\mu/\rho$ [m²/s] — used in Reynolds number
> ✅ **Bulk modulus $K$** = $-dp/(dV/V)$ [Pa] — measure of incompressibility
> ✅ **Compressibility $\beta$** = $1/K$ [Pa⁻¹]
> ✅ **Surface tension $\sigma$** = $F/L$ [N/m] — causes capillary rise
> ✅ **Capillary rise $h$** = $4\sigma\cos\theta / (\rho g d)$
> ✅ **Vapor pressure $p_v$** = function of $T$ — controls cavitation
> ✅ **Newton's law** = $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ — foundation of viscous flow
> ✅ **Newtonian fluids** = constant $\mu$; **Non-Newtonian** = variable $\mu$
> ✅ **Liquids** become **less viscous** with temperature; **Gases** become **more viscous**
> ✅ **Water at 4°C** is the universal reference: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $S = 1.0$, $\gamma = 9810$ N/m³
> ✅ **Capillary action** requires $\theta < 90°$ for rise, $\theta > 90°$ for depression
> ✅ **Cavitation** = local pressure $\leq p_v$ → pump impeller damage
> ✅ **NPSH calculation** in pumps uses vapor pressure directly
> ✅ **Memorize dimensions**: $[\rho] = ML^{-3}$, $[\mu] = ML^{-1}T^{-1}$, $[\nu] = L^{2}T^{-1}$
> ✅ **All real fluids** have some viscosity; the **ideal fluid** is a useful theoretical abstraction with $\mu = 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Note:** Module 1 questions on Fluid Properties typically appear as **3-mark short questions** in Part A and as **supporting sub-parts in 14-mark derivations** in Part B. Direct "list the properties" questions are rare — examiners expect you to **apply** these properties to pump-related scenarios. Always connect your answer back to a pump or hydraulic system context wherever possible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Flow: Laminar, Turbulent, and Transitional
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Types of Flow: Laminar, Turbulent, and Transitional ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) In **fluid mechanics**, the **type of flow** refers to the manner in which fluid particles move relative to one another within a conduit or around a body. The classification is fundamentally governed by the **Reynolds Number (Re)**, a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** acting on the fluid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEAGT522, Module 1):** > The student must be able to classify flow regimes using Reynolds Number criteria, describe the physical characteristics of each regime, and recognize the significance of the **critical Reynolds Number** in pump and hydraulic system design. The three principal classifications are: | Flow Type | Reynolds Number Range | Physical Behavior | |-----------|----------------------|-------------------| | **Laminar** | $Re < 2000$ | Smooth, parallel, layered streamlines | | **Transitional** | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ | Unstable; intermittent bursts of turbulence | | **Turbulent** | $Re > 4000$ | Chaotic, irregular, mixing eddies | > [!NOTE] > These threshold values apply specifically to **flow in circular pipes**. The critical Reynolds number varies for flow over flat plates, in non-circular ducts, and in open channels. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Traffic on a Narrow Lane" Imagine a **single-lane village road** during the early morning hours. - **Laminar Flow** is like cars moving in a perfect single-file line at the same speed. Each vehicle (fluid particle) follows the one ahead without overtaking or crossing paths. The motion is highly **orderly** and **predictable**. - **Transitional Flow** is like that same road when a few impatient drivers begin to overtake, but the road is narrow and conditions keep fluctuating. Sometimes order returns, sometimes chaos spikes. - **Turbulent Flow** is like a **peak-hour city traffic jam** with vehicles weaving in all directions, honking, accelerating, and decelerating unpredictably. The bulk movement is forward, but at the microscopic level, everything is swirling and mixing violently. In a pipe, the **viscosity** of the fluid acts like the **discipline of the drivers** — higher viscosity (think: thick oil vs. water) promotes order, while higher velocity (think: racing cars) promotes chaos. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Kinematic Viscosity of Water at 20 °C:** $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ - **Dynamic Viscosity of Water at 20 °C:** $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ - **Standard Acceleration due to Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m}/\text{s}^2$ - **Density of Water at 4 °C:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg}/\text{m}^3$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Reynolds Number — The Master Key Osborne Reynolds (1883) demonstrated that the flow regime is not determined by velocity or pipe size alone, but by the **ratio** of inertial to viscous forces. **Why does this ratio matter?** - **Inertial forces** ($\rho V^2$) try to keep the fluid moving and create disturbances. - **Viscous forces** ($\mu V / D$) try to dampen disturbances and maintain order. When inertia dominates ($Re$ large), eddies and mixing prevail — **turbulence**. When viscosity dominates ($Re$ small), disturbances are suppressed — **laminar flow**. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Flow Classification 1. **Identify the characteristic length scale** ($D$ for circular pipe, $L$ for flat plate, $4R_h$ for non-circular ducts). 2. **Compute the Reynolds Number** using the appropriate formula. 3. **Compare with critical values** (2000 and 4000 for pipes). 4. **Classify** as Laminar, Transitional, or Turbulent. 5. **Use the corresponding friction factor equation** (Darcy-Weisbach $f$ or Fanning friction factor $f'$) for head loss calculations. ### 2.3 Characteristics of Each Flow Type #### (A) Laminar Flow - Fluid particles move in **parallel, non-intersecting layers (laminae)**. - **Velocity profile** is **parabolic** — maximum at centerline, zero at the wall (no-slip condition). - **Shear stress** is linearly proportional to velocity gradient: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$. - **Mixing** at the molecular level only — no macroscopic mixing. - Friction factor follows **Hagen-Poiseuille law**: $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$. #### (B) Transitional Flow - **Unstable and intermittent**; small disturbances grow. - Flow may flip between laminar and turbulent patterns. - **Empirical friction factor** is unpredictable; design codes mandate a conservative turbulent assumption. - Often called the **"danger zone"** because small changes in $Re$ cause large changes in pressure drop. #### (C) Turbulent Flow - **Random, three-dimensional, chaotic motion** of fluid particles. - **Velocity profile** is much **flatter** in the core, with a steep gradient near the wall (the "viscous sublayer"). - **Mixing is intense** — momentum, heat, and mass transfer are dramatically enhanced. - A small **laminar sublayer** exists right at the pipe wall where viscous effects dominate. - Friction factor is given by the **Colebrook-White equation** (implicit) or the **Haaland equation** (explicit approximation). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Valid Range / Units | |---|---------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (dynamic form) | Dimensionless | | 2 | $Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (kinematic form) | Dimensionless | | 3 | $Re = \dfrac{4 \rho Q}{\pi D \mu}$ | In terms of volumetric flow rate $Q$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Average velocity in circular pipe | m/s | | 5 | $Re < 2000$ | **Laminar** flow criterion | Circular pipe | | 6 | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ | **Transitional** flow criterion | Circular pipe | | 7 | $Re > 4000$ | **Turbulent** flow criterion | Circular pipe | | 8 | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Darcy friction factor (laminar) | $Re < 2000$ | | 9 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$ | Colebrook-White equation | Turbulent, $Re > 4000$ | | 10 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \dfrac{6.9}{Re}\right]$ | Haaland equation (explicit) | Turbulent, approximate | | 11 | $V_{max} = 2 V_{avg}$ | Max-to-average velocity ratio | Laminar parabolic profile | | 12 | $V_{max} \approx (1.02 \text{ to } 1.20)\, V_{avg}$ | Max-to-average velocity ratio | Turbulent (Re-dependent) | | 13 | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Darcy-Weisbach head loss | All flow regimes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** > In pump engineering, the Reynolds number determines (i) the friction losses in suction and delivery piping, (ii) the **NPSH available**, (iii) the type of pump impeller (radial, mixed, axial) suited to the system, and (iv) the **cavitation risk**. Misclassifying a flow regime can lead to **gross underestimation of head loss** and pump failure. ### 2.5 The Reynolds Experiment — Historical Foundation Osborne Reynolds (1883) injected a **thin filament of colored dye** into water flowing through a **transparent glass tube**. At low velocities, the dye traveled as a **straight, unbroken line** — **laminar flow**. As velocity increased, the dye filament began to waver and finally **broke into eddies** — **turbulent flow**. The transition occurred at a specific, reproducible velocity that he defined in dimensionless form as $Re \approx 2000$. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Number from Dimensional Analysis The Reynolds number is fundamentally a dimensionless group. Let us derive it from first principles. **Step 1: Identify governing variables.** For flow in a pipe, the pressure drop $\Delta p$ depends on: - Pipe diameter: $D$ [L] - Average velocity: $V$ [L T⁻¹] - Fluid density: $\rho$ [M L⁻³] - Fluid dynamic viscosity: $\mu$ [M L⁻¹ T⁻¹] - Pipe length: $L$ [L] - Pipe roughness: $\varepsilon$ [L] **Step 2: Apply Buckingham's $\pi$-theorem** (using $D$, $V$, $\mu$ as repeating variables). The dimensionless groups that emerge are: $$ \Pi_1 = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = Re $$ $$ \Pi_2 = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2} = \text{Euler Number (Eu)} $$ $$ \Pi_3 = \frac{L}{D} = \text{Geometric ratio} $$ $$ \Pi_4 = \frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \text{Relative roughness} $$ The functional relationship is therefore: $$ Eu = f(Re,\ L/D,\ \varepsilon/D) $$ **Step 3: Interpretation.** $Re$ is the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces. This is the key dimensionless group that determines the flow regime. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Laminar Velocity Profile (Hagen-Poiseuille Flow) Consider steady, fully-developed, incompressible laminar flow in a horizontal circular pipe of radius $R$. **Step 1: Apply Newton's Second Law to a cylindrical fluid element** of radius $r$, length $dx$. The net pressure force pushes the element forward, balanced by the shear force on its curved surface. $$ \Delta p \cdot \pi r^2 = -\tau \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dx $$ **Step 2: Substitute Newton's law of viscosity** for a Newtonian fluid: $$ \tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr} $$ The negative sign indicates that velocity decreases as $r$ increases (toward the wall). **Step 3: Combine the two equations:** $$ \Delta p \cdot \pi r^2 = -\left(-\mu \frac{du}{dr}\right) \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dx $$ $$ \Delta p \cdot \pi r^2 = 2\pi r \mu \frac{du}{dr} \, dx $$ **Step 4: Let the pressure gradient be constant** $\left(\dfrac{\Delta p}{dx} = \text{constant}\right)$. Rearranging: $$ \frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta p}{2 \mu} \cdot \frac{r}{dx} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) r $$ **Step 5: Integrate with respect to $r$:** $$ u(r) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) \frac{r^2}{2} + C $$ $$ u(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) r^2 + C $$ **Step 6: Apply the no-slip boundary condition** at the wall: $u(R) = 0$. $$ 0 = \frac{1}{4\mu}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) R^2 + C $$ $$ C = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) R^2 $$ **Step 7: Substitute back to obtain the parabolic velocity profile:** $$ \boxed{u(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right)\left(R^2 - r^2\right)} $$ This is the classical **Hagen-Poiseuille parabolic profile**. Note that the pressure gradient is negative (pressure drops in the direction of flow), and $u$ is positive. **Step 8: Find the maximum velocity** at the centerline ($r = 0$): $$ u_{max} = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) R^2 $$ **Step 9: Find the average velocity** by integrating over the cross-section: $$ V_{avg} = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr $$ Substituting and integrating: $$ V_{avg} = \frac{2}{R^2} \int_0^R \left[-\frac{1}{4\mu}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right)(R^2 - r^2)\right] r \, dr $$ $$ V_{avg} = -\frac{1}{2\mu R^2}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) \, dr $$ $$ V_{avg} = -\frac{1}{2\mu R^2}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) \left[\frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R $$ $$ V_{avg} = -\frac{1}{2\mu R^2}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) \left[\frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right] $$ $$ V_{avg} = -\frac{1}{2\mu R^2}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} $$ $$ V_{avg} = -\frac{R^2}{8\mu}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) $$ **Step 10: Establish the relationship** between $V_{avg}$ and $u_{max}$: $$ \boxed{V_{avg} = \frac{u_{max}}{2} \quad \text{or} \quad u_{max} = 2\, V_{avg}} $$ This is a **defining signature of laminar flow** and is a frequently asked question in KTU examinations. ### 3.3 Derivation of the Darcy Friction Factor for Laminar Flow The wall shear stress from the equilibrium equation is: $$ \tau_w = -\frac{R}{2}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) $$ The Darcy-Weisbach equation defines the friction factor: $$ \tau_w = f \cdot \frac{\rho V_{avg}^2}{8} $$ Equating the two expressions and using the Hagen-Poiseuille result for $V_{avg}$: $$ -\frac{R}{2}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) = f \cdot \frac{\rho V_{avg}^2}{8} $$ Substituting $V_{avg} = -\dfrac{R^2}{8\mu}\left(\dfrac{\Delta p}{dx}\right)$: After algebraic manipulation (substituting $\Delta p/dx$ and simplifying), the celebrated result is: $$ \boxed{f = \frac{64}{Re}} $$ This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille law for friction factor in laminar flow** — valid only for $Re < 2000$. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Laminar | Transitional | Turbulent | |----------|---------|--------------|-----------| | **Velocity profile** | Parabolic | Distorted, in-between | Flat, blunt (logarithmic near wall) | | **$V_{max}/V_{avg}$** | $2.00$ | $1.20$ to $1.50$ | $\approx 1.02$ to $1.20$ | | **Friction factor $f$** | $f = 64/Re$ | Unreliable (use turbulent) | Colebrook-White / Haaland | | **Pressure drop** | Linear in $V$ | Non-linear, fluctuating | Approximately $\propto V^{1.75}$ to $V^2$ | | **Mixing** | Molecular only | Incipient macroscopic | Vigorous macroscopic | | **Diffusivity of momentum** | $\nu$ | $>> \nu$ | $>> \nu$ (eddy diffusivity dominates) | | **Energy dissipation** | Low | Moderate | High | | **Design safety margin** | Tight | Conservative (use turbulent) | Standard turbulent correlations | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Flow Classification Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision logic** for classifying flow and selecting appropriate engineering correlations. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Pipe Flow Problem] --> B[Identify D, V, rho, mu, epsilon] B --> C["Compute Reynolds Number
Re = rho V D / mu"] C --> D{Re less than 2000?} D -->|Yes| E[LAMINAR FLOW] E --> F["f = 64 / Re
u_max = 2 V_avg
Parabolic profile"] D -->|No| G{Re between 2000 and 4000?} G -->|Yes| H[TRANSITIONAL FLOW] H --> I["Design with turbulent assumption
for safety margin"] G -->|No| J[TURBULENT FLOW] J --> K{Relative roughness known?} K -->|Yes| L["Colebrook-White equation
(iterative)"] K -->|No| M["Haaland equation
(explicit)"] L --> N[Compute head loss h_f] M --> N F --> N I --> N N --> O[End: Pump Selection and Sizing] ``` ### 4.2 Reynolds Number Decision Tree (Textual Schematics) ``` ┌───────────────────────┐ │ Compute Re = ρVD/μ │ └───────────┬───────────┘ │ ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────┐ │ │ │ Re < 2000 2000 ≤ Re ≤ 4000 Re > 4000 │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ ┌─────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ │ LAMINAR │ │TRANSITION│ │TURBULENT │ └────┬────┘ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ f = 64/Re Use turbulent Colebrook V_max = 2V̄ correlations or Haaland Parabolic (conservative) logarithmic profile velocity profile ``` ### 4.3 Velocity Profile Schematic (ASCII Block Diagram) ``` LAMINAR FLOW TURBULENT FLOW (Parabolic Profile) (Blunt Logarithmic Profile) Vmax ▲■■ Vmax ▲■■■■■■■■ │ ■■ │■■■■■■■■■■ │ ■■ │■■■■■■■■■■■ │ ■■ │■■■■■■■■■■■■ │ ■■ │■■■■■■■■■■■■■ │ ■■ │■■■■■■■■■■■■■■ │ ■■ │■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■ ──────┼──────────────■─────► r ──────┼─────────────────► r │ ■■■ │■■■■■■■■■■■■■■ 0 R 0 R 0 ≤ r ≤ R 0 ≤ r ≤ R Vmax = 2 Vavg Vmax ≈ 1.1 to 1.2 Vavg ``` ### 4.4 Reynolds Experiment Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Constant Head
Overflow Tank] -->|Gravity feed| B[Glass Test Pipe] B -->|Controlled flow| C[Discharge
Measuring Tank] D[Dye Injection
Nozzle] -->|Thin filament| B E[Control Valve] -->|Regulates velocity| B F[Observation Section] -.->|Visual study| B ``` **Operating sequence in the experiment:** 1. Open the **control valve** slightly → low velocity in the glass pipe. 2. Inject the **dye filament** at the inlet. 3. Observe: dye travels as a **straight, unbroken line** → **Laminar** ($Re < 2000$). 4. Slowly **increase the flow rate** by opening the valve further. 5. Observe: dye filament begins to **oscillate and waver** → **Transitional** ($2000 \le Re \le 4000$). 6. Further increase: dye filament **breaks into eddies and mixes violently** → **Turbulent** ($Re > 4000$). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]** Define the Reynolds number. What is its physical significance in classifying fluid flow regimes? **Model Answer:** The **Reynolds number** is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** within a fluid. For flow in a circular pipe, it is defined as: $$ Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu} $$ **Physical significance:** - It determines whether the flow will be **laminar, transitional, or turbulent**. - At **low $Re$** (viscous forces dominant), disturbances are damped → **laminar flow**. - At **high $Re$** (inertial forces dominant), disturbances grow → **turbulent flow**. - It is the **key similarity parameter** for dynamic similarity between model and prototype in hydraulic systems. The critical values for circular pipes are $Re < 2000$ (laminar), $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ (transitional), and $Re > 4000$ (turbulent). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]** Distinguish between laminar and turbulent flow in terms of velocity profile and the ratio of maximum to average velocity. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow | |---------|-------------|----------------| | **Velocity profile** | Parabolic (Hagen-Poiseuille) | Flat, blunt, logarithmic near wall | | **$V_{max}/V_{avg}$** | Exactly $2.0$ | Approximately $1.02$ to $1.20$ | | **Cause** | Strong viscous damping | Inertia dominates, eddies mix core | | **Mathematical form** | $u(r) = -\dfrac{1}{4\mu}\dfrac{\Delta p}{dx}(R^2 - r^2)$ | Empirical (power-law or log-law) | The parabolic profile in laminar flow arises because viscous shear stress ($\tau = \mu \, du/dy$) is balanced by the pressure gradient, leading to a second-order ODE whose solution is a parabola. In turbulent flow, **eddy mixing** flattens the core profile because momentum is transported across streamlines by turbulent eddies in addition to molecular diffusion. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** Derive the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for laminar flow through a circular pipe, and obtain the relationship between maximum and average velocity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A fluid of **dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.5 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$** and **density $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$** flows through a horizontal pipe of **diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm}$** at an **average velocity of $V = 2 \text{ m/s}$**. Determine: (i) the **Reynolds number**, (ii) the **type of flow regime**, and (iii) the **Darcy friction factor**. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution to Question A** ##### **Part (a) — Derivation** [See the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes.] The final results are: **Parabolic velocity profile:** $$ u(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right)\left(R^2 - r^2\right) $$ **Maximum velocity** at the centerline ($r = 0$): $$ u_{max} = -\frac{R^2}{4\mu}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) $$ **Average velocity:** $$ V_{avg} = -\frac{R^2}{8\mu}\left(\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) $$ **Key relationship:** $$ \boxed{V_{avg} = \frac{u_{max}}{2} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad u_{max} = 2\, V_{avg}} $$ **Hagen-Poiseuille equation for volumetric flow rate:** $$ Q = \frac{\pi R^4}{8\mu}\left(-\frac{\Delta p}{dx}\right) = \frac{\pi D^4}{128 \mu L}\,\Delta p $$ ##### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem** **Given:** - $\mu = 0.5 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ - $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ - $V = 2 \text{ m/s}$ **(i) Reynolds number:** $$ Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 2 \times 0.05}{0.5} $$ $$ Re = \frac{90}{0.5} = 180 $$ **(ii) Flow classification:** Since $Re = 180 < 2000$, the flow is **LAMINAR**. **(iii) Darcy friction factor:** $$ f = \frac{64}{Re} = \frac{64}{180} = 0.3556 $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023] **(a)** Explain the **Reynolds experiment** with a neat sketch. Discuss the observations that led to the concept of the critical Reynolds number. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Water at **20 °C** ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$) flows through a smooth pipe of **diameter $D = 100 \text{ mm}$**. The **flow rate is $Q = 15 \text{ L/s}$**. Determine (i) the average velocity, (ii) the Reynolds number, (iii) the type of flow, and (iv) the **maximum velocity** at the pipe centerline. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution to Question B** ##### **Part (a) — Reynolds Experiment** **Apparatus:** 1. A **constant-head overflow tank** maintaining a steady water level. 2. A **transparent glass test pipe** with smooth, bell-mouthed entry. 3. A **dye reservoir** with a thin nozzle injecting a colored dye filament along the centerline. 4. A **control valve** at the downstream end to regulate flow rate. 5. A **discharge measuring tank** with a V-notch or volumetric calibration. **Procedure and Observations:** | Stage | Valve Setting | Observation | Reynolds Number | |-------|--------------|-------------|-----------------| | 1 | Slightly open | Dye travels as a **straight, unbroken line** | $Re < 2000$ (Laminar) | | 2 | Partially open | Dye filament begins to **waver and oscillate** | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ (Transitional) | | 3 | Fully open | Dye filament **breaks into eddies**, mixes rapidly with water | $Re > 4000$ (Turbulent) | **Conclusion:** Reynolds demonstrated that the transition is governed by a **dimensionless parameter**: $$ Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} $$ and that the **critical Reynolds number** for circular pipes is approximately **$Re_{cr} \approx 2000$**. ##### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem** **Given:** - $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ - $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$ - $Q = 15 \text{ L/s} = 0.015 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ **(i) Average velocity:** $$ A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.015}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.910 \text{ m/s} $$ **(ii) Reynolds number:** $$ Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 1.910 \times 0.1}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} $$ $$ Re = \frac{191.0}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.906 \times 10^5 $$ **(iii) Flow classification:** Since $Re = 1.906 \times 10^5 \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully TURBULENT**. **(iv) Maximum velocity:** For **laminar** flow, $u_{max} = 2 V_{avg}$. However, since the flow here is **turbulent**, we use the turbulent correlation. A reasonable estimate is: $$ u_{max} \approx 1.15 \text{ to } 1.20 \, V_{avg} $$ Taking $u_{max} \approx 1.18 \, V_{avg}$ (typical for smooth pipes at high $Re$): $$ u_{max} \approx 1.18 \times 1.910 = 2.254 \text{ m/s} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors** — forgetting to convert mm to m, L/s to m³/s, or cP to Pa·s. Marks are deducted in **Part (i)** of numerical problems. > 2. **Applying $u_{max} = 2 V_{avg}$ blindly** — this is valid **only for laminar flow**. In turbulent flow, using $u_{max} = 2 V_{avg}$ is a **conceptual error** worth losing 2–3 marks. > 3. **Forgetting the no-slip condition** — always state $u(R) = 0$ in derivations; it is the **boundary condition** that uniquely determines the integration constant. > 4. **Mixing up Fanning and Darcy friction factors** — Darcy $f$ is 4× the Fanning $f'$. The KTU syllabus uses **Darcy**. > 5. **Critical $Re$ values** — quoting 2100 and 4000 (instead of 2000 and 4000) may not be penalized, but quoting wrong values like 1200 and 3000 will be. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Reynolds Number** $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ is the **dimensionless master parameter** for flow classification. - [ ] **Critical values for circular pipes:** $Re < 2000$ → **Laminar**; $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ → **Transitional**; $Re > 4000$ → **Turbulent**. - [ ] **Laminar velocity profile** is **parabolic**; **turbulent profile** is **flat/blunt** in the core with steep gradient near the wall. - [ ] **Laminar signature:** $V_{max} = 2 \, V_{avg}$. - [ ] **Turbulent signature:** $V_{max} \approx (1.02 \text{ to } 1.20) \, V_{avg}$ (increases with $Re$). - [ ] **Darcy friction factor for laminar flow:** $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$. - [ ] **Hagen-Poiseuille flow rate equation:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^4 \Delta p}{128 \mu L}$. - [ ] **No-slip condition:** $u(R) = 0$ at the pipe wall — this is the **essential boundary condition** for derivations. - [ ] **Newton's law of viscosity** for Newtonian fluids: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$. - [ ] The **Reynolds experiment (1883)** with dye filament injection established the **concept of flow regimes** and the **critical Reynolds number**. - [ ] In **transitional flow**, design codes mandate a **conservative turbulent assumption** for head loss and pump sizing. - [ ] **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ is temperature-dependent — for water it **decreases** with increasing temperature, which can shift a flow toward turbulence. - [ ] **Reynolds number is a similarity parameter** — model and prototype flows are dynamically similar when $Re$ matches (geometric similarity also required). - [ ] **Cavitation, NPSH, and pump efficiency** are all influenced by the flow regime in the suction piping. - [ ] In the **KTU exam**, always show **units** in every step of numerical problems; examiners allocate partial marks for **dimensional consistency**.

topics.title: Bernoulli's Equation and Its Relevance to Pump Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bernoulli's Equation and Its Relevance to Pump Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bernoulli's Equation (KTU 2024 Standard Formulation)**
> Bernoulli's equation is the **steady-state, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless), and along a streamline** statement of conservation of mechanical energy for a flowing fluid. In the context of **pump engineering**, it is the fundamental energy balance used to relate pressure head, velocity head, elevation head, and the energy added (or removed) by a pump between two points in a hydraulic system.

For a real fluid flowing through a pump system between section (1) (suction/reservoir) and section (2) (discharge/reservoir), the **Extended Bernoulli Equation with Pump and Friction** is written as:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_{pump} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

Where each term has the units of **head (meters of fluid)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** Head is always expressed in **meters (m)** of the working fluid, and energy terms per unit weight are universally used in pump engineering because the resulting "head" is independent of the fluid density in many dimensionless comparisons (specific speed, head coefficient, etc.).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Slide of Energy

Imagine a water slide in an amusement park:
- The **height of the slide** represents the **elevation head (z)** — stored gravitational potential energy.
- The **speed at which you slide down** represents the **velocity head (V²/2g)** — kinetic energy.
- The **water pressure pushing you** represents the **pressure head (P/ρg)** — flow energy.
- The **pump** is like a mechanical helper at the bottom of the slide that **lifts water back up** to restart the ride — this is the **pump head (H_pump)**.
- The **friction between your skin and the slide** represents the **head loss (h_L)** — energy lost to turbulence and viscous effects.

Bernoulli's equation simply says: **"The total 'mechanical energy content' of the water, plus what the pump adds, minus what's lost to friction, is the same at the top and bottom — energy is just being swapped between height, speed, and pressure forms."**

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Reference)

- **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²** (standard reference value used in all KTU numerical problems)
- **Density of water, ρ = 1000 kg/m³** at 4°C (assumed constant in incompressible flow analysis)
- **Specific weight of water, γ = ρg = 9810 N/m³**
- **Atmospheric pressure, P_atm = 101.325 kPa = 10.33 m of water** (used for open reservoir boundaries)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always convert all pressures to **gauge pressure** (P_gauge = P_absolute − P_atm) for open reservoirs; using absolute pressure in Bernoulli's equation with atmospheric reference produces an automatic cancellation of atmospheric head on both sides.

### 1.4 Why This Topic is Foundational in PEAGT522

Module 1 of **PEAGT522 — Pump Engineering and Hydraulic Control** lays the groundwork for every subsequent module. Bernoulli's equation is the mathematical backbone for:

1. **Determining Pump Head (H)** — the primary performance parameter of any pump.
2. **Computing Net Positive Suction Head Available (NPSHA)** — critical for cavitation prevention.
3. **Plotting System Characteristic Curves** — H_system vs. Q.
4. **Analyzing Hydraulic Networks** — series/parallel pump arrangements, multi-reservoir problems.

Without mastery of Bernoulli's equation, the student cannot solve a single KTU 2024 Part B numerical problem on pump systems.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions Underlying Bernoulli's Equation

The classical Bernoulli equation is derived under five restrictive assumptions. Every KTU problem must first verify these:

1. **Steady flow** — flow parameters (V, P, ρ) at any point do not change with time.
2. **Incompressible flow** — fluid density is constant (ρ ≠ f(P)). Valid for liquids at moderate pressures and for gases when Mach number < 0.3.
3. **Inviscid (frictionless) flow** — viscous effects are neglected in the streamline integration. For real systems, head loss h_L is added externally.
4. **Flow along a streamline** — the equation strictly applies between two points on the same streamline (extended to the entire flow only for irrotational flow).
5. **No shaft work or heat transfer across the control volume** — except for the pump head H_pump, which is added as an external energy input term.

### 2.2 Decomposition of Total Head — The "Energy Per Unit Weight" Concept

Each term in Bernoulli's equation represents **energy per unit weight of fluid (N·m/N = m)**, which is called a **head**:

| Head Type | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Energy Form |
|-----------|--------|------------------|-------------|
| Pressure Head | P/ρg | Pressure energy of the fluid | Flow energy |
| Velocity Head | V²/2g | Kinetic energy of motion | Kinetic energy |
| Elevation Head | z | Height above a datum | Potential energy |
| Pump Head | H_pump | Energy added by pump | Mechanical input |
| Head Loss | h_L | Energy lost to friction, fittings, etc. | Dissipated energy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by weight (ρg) and not mass (ρ)?**
> Engineers prefer "head" (meters) over "energy per unit mass" (J/kg) because the resulting quantity is independent of fluid density for gravity-driven flows, and pump performance curves are universally plotted as Head (m) vs. Flow (m³/s or L/s).

### 2.3 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Conceptual Flow

1. **Start with Euler's Equation of Motion** — Newton's second law for an inviscid fluid particle.
2. **Integrate along a streamline** from point 1 to point 2.
3. **Recognize that the integration produces three natural terms** — pressure work, kinetic energy change, and potential energy change.
4. **Add the pump head term H_pump** to account for external mechanical work added by the impeller.
5. **Add the head loss term h_L** to correct the ideal equation for real (viscous) flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Pump Head Term H_pump in Bernoulli's equation is NOT a "head loss" — it is a positive energy INPUT.** This is a common conceptual pitfall. The pump increases the mechanical energy of the fluid; head losses always decrease it.

### 2.4 Application of Bernoulli's Equation to a Pump System — The Two-Reservoir Problem

Consider the most common KTU exam scenario: **Water is pumped from a lower reservoir (point 1) to an upper reservoir (point 2)** through a suction pipe, the pump itself, and a delivery pipe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Simplification for Open Reservoirs:**
> - At the surface of both reservoirs: **P₁ = P₂ = P_atm** (gauge pressure = 0).
> - **V₁ ≈ V₂ ≈ 0** (large reservoir surface, negligible velocity).
> - The free surfaces are the reference points for the equation.

The simplified Bernoulli equation for this configuration becomes:

$$H_{pump} = (z_2 - z_1) + h_{L,total}$$

Where:
- **(z₂ − z₁) = Static Head (H_static)** — the vertical lift the pump must overcome.
- **h_L,total = Friction Head Loss + Minor Losses** in suction and delivery piping.

This is the **most commonly tested 14-mark derivation** in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | KTU Application |
|---|------------------|-------------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Ideal Bernoulli Equation | $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ | m | Frictionless fluid flow |
| 2 | Extended Bernoulli with Pump | $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_{pump} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | m | Real pump systems |
| 3 | Pump Head for Two-Reservoir System | $H_{pump} = (z_2 - z_1) + h_{L,suct} + h_{L,del} + h_{L,minor}$ | m | Standard KTU numerical |
| 4 | Static Head | $H_{static} = z_2 - z_1$ | m | Vertical lift only |
| 5 | Friction Head Loss (Darcy-Weisbach) | $h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | m | Pipe friction |
| 6 | Minor Loss (fittings, valves) | $h_m = K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | m | Elbows, tees, foot valve |
| 7 | Velocity Head | $h_v = \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | m | Kinetic energy term |
| 8 | Pressure Head | $h_p = \frac{P}{\rho g}$ | m | Pressure energy term |
| 9 | Power Delivered to Fluid | $P_{fluid} = \rho g Q H_{pump}$ | W (Watts) | Pump hydraulic power |
| 10 | Shaft Power (brake power) | $P_{shaft} = \frac{\rho g Q H_{pump}}{\eta_{pump}}$ | W | After efficiency |
| 11 | NPSHA | $NPSHA = \frac{P_{atm} - P_{vapor}}{\rho g} - h_{f,suct} - z_{suct}$ | m | Cavitation check |
| 12 | Energy Grade Line (EGL) elevation | $EGL = z + \frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | m | Total mechanical energy line |
| 13 | Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL) elevation | $HGL = z + \frac{P}{\rho g}$ | m | Pressure head line (no velocity) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bernoulli's equation is the **operational tool** in the following engineering domains:

- **Centrifugal Pump Selection** — Determining the duty point (Q, H) for a pump serving a building's water supply.
- **HVAC Chilled Water Systems** — Calculating the pump head required to overcome riser height and pipe friction.
- **Fire-Fighting Networks** — Ensuring the pump can deliver the required pressure at the hydrant nozzle.
- **Municipal Water Supply** — Designing main pumping stations between treatment plants and elevated service reservoirs.
- **Boiler Feed Pumps in Thermal Power Plants** — Pumping condensate against high pressure and elevation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation of Motion

We start with Euler's equation of motion for an inviscid, steady, incompressible flow along a streamline:

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} = \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$$

**Step 1: Rearrange to bring all terms to one side**

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} = 0$$

**Step 2: Recognize that each term is a perfect differential along the streamline s**

Since flow is steady and properties vary only along the streamline, the partial derivatives become total derivatives:

$$\frac{dP}{ds} + \rho V \frac{dV}{ds} + \rho g \frac{dz}{ds} = 0$$

**Step 3: Note that the term V dV/ds is the derivative of (V²/2)**

Using the chain rule: $\frac{d}{ds}\left(\frac{V^2}{2}\right) = V \frac{dV}{ds}$

**Step 4: Substitute the identity into the equation**

$$\frac{dP}{ds} + \rho \frac{d}{ds}\left(\frac{V^2}{2}\right) + \rho g \frac{dz}{ds} = 0$$

**Step 5: Group the differentials into a single total derivative**

$$\frac{d}{ds}\left(P + \rho \frac{V^2}{2} + \rho g z\right) = 0$$

**Step 6: Interpret the result — the bracket is a constant along the streamline**

$$P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z = \text{constant}$$

**Step 7: Write the constant in two-point form between section 1 and section 2**

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$

**Step 8: Divide the entire equation by the specific weight ρg to convert to "head" form**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step 9: Extend to a real pump system by adding H_pump and h_L**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_{pump} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Added by Pump (H_pump):** This term is added on the left side (upstream side) because energy is being added to the fluid as it passes through the pump.
> **Head Loss (h_L):** This term is added on the right side (downstream side) because energy is being removed from the fluid due to friction.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Pump Head for a Two-Reservoir System

**Problem Setup:** Water is pumped from a lower reservoir (free surface 1) to an upper reservoir (free surface 2) through a piping system comprising a suction pipe of length L_s, a delivery pipe of length L_d, and various fittings.

**Step 1: Apply the extended Bernoulli equation between points 1 and 2**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_{pump} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Step 2: Apply boundary conditions for open reservoir surfaces**

- $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$ → Gauge pressure: $P_1 = P_2 = 0$ (when using gauge scale)
- $V_1 = V_2 \approx 0$ (negligible velocity at large reservoir surfaces)
- $z_1$ and $z_2$ are measured from a common datum (often ground level or the lower reservoir surface)

**Step 3: Substitute boundary conditions into the equation**

$$\frac{0}{\rho g} + \frac{0^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_{pump} = \frac{0}{\rho g} + \frac{0^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Step 4: Simplify the zero terms**

$$z_1 + H_{pump} = z_2 + h_L$$

**Step 5: Isolate the pump head term**

$$H_{pump} = (z_2 - z_1) + h_L$$

**Step 6: Expand the head loss into its components**

$$h_L = h_{f,suct} + h_{f,del} + h_{m,minor}$$

**Step 7: Write the complete final expression**

$$H_{pump} = (z_2 - z_1) + f_s \cdot \frac{L_s}{D_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + f_d \cdot \frac{L_d}{D_d} \cdot \frac{V_d^2}{2g} + \sum K_i \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

Where:
- $f_s, f_d$ = Darcy friction factors in suction and delivery pipes
- $L_s, L_d$ = Lengths of suction and delivery pipes
- $D_s, D_d$ = Diameters of suction and delivery pipes
- $V_s, V_d$ = Flow velocities in suction and delivery pipes
- $K_i$ = Loss coefficients of fittings (elbows, valves, foot valve, etc.)

**Step 8: Express the velocity in terms of flow rate Q**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Step 9: Rewrite the complete formula in terms of Q (preparing it for plotting the system curve)**

$$H_{pump} = (z_2 - z_1) + \left[f_s \cdot \frac{L_s}{D_s} \cdot \frac{8}{g \pi^2 D_s^4} + f_d \cdot \frac{L_d}{D_d} \cdot \frac{8}{g \pi^2 D_d^4} + \sum \frac{K_i \cdot 8}{g \pi^2 D_i^4}\right] \cdot Q^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Curve Form:** This final expression is the **system characteristic equation**, which is a quadratic in Q: $H_{pump} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^2$. The intersection of this curve with the pump's H–Q curve gives the operating point.

### 3.3 Derivation of Energy Grade Line (EGL) and Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL)

**Step 1: Define the Total Head at any point in the flow**

$$H_{total}(x) = \frac{P(x)}{\rho g} + \frac{V(x)^2}{2g} + z(x)$$

**Step 2: Define the EGL as the locus of total head values**

$$EGL(x) = \frac{P(x)}{\rho g} + \frac{V(x)^2}{2g} + z(x)$$

**Step 3: Define the HGL by subtracting the velocity head**

$$HGL(x) = \frac{P(x)}{\rho g} + z(x) = EGL(x) - \frac{V(x)^2}{2g}$$

**Step 4: Note the vertical separation between EGL and HGL**

The vertical distance between the EGL and HGL at any point is exactly equal to the **velocity head** at that point. In a pipe of constant diameter (constant V), the EGL and HGL are **parallel straight lines**, separated by $V^2/2g$.

**Step 5: Account for head losses — the EGL drops**

$$EGL_2 = EGL_1 - h_L$$

The EGL is **always monotonically decreasing** along the flow direction in a real system (energy is being continuously dissipated). The HGL decreases by the same amount as the EGL in regions of constant diameter, and differs from EGL only when the pipe diameter (and hence velocity) changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 6 — The Effect of a Pump:** At the pump location, the EGL experiences a **sudden vertical jump** equal to the pump head H_pump. The HGL also jumps by the same amount (assuming the pipe diameter on both sides of the pump is the same). This jump is the visual signature of the pump in an EGL/HGL diagram.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Concepts to Equations

| Physical Phenomenon | Equation Used | Direction of Change | KTU Marks Weightage |
|--------------------|--------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Pipe friction | Darcy-Weisbach | EGL decreases by h_f | 3–4 marks |
| Pipe diameter change | Bernoulli (continuity) | EGL & HGL separation changes | 2 marks |
| Pump operation | Extended Bernoulli | EGL jumps up by H_pump | 4–5 marks |
| Open reservoir surface | Boundary condition | P = 0 (gauge), V = 0 | 1 mark |
| Closed pressurized tank | Boundary condition | P = tank pressure (gauge), V = 0 | 1 mark |
| Pipe exit to reservoir | Kinetic energy loss | EGL drops by V²/2g | 1–2 marks |
| Pipe entrance from reservoir | Kinetic energy gain | HGL drops by V²/2g, EGL stays | 1–2 marks |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Bernoulli's Equation in a Pump System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow of energy transformations** in a typical pump system, as described by the Extended Bernoulli Equation. This is the KTU-preferred schematic style for Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lower Reservoir Surface Point 1] -->|Static Suction Lift z1| B[Suction Pipe Entry]
    B -->|Velocity Head V1² over 2g develops| C[Suction Pipe Flow]
    C -->|Friction Loss hf_suct| D[Foot Valve and Strainer]
    D -->|Minor Loss K_fv| E[Suction Pipe Exit to Pump Inlet]
    E -->|NPSH Available Check| F[Pump Impeller Eye]
    F -->|Energy Added by Pump H_pump| G[Pump Impeller Discharge]
    G -->|Velocity Head V2² over 2g| H[Delivery Pipe Entry]
    H -->|Friction Loss hf_del| I[Elbows and Fittings]
    I -->|Minor Loss K_elbow| J[Delivery Pipe Exit]
    J -->|Exit Loss V2² over 2g| K[Upper Reservoir Surface Point 2]
    K -->|Static Discharge Lift z2 minus z1| L[Final Energy Balance]

    M[Atmospheric Pressure Patm] -.->|P equals 0 gauge| A
    M -.->|P equals 0 gauge| K
    N[Gravity g equals 9.81 m per s²] -.-> A
    N -.->|Acts on all elevations| L

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style K fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style L fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Follow the flow from A (lower reservoir surface, point 1) to K (upper reservoir surface, point 2). Each arrow represents a **change in head** — the sum of all changes equals zero when the closed loop of Bernoulli's equation is satisfied. The pump (between F and G) is the only component that **adds** head; everything else either dissipates head or simply reclassifies it between pressure, velocity, and elevation forms.

### 4.2 Energy Grade Line (EGL) and Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL) — Block Schematic

The EGL and HGL are graphical representations of Bernoulli's equation. The following Mermaid block describes the **functional architecture** of how these lines behave in a pump system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Reservoir1 [Lower Reservoir]
        R1A[Free Surface P1 equals Patm V1 equals 0]
    end

    subgraph SuctionSection [Suction Pipe Section]
        S1[Pipe Entry V1 squared over 2g develops]
        S2[Friction Drop hf_suct]
        S3[Minor Loss K_fv]
    end

    subgraph PumpBlock [Centrifugal Pump]
        P1[Suction Eye NPSH Check]
        P2[Sudden EGL Jump Up by H_pump]
        P3[Discharge Outlet]
    end

    subgraph DeliverySection [Delivery Pipe Section]
        D1[Pipe Entry V2 squared over 2g]
        D2[Friction Drop hf_del]
        D3[Elbow and Valve Losses]
        D4[Exit Loss V2 squared over 2g]
    end

    subgraph Reservoir2 [Upper Reservoir]
        R2A[Free Surface P2 equals Patm V2 equals 0]
    end

    EGL1[EGL Line Monotonically Decreasing] -.-> R1A
    EGL1 -.-> S1
    EGL1 -.-> S2
    EGL1 -.-> S3
    EGL1 -.-> P1
    EGL1 -.->|Jumps Up| P2
    EGL1 -.-> P3
    EGL1 -.-> D1
    EGL1 -.-> D2
    EGL1 -.-> D3
    EGL1 -.-> D4
    EGL1 -.-> R2A

    HGL1[HGL Line Equals EGL Minus Velocity Head] -.-> R1A
    HGL1 -.-> S1
    HGL1 -.-> S2
    HGL1 -.-> S3
    HGL1 -.-> P1
    HGL1 -.->|Jumps Up by Same H_pump| P2
    HGL1 -.-> P3
    HGL1 -.-> D1
    HGL1 -.-> D2
    HGL1 -.-> D3
    HGL1 -.-> D4
    HGL1 -.-> R2A

    style P2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px
    style EGL1 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
    style HGL1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visual Insight for KTU Exams:**
> 1. The **EGL** is the upper line — represents **total head** (pressure + velocity + elevation).
> 2. The **HGL** is the lower line — represents **piezometric head** (pressure + elevation only).
> 3. The vertical gap between EGL and HGL at any point = **velocity head** at that point.
> 4. The EGL always slopes **downward** in the direction of flow, except at the pump where it **jumps up vertically** by H_pump.
> 5. The HGL can sometimes be **below the pipe** (vacuum condition on suction side) — this is a critical observation for **NPSH analysis** in Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    StepB --> StepC[List All Known Quantities]

```

> **Use this block-flow as a checklist** for every KTU 2024 numerical on Bernoulli's equation. Skipping the "Cross Check Units and Sign Convention" step is the #1 reason for lost marks in Part B solutions.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Bernoulli's equation for an ideal fluid flow along a streamline. List all the assumptions involved.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Bernoulli's equation states that for a **steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline**, the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head is constant:

$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$$

**Assumptions (1 Mark for listing, 1 Mark for explaining):**
1. **Steady flow** — flow parameters do not change with time.
2. **Incompressible flow** — fluid density is constant.
3. **Inviscid flow** — viscous (friction) effects are negligible.
4. **Flow along a streamline** — the equation applies between two points on the same streamline.
5. **No external work or heat transfer** other than the pump head.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (–1 Mark):** Students often forget to mention that the equation is restricted to **flow along a streamline**. Writing "for any fluid flow" loses a mark.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the physical significance of each term in the extended Bernoulli equation used for pump system analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_{pump} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

| Term | Physical Significance |
|------|----------------------|
| P/ρg | **Pressure head** — energy per unit weight due to fluid pressure |
| V²/2g | **Velocity head** — energy per unit weight due to fluid motion |
| z | **Elevation head** — energy per unit weight due to gravity |
| H_pump | **Pump head** — mechanical energy added per unit weight by the pump |
| h_L | **Head loss** — mechanical energy dissipated per unit weight due to friction and minor losses |

> [!NOTE]
> **Each term has the dimension of length (meters)** and represents energy per unit weight of the fluid.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

**Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive Bernoulli's equation for a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers water at the rate of **0.05 m³/s** from a lower reservoir to an upper reservoir, with a static lift of **15 m**. The suction pipe is **10 m long, 100 mm diameter**, and the delivery pipe is **50 m long, 80 mm diameter**. Take the Darcy friction factor as **0.02** for both pipes. Calculate the **total head developed by the pump**, neglecting minor losses. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Write Euler's equation of motion for an inviscid fluid along a streamline s** (1 Mark)

$$-\frac{dP}{ds} = \rho V \frac{dV}{ds} + \rho g \frac{dz}{ds}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange and recognize perfect differentials** (1 Mark)

$$\frac{dP}{ds} + \rho \frac{d}{ds}\left(\frac{V^2}{2}\right) + \rho g \frac{dz}{ds} = 0$$

$$\frac{d}{ds}\left(P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z\right) = 0$$

**Step 3: Integrate along the streamline from point 1 to point 2** (1 Mark)

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$

**Step 4: Divide by ρg to get the head form** (1 Mark)

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step 5: List the assumptions** (3 Marks — 1 Mark each for any 3):
- Steady flow, incompressible flow, inviscid flow, along a streamline, no heat transfer.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Q = 0.05 m³/s
- H_static = z₂ − z₁ = 15 m
- Suction pipe: L_s = 10 m, D_s = 0.100 m
- Delivery pipe: L_d = 50 m, D_d = 0.080 m
- f_s = f_d = 0.02
- Minor losses: neglected
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1: Apply extended Bernoulli equation between lower and upper reservoir surfaces** (1 Mark)

$$H_{pump} = (z_2 - z_1) + h_{f,suct} + h_{f,del}$$

**Step 2: Calculate velocity in suction pipe** (1 Mark)

$$V_s = \frac{4Q}{\pi D_s^2} = \frac{4 \times 0.05}{\pi \times (0.100)^2} = 6.366 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate velocity in delivery pipe** (1 Mark)

$$V_d = \frac{4Q}{\pi D_d^2} = \frac{4 \times 0.05}{\pi \times (0.080)^2} = 9.947 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate friction head loss in suction pipe** (1 Mark)

$$h_{f,suct} = f_s \cdot \frac{L_s}{D_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{2g} = 0.02 \times \frac{10}{0.100} \times \frac{(6.366)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_{f,suct} = 0.02 \times 100 \times 2.065 = 4.130 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Calculate friction head loss in delivery pipe** (1 Mark)

$$h_{f,del} = f_d \cdot \frac{L_d}{D_d} \cdot \frac{V_d^2}{2g} = 0.02 \times \frac{50}{0.080} \times \frac{(9.947)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_{f,del} = 0.02 \times 625 \times 5.041 = 63.01 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Calculate total pump head** (1 Mark)

$$H_{pump} = 15 + 4.130 + 63.01 = 82.14 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7: State the final answer with units** (1 Mark)

$$\boxed{H_{pump} = 82.14 \text{ m of water}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Velocity Calculation (–2 Marks):** Students frequently use the same velocity for both pipes. **Suction and delivery pipes have different diameters** — compute V for each.
> 2. **Friction Factor Misuse (–1 Mark):** The Darcy friction factor f is dimensionless, but the ratio L/D has units of (m/m), so the result is dimensionless × (V²/2g) → gives head in meters. Do not multiply by g again.
> 3. **Sign Convention (–1 Mark):** H_pump is **positive** when the pump adds energy. The final answer must always be a **positive head**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Adapted]`

**(a)** Starting from the extended Bernoulli equation, derive an expression for the **pump head required to deliver water between two open reservoirs** through a piping system with suction and delivery pipes. State clearly the boundary conditions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pump is used to transfer oil (specific gravity = 0.85) from a lower tank to an upper tank with a **static head of 8 m**. The suction line has a length of **6 m** with a diameter of **75 mm** and includes a **foot valve (K = 5.0)**. The delivery line has a length of **30 m** with a diameter of **60 mm** and includes **two standard elbows (K = 0.9 each)** and a **fully open globe valve (K = 10.0)**. Take the friction factor as **0.025** for both pipes. The flow rate is **0.012 m³/s**. Calculate the **pump head in meters of oil**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Write the extended Bernoulli equation between reservoir surfaces (1) and (2)** (1 Mark)

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_{pump} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Step 2: Apply boundary conditions** (1 Mark)
- Open reservoirs: $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$ → gauge pressures = 0
- Large reservoir surface: $V_1 = V_2 \approx 0$

**Step 3: Substitute and simplify** (1 Mark)

$$H_{pump} = (z_2 - z_1) + h_L$$

**Step 4: Define the static head** (1 Mark)

$$H_{static} = z_2 - z_1$$

**Step 5: Expand the head loss** (1 Mark)

$$h_L = f_s \frac{L_s}{D_s} \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + f_d \frac{L_d}{D_d} \frac{V_d^2}{2g} + \sum K_i \frac{V_i^2}{2g}$$

**Step 6: Write the final compact expression** (1 Mark)

$$H_{pump} = H_{static} + f_s \frac{L_s}{D_s} \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + f_d \frac{L_d}{D_d} \frac{V_d^2}{2g} + \sum K_i \frac{V_i^2}{2g}$$

**Step 7: Note that Q appears via V = 4Q/(πD²), so H_pump = H_static + K·Q²** (1 Mark)

This is the **system characteristic curve equation** in its standard quadratic form.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- SG = 0.85, ρ_oil = 850 kg/m³
- H_static = 8 m
- Suction: L_s = 6 m, D_s = 0.075 m, foot valve K = 5.0
- Delivery: L_d = 30 m, D_d = 0.060 m, 2 elbows K = 0.9 each, globe valve K = 10.0
- f = 0.025 for both pipes
- Q = 0.012 m³/s
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1: Apply the derived formula** (1 Mark)

$$H_{pump} = H_{static} + h_{f,suct} + h_{f,del} + h_{m,minor}$$

**Step 2: Calculate velocity in suction pipe** (0.5 Mark)

$$V_s = \frac{4 \times 0.012}{\pi \times (0.075)^2} = 2.716 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate velocity in delivery pipe** (0.5 Mark)

$$V_d = \frac{4 \times 0.012}{\pi \times (0.060)^2} = 4.244 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate suction friction loss** (0.5 Mark)

$$h_{f,suct} = 0.025 \times \frac{6}{0.075} \times \frac{(2.716)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.025 \times 80 \times 0.376 = 0.752 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Calculate delivery friction loss** (0.5 Mark)

$$h_{f,del} = 0.025 \times \frac{30}{0.060} \times \frac{(4.244)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.025 \times 500 \times 0.918 = 11.47 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Calculate total minor loss (foot valve + elbows + globe valve)** (1 Mark)

$$\sum K = K_{foot} + 2 K_{elbow} + K_{globe} = 5.0 + 2(0.9) + 10.0 = 16.8$$

> [!NOTE]
> The foot valve is in the suction line → use V_s. The elbows and globe valve are in the delivery line → use V_d.

$$h_{m,suct} = 5.0 \times \frac{(2.716)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 5.0 \times 0.376 = 1.880 \text{ m}$$

$$h_{m,del} = (1.8 + 10.0) \times \frac{(4.244)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 11.8 \times 0.918 = 10.83 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7: Sum all components** (0.5 Mark)

$$H_{pump} = 8 + 0.752 + 11.47 + 1.880 + 10.83 = 32.93 \text{ m of oil}$$

**Step 8: State the final answer with units** (0.5 Mark)

$$\boxed{H_{pump} = 32.93 \text{ m of oil}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Velocity in Minor Loss Calculation (–2 Marks):** Each minor loss coefficient K is paired with the **velocity in the pipe in which that fitting is installed**, not the system velocity.
> 2. **Specific Gravity of Oil (–1 Mark):** When the fluid is **not water**, the head is expressed in **meters of that fluid**, NOT meters of water. The equation does not change — only the unit declaration.
> 3. **Datum Selection (–1 Mark):** Always take the lower reservoir surface as datum (z₁ = 0) for clarity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Sheet for KTU 2024 — Bernoulli's Equation in Pump Systems**

- ✅ Bernoulli's equation is an **energy balance per unit weight** of fluid — every term has units of **meters (head)**.
- ✅ The five assumptions: **Steady, Incompressible, Inviscid, Along a streamline, No heat transfer**.
- ✅ The **Extended Bernoulli equation** adds H_pump on the left and h_L on the right.
- ✅ For **open reservoirs**, set P = 0 (gauge) and V = 0 at both free surfaces — this simplifies the equation dramatically.
- ✅ **Static head** = (z₂ − z₁) = vertical lift between reservoirs. This is the **minimum** pump head.
- ✅ **Pump head** = Static head + Friction loss + Minor loss.
- ✅ Friction loss (Darcy-Weisbach): $h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$
- ✅ Minor loss: $h_m = K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$ — K depends on the specific fitting.
- ✅ Always use **gauge pressure** for open reservoir boundary conditions.
- ✅ System curve is a **quadratic in Q**: $H = H_{static} + K Q^2$.
- ✅ EGL and HGL are graphical interpretations of Bernoulli's equation — the **pump causes a sudden vertical jump** in both lines equal to H_pump.
- ✅ Power to fluid: $P_{fluid} = \rho g Q H_{pump}$ (W). Shaft power: divide by pump efficiency η.
- ✅ Use **g = 9.81 m/s²** and **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³** unless otherwise specified.
- ✅ For oils and other liquids, express head in **meters of that liquid**, not meters of water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this topic thoroughly — every subsequent module of PEAGT522 (NPSH, system curves, pump performance, cavitation, hydraulic control) builds directly on Bernoulli's equation.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipe Friction Losses and Head Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pipe Friction Losses and Head Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pipe Friction Losses** refer to the irreversible loss of mechanical energy (pressure head, velocity head, or elevation head) experienced by a real fluid as it flows through a conduit due to viscous shear stresses generated at the fluid-wall boundary and within the fluid body itself. In KTU 2024 scheme fluid mechanics terminology, these losses are categorized into:

- **Major (Primary) Losses** — Distributed continuously along the straight length of the pipe due to viscous friction. Governed by the **Darcy-Weisbach equation**.
- **Minor (Secondary) Losses** — Localized losses caused by pipe fittings, valves, bends, sudden expansions, sudden contractions, entrances, and exits. Governed by the **Loss Coefficient (K)** approach.

The sum total of these losses, expressed in metres of fluid head, constitutes the **Head Loss ($h_L$)**, which directly subtracts from the available energy in the modified Bernoulli equation applied across a pipe system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to derive the Darcy-Weisbach equation, distinguish between major and minor losses, compute the friction factor using the **Moody Chart** and **Colebrook-White equation**, and apply the energy equation to practical pump-pipeline systems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are drinking a thick milkshake through a long, narrow straw versus a wide smoothie straw. The narrow straw requires much more sucking effort because the viscous resistance along the straw walls (and within the fluid layers) is greater. Now imagine inserting a sharp 90° bend in the middle of the straw — you need to suck even harder, not because of the straw's length, but because the flow must suddenly change direction, creating turbulence (eddies) at the bend. The "sucking effort" you feel is analogous to the **head loss** a pump must overcome.

In a real water distribution network:
- **Straight, long pipes** → Major losses (like the friction along the straw's length).
- **Elbows, valves, T-junctions** → Minor losses (like the extra effort at the bend).
- **Pump** → The "sucking device" providing the energy to overcome these losses and deliver water.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants & Units (SI System):**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
> - Density of water (at 4 °C): **$\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
> - Kinematic viscosity of water (at 20 °C): **$\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$**
> - Dynamic viscosity of water (at 20 °C): **$\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$**
> - Head ($h$): expressed in **metres of fluid (m)** or **metres of water column (mwc)**
> - Pressure: **1 bar = $10^5$ Pa = 10.197 mwc** (approx.)

## 1.4 Energy Equation Context — The General Energy Balance

For any two points (1) and (2) along a pipe carrying real fluid with a pump and/or turbine:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_{pump} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_{turbine} + h_L$$

Where:
- $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ = Pressure head
- $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ = Velocity (kinetic) head
- $z$ = Elevation (potential) head
- $h_{pump}$ = Head added by the pump
- $h_{turbine}$ = Head extracted by the turbine
- $h_L$ = **Total head loss** (sum of major + minor losses)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Holy Grail of Pipe Flow:** A pump engineer calculates $h_{pump}$ such that this equation is balanced. Thus, mastering head loss calculation IS mastering pump selection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major (Friction) Losses — The Darcy-Weisbach Equation

The Darcy-Weisbach equation is the universally accepted standard for computing frictional head loss in full-flowing pipes. It applies to **laminar, transitional, and turbulent** flow regimes uniformly.

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

Where:
- $h_f$ = Frictional head loss (m)
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless)
- $L$ = Length of the pipe (m)
- $D$ = Internal diameter of the pipe (m)
- $V$ = Mean flow velocity (m/s)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (m/s²)

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The original form used the **Fanning friction factor** ($f_{Fanning}$), which is 4× smaller than the Darcy factor ($f_{Darcy} = 4 f_{Fanning}$). KTU 2024 scheme universally adopts the **Darcy friction factor**.

## 2.2 The Friction Factor ($f$) — Determining the Heart of the Equation

The friction factor depends on:
1. **Reynolds Number** ($Re$) — Ratio of inertial to viscous forces.
2. **Relative Roughness** ($\varepsilon/D$) — Ratio of pipe wall roughness height to internal diameter.

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \text{Relative roughness}$$

### 2.2.1 Three Flow Regimes & Friction Factor Formulas

| **Flow Regime** | **Reynolds Number Range** | **Friction Factor Formula** | **Source / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laminar | $Re < 2000$ | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Exact analytical derivation (Hagen-Poiseuille) |
| Turbulent (Smooth) | $Re > 4000$ | $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ | Blasius equation (smooth pipes only) |
| Turbulent (General) | All $Re$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}}\right)$ | **Colebrook-White** (implicit) |
| Fully Rough Turbulent | $Re \to \infty$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right)$ | Von Kármán equation (asymptotic) |
| Transitional | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ | Use Moody chart interpolation | No single valid equation; undefined |

### 2.2.2 Moody Chart — The Engineer's Quick Reference

The **Moody Diagram** plots $f$ vs. $Re$ on logarithmic axes with curves parameterized by relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$. It is the standard graphical tool used in KTU board exams when an explicit friction factor value is not provided in the question data.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** If the problem gives a roughness value and Reynolds number but asks you to use the "Moody chart," simply read the corresponding $f$ value and proceed with Darcy-Weisbach.

## 2.3 Minor (Local) Losses — The Loss Coefficient Approach

Localized disturbances cause additional head loss:

$$h_m = K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

Where $K$ is the dimensionless **loss coefficient** for the fitting.

| **Fitting Type** | **Typical $K$ Value** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Sudden Enlargement | $K = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$ | Borda-Carnot equation (exact) |
| Sudden Contraction | $K = 0.5 \left(1 - \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$ | Empirical (approx.) |
| 90° Standard Elbow | $K \approx 0.9$ | Standard long-radius |
| 90° Long-Radius Elbow | $K \approx 0.3$ | Smooth bend |
| 45° Elbow | $K \approx 0.4$ | |
| Gate Valve (Fully Open) | $K \approx 0.17$ | |
| Globe Valve (Fully Open) | $K \approx 10.0$ | High resistance |
| Check Valve | $K \approx 2.5$ | Swing type |
| Pipe Entrance (Sharp) | $K \approx 0.5$ | Re-entrant: $K \approx 0.8$ |
| Pipe Exit (Submerged) | $K = 1.0$ | All kinetic energy lost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Equivalent Length Method:** Each fitting can be expressed as an equivalent straight length of pipe that would produce the same head loss:
> $$L_{eq} = \frac{K \cdot D}{f}$$
> The total effective length then becomes $L_{eff} = L_{straight} + \sum L_{eq}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Supply Schemes:** Designing pumping mains to ensure adequate residual pressure at consumers' taps.
- **HVAC Systems:** Sizing chilled water piping to minimize pump power consumption.
- **Oil & Gas Pipelines:** Long-distance transport of crude oil, natural gas, and refined products.
- **Fire Protection Systems:** Ensuring sprinkler and hydrant systems deliver the required discharge pressure.
- **Industrial Process Plants:** Sizing chemical transfer lines for reactors and heat exchangers.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Formula Name** | **Equation** | **Application / Regime** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | All flows | dimensionless |
| Darcy-Weisbach | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Major loss (all regimes) | m |
| Hagen-Poiseuille (laminar) | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | $Re < 2000$ | dimensionless |
| Blasius (smooth turbulent) | $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ | $Re < 10^5$, smooth pipes | dimensionless |
| Colebrook-White (implicit) | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$ | Turbulent, rough or smooth | dimensionless |
| Minor Loss | $h_m = K \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Local fittings | m |
| Borda-Carnot (expansion) | $K = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$ | Sudden enlargement | dimensionless |
| Equivalent Length | $L_{eq} = \dfrac{K D}{f}$ | Replacing fittings with pipe length | m |
| Total Head Loss | $h_L = h_f + \sum h_m$ | Combined system | m |
| Energy Equation | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_{pump} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | Complete system balance | m |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Darcy-Weisbach Equation (Physical Shear Stress Approach)

We begin from the principle that the frictional force on a fluid element must equal the available driving pressure force over a finite pipe length.

**Step 1:** Consider a horizontal cylindrical pipe element of length $L$ and diameter $D$, carrying fluid with mean velocity $V$. Let $\tau_0$ be the wall shear stress.

The shear force resisting flow on the inner wall surface (area = $\pi D L$):
