$$F_{shear} = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D L$$

**Step 2:** The driving force is the pressure drop across the length $L$. With cross-sectional area $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$:

$$F_{pressure} = \Delta P \cdot A = \Delta P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3:** At steady state, the driving force balances the resisting force (Newton's first law for the control volume):

$$\Delta P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D L$$

**Step 4:** Divide both sides by $\pi D$ and isolate $\Delta P$:

$$\Delta P = \frac{4 \tau_0 L}{D}$$

**Step 5:** Introduce the dimensionless friction factor $f$ defined such that wall shear stress is proportional to the dynamic pressure:

$$\tau_0 = f \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{8}$$

> **Why $\frac{\rho V^2}{8}$?** The factor 8 (not 2) arises because $f$ is defined to make the laminar equation $f = 64/Re$ come out cleanly. This is the standard convention for the **Darcy** friction factor.

**Step 6:** Substitute $\tau_0$ into the $\Delta P$ equation:

$$\Delta P = \frac{4 L}{D} \cdot \left( f \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{8} \right) = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

**Step 7:** Convert pressure drop to head loss by dividing by $\rho g$:

$$h_f = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

This is the **Darcy-Weisbach equation**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Friction Factor for Laminar Flow ($f = 64/Re$)

**Step 1:** For fully-developed laminar flow in a circular pipe, the velocity profile is parabolic (from Navier-Stokes solution):

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)$$

**Step 2:** The mean velocity is obtained by integrating across the cross-section:

$$V = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 3:** Carry out the integration:

$$V = \frac{2}{R^2} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 - r^2) r \, dr$$

**Step 4:** Expand the integral:

$$\int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) dr = \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{R^4}{4}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:

$$V = \frac{2}{R^2} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{8 \mu L}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the pressure gradient:

$$\Delta P = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^2}$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**.

**Step 7:** Equate this $\Delta P$ with the Darcy-Weisbach form $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$:

$$f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2} = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^2}$$

**Step 8:** Cancel $L$ on both sides, rearrange:

$$f = \frac{64 \mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{64}{Re}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The friction factor for laminar flow is **independent of pipe roughness** — only viscosity and flow conditions matter. This is a defining characteristic of viscous-dominated flow.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Borda-Carnot Loss (Sudden Enlargement)

Consider flow from a small pipe (area $A_1$, velocity $V_1$) into a larger pipe (area $A_2$, velocity $V_2$).

**Step 1:** Apply momentum conservation to the control volume between sections (1) and (2). Pressure forces act on areas $A_1$ and $A_2$:

$$P_1 A_2 - P_2 A_2 = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$$

where $Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$.

**Step 2:** Divide by $A_2$:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho V_2 (V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the steady-flow energy equation (Bernoulli with loss):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $\rho g$:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho \left( \frac{V_2^2}{2} - \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g h_L \right)$$

**Step 5:** Equate the two expressions for $P_1 - P_2$:

$$\rho V_2 (V_2 - V_1) = \rho \left( \frac{V_2^2}{2} - \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g h_L \right)$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the left side:

$$V_2^2 - V_1 V_2 = \frac{V_2^2}{2} - \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g h_L$$

**Step 7:** Isolate $g h_L$:

$$g h_L = V_2^2 - V_1 V_2 - \frac{V_2^2}{2} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = \frac{V_2^2}{2} - V_1 V_2 + \frac{V_1^2}{2}$$

**Step 8:** Recognise the perfect square:

$$g h_L = \frac{1}{2}(V_2 - V_1)^2$$

**Step 9:** Divide by $g$ and substitute $V_2 = V_1 \cdot (A_1/A_2)$:

$$h_L = \frac{(V_2 - V_1)^2}{2g} = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{2g}$$

Therefore:

$$K = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The lost energy is the kinetic energy of the *difference* in velocities — i.e., the kinetic energy that must be dissipated as the high-velocity jet decelerates to fill the larger cross-section.

## 3.4 Colebrook-White Equation — Implicit Friction Factor Resolution

The Colebrook-White equation is **implicit** in $f$ (appears on both sides). It must be solved iteratively. The KTU board expects you to:

**Step 1:** Guess an initial $f$ (e.g., use Blasius if smooth, or Von Kármán if rough).

**Step 2:** Substitute into RHS of Colebrook-White.

**Step 3:** Compute new $f$ from LHS.

**Step 4:** Repeat until $f_{new} \approx f_{old}$ (convergence tolerance: 0.5%).

**Alternative (Explicit Approximation):** The Swamee-Jain equation is explicit and within 2% of Colebrook-White:

$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} \right) \right]^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If the question says "using Moody chart," you simply **read off** $f$ — no iterative calculation is required and no marks are deducted for not using Colebrook-White explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pipe Flow Energy Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Energy at Inlet E1] --> B[Pump Head h_pump]
    B --> C[Available Energy for Transport]
    C --> D[Straight Pipe Section]
    D --> E[Frictional Loss h_f = f L V² / 2gD]
    C --> F[Fittings Bank]
    F --> G[Minor Losses sum h_m = Σ K V² / 2g]
    E --> H[Residual Energy]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Outlet Energy E2]
    I --> J{Pressure Satisfies Demand?}
    J -- Yes --> K[System Design Valid]
    J -- No --> L[Resize Pump or Pipe]
```

## 4.2 Moody Chart Topology (Friction Factor Selection Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Reynolds Number Re] --> B{Re < 2000?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Laminar: f = 64 / Re]
    B -- No --> D{2000 ≤ Re ≤ 4000?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Transitional: Use Moody Chart]
    D -- No --> F{Re > 4000?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Read ε/D from material table]
    G --> H{Smooth Pipe ε/D → 0?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Blasius f = 0.316 / Re^0.25]
    H -- No --> J[Use Colebrook-White or Moody Chart]
    J --> K[Iterate f to Convergence]
    K --> L[Apply Darcy-Weisbach]
    I --> L
    C --> L
    E --> L
```

## 4.3 Minor Loss Coefficient Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fittings and Local Losses] --> B[Direction Change]
    A --> C[Area Change]
    A --> D[Flow Control]
    A --> E[Entrance / Exit]
    B --> B1[90° Elbow K = 0.9]
    B --> B2[45° Elbow K = 0.4]
    B --> B3[Tee Branch K = 1.8]
    C --> C1[Sudden Expansion: Borda-Carnot]
    C --> C2[Sudden Contraction K = 0.5]
    C --> C3[Gradual Diffuser K = 0.2]
    D --> D1[Gate Valve K = 0.17]
    D --> D2[Globe Valve K = 10.0]
    D --> D3[Check Valve K = 2.5]
    E --> E1[Sharp Entrance K = 0.5]
    E --> E2[Re-entrant K = 0.8]
    E --> E3[Submerged Exit K = 1.0]
```

## 4.4 Head Loss Decomposition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Head Loss h_L] --> B[Major Loss h_f]
    A --> C[Minor Loss h_m]
    B --> B1[Continuous along length L]
    B --> B2[Proportional to L/D]
    B --> B3[Function of f, V, g]
    C --> C1[Localized at fittings]
    C --> C2[Function of K and V²/2g]
    C --> C3[Independent of fitting spacing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define: (i) Major head loss, (ii) Minor head loss, and (iii) Hydraulic gradient line.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Major Head Loss:** The head loss due to viscous friction acting continuously along the straight length of a pipe. It is computed using the **Darcy-Weisbach equation**:
$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$
This loss is *distributed* along the pipe and is proportional to its length.

**(ii) Minor Head Loss:** The head loss caused by localized disturbances such as bends, valves, expansions, contractions, and pipe entrances/exits. It is computed using the **loss coefficient** $K$:
$$h_m = K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$
The term "minor" is historical — in systems with many fittings, their sum can exceed the major loss.

**(iii) Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL):** The line joining the piezometric pressure heads ($\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + z$) at successive points along the pipe. It represents the height to which water would rise in a series of piezometer tubes connected along the pipe. The HGL always drops in the direction of flow due to head loss.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the Darcy-Weisbach equation. Explain how the friction factor $f$ is determined for (a) laminar flow and (b) turbulent flow in a rough pipe.**

**Model Answer:**

The Darcy-Weisbach equation for frictional head loss is:

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**(a) Laminar Flow ($Re < 2000$):** The friction factor is given by the exact analytical result $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$. It depends only on Reynolds number and is independent of pipe roughness.

**(b) Turbulent Flow in a Rough Pipe ($Re > 4000$, finite $\varepsilon/D$):** The friction factor is determined from the **Colebrook-White equation**:
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$$
This is implicit in $f$ and solved iteratively or read from the **Moody chart** using $Re$ and $\varepsilon/D$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Water at 20 °C flows through a horizontal commercial steel pipe of diameter 150 mm and length 200 m at a velocity of 3 m/s. Determine: (i) the friction factor using the Moody chart, (ii) the head loss due to friction, and (iii) the pressure drop. Given: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$, $\varepsilon = 0.046 \text{ mm}$ for commercial steel.** [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the Reynolds number:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 3 \times 0.15}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{450}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.491 \times 10^5$$

**Step 2:** Calculate relative roughness:

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.046 \times 10^{-3}}{0.15} = 3.067 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3:** From the Moody chart at $Re = 4.491 \times 10^5$ and $\varepsilon/D = 3.067 \times 10^{-4}$:

$$f \approx 0.0195$$

(Alternatively, solving Colebrook-White iteratively yields $f = 0.01948$.)

**Step 4:** Apply Darcy-Weisbach for head loss:

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.0195 \times \frac{200}{0.15} \times \frac{(3)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.0195 \times 1333.33 \times \frac{9}{19.62} = 0.0195 \times 1333.33 \times 0.4587$$

$$h_f \approx 11.92 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Compute pressure drop:

$$\Delta P = \rho g h_f = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 11.92 = 1.169 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.169 \text{ bar}$$

---

**(b) A pipe of diameter 200 mm carrying water at 2 m/s branches into two parallel pipes of diameters 100 mm and 150 mm, both 50 m long. The branches rejoin into a 200 mm pipe. If $f = 0.02$ for all pipes, find the discharge in each branch. The total discharge is 0.1 m³/s.** [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Continuity in the main pipe (200 mm diameter):

$$Q_{total} = A_{main} \cdot V_{main} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 \times 2 = 0.0628 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

(Note: This is inconsistent with the stated total of 0.1 m³/s; the problem implies we must use $Q_{total} = 0.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ as the design figure. We will use $Q_{total} = 0.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.)

**Step 2:** For parallel pipes, the head loss across both branches must be equal (since they share common start and end nodes):

$$h_{f,1} = h_{f,2}$$

$$f_1 \cdot \frac{L_1}{D_1} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = f_2 \cdot \frac{L_2}{D_2} \cdot \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

Since $f_1 = f_2 = 0.02$ and $L_1 = L_2 = 50$ m:

$$\frac{V_1^2}{D_1} = \frac{V_2^2}{D_2}$$

$$\frac{V_1^2}{0.1} = \frac{V_2^2}{0.15}$$

$$\frac{V_2^2}{V_1^2} = \frac{0.15}{0.1} = 1.5 \implies V_2 = V_1 \sqrt{1.5} = 1.2247 V_1$$

**Step 3:** Apply continuity:

$$Q_1 + Q_2 = Q_{total} = 0.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$A_1 V_1 + A_2 V_2 = 0.1$$

$$\frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 V_1 + \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 (1.2247 V_1) = 0.1$$

$$\frac{\pi}{4} \left[ 0.01 V_1 + 0.0225 \times 1.2247 V_1 \right] = 0.1$$

$$\frac{\pi}{4} \left[ 0.01 V_1 + 0.02756 V_1 \right] = 0.1$$

$$\frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.03756 \, V_1 = 0.1$$

$$V_1 = \frac{0.1 \times 4}{0.03756 \pi} = \frac{0.4}{0.1180} = 3.39 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate $V_2$:

$$V_2 = 1.2247 \times 3.39 = 4.15 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate discharges:

$$Q_1 = A_1 V_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 \times 3.39 = 0.0266 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_2 = A_2 V_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 \times 4.15 = 0.0734 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Verification:** $Q_1 + Q_2 = 0.0266 + 0.0734 = 0.1000 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** In parallel pipe problems, students often equate **velocity** instead of **head loss** — this is the most common error and costs 3-4 marks. **Remember:** parallel pipes share the same head loss, NOT the same velocity. Also, do not forget to verify continuity at the end.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Choice]

**(a) Derive the Darcy-Weisbach equation from first principles, clearly stating the significance of each term. A horizontal pipe 100 m long with diameter 25 cm carries oil ($\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.09 \text{ Pa·s}$) at a velocity of 1.2 m/s. Calculate the head loss and pressure drop.** [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Consider a cylindrical fluid element in a horizontal pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$, with mean velocity $V$.

The wall shear stress $\tau_0$ exerts a retarding force on the fluid over the wetted surface area $\pi D L$:

$$F_{retarding} = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D L$$

The driving pressure force acts over the cross-section $A = \pi D^2 / 4$:

$$F_{driving} = \Delta P \cdot A = \Delta P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Force balance at steady state:

$$\Delta P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D L \implies \Delta P = \frac{4 \tau_0 L}{D}$$

By definition of the Darcy friction factor:

$$\tau_0 = f \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{8}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta P = \frac{4 L}{D} \cdot f \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{8} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

Dividing by $\rho g$ to convert to head:

$$h_f = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Significance of Terms:**
- $f$: Friction factor (depends on $Re$ and $\varepsilon/D$)
- $L$: Pipe length (longer pipe = more loss)
- $D$: Pipe diameter (larger diameter = less loss)
- $V^2/2g$: Velocity head (higher velocity = exponentially more loss)

**Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Reynolds number:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 1.2 \times 0.25}{0.09} = \frac{270}{0.09} = 3000$$

Since $Re = 3000$, the flow is in the **transitional regime** ($2000 < Re < 4000$).

**Step 2:** For transitional flow, use the **Moody chart** interpolation. If a roughness is given (say $\varepsilon = 0.046$ mm for commercial steel), $\varepsilon/D = 0.046/250 = 0.000184$. Reading the Moody chart at $Re = 3000$ and $\varepsilon/D = 1.84 \times 10^{-4}$:

$$f \approx 0.044$$

**Step 3:** Head loss:

$$h_f = 0.044 \times \frac{100}{0.25} \times \frac{(1.2)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.044 \times 400 \times \frac{1.44}{19.62} = 0.044 \times 400 \times 0.0734$$

$$h_f = 1.292 \text{ m of oil}$$

**Step 4:** Pressure drop:

$$\Delta P = \rho g h_f = 900 \times 9.81 \times 1.292 = 1.141 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} = 11.41 \text{ kPa}$$

---

**(b) A sudden enlargement from a 50 mm diameter pipe to a 100 mm diameter pipe carries water at 30 L/s. Determine: (i) the head loss across the enlargement, (ii) the pressure rise in the larger pipe, and (iii) the loss as a percentage of the upstream kinetic energy. Assume horizontal pipe.** [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate velocities.

Cross-sectional areas:

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Velocities:

$$V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{0.030}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 15.28 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{0.030}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.82 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Head loss using Borda-Carnot:

$$K = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 = \left(1 - \frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}\right)^2 = (1 - 0.25)^2 = (0.75)^2 = 0.5625$$

$$h_L = K \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = 0.5625 \times \frac{(15.28)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.5625 \times \frac{233.48}{19.62}$$

$$h_L = 0.5625 \times 11.898 = 6.694 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Pressure rise using the energy equation (horizontal, no pump):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

$$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{V_1^2}{2g} - \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - h_L$$

$$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{(15.28)^2 - (3.82)^2}{2 \times 9.81} - 6.694$$

$$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{233.48 - 14.59}{19.62} - 6.694 = \frac{218.89}{19.62} - 6.694$$

$$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} = 11.157 - 6.694 = 4.463 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta P = \rho g \times 4.463 = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4.463 = 4.378 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} \approx 43.78 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4:** Percentage of upstream KE lost:

$$\% \text{ loss} = \frac{h_L}{V_1^2/2g} \times 100 = \frac{6.694}{11.898} \times 100 = 56.25\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:** Students frequently confuse which velocity to use in Borda-Carnot. The $K$ factor is computed with respect to the **smaller (upstream) velocity** $V_1$ when using the formula $K = (1 - A_1/A_2)^2$. If you use the smaller area as $A_2$, you must use the corresponding smaller velocity. Always cross-check that $K \le 1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Darcy-Weisbach equation** is the **universal standard**: $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$
- ✅ **Three regimes** based on $Re$: Laminar (<2000), Transitional (2000-4000), Turbulent (>4000)
- ✅ **Laminar:** $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ — independent of roughness
- ✅ **Turbulent smooth:** Blasius $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ (valid only for $Re < 10^5$, smooth pipes)
- ✅ **Turbulent rough/general:** Colebrook-White (implicit) or Moody chart (graphical)
- ✅ **Minor losses:** $h_m = K \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$; sum all $K$ values for fittings
- ✅ **Borda-Carnot:** Sudden expansion loss $K = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$
- ✅ **Parallel pipes:** Equal head loss, NOT equal velocity — flows distribute inversely with resistance
- ✅ **Energy equation:** Pressure head + Velocity head + Elevation head + Pump head − Losses = constant
- ✅ **Equivalent length:** $L_{eq} = \dfrac{KD}{f}$ — useful for converting minor losses to equivalent pipe length
- ✅ **Moody chart axes:** $Re$ (log) on x-axis, $f$ (log) on y-axis, parameterized by $\varepsilon/D$
- ✅ **Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL)** = $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + z$; drops in flow direction
- ✅ **Energy Gradient Line (EGL)** = HGL + $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$; parallel to HGL in constant-diameter pipes
- ✅ **Always use SI units:** diameters in metres, velocities in m/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- ✅ **Convert head to pressure:** $\Delta P = \rho g h_L$ when final answer is needed in Pa or bar
- ✅ **Water properties at 20 °C:** $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reynolds Number and Its Significance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reynolds Number and Its Significance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition and Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reynolds Number (Re)** is a **dimensionless quantity** (a pure number with no units) that is universally used in fluid mechanics to predict the **flow regime** of a fluid. Mathematically, it represents the **ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces** acting on a fluid element, and is given by:
>
> $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \, V \, D}{\mu} = \frac{V \, D}{\nu}$$
>
> where:
> - $\rho$ = mass density of the fluid (kg/m³)
> - $V$ = average velocity of flow (m/s)
> - $D$ = characteristic length (pipe diameter) (m)
> - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the fluid (Ns/m² or Pa·s)
> - $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of the fluid (m²/s)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Reynolds Number is classified as a kinematic similarity parameter**, alongside Froude Number, Mach Number, and Euler Number. It is a cornerstone concept in **hydraulic machinery and pump engineering** because it governs the head loss, friction factor, and overall hydraulic efficiency of conduits and impeller passages.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **"The River vs The Honey Jar" Analogy**
>
> Imagine pouring **water** and **honey** from the same height into two identical pipes. The water rushes forward in a chaotic, swirling manner — this is **turbulent flow**. The honey, being very "sticky", glides forward in smooth, parallel sheets — this is **laminar flow**.
>
> Reynolds Number is essentially a mathematical "judge" that decides *which behavior the fluid will adopt*. If the inertial push (the desire to keep moving and mix) is much stronger than the viscous stickiness (the internal friction resisting motion), we get turbulence (high Re). If the stickiness wins, the flow remains calm and layered (low Re).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Value (Water at 20°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density | $\rho$ | kg/m³ | **998.2** |
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | Ns/m² or Pa·s | **1.002 × 10⁻³** |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | m²/s | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶** |

These constants are critical for **pump selection**, **pipeline design**, and **head loss calculations** in hydraulic control systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Significance — The "Why" Behind Reynolds Number

The Reynolds Number captures a **force balance** at the microscopic level of fluid particles:

1. **Inertial Forces** ($\rho V^2$): These forces represent the *momentum* of the fluid — its tendency to keep moving and resist changes in direction. They promote **mixing and turbulence**.
2. **Viscous Forces** ($\mu V / D$): These forces represent the *internal friction* of the fluid — its resistance to shear deformation. They tend to **dampen disturbances** and keep the flow smooth.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** When **Inertial Forces > Viscous Forces** → Re is large → **Turbulent flow**. When **Viscous Forces > Inertial Forces** → Re is small → **Laminar flow**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for all numerical problems on Reynolds Number in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Boundary / Critical Value | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number (general) | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless | Flow regime prediction |
| 2 | Reynolds Number (kinematic form) | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless | Simplified calculation |
| 3 | Critical Reynolds Number (Pipe Flow) | $\text{Re}_{\text{cr}} \approx 2000$ | Transition zone: 2000 $\lt$ Re $\lt$ 4000 | Pipe design |
| 4 | Laminar Regime | $\text{Re} \lt 2000$ | Smooth, parallel streamlines | Viscometers, capillary tubes |
| 5 | Turbulent Regime | $\text{Re} \gt 4000$ | Chaotic, mixing flow | Water supply lines, pump impellers |
| 6 | Transition Regime | $2000 \lt \text{Re} \lt 4000$ | Unstable, intermittent | Avoid in design |
| 7 | Darcy Friction Factor (Laminar) | $f = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}}$ | Re $\lt 2000$ | Head loss in laminar pipe flow |
| 8 | Critical Velocity | $V_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{\text{Re}_{\text{cr}} \, \mu}{\rho \, D}$ | Used to find onset of turbulence | Pipeline design |
| 9 | Hydraulic Diameter | $D_h = \dfrac{4 A}{P}$ | Non-circular ducts | Duct and channel design |
| 10 | Average Velocity | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | From continuity equation | Volumetric flow rate problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** Critical Reynolds Number of **2000** and **4000** are the standard textbook values (Reynolds' original 1883 experiment). Modern smooth pipes can maintain laminar flow up to **Re ≈ 100,000**, but for engineering design, the conservative value of 2000 is used.

## 2.3 Significance in Pump Engineering and Hydraulic Control

1. **Pump Impeller Design**: The Reynolds Number inside the impeller passages determines whether the flow is laminar (high viscous loss) or turbulent (high inertia-driven loss). Centrifugal pumps typically operate in the turbulent regime.
2. **Cavitation Prediction**: The local Reynolds Number affects the boundary layer thickness on the impeller blades, influencing the onset of cavitation.
3. **Friction Loss in Suction and Delivery Pipes**: The Darcy-Weisbach equation uses the friction factor, which is directly a function of Re.
4. **Similarity and Scaling**: Model testing of pumps (per **Affinity Laws** and **similitude theory**) requires matching Reynolds Number to ensure dynamic similarity.
5. **Valve and Orifice Flow**: Reynolds Number governs the discharge coefficient ($C_d$) in valves used in hydraulic control systems.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

In practical engineering, Reynolds Number is the **first parameter** calculated before designing any hydraulic conduit. It dictates the choice of:
- **Pipe material** (smooth vs rough)
- **Pump type** (centrifugal vs positive displacement)
- **Flow measurement devices** (orifice, venturi, rotameter calibration)
- **Heat exchanger design** (laminar flow = poor heat transfer; turbulent flow = excellent)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Reynolds Number from First Principles

### Step 1: Define the Inertial Force
Inertial force per unit area arises from the fluid's momentum flux:

$$F_{\text{inertia}} \propto \rho \, V^2$$

### Step 2: Define the Viscous Force
Viscous (shear) force per unit area follows Newton's law of viscosity, with velocity gradient approximated as $V/D$:

$$F_{\text{viscous}} \propto \mu \frac{V}{D}$$

### Step 3: Form the Ratio
By the principle of dimensional analysis, the dimensionless group is:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{F_{\text{inertia}}}{F_{\text{viscous}}} = \frac{\rho V^2}{\mu V / D} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

### Step 4: Simplify Using Kinematic Viscosity
Since kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu / \rho$:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

This completes the **analytical derivation** of the Reynolds Number formula.

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Velocity

The critical velocity is the **upper limit of velocity** below which laminar flow can be sustained. It is obtained by setting $\text{Re} = \text{Re}_{\text{cr}} = 2000$:

$$V_{\text{cr}} = \frac{\text{Re}_{\text{cr}} \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot D} = \frac{2000 \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot D}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Water at 20°C flows through a smooth circular pipe of diameter $D = 50$ mm with an average velocity of $V = 2.5$ m/s. Given: $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Determine:
> (a) The Reynolds Number
> (b) The flow regime
> (c) The Darcy friction factor (assuming laminar flow)

### Solution:

**Part (a): Reynolds Number**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{998 \times 2.5 \times 0.050}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{124.75}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.2475 \times 10^{5}$$

**Part (b): Flow Regime**

Since $\text{Re} = 1.2475 \times 10^{5} \gg 4000$:

$$\boxed{\text{The flow is fully TURBULENT.}}$$

**Part (c): Friction Factor**

Because the flow is turbulent (not laminar), the laminar formula $f = 64/\text{Re}$ does **not** apply. We would use the **Blasius equation** for smooth pipes (Re up to $10^5$):

$$f = 0.316 \cdot \text{Re}^{-0.25}$$

$$f = 0.316 \times (1.2475 \times 10^5)^{-0.25}$$

$$f = 0.316 \times (1.2475 \times 10^5)^{-0.25} = 0.316 \times 0.01678 = 0.00530$$

> [!TIP]
> **Note for KTU Students:** Examiners often test whether students correctly identify that the laminar friction factor formula $f = 64/\text{Re}$ is **only valid for Re $\lt 2000$**. Using it for turbulent flow is a common error and results in **mark deduction**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Flow Regime | Re Range | Velocity Profile | Head Loss Behavior | Friction Factor Formula |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Laminar | $\text{Re} \lt 2000$ | Parabolic (Hagen-Poiseuille) | Linear with V | $f = 64/\text{Re}$ |
| Transitional | $2000 \lt \text{Re} \lt 4000$ | Unstable | Erratic | Use Moody chart |
| Turbulent | $\text{Re} \gt 4000$ | Flattened, logarithmic | Proportional to $V^{1.75}$ to $V^2$ | Blasius / Colebrook equations |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of how Reynolds Number governs the design of hydraulic systems. It maps the chain of influence from Re calculation to engineering outcomes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID PROPERTIES INPUT] --> B[DENSITY rho]
    A --> C[VISCOSITY mu]
    A --> D[GEOMETRY D]
    A --> E[VELOCITY V]
    B --> F[REYNOLDS NUMBER CALCULATION]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{RE COMPARISON WITH RE CRITICAL}
    G -->|Re less than 2000| H[LAMINAR REGIME]
    G -->|Re between 2000 and 4000| I[TRANSITION REGIME]
    G -->|Re greater than 4000| J[TURBULENT REGIME]
    H --> K[FRICTION FACTOR f = 64 by Re]
    I --> L[USE MOODY CHART]
    J --> M[BLASIUS OR COLEBROOK EQUATION]
    K --> N[HEAD LOSS CALCULATION]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[PUMP PERFORMANCE PREDICTION]
    N --> P[PIPE DIAMETER SELECTION]
    N --> Q[VALVE AND ORIFICE DESIGN]
    O --> R[FINAL HYDRAULIC SYSTEM]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[KTU EXAM APPLICATION]
```

## 4.2 Reynolds Original Experiment — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Constant Head Tank] --> B[Inlet Bellmouth]
    B --> C[Glass Test Pipe]
    C --> D[Flow Control Valve]
    D --> E[Discharge Tank]
    F[Injector of Dye] --> C
    G[Observation] --> C
```

**Description:** Water flows from the constant head tank through a transparent glass pipe. A thin stream of colored dye is injected at the inlet. At low velocities, the dye travels as a **single straight line** (laminar flow). As velocity increases, the dye **oscillates** (transition) and finally **mixes uniformly** (turbulent flow) throughout the pipe cross-section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **classical Reynolds experiment (1883)** that is frequently asked about in KTU viva voce and short-answer questions. Remember the year and the flow visualization principle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Reynolds Number. State its significance in pump engineering.**

**Model Answer:**
Reynolds Number is a **dimensionless parameter** defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

**Significance in Pump Engineering:**
1. It predicts the **flow regime** (laminar, transitional, or turbulent) inside pump impellers and connecting pipes.
2. It determines the **friction factor** and hence the **head loss** in suction and delivery pipelines.
3. It governs **dynamic similarity** in pump model testing, enabling reliable scale-up of prototypes.
4. It influences the **boundary layer behavior** on impeller blades, affecting the onset of cavitation.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is the critical Reynolds Number for flow through a pipe? Differentiate between laminar and turbulent flow.**

**Model Answer:**
The **critical Reynolds Number** for flow through a pipe is approximately **2000** (lower limit) and **4000** (upper limit of transition zone). It is the value of Re at which the flow regime changes.

| Feature | Laminar Flow (Re $\lt 2000$) | Turbulent Flow (Re $\gt 4000$) |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity profile | Parabolic | Flattened (logarithmic) |
| Fluid layers | Distinct, parallel | Mixed, chaotic |
| Head loss | Linear with V | Proportional to $V^{1.75}$ to $V^2$ |
| Friction factor | $f = 64/\text{Re}$ | Blasius / Colebrook formula |
| Mixing | Negligible | Strong |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must attempt **one full choice**.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Derive the Reynolds Number from dimensional analysis and explain its physical significance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The Reynolds Number is a function of fluid properties and flow geometry:
$$\text{Re} = f(\rho, V, D, \mu)$$

**Step 2:** The dimensions of each variable are:
- $[\rho] = M L^{-3}$
- $[V] = L T^{-1}$
- $[D] = L$
- $[\mu] = M L^{-1} T^{-1}$

**Step 3:** Assume the functional form:
$$\text{Re} = \rho^a V^b D^c \mu^d$$

**Step 4:** Substituting dimensions:
$$M^0 L^0 T^0 = (M L^{-3})^a (L T^{-1})^b (L)^c (M L^{-1} T^{-1})^d$$

**Step 5:** Equating exponents:
- $M$: $a + d = 0$
- $L$: $-3a + b + c - d = 0$
- $T$: $-b - d = 0$

**Step 6:** Solving simultaneously: $a = 1$, $b = 1$, $c = 1$, $d = -1$:

$$\boxed{\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}}$$

**Physical Significance:** Re represents the ratio of inertial to viscous forces. Low Re indicates viscous-dominated (laminar) flow, while high Re indicates inertia-dominated (turbulent) flow.

---

**(b) Oil with density 870 kg/m³ and dynamic viscosity 0.0724 Pa·s flows through a 100 mm diameter pipe at a rate of 0.0116 m³/s. Determine the Reynolds Number and the flow regime. If the pipe length is 50 m, calculate the head loss using the Darcy-Weisbach equation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Average Velocity**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.0116}{\frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.1)^2} = \frac{0.0116}{0.007854} = 1.477 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Reynolds Number**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{870 \times 1.477 \times 0.1}{0.0724}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{128.50}{0.0724} = 1774.86$$

**Step 3: Determine Flow Regime**

Since $\text{Re} = 1774.86 \lt 2000$:

$$\boxed{\text{The flow is LAMINAR.}}$$

**Step 4: Friction Factor**

$$f = \frac{64}{\text{Re}} = \frac{64}{1774.86} = 0.03606$$

**Step 5: Head Loss (Darcy-Weisbach)**

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{0.03606 \times 50 \times (1.477)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1}$$

$$h_f = \frac{0.03606 \times 50 \times 2.181}{1.962} = \frac{3.933}{1.962} = 2.005 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_f \approx 2.00 \text{ m of oil}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) Describe Reynolds' experiment to demonstrate laminar and turbulent flow. What conclusions did he draw regarding the critical velocity? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus:** A constant-head water tank supplies water to a horizontal transparent glass pipe. A thin stream of colored dye is introduced at the entrance of the pipe through a fine nozzle. A flow control valve at the discharge end regulates the velocity.

**Procedure and Observations:**
1. **Low Velocity:** The dye appears as a **single straight, unbroken line** through the pipe. The flow consists of parallel streamlines with no mixing between layers. This is **LAMINAR FLOW**.
2. **Increasing Velocity:** As velocity increases, the dye line begins to **oscillate and waver**. Small disturbances appear intermittently. This marks the **TRANSITION ZONE** (2000 $\lt$ Re $\lt$ 4000).
3. **High Velocity:** Beyond a certain velocity, the dye **disperses uniformly** across the entire pipe cross-section, indicating **intense mixing and chaotic motion**. This is **TURBULENT FLOW**.

**Conclusions Drawn by Reynolds:**
- The nature of flow depends on a **dimensionless parameter** combining $\rho, V, D, \mu$.
- He defined this as the Reynolds Number: $\text{Re} = \rho V D / \mu$.
- For pipe flow, the **lower critical Reynolds Number is approximately 2000**, below which flow is always laminar.
- The **upper critical Reynolds Number is approximately 4000**, above which flow is always turbulent.
- Between these values, the flow is **unstable** and can be either laminar or turbulent depending on entry conditions.

---

**(b) A centrifugal pump delivers water at 0.05 m³/s through a 75 mm diameter suction pipe and a 100 mm diameter delivery pipe. The kinematic viscosity of water is $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Determine the Reynolds Number in both pipes and comment on the flow regime. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Suction Pipe Velocity**

$$V_s = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{0.05}{\frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.075)^2} = \frac{0.05}{0.004418} = 11.32 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Suction Pipe Reynolds Number**

$$\text{Re}_s = \frac{V_s D_s}{\nu} = \frac{11.32 \times 0.075}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.49 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 3: Delivery Pipe Velocity**

$$V_d = \frac{0.05}{\frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.1)^2} = \frac{0.05}{0.007854} = 6.366 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Delivery Pipe Reynolds Number**

$$\text{Re}_d = \frac{V_d D_d}{\nu} = \frac{6.366 \times 0.1}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.37 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 5: Comments**

$$\boxed{\text{Re}_s = 8.49 \times 10^{5} \text{ and Re}_d = 6.37 \times 10^{5}}$$

Both values are **vastly greater than 4000**, indicating **fully turbulent flow** in both the suction and delivery pipes. The smaller diameter suction pipe has a higher Reynolds Number for the same discharge, which explains why suction pipe friction losses are particularly critical in pump system design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students often mix cm and mm with m in the Reynolds Number formula. Always convert **all dimensions to SI units** (meters, m/s, Pa·s) before calculation.
> 2. **Wrong Friction Factor Formula:** Using $f = 64/\text{Re}$ for turbulent flow is a **fatal error** worth **2–3 mark deduction**. The formula is strictly valid only for laminar flow.
> 3. **Forgetting Kinematic Form:** The simplified form $\text{Re} = VD/\nu$ is often expected when kinematic viscosity is directly given. Failing to use it wastes calculation time.
> 4. **Critical Value Confusion:** Writing 2300 (the boundary between laminar and turbulent in some references) instead of **2000** (the conservative KTU-recommended value) may lose marks.
> 5. **No Units Mentioned in Final Answer:** Reynolds Number is dimensionless — explicitly state "**Re is dimensionless**" in your answer to score full conceptual marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Reynolds Number**

- Reynolds Number is the **ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces**; it is **dimensionless**.
- Master formula: $\text{Re} = \rho V D / \mu = V D / \nu$.
- **Critical Re for pipe flow:** Lower = **2000**; Upper = **4000**.
- **Laminar flow:** Re $\lt 2000$ → smooth, parallel, parabolic profile.
- **Turbulent flow:** Re $\gt 4000$ → chaotic, flattened logarithmic profile.
- **Transition flow:** 2000 $\lt$ Re $\lt$ 4000 → unstable, avoid in design.
- **Laminar friction factor:** $f = 64/\text{Re}$ (Hagen-Poiseuille equation).
- **Turbulent friction factor:** Blasius ($f = 0.316 \text{Re}^{-0.25}$) for smooth pipes; Colebrook-White equation for rough pipes.
- **Reynolds Experiment (1883):** Uses dye injection in a glass pipe to visualize flow regimes.
- **Dynamic similarity in pumps** requires matching Re between model and prototype.
- **Hydraulic diameter** $D_h = 4A/P$ is used for non-circular conduits.
- Reynolds Number influences **cavitation, head loss, valve discharge coefficients, and pump efficiency**.
- Always state units explicitly during intermediate steps; final Re value is **dimensionless**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme questions on Reynolds Number are almost always worth **3 marks (definition) or 7 marks (numerical with sub-parts)**. Practice the two forms of the formula and remember the critical value of **2000** for the laminar-turbulent boundary.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Gradients in Pump Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hydraulic Gradients in Pump Systems # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In a **pump-piping system**, the **Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL)**, also called the **Piezometric Head Line**, is the locus of points representing the sum of the **pressure head** and the **elevation head** of a flowing fluid at every cross-section along the pipe. The **Energy Gradient Line (EGL)**, also known as the **Total Energy Line (TEL)**, is the locus of points representing the total mechanical energy per unit weight of the fluid (pressure head + velocity head + elevation head) along the pipeline. Mathematically, the two gradient lines are related as: $$\text{EGL} = \text{HGL} + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ where the term $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ is the **kinetic (velocity) head** of the fluid, and **g = 9.81 m/s²** is the acceleration due to gravity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** > Hydraulic gradients are the fundamental graphical tool used to visualize how **total energy is consumed** along the suction and delivery sides of a pump. They form the basis for the concept of **Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)**, system head curves, and pump selection. ## 1.2 The Three Reference Heads Every fluid particle in a pump system carries three forms of head: | Head Type | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Units | |-----------|--------|------------------|-------| | Elevation Head | $z$ | Height above a chosen datum | **m** | | Pressure Head | $\frac{p}{\rho g}$ | Pressure energy per unit weight | **m** | | Velocity Head | $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Kinetic energy per unit weight | **m** | - **Total Head (H)** at any section $= \frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z$ - **Piezometric Head** $= \frac{p}{\rho g} + z$ (plotted as the HGL) ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Roller Coaster" Imagine a marble rolling along a track with hills and valleys. The **EGL** is like the *total height of the marble's energy above a ground line* — it always starts high and keeps falling because energy is consumed by friction (the track's roughness). The **HGL** is the marble's height *without counting its spinning speed* — it is a little lower than EGL. At a **pump**, the EGL takes a sudden "jump" upward, just like a *boost from a motor* that gives the marble extra height to climb the next hill. **Friction** in pipes makes the EGL slope downward like a ramp losing altitude. **Static lift** is the unavoidable vertical climb from one reservoir to another. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module:** > - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²** > - Density of water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³** > - Specific weight of water: **γ = ρg = 9810 N/m³** > - Atmospheric pressure head: **≈ 10.3 m of water** (sea level) ## 1.4 Significance in Pump Engineering Hydraulic gradients serve three engineering purposes in KTU 2024 scheme coursework: 1. **Visualization** — Single diagram conveys where energy is being lost in a system. 2. **NPSH Assessment** — The vertical drop of the HGL below the free surface of the suction sump on the suction side directly determines whether the pump will cavitate. 3. **System Design** — Slope of HGL = rate of head loss per unit length; engineers use this to size pipes (steeper slope = higher pumping cost). # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Pump-Piping System A typical pump installation consists of the following elements, in the order encountered by the fluid: 1. **Suction Sump / Lower Reservoir** — water source. 2. **Suction Pipe** with: foot valve, strainer, entry loss, straight pipe friction. 3. **Pump** — adds energy to the fluid. 4. **Delivery Pipe** with: gate/globe valves, bends, exit loss, straight pipe friction. 5. **Delivery Reservoir / Upper Tank** — destination. The HGL and EGL must be drawn along the *entire flow path* — from the suction reservoir's free surface to the delivery reservoir's free surface. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — How the Gradients Behave ### (a) On the Suction Side (Lower Reservoir → Pump Inlet) - At the free surface of the lower reservoir: **V ≈ 0**, **p = p_atm**, so the HGL and EGL both coincide with the water surface. - As the fluid moves up the suction pipe, friction loss $h_{f,s}$ and minor losses cause the **EGL to slope downward** in the direction of flow. - The **HGL lies below the EGL** by exactly the velocity head $\frac{V^2}{2g}$. - If the pump is *above* the sump: the HGL must be *lower* at the pump inlet than at the sump (suction lift case). - If the pump is *below* the sump: the HGL must be *higher* at the pump inlet (flooded suction case). - The **vertical drop** of the HGL between the sump water surface and the pump inlet (accounting for friction) is the indicator of cavitation risk. ### (b) At the Pump - The pump **adds energy** $\Delta H$ to the fluid, equal to the pump head at the operating flow. - Both HGL and EGL exhibit a **sudden vertical jump** of magnitude equal to the pump head $H_p$ at the pump location. - The pump does not change the velocity head appreciably if the suction and delivery pipes have similar diameters. ### (c) On the Delivery Side (Pump Outlet → Upper Reservoir) - The EGL slopes downward due to friction $h_{f,d}$ and minor losses (valves, bends, exit loss). - The HGL again lies below EGL by $\frac{V^2}{2g}$. - At the exit into the upper reservoir, the velocity head is fully **lost** (exit loss $K = 1.0$), so the EGL and HGL *converge onto the free surface of the upper reservoir*. ## 2.3 Key Equation — Total Dynamic Head (TDH) The **Total Dynamic Head** is the net energy added by the pump per unit weight of fluid. Applying the energy equation between the suction reservoir free surface (point 1) and the delivery reservoir free surface (point 2): $$H_{TDH} = \left(z_2 - z_1\right) + \frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + h_L$$ Since both reservoirs are at atmospheric pressure and negligible velocity: $$\boxed{H_{TDH} = H_{static} + h_f + h_m}$$ where $H_{static}$ is the **static head** (vertical difference between reservoir water surfaces), $h_f$ is the **sum of friction losses**, and $h_m$ is the **sum of minor losses**. ## 2.4 Friction and Minor Loss Equations **Darcy–Weisbach Friction Loss (straight pipe):** $$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ - $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless) - $L$ = pipe length in **m** - $D$ = pipe internal diameter in **m** - $V$ = mean flow velocity in **m/s** **Minor Loss (fittings, valves, entry/exit):** $$h_m = \sum K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ - $K$ = loss coefficient (dimensionless) — specific to each fitting. ## 2.5 NPSH Formulation from Hydraulic Gradients **NPSH Available** is the vertical distance of the HGL *above* the vapor pressure head, measured at the pump suction nozzle: $$\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_{f,s} - h_{m,s} - \frac{p_v}{\rho g}$$ - $h_s$ = static suction lift (positive if pump is above sump) or static suction head (negative if pump is below sump). - $h_{f,s}, h_{m,s}$ = friction and minor losses on the suction side. **Cavitation Safety Criterion:** $$\text{NPSH}_A > \text{NPSH}_R$$ > [!NOTE] > The Hydraulic Gradient Line on the suction side must **never fall below the vapor pressure head line** of the fluid, otherwise flashing and cavitation occur. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Meaning | Standard Units | |---|----------|---------|----------------| | 1 | $H = \dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z$ | Total head at any section | m | | 2 | $\text{HGL} = \dfrac{p}{\rho g} + z$ | Piezometric head (HGL) | m | | 3 | $\text{EGL} - \text{HGL} = \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Gap between two gradient lines | m | | 4 | $H_{TDH} = H_{static} + h_f + h_m$ | Total dynamic head developed by pump | m | | 5 | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | m | | 6 | $h_m = \Sigma K \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Minor loss through fittings | m | | 7 | $\text{NPSH}_A = \dfrac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_{f,s} - h_{m,s} - \dfrac{p_v}{\rho g}$ | Net positive suction head available | m | | 8 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_{TDH}}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Pump input (shaft) power | W | | 9 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Mean velocity from discharge | m/s | | 10 | $\text{Slope of HGL} = \dfrac{h_f}{L} = \dfrac{f V^2}{2gD}$ | Hydraulic gradient | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In KTU answer scripts, never use the vertical bar $\vert$ inside a sentence or table cell for absolute value. Always write $\left|x\right\vert$ if absolute value is required, and use the formula symbols above as written. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Hydraulic gradient analysis is the *core design check* used in: - **Municipal water supply** schemes — sizing rising mains to overhead tanks. - **Industrial cooling water** circulation — preventing pump cavitation in hot systems. - **Irrigation pumping stations** — minimizing energy bills by choosing optimal pipe diameters (lower $h_f$). - **High-rise building boosters** — ensuring HGL stays above fixture pressure requirements on upper floors. - **Power plant boiler feed pumps** — strict NPSH margins to prevent costly cavitation damage. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — The Total Head Equation (Steady Flow Energy Equation) Consider an ideal incompressible fluid flowing steadily between section **1** (suction reservoir free surface) and section **2** (delivery reservoir free surface). Apply **Euler's equation of motion** along a streamline, and integrate from 1 to 2, adding the head added by the pump $H_p$ and the head lost due to friction and fittings $h_L$: $$\int_1^2 \frac{dp}{\rho g} + \int_1^2 \frac{V \, dV}{g} + \int_1^2 dz = H_p - h_L$$ Evaluating each integral: $$\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + (z_2 - z_1) = H_p - h_L$$ **Step 1:** Apply boundary conditions at the free surfaces of both reservoirs. At both surfaces, the pressure equals atmospheric pressure: $$p_1 = p_2 = p_{atm}$$ **Step 2:** Velocities at the free surfaces are negligible compared to pipe velocities: $$V_1 \approx V_2 \approx 0$$ **Step 3:** Substitute to simplify: $$\frac{p_{atm} - p_{atm}}{\rho g} + \frac{0 - 0}{2g} + (z_2 - z_1) = H_p - h_L$$ **Step 4:** Define static head $H_{static} = z_2 - z_1$ (vertical rise between reservoir surfaces). **Step 5:** Rearrange to express the pump head: $$\boxed{H_p = H_{static} + h_L = H_{static} + h_f + h_m}$$ **Conclusion:** The pump head equals the static head plus all losses in the system. This is the **fundamental equation of pump-system matching** used in KTU 2024 problems. ## 3.2 Derivation — Hydraulic Gradient Slope in a Straight Pipe Consider a uniform straight pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ carrying fluid at mean velocity $V$. Apply the steady-flow energy equation between the inlet (1) and outlet (2) of this pipe segment (with no pump or fitting): $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$ The velocity head cancels (since $V$ is constant for uniform pipe): $$\left(\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1\right) - \left(\frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2\right) = h_f$$ **Step 1:** Recognize that the left side is the drop in **HGL** from section 1 to section 2. $$\Delta \text{HGL} = h_f$$ **Step 2:** Substituting Darcy–Weisbach: $$\Delta \text{HGL} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3:** The **hydraulic gradient** $S$ is the head loss per unit length: $$\boxed{S = \frac{\Delta \text{HGL}}{L} = \frac{f \cdot V^2}{2gD}}$$ **Step 4:** Units check: $f$ is dimensionless, $V^2$ is m²/s², $g$ is m/s², $D$ is m. $$S = \frac{1 \cdot \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2}{\text{m/s}^2 \cdot \text{m}} = \frac{1}{\text{m}} \cdot \text{m} = \text{dimensionless} \quad \checkmark$$ (Since the LHS $\frac{\Delta \text{HGL}}{L}$ has units of $\frac{\text{m}}{\text{m}}$, it is dimensionless — sometimes called "head loss per metre of pipe" or expressed as **m/km** for clarity.) ## 3.3 Derivation — NPSH Available from Suction Side Hydraulic Gradient Apply the energy equation between the **suction reservoir free surface (point s)** and the **pump suction nozzle (point p)**: $$\frac{p_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + z_s = \frac{p_p}{\rho g} + \frac{V_p^2}{2g} + z_p + h_{L,s}$$ **Step 1:** Substitute boundary conditions. - At the free surface: $p_s = p_{atm}$, $V_s \approx 0$, and the elevation is $z_s$. - At the pump suction: pressure $= p_p$, velocity $= V_p$, elevation $= z_p$. **Step 2:** Define static suction lift: $h_s = z_p - z_s$ (positive if pump is above sump). **Step 3:** Rearrange to solve for the pressure head at the pump suction nozzle: $$\frac{p_p}{\rho g} = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} - h_s - \frac{V_p^2}{2g} - h_{L,s}$$ **Step 4:** NPSH available is the absolute pressure head at pump suction **above the vapor pressure head**: $$\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{p_p}{\rho g} - \frac{p_v}{\rho g}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute the expression for $\frac{p_p}{\rho g}$: $$\boxed{\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} - h_s - \frac{V_p^2}{2g} - h_{L,s} - \frac{p_v}{\rho g}}$$ For a **flooded suction** (pump below sump), $h_s$ becomes negative (i.e., a static suction head), and the term $\pm h_s$ improves the NPSH margin. > [!NOTE] > Notice that the **velocity head at the pump suction nozzle** $\frac{V_p^2}{2g}$ appears in the NPSH formula because the HGL at the pump is measured *after* the kinetic energy has been accounted for in the energy balance. This is a frequent source of marks lost in KTU scripts. ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example **Problem (KTU-Style Calculation):** A centrifugal pump draws water from a sump **3 m below** its centerline. The suction pipe is **8 m long, 100 mm diameter**, with a Darcy friction factor of **0.025**. The entry loss coefficient is **K = 0.5**. Calculate the **NPSH available** at a discharge of **20 L/s**. Assume atmospheric pressure head = **10.3 m** and water vapor pressure head = **0.23 m** at 20 °C. **Step 1 — Compute the mean velocity in the suction pipe:** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.546 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute velocity head:** $$\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{(2.546)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{6.482}{19.62} = 0.3304 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Friction loss in suction pipe:** $$h_{f,s} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.025 \times \frac{8}{0.1} \times 0.3304$$ $$h_{f,s} = 0.025 \times 80 \times 0.3304 = 0.6608 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Minor (entry) loss:** $$h_{m,s} = K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.5 \times 0.3304 = 0.1652 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Total suction-side loss:** $$h_{L,s} = h_{f,s} + h_{m,s} = 0.6608 + 0.1652 = 0.8260 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6 — Static suction lift:** $$h_s = +3 \text{ m} \quad (\text{pump is above sump})$$ **Step 7 — Substitute into NPSH equation:** $$\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} - h_s - \frac{V_p^2}{2g} - h_{L,s} - \frac{p_v}{\rho g}$$ $$\text{NPSH}_A = 10.3 - 3 - 0.3304 - 0.8260 - 0.23$$ $$\boxed{\text{NPSH}_A = 5.9136 \text{ m} \approx 5.91 \text{ m}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** If the manufacturer's NPSH required at 20 L/s is, say, 4 m, then the margin is $5.91 - 4.0 = 1.91$ m, which is acceptable. If NPSH required exceeded 5.91 m, the pump would cavitate and the suction pipe would need to be redesigned (larger diameter, shorter length, or pump moved closer to the sump). # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System Schematic with HGL and EGL The diagram below shows the **functional architecture of energy transfer** in a typical pump system. It maps the elevation and pressure relationships of the Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL) and Energy Gradient Line (EGL) along the suction pipe, the pump, and the delivery pipe. ```mermaid graph LR A["SUMP FREE SURFACE
V=0, p=p_atm
HGL and EGL coincide here"] --> B["SUCTION PIPE INLET
Entry loss K=0.5
HGL drops slightly"] B --> C["ALONG SUCTION PIPE
Friction loss h_f_s
HGL slopes down"] C --> D["PUMP SUCTION NOZZLE
Lowest HGL on suction side
Pressure = p_p"] D --> E["CENTRIFUGAL PUMP
Energy added = H_pump
HGL and EGL jump up by H_pump"] E --> F["PUMP DELIVERY NOZZLE
Highest EGL point
Pressure = high"] F --> G["ALONG DELIVERY PIPE
Friction loss h_f_d
HGL and EGL slope down"] G --> H["DELIVERY VALVE
Minor loss K_valve
Sudden HGL drop"] H --> I["PIPE EXIT
Exit loss K=1.0
Velocity head lost"] I --> J["UPPER TANK FREE SURFACE
V=0, p=p_atm
HGL and EGL coincide again"] ``` ## 4.2 Suction Side — HGL Behaviour (Detail) ```mermaid graph TD P1["DATUM LINE z=0"] --> P2["Pump Centerline
z = +3 m above sump"] P3["Sump Free Surface
z = 0, p_atm"] --> P4["Pump Suction Nozzle
z = +3 m, p_p"] P3 --> P5["HGL at Sump = 0
EGL at Sump = 0"] P4 --> P6["HGL at Pump = -h_s - h_L_s"] P4 --> P7["EGL at Pump = HGL + V_sq/2g"] P4 --> P8["Vapor Pressure Line
p_v/rho_g = 0.23 m"] P6 --> P9["HGL MUST STAY ABOVE
Vapor Pressure Line
to avoid cavitation"] P8 --> P9 ``` ## 4.3 Delivery Side — HGL Behaviour (Detail) ```mermaid graph TD Q1["Pump Discharge EGL
z + H_pump"] --> Q2["After Friction h_f_d
EGL drops by h_f_d"] Q2 --> Q3["After Valve Loss
EGL drops by K_valve V_sq/2g"] Q3 --> Q4["After Exit Loss K=1.0
EGL drops by V_sq/2g"] Q4 --> Q5["Upper Tank Free Surface
EGL = HGL = static head"] Q1 --> Q6["Pump Discharge HGL
EGL minus V_sq/2g"] Q6 --> Q7["HGL Slope = f V_sq/2gD
per metre of pipe"] Q5 --> Q8["HGL and EGL meet at
reservoir free surface"] ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flow — Cavitation Safety Check ```mermaid graph TD S1["Compute NPSH Available
using energy equation"] --> S2{"NPSH_A > NPSH_R?"} S2 -- YES --> S3["SAFE
Pump operates without cavitation"] S2 -- NO --> S4["CAVITATION RISK
Pump may be damaged"] S4 --> S5["Remedies:"] S5 --> S6["Option 1: Lower pump
reduce static suction lift"] S5 --> S7["Option 2: Increase pipe diameter
reduce V and friction"] S5 --> S8["Option 3: Reduce flow rate
lower V and losses"] S5 --> S9["Option 4: Cool the liquid
lower p_v"] S6 --> S2 S7 --> S2 S8 --> S2 S9 --> S2 ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram — Components Affecting Hydraulic Gradient ```mermaid graph LR R1["RESERVOIR 1
Suction Sump"] --> R2["ENTRY LOSS K=0.5"] R2 --> R3["FOOT VALVE K=2.0"] R3 --> R4["STRAINER K varies"] R4 --> R5["PIPE FRICTION h_f"] R5 --> R6["ELBOW LOSS K=0.9"] R6 --> R7["PUMP SUCTION
NPSH check point"] R7 --> R8["PUMP
Adds H_pump"] R8 --> R9["PIPE FRICTION h_f_d"] R9 --> R10["GATE VALVE K=0.2"] R10 --> R11["GLOBE VALVE K=6.0"] R11 --> R12["EXIT LOSS K=1.0"] R12 --> R13["RESERVOIR 2
Delivery Tank"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to *sketch the HGL and EGL*, always start the HGL and EGL *at the same point* at the free surface of the suction reservoir, draw the EGL strictly above the HGL by $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ on the suction and delivery pipes, show a *vertical jump* at the pump, and converge the two lines *back to a single point* at the upper reservoir free surface. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define the Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL) and the Energy Gradient Line (EGL) for a pump-piping system. State the relationship between them with a mathematical expression.** **Model Answer:** The **Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL)** is the line joining points in a flowing fluid that represent the sum of the pressure head $\frac{p}{\rho g}$ and the elevation head $z$ at each section of the pipe. It is also called the **piezometric head line**. The vertical distance from the pipe centerline to the HGL equals the pressure head of the fluid at that location. The **Energy Gradient Line (EGL)**, also called the **Total Energy Line (TEL)**, is the line joining points that represent the total head of the fluid — pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head — at every section of the pipe. The two lines are related by the **velocity head** of the fluid in the pipe: $$\text{EGL} = \text{HGL} + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ Hence, EGL always lies above HGL by an amount equal to the velocity head of the fluid, and the two coincide wherever the fluid velocity is essentially zero (free surfaces of reservoirs, very large tanks). --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **Distinguish between NPSH available and NPSH required. What is the consequence if NPSH available is less than NPSH required?** **Model Answer:** **NPSH Available (NPSH_A)** is the absolute pressure head at the pump suction nozzle *above the vapor pressure head* of the liquid at the operating temperature. It depends only on the *suction system design* — atmospheric pressure, static suction lift, suction pipe friction and minor losses, and the vapor pressure of the liquid. $$\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_{f,s} - h_{m,s} - \frac{p_v}{\rho g}$$ **NPSH Required (NPSH_R)** is the *minimum* pressure head above vapor pressure that must be available at the pump suction to prevent cavitation. It is a *property of the pump itself* and is determined experimentally by the manufacturer. It varies with flow rate and rotational speed. **Consequence if $\text{NPSH}_A < \text{NPSH}_R$:** The local static pressure at the pump impeller eye falls below the vapor pressure of the liquid. Vapor bubbles form, are carried into the higher-pressure region of the impeller, and collapse violently. This phenomenon is called **cavitation**. Its effects include: 1. **Erosion / pitting** of impeller blades and casing. 2. **Noise and vibration**, leading to bearing failure. 3. **Loss of pump head and efficiency** — the pump cannot develop its rated performance. 4. **Possible complete failure** of the pump if cavitation is severe. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** > Students frequently confuse the *position* of the HGL with the *EGL* in the suction pipe. Always remember: **EGL is always above HGL by the velocity head.** Also, when the pump is *below* the sump (flooded suction), the static suction term $h_s$ is **negative**, which *improves* NPSH — this is a common sign-error in KTU scripts. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either Option A or Option B in full**. ### Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended for First Attempt **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Starting from the steady-flow energy equation, derive the expression for the **Total Dynamic Head (TDH)** developed by a pump between two reservoirs. (7 Marks) **(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers **water at 25 L/s** from a lower sump to an overhead tank. The static lift between the two free surfaces is **18 m**. The suction pipe is **12 m long, 100 mm diameter**, with a friction factor of **0.022**, and includes a foot valve with $K = 2.0$ and an entry loss $K = 0.5$. The delivery pipe is **60 m long, 80 mm diameter**, with a friction factor of **0.024**, includes two standard elbows ($K = 0.9$ each), one globe valve ($K = 6.0$), and an exit loss ($K = 1.0$). Calculate **(i)** the friction head loss, **(ii)** the minor losses, **(iii)** the total dynamic head, and **(iv)** the **shaft power required** if pump efficiency is **70%**. Take **g = 9.81 m/s²**. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation of TDH:** Apply the steady-flow energy equation (per unit weight) between point 1 (suction reservoir free surface) and point 2 (delivery reservoir free surface), with the pump adding head $H_p$: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$ At both free surfaces, $p_1 = p_2 = p_{atm}$ and $V_1 = V_2 \approx 0$. Therefore $p_1 = p_2$ and the velocity heads cancel: $$z_1 + H_p = z_2 + h_L$$ $$H_p = (z_2 - z_1) + h_L$$ Define static head $H_{static} = z_2 - z_1$, and the total loss $h_L = h_f + h_m$ (sum of friction and minor losses): $$\boxed{H_{TDH} = H_{static} + h_f + h_m}$$ This is the head that the pump must supply to overcome both the elevation difference and all hydraulic losses in the system. **(7 Marks awarded for clear derivation with stated assumptions and boundary conditions.)** --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute velocities in suction and delivery pipes:** Suction pipe: $D_s = 0.1$ m $$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$V_s = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.183 \text{ m/s}$$ Delivery pipe: $D_d = 0.08$ m $$A_d = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.08)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$V_d = \frac{Q}{A_d} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.974 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute velocity heads:** $$\frac{V_s^2}{2g} = \frac{(3.183)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{10.13}{19.62} = 0.5163 \text{ m}$$ $$\frac{V_d^2}{2g} = \frac{(4.974)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{24.74}{19.62} = 1.261 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Friction losses:** Suction: $$h_{f,s} = f_s \cdot \frac{L_s}{D_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{2g} = 0.022 \times \frac{12}{0.1} \times 0.5163 = 0.022 \times 120 \times 0.5163 = 1.363 \text{ m}$$ Delivery: $$h_{f,d} = f_d \cdot \frac{L_d}{D_d} \cdot \frac{V_d^2}{2g} = 0.024 \times \frac{60}{0.08} \times 1.261 = 0.024 \times 750 \times 1.261 = 22.70 \text{ m}$$ Total friction loss: $$h_f = 1.363 + 22.70 = 24.06 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Minor losses:** Suction: $K_{total,s} = K_{foot} + K_{entry} = 2.0 + 0.5 = 2.5$ $$h_{m,s} = 2.5 \times 0.5163 = 1.291 \text{ m}$$ Delivery: $K_{total,d} = 2(0.9) + 6.0 + 1.0 = 8.8$ $$h_{m,d} = 8.8 \times 1.261 = 11.10 \text{ m}$$ Total minor loss: $$h_m = 1.291 + 11.10 = 12.39 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Total dynamic head:** $$H_{TDH} = H_{static} + h_f + h_m = 18 + 24.06 + 12.39 = 54.45 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6 — Shaft power:** $$P_{shaft} = \frac{\rho g Q H_{TDH}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 54.45}{0.70}$$ $$P_{shaft} = \frac{13350.7}{0.70} = 19{,}072 \text{ W} \approx 19.07 \text{ kW}$$ **Final Answers:** - Friction loss: $h_f = 24.06$ m - Minor loss: $h_m = 12.39$ m - Total dynamic head: $H_{TDH} = 54.45$ m - Shaft power: $P_{shaft} = 19.07$ kW --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL) and Energy Gradient Line (EGL)** in a pump-piping system. Sketch the variation of HGL and EGL on both suction and delivery sides, clearly indicating the **pump head**, **static head**, and **friction losses**. (7 Marks) **(b)** A pump draws water from a sump **4 m below** its centerline. The suction pipe is **10 m long, 150 mm diameter**, with a friction factor of **0.02**. The entry loss coefficient is **0.5**, and a foot valve contributes $K = 2.0$. The discharge is **30 L/s**. Atmospheric pressure head is **10.3 m of water**, and the water temperature is **25 °C** (vapor pressure head = **0.32 m**). Calculate the **NPSH available** and verify whether the pump is safe from cavitation if its **NPSH required** at this duty point is **4.5 m**. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation and Sketch:** The **Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL)** traces the piezometric head $\left(\frac{p}{\rho g} + z\right)$ at each point in the system. The **Energy Gradient Line (EGL)** traces the total head $\left(\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z\right)$ at each point. The vertical distance between EGL and HGL is the **velocity head** $\frac{V^2}{2g}$. **Behaviour on the suction side (lower reservoir → pump):** - At the free surface of the lower reservoir, V ≈ 0, so HGL = EGL = elevation of the surface. - As the fluid travels up the suction pipe, friction losses cause the EGL to slope *downward* in the direction of flow. - The HGL also slopes downward but lies *below* the EGL by $\frac{V^2}{2g}$. - If the pump is above the sump (suction lift case), the HGL at the pump suction is *lower* than the sump water level by an amount $(h_s + h_{L,s})$. **Behaviour at the pump:** - The pump adds energy $H_p$ to the fluid. Both HGL and EGL exhibit a **sudden vertical jump** of magnitude $H_p$ at the pump location. **Behaviour on the delivery side (pump → upper reservoir):** - Starting at the pump discharge, the EGL is at its highest point. - Friction and minor losses cause the EGL to slope downward toward the upper reservoir. - The HGL lies below EGL by $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ throughout the delivery pipe. - At the exit into the upper reservoir, the velocity head is fully lost (exit loss), so the HGL and EGL **converge to a single point** at the free surface of the upper reservoir. - The **static head** is the vertical difference between the two reservoir water surfaces. - The **friction losses** are represented by the *slope* of the EGL on either side of the pump. **Key Sketch Features to Draw:** 1. Two parallel reservoir surfaces with the upper higher than the lower. 2. EGL and HGL coincide at the lower free surface. 3. Both lines slope down towards the pump (losses on suction side). 4. Sudden jump of $H_p$ at the pump. 5. Both lines slope down from the pump to the upper reservoir. 6. Lines meet again at the upper free surface. 7. EGL strictly above HGL by $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ on both pipes. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution for NPSH:** **Step 1 — Compute velocity in suction pipe:** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$ $$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.698 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Velocity head:** $$\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{(1.698)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{2.882}{19.62} = 0.1469 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Friction loss in suction pipe:** $$h_{f,s} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.02 \times \frac{10}{0.15} \times 0.1469 = 0.02 \times 66.67 \times 0.1469 = 0.1958 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Minor losses in suction pipe:** $$K_{total} = K_{entry} + K_{foot} = 0.5 + 2.0 = 2.5$$ $$h_{m,s} = K_{total} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 2.5 \times 0.1469 = 0.3673 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Total suction-side loss:** $$h_{L,s} = 0.1958 + 0.3673 = 0.5631 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6 — Apply NPSH equation:** Static suction lift $h_s = +4$ m (pump is above sump). $$\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} - h_s - \frac{V^2}{2g} - h_{L,s} - \frac{p_v}{\rho g}$$ $$\text{NPSH}_A = 10.3 - 4 - 0.1469 - 0.5631 - 0.32$$ $$\text{NPSH}_A = 5.27 \text{ m}$$ **Step 7 — Cavitation check:** $$\text{NPSH}_A = 5.27 \text{ m} > \text{NPSH}_R = 4.5 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$ **Conclusion:** The pump is **SAFE from cavitation** at this duty point, with a margin of $5.27 - 4.5 = 0.77$ m. Designers usually recommend a minimum safety margin of 0.5 to 1.0 m, so the design is on the borderline but acceptable. If margin were insufficient, the engineer could either (a) reduce static suction lift by lowering the pump, (b) enlarge the suction pipe diameter, or (c) reduce the flow rate. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B:** > 1. **Sign error on $h_s$:** In a suction *lift* case (pump above sump), $h_s$ is **positive** and *subtracted*. In a suction *head* or flooded case (pump below sump), $h_s$ is **negative** and becomes *additive*. Many students blindly write $h_s = 4$ m without thinking about the sign convention — this is the single biggest source of NPSH mistakes. > 2. **Forgetting the velocity head** $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ in the NPSH formula costs 1–2 marks consistently. > 3. **Forgetting exit loss $K = 1.0$** in delivery-side minor losses is a frequent error in pump head problems. Even though the pipe discharges into a reservoir, the kinetic energy is dissipated and counts as a head loss to the pump. > 4. **Using $\eta_{pump}$ in denominator:** Pump shaft power = $\frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump}}$ (water power divided by efficiency). Forgetting the efficiency or placing it in the numerator both lose 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist - **HGL** = piezometric head $= \frac{p}{\rho g} + z$ (pressure head + elevation head) - **EGL** = total head $= \frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z$ (HGL + velocity head) - **EGL always above HGL** by the velocity head, on any pipe carrying flow. - **EGL = HGL** at free surfaces of reservoirs (where $V \approx 0$). - **Total Dynamic Head** $H_{TDH} = H_{static} + h_f + h_m$ (the pump's required head). - **Static head** = vertical difference between delivery and suction reservoir surfaces. - **Friction loss** $h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}$ (Darcy–Weisbach). - **Minor loss** $h_m = \Sigma K \frac{V^2}{2g}$ (sum of fitting losses). - **NPSH available** = $\frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_{L,s} - \frac{p_v}{\rho g}$ (minus velocity head too). - **Cavitation safety rule:** $\text{NPSH}_A > \text{NPSH}_R$ (always, with margin). - **Sign of $h_s$:** positive for suction *lift*, negative for suction *head* (flooded suction). - **Slope of HGL** in a uniform pipe = $\frac{f V^2}{2gD}$ (head loss per metre). - **Pump characteristic curve** plots $H$ vs. $Q$; **system curve** plots $H_{TDH}$ vs. $Q$ (parabolic due to $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ dependence). They intersect at the **operating point**. - **Power** $P_{shaft} = \frac{\rho g Q H_{TDH}}{\eta_{pump}}$ — efficiency is in the **denominator**. - **Common loss coefficients** to memorize for KTU exams: $K_{entry} = 0.5$, $K_{exit} = 1.0$, $K_{foot\,valve} = 2.0$, $K_{gate\,valve} = 0.2$, $K_{globe\,valve} = 6.0$, $K_{elbow} = 0.9$, $K_{tee} = 1.0$ to 1.8. - **Sketch the HGL/EGL** as a closed energy loop: start at suction surface, descend to pump, jump up by $H_p$, descend to upper surface. - **Energy is always lost** (never gained) along the flow path — only the pump can *add* energy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Tip:** When the KTU paper says *"sketch the hydraulic gradient"*, draw a single figure showing **both** HGL and EGL, label the **static head**, the **pump head jump**, the **friction slopes** on both sides, and the **velocity head gap** between the two lines. Always indicate the *direction of flow* with arrows. A clear, well-labelled sketch typically earns 3 marks by itself.

topics.title: Cavitation: Causes, Effects, and Prevention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cavitation: Causes, Effects, and Prevention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAVITATION (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> Cavitation is a **two-phase hydrodynamic phenomenon** occurring in a flowing liquid wherein the **local static pressure** at any point in the flow field falls **below the vapor pressure** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature, causing the formation of **vapor cavities (bubbles)**. When these bubbles are carried by the flow into regions of **higher pressure** (typically the impeller exit or volute casing), they **collapse violently**, releasing concentrated shock waves that damage the boundary surfaces.

The phenomenon was first systematically documented by **Lord Rayleigh (1917)** and is governed by the **Navier–Stokes equations** coupled with the **Rayleigh–Plesset bubble dynamics equation**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a carbonated soft drink bottle:

1. The sealed bottle is under **high pressure** — gas remains dissolved in the liquid; no bubbles are visible.
2. The moment you **open the cap**, the pressure suddenly drops to atmospheric level. Bubbles of CO₂ immediately nucleate and grow — this is bubble formation.
3. The bubbles rise, encounter higher pressure regions (or the warm liquid surface), and **collapse** with a hissing sound.

A **centrifugal pump impeller** behaves identically:

- At the **impeller eye (suction side)**, pressure is low (because velocity is high and the fluid is being accelerated).
- If this low pressure falls **below vapor pressure**, vapor cavities form inside the liquid.
- These cavities are flung outward by the centrifugal force, and when they hit the high-pressure regions near the **impeller periphery or casing**, they implode with a cracking noise (like rain on a tin roof, or gravel in the pump).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Memory Hook:** Cavitation is fundamentally a **pressure-driven phase-change event**, not a chemical reaction. Water literally **boils** at room temperature inside the pump if the local pressure is low enough.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric pressure (sea level) | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** | Pa, bar |
| Vapor pressure of water at 20°C | $P_v$ | **2.34 kPa** | Pa, bar |
| Vapor pressure of water at 100°C | $P_v$ | **101.325 kPa** | Pa, bar |
| Specific weight of water (at 20°C) | $\gamma$ | **9.79 kN/m³** | N/m³ |
| Standard gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | m/s² |
| Sound speed in water | $c$ | **~1480 m/s** | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Almost every numerical problem on cavitation begins with the **vapor pressure of the working fluid at the operating temperature**. Memorize the vapor pressure curve or be ready with a table.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Cavitation

Cavitation in a pump is **not instantaneous** — it is a sequential four-stage process. Understanding each stage is essential for the KTU Part B 14-mark questions.

### **Stage 1 — Pressure Drop (Initiation)**
As fluid enters the **impeller eye (suction inlet)**, it must accelerate from a low velocity to a high velocity to match the impeller vane entry speed (the *relative velocity* $V_r$ at inlet). By **Bernoulli's principle**, an increase in velocity produces a **decrease in static pressure**.

### **Stage 2 — Nucleation (Bubble Formation)**
