When the local static pressure $P_{local}$ drops **below or equal to** the vapor pressure $P_v$ of the liquid, dissolved gases and liquid vapor come out of solution. **Microscopic gas pockets** trapped in surface imperfections of the metal act as **nucleation sites**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pure, degassed water is *much* harder to cavitate than tap water because there are fewer nucleation sites. This is why KTU problems use the simple criterion $P_{local} \le P_v$ as the **onset condition**.

### **Stage 3 — Bubble Growth & Transport**
The cavity grows rapidly (the **Rayleigh equation** describes this growth rate). Centrifugal action in rotating machinery throws the bubbles **outward** toward the impeller periphery or volute where pressure is high.

### **Stage 4 — Violent Collapse (Implosion)**
The bubbles encounter regions where local pressure far exceeds vapor pressure. They collapse in **microseconds**, producing:
- **Shock waves** with peak pressures of the order of **hundreds of MPa**
- Local temperatures up to several hundred degrees Celsius (for very short durations)
- **Micro-jets** of liquid that impinge on the metal surface

---

## 2.2 NPSH — The Master Variable of Cavitation Analysis

**Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)** is the single most important concept. There are *two* flavors:

### **A. NPSH Available (NPSH_A) — determined by the system**
$$\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - \frac{P_v}{\gamma} \pm h_s - h_f$$

Where:
- $h_s$ = static suction head (positive if liquid level is **above** pump centerline, negative if below)
- $h_f$ = head loss due to friction in the suction piping
- $\gamma$ = specific weight of the liquid

### **B. NPSH Required (NPSH_R) — determined by the pump manufacturer**
- A function of pump design, speed, and flow rate.
- Determined experimentally by the manufacturer, typically defined as the NPSH at which the head **drops by 3%** from the normal curve (per **ISO 9906** / **HI 9.6.6**).

### **C. The Fundamental Cavitation Rule**
$$\boxed{\text{NPSH}_A \;\geq\; \text{NPSH}_R \;+\; \text{Safety Margin (typically } 0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ m)}}$$

If this inequality is violated, cavitation **will** occur.

---

## 2.3 Thoma's Cavitation Parameter (σ)

Professor **Dietrich Thoma** (1920s) introduced a **dimensionless number** that correlates cavitation tendency with pump head:

$$\sigma = \frac{\text{NPSH}_A}{H}$$

Where $H$ is the pump head at the operating point.

**Critical cavitation number** $\sigma_c$ is determined experimentally. The pump operates **without cavitation** when:

$$\boxed{\sigma \;\geq\; \sigma_c}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{NPSH}_A = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma} \pm h_s - h_f$ | System NPSH | **m** | Suction system design |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{NPSH}_A}{H}$ | Thoma parameter | dimensionless | Cavitation comparison |
| 3 | $P_{local} \le P_v$ | Cavitation onset criterion | Pa, bar | Identifying cavitation region |
| 4 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{\text{NPSH}_R}{H}$ | Critical cavitation number | dimensionless | Manufacturer's spec |
| 5 | $h_{sv} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma}$ | Static suction head (max) | **m** | Max theoretical suction lift |
| 6 | $H_{max} = h_{sv} - h_f$ | Max practical suction head | **m** | Suction pipe design |
| 7 | $T_v = T_{sat} \,@\, P_v$ | Vapor pressure relation | °C, K | Temperature correction |
| 8 | $P_v \approx \exp\left(A - \dfrac{B}{T+C}\right)$ | Antoine equation for $P_v$ | Pa | Compute $P_v$ at any $T$ |
| 9 | $H = H_0\left(1 - k\sqrt{\text{NPSH}_R - \text{NPSH}_A}\right)$ | Head drop due to cavitation | **m** | Cavitation performance curve |
| 10 | $P_{vapor} \propto e^{\text{-}T}$ | Vapor pressure increases with $T$ | — | Hot fluid → more cavitation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER use the pipe symbol `\|` inside a markdown table cell.** Use `\vert` or `\mid` if you need a vertical bar within formulas. This avoids breaking KTU question-paper rendering.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear power plant feedwater pumps**: Cavitation at high temperature is a critical concern; pump suction pipes are designed with strict NPSH margins.
- **Marine pumps**: Operate with hot seawater (high $P_v$); NPSH is hard to maintain.
- **Hydraulic turbines (Francis/Kaplan)**: Cavitation on the trailing edges of blades is a routine design problem — countered with **stainless steel runners** and controlled operating ranges.
- **Rocket turbopumps**: Operate with cryogenic propellants at near-vacuum pressures; cavitation can be catastrophic. **Inducer stages** are added specifically to suppress cavitation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Theoretical Suction Lift (Static Head Limit)

### **Problem Statement:**
A centrifugal pump is to lift water at 40°C from an open sump located 5 m below the pump centerline. The local barometric pressure is **96 kPa**. Determine the maximum **theoretical suction lift** $h_{sv}$ for cavitation-free operation.

### **Given:**
- $P_{atm} = 96 \text{ kPa} = 96000 \text{ Pa}$
- $T = 40°\text{C}$ → From steam tables, $P_v = 7.375 \text{ kPa} = 7375 \text{ Pa}$
- $\gamma_{water, 40°C} = 9.73 \text{ kN/m}^3 = 9730 \text{ N/m}^3$

### **Step 1 — Recall the NPSH Available Expression at Zero Velocity and Zero Friction Loss**

For a static column with the liquid at atmospheric pressure and the impeller eye at vapor pressure limit:

$$\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - \frac{P_v}{\gamma} - h_s$$

Setting NPSH_A = 0 (the absolute limit, ignoring NPSH_R for the *static* case):

$$0 = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma} - h_{sv}$$

### **Step 2 — Solve for $h_{sv}$**

$$h_{sv} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma}$$

### **Step 3 — Substitute Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{sv} &= \frac{96000 \;-\; 7375}{9730} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{88625}{9730} \\[6pt]
       &= 9.108 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 4 — Physical Interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> $h_{sv} = 9.108$ m is the **theoretical maximum suction lift**. In practice, due to friction losses in the suction pipe ($h_f$) and the pump's own NPSH_R requirement, the safe operating suction lift is significantly lower — typically **5 to 6 m** for water at 40°C.

### **Step 5 — Practical Safe Suction Lift**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{s,\text{actual}} &= h_{sv} - h_f - \text{NPSH}_R \\
&= 9.108 - 0.6 - 1.5 \\
&= 7.008 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Here, $h_f = 0.6$ m, NPSH_R = 1.5 m are typical assumed values.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Thoma's Parameter and NPSH

### **Starting Point: Pump Total Head Expression**
The head developed by a centrifugal pump at the impeller eye, using Euler's turbomachinery equation:

$$H = \frac{u_2 V_{u2} - u_1 V_{u1}}{g}$$

For **radial-entry flow** at the impeller eye, $V_{u1} = 0$, so:

$$H = \frac{u_2 V_{u2}}{g}$$

### **Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli Between the Free Liquid Surface (0) and the Impeller Eye (1)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + \frac{V_0^2}{2g} + z_0
&= \frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_f \\
\frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - h_s - h_f
&= \frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Here, $V_0 \approx 0$ for a large sump, $z_0 - z_1 = h_s$).

### **Step 2 — Rearrange for $P_1 / \gamma$ (Pressure at the Impeller Eye)**

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - h_s - h_f - \frac{V_1^2}{2g}$$

### **Step 3 — Recognize the Velocity Head at Impeller Eye as Related to $H$**

From the velocity triangle at the impeller inlet, and through Euler's equation coupling, the velocity head at the impeller eye is related to the **manometric head $H$** by an empirical factor $k$:

$$\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = k H$$

### **Step 4 — Define NPSH_A in Terms of $H$**

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPSH}_A &= \frac{P_1}{\gamma} - \frac{P_v}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} \\
&= \left[\frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - h_s - h_f - \frac{V_1^2}{2g}\right] - \frac{P_v}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} \\
&= \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma} - h_s - h_f
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5 — Form Thoma's Parameter**

By definition:

$$\sigma = \frac{\text{NPSH}_A}{H}$$

Substituting the result from Step 4:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{1}{H}\left[\frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma} - h_s - h_f\right]}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cavitation Effects and Their Quantitative Impact

| Effect | Physical Mechanism | Quantitative Impact | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Head loss** | Vapor bubbles displace liquid, reducing effective flow area | Head drops by 3% at incipient cavitation; full breakdown at $H = 0$ | **Very High** |
| **Efficiency drop** | Energy wasted in bubble formation & collapse | $\eta$ drops 5–15% in severe cavitation | High |
| **Noise** | Bubble collapse produces shock waves in kHz range | Sound pressure level rises 20–40 dB | Moderate |
| **Vibration** | Unbalanced mass flow due to cavities | Bearing vibration increases 200–500% | Moderate |
| **Surface erosion** | Micro-jet impingement at implosion sites | Pit depth 0.1–5 mm/1000 hrs | **Very High** |
| **Discharge pulsation** | Cyclic shedding of cavities | Flow becomes unsteady; gauge needle oscillates | Moderate |
| **Material fatigue** | Cyclic stress from collapses | Crack initiation in impeller, lifetime reduced 50–80% | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** Many students write *"cavitation reduces head"* without stating **how much** or under what criterion. Always specify the **3% head drop criterion (ISO 9906)** when discussing incipient cavitation.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Finding the Maximum Permissible Suction Lift

### **Given:**
- Water temperature: $T = 30°\text{C}$ → $P_v = 4.246 \text{ kPa}$
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- Specific weight of water: $\gamma = 9.77 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- Friction loss in suction pipe: $h_f = 0.8 \text{ m}$
- Pump NPSH_R: $1.2 \text{ m}$
- Pump head $H = 20 \text{ m}$
- Safety margin: $0.5 \text{ m}$

### **Find:** Maximum suction lift $h_s$ for cavitation-free operation.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the NPSH equation:
$$\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma} - h_s - h_f$$

**Step 2:** Compute the static suction term:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma} &= \frac{101.325 - 4.246}{9.77} \times 10^3 \\
&= \frac{97079}{9.77} \\
&= 9936.4 \text{ mm} = 9.936 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the cavitation criterion $\text{NPSH}_A \ge \text{NPSH}_R + \text{safety}$:
$$9.936 - h_s - 0.8 \ge 1.2 + 0.5$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $h_s$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
9.936 - 0.8 - 1.7 &\ge h_s \\
7.436 &\ge h_s \\
\therefore \; h_{s,\text{max}} &= 7.44 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **pump must be located no more than 7.44 m above the sump water level.** If installed higher, the pressure at the impeller eye will fall below vapor pressure, and cavitation will damage the impeller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D -- P_1 less than P_v --> G[Stable Cavities form and grow]

    H --> I[Bubbles reach high pressure region at impeller periphery]
    I --> J[Violent bubble collapse: shock wave and micro-jet]
    J --> K[Metal surface erosion and noise]
    K --> L[Reduced head, efficiency, and mechanical damage]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`
**Define cavitation. State any two effects of cavitation in a centrifugal pump.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Cavitation is the phenomenon of formation of vapor bubbles in a liquid when the local static pressure falls **below the vapor pressure** of the liquid, followed by the **violent collapse** of these bubbles when they enter higher-pressure regions.

**Any two effects (1 mark):**
1. **Erosion/pitting** of the impeller and casing surfaces due to repeated bubble collapse.
2. **Reduction in pump head and efficiency** due to disturbances in the flow.
3. **Noise and vibration** in the pump assembly.
*(Any two of the above or equivalents from the analytical matrix are acceptable.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`
**What is NPSH? Why must NPSH_A be greater than NPSH_R?**

**Model Answer:**

**NPSH (2 marks):** *Net Positive Suction Head* is the total head available at the pump suction above the vapor pressure of the liquid. It is a measure of the margin against cavitation.
- **NPSH_A (Available)**: Determined by the **suction system** — atmospheric pressure, static suction head, vapor pressure, and friction losses.
- **NPSH_R (Required)**: Determined by the **pump design** — a function of flow rate, speed, and impeller geometry.

**Why NPSH_A must exceed NPSH_R (1 mark):**
If $\text{NPSH}_A < \text{NPSH}_R$, the pressure at the impeller eye will fall below the vapor pressure of the liquid, leading to cavitation. Maintaining $\text{NPSH}_A > \text{NPSH}_R$ ensures the pressure at the impeller eye stays above the vapor pressure, preventing bubble formation and ensuring cavitation-free operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **causes and effects of cavitation** in a centrifugal pump. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump handles water at **60°C** from a sump. The pump is installed **4 m above** the water level. Suction pipe diameter is **100 mm**, length is **12 m**, and friction factor is **0.02**. Atmospheric pressure is **101.325 kPa**. The pump NPSH_R is **2.5 m**. Check whether the pump will cavitate. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Causes and Effects (7 marks)**

**Causes (3.5 marks):**
1. **Excessive suction lift** — when the pump is installed too high above the liquid source, the suction head $h_s$ becomes too large, dropping $P_1$ below $P_v$.
2. **High fluid temperature** — increases vapor pressure $P_v$, reducing the available margin.
3. **Inadequate suction pipe design** — narrow pipes, sharp bends, foot valve obstructions cause high friction loss $h_f$.
4. **High pump speed** — increases velocity head $V_1^2/2g$ at the impeller eye, dropping $P_1$.
5. **Operating at off-design flow rates** — the recirculation zones at the impeller eye can develop local low pressure.
6. **Low atmospheric pressure** — operation at high altitudes reduces $P_{atm}$.

**Effects (3.5 marks):**
1. **Material erosion** — repeated collapse of cavities near the metal surface causes pitting and eventual perforation of the impeller blades.
2. **Loss of head and efficiency** — cavities block flow passages, reducing the effective head developed.
3. **Noise and vibration** — collapse of cavities produces characteristic high-frequency noise (like gravel tumbling) and mechanical vibration.
4. **Mechanical damage** — sustained cavitation can lead to fatigue failure of impeller blades and shaft bearings.
5. **Discharge pulsation** — flow becomes unsteady, leading to fluctuating discharge pressures.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $T = 60°\text{C}$ → $P_v = 19.94 \text{ kPa}$
- $h_s = 4 \text{ m}$ (suction lift, positive since pump is above water)
- $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $L = 12 \text{ m}$, $f = 0.02$
- $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$, $\gamma_{water, 60°C} = 9.65 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- $\text{NPSH}_R = 2.5 \text{ m}$, assume discharge $Q = 0.025 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ (typical, given to compute $h_f$).

**Step 1 — Velocity in suction pipe:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.025}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.183 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Friction head loss:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 12 \times (3.183)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1} \\
    &= \frac{0.9727}{1.962} = 0.496 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute NPSH_A:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPSH}_A &= \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma} - h_s - h_f \\
&= \frac{(101.325 - 19.94) \times 10^3}{9650} - 4 - 0.496 \\
&= \frac{81385}{9650} - 4.496 \\
&= 8.434 - 4.496 = 3.938 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with NPSH_R + safety margin:**
$$\text{NPSH}_A = 3.94 \text{ m} \;\text{vs.}\; \text{NPSH}_R + 0.5 = 3.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
Since $\text{NPSH}_A = 3.94 \text{ m} > \text{NPSH}_R + \text{safety} = 3.0 \text{ m}$, the pump is operating within safe limits. **The pump will NOT cavitate under these conditions.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **concept of Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)**. Derive the expression for $\text{NPSH}_A$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **methods of prevention of cavitation** in pumps. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — NPSH Concept and Derivation (7 marks)**

**Concept (3 marks):**
NPSH is the net positive head available at the pump suction above the vapor pressure of the liquid. It has two parts:
- $\text{NPSH}_A$: Determined by the *system* (suction piping layout, atmospheric conditions, liquid temperature).
- $\text{NPSH}_R$: Determined by the *pump* (impeller design, speed, flow rate).

A typical sketch should show a sump, suction pipe, pump centerline, and labels for $P_{atm}$, $h_s$, $h_f$, $V_1^2/2g$, and $P_v$.

**Derivation (4 marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.2, Derivation 2, for the full step-by-step derivation.)*

Apply Bernoulli's equation between the free liquid surface in the sump (point 0) and the impeller eye (point 1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + 0 + z_0 &= \frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_f \\
\frac{P_1}{\gamma} &= \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - h_s - h_f - \frac{V_1^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

The NPSH available is the total head at point 1 above the vapor pressure head:

$$
\boxed{\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} - \frac{P_v}{\gamma} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma} - h_s - h_f}
$$

The criterion for cavitation-free operation:

$$\text{NPSH}_A \geq \text{NPSH}_R + \text{safety margin}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Prevention Methods (7 marks)**

1. **Reduce suction lift** (1.5 marks): Install the pump as close to the liquid source as possible. Submerge the suction pipe below the liquid surface using a foot valve to maintain priming.
2. **Minimize friction losses in suction line** (1 mark): Use shorter, larger-diameter suction pipes with smooth bends; install a properly sized foot valve and strainer.
3. **Reduce liquid temperature** (1 mark): Cool the liquid before pumping to lower its vapor pressure $P_v$, increasing the available NPSH margin.
4. **Lower pump operating speed** (1 mark): Reducing $N$ lowers the velocity head at the impeller eye, increasing NPSH_A.
5. **Use a pump with lower NPSH_R** (1 mark): Select a pump specifically designed for low-NPSH applications, e.g., pumps with **inducer stages** or larger impeller eyes.
6. **Operate near the Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** (1 mark): Off-design operation creates recirculation and local low-pressure zones that trigger cavitation.
7. **Use cavitation-resistant materials** (0.5 marks): Stainless steel, bronze, or special alloys (e.g., **17-4 PH**, **Monel**) extend impeller life in borderline cavitation conditions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** $\text{NPSH}_A$ (system) with $\text{NPSH}_R$ (pump) — examiners frequently deduct **2 marks** for this mix-up.
> - **Always include units** (m of liquid) when reporting NPSH values.
> - **Always add a safety margin** (typically 0.5 to 1.0 m) when comparing NPSH_A and NPSH_R.
> - For hot water or volatile liquids, **vapor pressure corrections** are mandatory — forgetting to use the correct $P_v$ at the operating temperature costs full marks on numericals.
> - **Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude** — for high-altitude installations, use $P_{atm} \approx 101.325 \times (1 - 0.0000226 h)^{5.255}$ where $h$ is altitude in meters.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [ ] **Cavitation definition:** Formation of vapor cavities when local $P \le P_v$, followed by violent collapse at high $P$.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $\text{NPSH}_A = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma} \pm h_s - h_f$
- [ ] **Cavitation criterion:** $\text{NPSH}_A \ge \text{NPSH}_R + \text{safety margin (0.5 to 1.0 m)}$
- [ ] **Thoma's parameter:** $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{NPSH}_A}{H}$; cavitation-free if $\sigma \ge \sigma_c$
- [ ] **Vapor pressure increases with temperature** — hot fluids cavitate more easily.
- [ ] **Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude** — high-altitude pumps need lower suction lift.
- [ ] **Pump should be installed as low as possible** relative to the liquid source to minimize $h_s$.
- [ ] **Use large-diameter, short suction pipes** with smooth bends to minimize $h_f$.
- [ ] **NPSH_R increases with flow rate and speed** — operating far from BEP is risky.
- [ ] **Inducer stages** are added to turbopumps specifically to suppress cavitation.
- [ ] **Effects to memorize:** erosion, head loss, efficiency drop, noise, vibration, discharge pulsation.
- [ ] **Prevention methods:** reduce suction lift, reduce $h_f$, lower temperature, lower speed, select low-NPSH_R pump, use cavitation-resistant alloys, operate near BEP.
- [ ] **3% head drop criterion** (ISO 9906) defines incipient cavitation.
- [ ] **First to study cavitation:** Lord Rayleigh (1917); introduced the dimensionless number: **Thoma (1920s)**.
- [ ] **In a cavitating pump, head and efficiency drop, NOT the discharge pressure directly** — this is a common conceptual trap.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH) and Its Importance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH) and Its Importance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)** is a measure of the energy available in a liquid at the suction port of a pump, expressed in terms of head (metres of fluid) above the vapour pressure of the liquid at the prevailing temperature. It quantifies the margin by which the absolute pressure at the pump impeller eye exceeds the vapour pressure of the liquid — a critical parameter that governs whether the pump will operate free from **cavitation**.

The NPSH concept yields two distinct but complementary quantities:

- **NPSH Available (NPSHA)** — The total head available at the pump suction nozzle, calculated from the upstream system geometry and the liquid properties. It is a *system* property.
- **NPSH Required (NPSHR)** — The minimum head needed at the suction nozzle to prevent the static pressure at the impeller eye from falling to the vapour pressure. It is a *pump* property, established by the manufacturer through cavitation testing as per **ISO 9906:2012** and **HI 9.6.6** standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation Rule (Governing Inequality):** For safe operation without cavitation, the inequality
> $$\text{NPSHA} \;\geq\; \text{NPSHR} + \text{Safety Margin}$$
> **must always be satisfied.** A typical industry safety margin is **0.5 m to 1.0 m** for water at normal temperatures, increasing for hot or volatile liquids.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Juice Box Straw

Imagine drinking a juice box through a long straw. If the straw is very long or the box is held too high above your mouth, the juice will not flow — in fact, a partial vacuum forms and the liquid vaporises in tiny pockets, producing a sputtering sound. The same physics governs a centrifugal pump:

- The **suction tank level, atmospheric pressure, and suction pipe friction losses** determine how much "energy push" the liquid has as it enters the pump.
- The pump itself needs a **minimum amount of this push** (NPSHR) to keep the liquid from boiling at the impeller eye.
- When the available push is less than the required push, the liquid **flashes to vapour** inside the impeller, forming collapsing bubbles that erode the metal surfaces.

Thus, **NPSHA is the "supply"** and **NPSHR is the "demand"**; the system must always supply more than the pump demands.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Vapour Pressure of Water at 20 °C:** $\mathbf{P_v \approx 2.34 \text{ kPa (abs)}}$
- **Vapour Pressure of Water at 100 °C:** $\mathbf{P_v = 101.325 \text{ kPa (abs)}}$
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $\mathbf{P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa (abs)}}$
- **Density of Water:** $\mathbf{\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ at 20 °C
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $\mathbf{g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}$
- **Recommended safety margin:** $\mathbf{0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ m of water column (mWC)}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern Tip:** Examiners frequently convert between **absolute pressure in kPa** and **head in metres** using $h = P / (\rho g)$. Always specify the units explicitly to avoid losing marks in step-marking.

### 1.4 The Physical Significance of NPSH

NPSH is fundamentally a **cavitation prevention index**. Cavitation is the formation, growth, and violent collapse of vapour bubbles in a flowing liquid when its local static pressure drops to (or below) its vapour pressure. The consequences of cavitation include:

1. **Performance degradation** — Head, flow, and efficiency fall below rated values.
2. **Mechanical damage** — Asymmetric bubble collapse creates micro-jets that pit and erode the impeller vanes and casing.
3. **Acoustic and vibration issues** — A characteristic "marbles-in-a-can" sound and elevated vibration spectra.
4. **Reduced bearing and seal life** — Shaft deflection from uneven loading accelerates wear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The phrase **"net positive"** literally means: *the head above vapour pressure that is still positive in sign.* Once this value becomes negative, vapour formation is thermodynamically guaranteed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Energy Balance at the Suction Port

The NPSHA is obtained by applying the **steady-flow energy equation (Bernoulli with losses)** between a free liquid surface in the suction reservoir and the pump suction flange (point 1 → point 2, where "2" is the suction nozzle centreline).

The general energy head balance is:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_{f,1\rightarrow 2}
$$

For a large reservoir, $V_1 \approx 0$ and $P_1 = P_{atm}$ (or system pressure). Solving for the absolute pressure head at the suction flange:

$$
\frac{P_2}{\rho g} \;=\; \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - h_{f} \mp z
$$

The **NPSHA** is defined as the total head at the suction flange *minus* the vapour pressure head:

$$
\text{NPSHA} \;=\; \left(\frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\right) - \frac{P_v}{\rho g}
$$

Combining the two relations gives the canonical KTU board formula:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NPSHA} \;=\; \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} \;\pm\; h_s \;-\; h_f\;}
$$

where the sign convention is:

- **+ve** when the liquid source is **above** the pump centreline (gravity-flooded suction).
- **−ve** when the liquid source is **below** the pump centreline (suction lift).
- $h_s$ is the static suction lift / submergence in metres.
- $h_f$ is the total friction and minor loss in the suction line in metres.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind the Formula

1. **Atmospheric contribution** $P_{atm}/(\rho g)$: The surrounding atmospheric pressure pushes the liquid down the suction pipe. On a hot day at high altitude, $P_{atm}$ drops sharply, and so does NPSHA.
2. **Vapour pressure subtraction** $P_v/(\rho g)$: Hot liquids have very high $P_v$ (it equals $P_{atm}$ at 100 °C), leaving **zero or negative** NPSHA. This is why hot-water pumps are notoriously cavitation-prone.
3. **Static suction head** $\pm h_s$: Gravity can either add to NPSHA (flooded suction) or subtract from it (suction lift). Each metre of lift costs 1 m of NPSHA.
4. **Friction loss penalty** $h_f$: Long, narrow, or fouled suction piping directly reduces NPSHA. Reducing the suction-line velocity below **1.5 to 2.0 m/s** is a standard design heuristic.
5. **Velocity head term** $V_2^2/(2g)$: The kinetic energy at the suction flange is sometimes included (especially in positive-displacement pump analyses), although for centrifugal pumps the flange velocity head is often small and lumped into NPSHR.

### 2.3 The NPSHR Side — What the Pump Demands

NPSHR is a function of:

- **Pump speed (N)** — NPSHR scales roughly with $N^2$ for geometrically similar pumps.
- **Flow rate (Q)** — NPSHR rises with $Q^{1.5 \text{ to } 2.0}$ on the normal operating range.
- **Impeller eye geometry** — Eye diameter, blade inlet angle, and throat area.
- **Liquid properties** — Vapour pressure and density at the suction temperature.

A useful empirical correlation often used in KTU problems:

$$
\text{NPSHR} \;\approx\; 0.1 \text{ to } 0.3 \text{ mWC per } 1000 \text{ rpm} \text{ (per impeller stage)}
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | NPSH Available (suction lift) | $\text{NPSHA}$ | $\dfrac{P_{atm}-P_v}{\rho g} - h_s - h_f$ | m | Source below pump centreline |
| 2 | NPSH Available (flooded suction) | $\text{NPSHA}$ | $\dfrac{P_{atm}-P_v}{\rho g} + h_s - h_f$ | m | Source above pump centreline |
| 3 | NPSH Available (closed tank) | $\text{NPSHA}$ | $\dfrac{P_s-P_v}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f$ | m | $P_s$ = tank top pressure |
| 4 | Cavitation Parameter (Thoma) | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{NPSHA}}{H}$ | dimensionless | Critical $\sigma_c$ used for incipient cavitation |
| 5 | Suction Specific Speed | $N_{ss}$ | $N_{ss} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(g \cdot \text{NPSHR})^{3/4}}$ | rpm | Pump design index |
| 6 | NPSH Margin | $\Delta\text{NPSH}$ | $\text{NPSHA} - \text{NPSHR}$ | m | Must be $\geq 0.5$ m for water |
| 7 | Pressure to Head Conversion | $h$ | $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | m | $P$ in Pa, $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 8 | Temperature Effect on $P_v$ | $P_v(T)$ | Antoine / steam-table lookup | kPa | Increases sharply above 60 °C |
| 9 | NPSH at Altitude | $P_{atm}(z)$ | $P_{atm}(z) = 101.325 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{0.0065 z}{288.15}\right)^{5.256}$ | kPa | $z$ = altitude in m |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, all absolute-value expressions and pipe-notation (e.g. $\vert h_s \vert$) are written using `\vert` or `\mid` for clean markdown rendering. Do **not** use the vertical pipe `|` inside table cells — it breaks the markdown parser.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Centrifugal pump selection catalogues** (e.g., KSB, Grundfos, Sulzer) provide NPSHR curves to be matched against system NPSHA.
- **Process industries** — boiler feed pumps handling near-saturated water are especially sensitive; even a 2 °C rise in suction temperature can trigger cavitation.
- **Shipbuilding and offshore platforms** — high ambient temperatures and low barometric pressures reduce NPSHA, mandating flooded suction designs.
- **Fire-fighting pumps** — net positive suction head of 3 m is a common NFPA-20 requirement to handle very low tank levels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the NPSHA Expression

**Starting point:** Apply the steady-flow energy equation (Bernoulli with friction) between the free surface of the suction reservoir (point 0) and the pump suction flange (point s). The equation per unit weight of liquid is:

$$
\frac{P_0}{\rho g} + \frac{V_0^2}{2g} + z_0 \;=\; \frac{P_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + z_s + h_f
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the reference conditions at the free surface.**

- The reservoir surface is large, so $V_0 \approx 0$ (kinetic energy negligible).
- The pressure at the surface is the system pressure acting on the liquid. For an open tank, $P_0 = P_{atm}$.
- The elevation $z_0$ is taken as the free-surface elevation.

**Step 2 — Define the static suction head.**

$$
h_s \;=\; z_0 - z_s
$$

By convention:

- $h_s > 0$ when the free surface is **above** the pump centreline (flooded / positive suction head).
- $h_s < 0$ when the free surface is **below** the pump centreline (suction lift).

**Step 3 — Rearrange for the absolute pressure head at the suction flange.**

$$
\frac{P_s}{\rho g} \;=\; \frac{P_0}{\rho g} - \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + h_s - h_f
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the definition of NPSH.**

NPSHA is the total head at the suction flange *above* the vapour pressure of the liquid:

$$
\text{NPSHA} \;=\; \left(\frac{P_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g}\right) - \frac{P_v}{\rho g}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute (Step 3) into (Step 4) and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPSHA} &= \left[\frac{P_0}{\rho g} - \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + h_s - h_f + \frac{V_s^2}{2g}\right] - \frac{P_v}{\rho g} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{P_0 - P_v}{\rho g} + h_s - h_f
\end{aligned}
$$

The velocity-head terms cancel, confirming that the canonical NPSHA formula is **independent of the suction pipe diameter** in the ideal sense (though $h_f$ itself depends on pipe diameter).

**Step 6 — Substitute the system pressure.**

- For an open reservoir: $P_0 = P_{atm}$.

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NPSHA} \;=\; \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} + h_s - h_f\;}
$$

- For a closed (pressurised) tank: $P_0 = P_t$ (tank headspace pressure).

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NPSHA} \;=\; \frac{P_t - P_v}{\rho g} + h_s - h_f\;}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Thoma's Cavitation Parameter

Thoma's parameter is the *dimensionless* cavitation index used to characterise incipient cavitation and to scale pump performance between fluids.

**Definition:**

$$
\sigma \;=\; \frac{\text{NPSHA}}{H}
$$

where $H$ is the total head developed by the pump at the operating duty point.

**Critical value $\sigma_c$:** Defined as the value of $\sigma$ at which NPSHA equals NPSHR (i.e., the onset of incipient cavitation).

$$
\sigma_c \;=\; \frac{\text{NPSHR}}{H}
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

- If $\sigma > \sigma_c$, then NPSHA $>$ NPSHR → safe, no cavitation.
- If $\sigma = \sigma_c$, then NPSHA $=$ NPSHR → incipient cavitation begins.
- If $\sigma < \sigma_c$, then NPSHA $<$ NPSHR → fully developed cavitation, severe damage imminent.

**Practical safety check used in design:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{design}} \;=\; 1.2 \text{ to } 1.5 \times \sigma_c
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Suction Specific Speed

The **suction specific speed** $N_{ss}$ is a single dimensionless-like index that captures the cavitation behaviour of a pump's impeller:

$$
N_{ss} \;=\; \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(g \cdot \text{NPSHR})^{3/4}}
$$

- $N$ in rpm.
- $Q$ in m³/s.
- NPSHR in m.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Consider the impeller eye flow. From dimensional analysis, the eye velocity scales as $V_{eye} \propto \sqrt{2 g \cdot \text{NPSHR}}$.
2. The flow rate is $Q = A_{eye} \cdot V_{eye}$, so $A_{eye} \propto Q / V_{eye}$.
3. The eye diameter scales as $D_{eye} \propto \sqrt{A_{eye}} \propto (Q / V_{eye})^{1/2} \propto (Q^2 / V_{eye})^{1/4}$.
4. For geometrically similar pumps, $D_{eye}/D \propto N_{ss}$, which after substitution of $V_{eye} \propto \sqrt{2g \cdot \text{NPSHR}}$ yields the formula above.

**Recommended range of $N_{ss}$:**

- $N_{ss} < 8000$ (rpm, metric): Single-suction centrifugal pumps, conservative design.
- $N_{ss} \approx 11000$: Modern high-efficiency single-suction impellers.
- $N_{ss} > 14000$: Double-suction or inducer-equipped pumps only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $N_{ss}$ matters:** Two pumps of the same head and flow can have very different cavitation tolerance purely due to $N_{ss}$. KTU problems often test the ability to identify a pump that is "cavitation prone" from a high $N_{ss}$ value.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (for student reference)

**Problem statement:** A centrifugal pump draws water at 45 °C from a sump whose water surface is 3.5 m below the pump centreline. The suction pipe is 75 mm in diameter, 6 m long, with a single foot valve and strainer. Calculate NPSHA and verify whether a pump with NPSHR = 4.2 m at duty is safe.

**Given:**

- $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa (abs)}$
- Water at 45 °C: $P_v = 9.59 \text{ kPa (abs)}$, $\rho = 990 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $h_s = -3.5 \text{ m}$ (suction lift)
- Pipe length $L = 6 \text{ m}$, foot valve $K = 4.5$, strainer $K = 2.0$, 90° bend $K = 0.9$, entry $K = 0.5$
- Flow $Q = 25 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 25/3600 = 0.00694 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Pipe area and velocity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
V &= \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.00694}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.572 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Friction factor.** Assume commercial steel, $\varepsilon = 0.045 \text{ mm}$, so $\varepsilon/D = 6 \times 10^{-4}$. From Moody chart at Re $\approx 1.6 \times 10^5$, $f \approx 0.020$.

**Step 3 — Compute head loss.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \left(f \frac{L}{D} + \sum K\right) \frac{V^2}{2g} \\[4pt]
&= \left(0.020 \cdot \frac{6}{0.075} + (4.5 + 2.0 + 0.9 + 0.5)\right) \frac{(1.572)^2}{2 \cdot 9.81} \\[4pt]
&= (1.60 + 7.90) \cdot 0.1260 \\[4pt]
&= 9.50 \cdot 0.1260 = 1.197 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute NPSHA.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPSHA} &= \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - h_s - h_f \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(101.325 - 9.59) \times 10^3}{990 \times 9.81} - 3.5 - 1.197 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{91735}{9711.9} - 4.697 \\[4pt]
&= 9.446 - 4.697 = 4.749 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with NPSHR.**

$$
\text{NPSHA} = 4.749 \text{ m} \;\;>\;\; \text{NPSHR} = 4.2 \text{ m}
$$

Margin $= 0.549 \text{ m}$, which meets the typical **0.5 m safety criterion**. **The pump is marginally safe** — operationally acceptable, but a flooded-suction arrangement would be preferable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This type of multi-step numerical problem is **highly recurring** in KTU University Exams. Always show: (i) the unit conversions, (ii) the sign convention for $h_s$, and (iii) an explicit margin comparison.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — NPSH System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Pressure P_atm] --> C[Net Energy Push at Suction Flange]
    B[Liquid Vapour Pressure P_v] --> C
    D[Static Suction Head h_s] --> C
    E[Suction Line Friction h_f] --> C
    C --> F{NPSHA Computed}
    F --> G[Pump Manufacturer NPSHR Curve]
    G --> H{NPSHA >= NPSHR + Margin}
    H -- Yes --> I[Safe Operation: No Cavitation]
    H -- No --> J[Cavitation: Vapour Bubble Formation]
    J --> K[Performance Loss]
    J --> L[Impeller Erosion]
    J --> M[Noise and Vibration]
    I --> N[Thoma Parameter sigma]
    I --> O[Suction Specific Speed N_ss]
    N --> P[Design Verification]
    O --> P
    P --> Q{sigma > 1.2 sigma_c AND N_ss in Range}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Final Design Approved]
    Q -- No --> S[Design Revision: Reduce N, Larger Impeller Eye, Add Inducer]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Cavitation Damage Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Liquid enters suction] --> A2[Pressure drops at impeller eye]
    A2 --> A3{Static pressure <= P_v}
    A3 -- Yes --> A4[Vapour bubble nucleation]
    A3 -- No --> A5[Smooth flow: no cavitation]
    A4 --> A6[Bubbles travel into high-pressure region]
    A6 --> A7[Rapid asymmetric bubble collapse]
    A7 --> A8[Micro-jet impact on metal]
    A8 --> A9[Material pitting and erosion]
    A7 --> A10[Local high-frequency pressure pulses]
    A10 --> A11[Vibration and structural fatigue]
    A9 --> A12[Reduced pump life]
    A11 --> A12
```

### 4.3 Suction System Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Define Suction System] --> S2{Source above or below pump?}
    S2 -- Above --> S3[Flooded Suction: h_s positive]
    S2 -- Below --> S4[Suction Lift: h_s negative]
    S3 --> S5[Compute NPSHA with plus h_s]
    S4 --> S6[Compute NPSHA with minus h_s]
    S5 --> S7[Select Pump: NPSHR from catalog]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8{NPSHA minus NPSHR >= 0.5 m}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Acceptable: Proceed]
    S8 -- No --> S10[Reduce h_f: Larger pipe or shorter run]
    S10 --> S11{Friction still too high}
    S11 -- Yes --> S12[Consider flooded suction redesign]
    S11 -- No --> S9
    S12 --> S9
    S9 --> S13[Check sigma and N_ss]
    S13 --> S14[Final Approval]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams use strictly alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters (e.g., `A1`, `S14`), avoiding reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph`. Node labels are clean alphanumeric text without markdown formatting, per the V10 safeguard protocol.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH) Available and NPSH Required. Why is NPSH critical in pump selection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **NPSHA (NPSH Available):** *(1 mark)* The total head available at the pump suction nozzle above the vapour pressure of the liquid, determined by the system upstream of the pump:

$$
\text{NPSHA} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f
$$

- **NPSHR (NPSH Required):** *(1 mark)* The minimum head that must exist at the suction nozzle to prevent the pressure at the impeller eye from falling to the vapour pressure. It is an intrinsic pump property, function of design and operating speed/flow.

- **Significance:** *(1 mark)* If $\text{NPSHA} < \text{NPSHR}$, cavitation occurs — leading to head loss, efficiency drop, vibration, noise, and impeller pitting. Hence NPSH is the primary check during pump selection and system design.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Distinguish between suction lift and flooded (positive) suction arrangements with the help of a sketch. How does each affect NPSHA?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Suction lift:** *(1 mark)* The liquid source is *below* the pump centreline. The static suction head $h_s$ is subtracted, reducing NPSHA.
- **Flooded suction:** *(1 mark)* The liquid source is *above* the pump centreline. The static suction head adds to NPSHA, providing a margin of safety.
- **Effect on NPSHA (with comparison):** *(1 mark)*

$$
\text{NPSHA}_{\text{lift}} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - h_s - h_f
$$

$$
\text{NPSHA}_{\text{flooded}} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} + h_s - h_f
$$

Flooded suction is always preferred for critical or hot-liquid service.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Derive the expression for Net Positive Suction Head Available (NPSHA) for a pump drawing liquid from an open reservoir. State clearly the sign convention used. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *A centrifugal pump handles water at 40 °C from an open sump 2.8 m below the pump centreline. The suction line is 50 mm bore, 8 m long, with a foot valve ($K = 5.0$) and one 90° elbow ($K = 0.9$). Flow rate is 20 m³/h. Pump manufacturer data gives NPSHR = 3.5 m. Determine whether the pump is safe, taking a margin of 0.5 m. Take $P_v$ at 40 °C = 7.38 kPa (abs). **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Apply the steady-flow energy equation between free surface (0) and suction flange (s):

$$
\frac{P_0}{\rho g} + \frac{V_0^2}{2g} + z_0 = \frac{P_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + z_s + h_f
$$

For a large reservoir: $V_0 = 0$, $P_0 = P_{atm}$. The static suction head is $h_s = z_0 - z_s$. Solving for $P_s/(\rho g)$ and substituting into the NPSH definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPSHA} &= \left(\frac{P_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g}\right) - \frac{P_v}{\rho g} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} + h_s - h_f
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sign convention:** *(+1 mark)* $h_s > 0$ for flooded suction (source above pump), $h_s < 0$ for suction lift. (Full 7 marks requires the inclusion of (i) the assumption of $V_0 = 0$, (ii) the algebraic manipulation, (iii) the substitution of the NPSH definition, and (iv) the explicit final boxed result.)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** *(1 mark)* Pipe cross-section and velocity:
- $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$
- $Q = 20/3600 = 0.005556 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $V = Q/A = 0.005556 / 1.963 \times 10^{-3} = 2.830 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 2:** *(1 mark)* Friction factor — assume commercial steel, $f \approx 0.022$ for $Re \approx 1.4 \times 10^5$, $\varepsilon/D = 0.045/50 = 9 \times 10^{-4}$.

**Step 3:** *(1.5 marks)* Head loss:
$$
h_f = \left(f \frac{L}{D} + \sum K\right) \frac{V^2}{2g} = \left(0.022 \cdot \frac{8}{0.05} + 5.0 + 0.9\right) \cdot \frac{(2.830)^2}{2 \cdot 9.81}
$$
$$
h_f = (3.52 + 5.9) \cdot 0.4083 = 9.42 \cdot 0.4083 = 3.846 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** *(1.5 marks)* NPSHA computation. $\rho_{40°C} \approx 992 \text{ kg/m}^3$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPSHA} &= \frac{(101.325 - 7.38) \times 10^3}{992 \times 9.81} - 2.8 - 3.846 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{93945}{9731.5} - 6.646 \\[4pt]
&= 9.653 - 6.646 = 3.007 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** *(1 mark)* Safety check:
- Required: $\text{NPSHR} + \text{margin} = 3.5 + 0.5 = 4.0 \text{ m}$.
- Available: $\text{NPSHA} = 3.007 \text{ m}$.
- Since $3.007 < 4.0$, the pump is **NOT safe**. Cavitation will occur.

**Step 6:** *(1 mark)* Recommendation: enlarge the suction pipe to 65 mm or convert to a flooded-suction arrangement.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Explain Thoma's cavitation parameter and suction specific speed. How are they used in pump design? **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *A single-stage centrifugal pump delivers 50 m³/h of water at a head of 30 m when running at 2900 rpm. The manufacturer specifies NPSHR = 4.0 m. Calculate the Thoma cavitation parameter at the duty point and the suction specific speed. Comment on the design from a cavitation standpoint. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

- **Thoma's cavitation parameter:** *(2 marks)*
$$
\sigma = \frac{\text{NPSHA}}{H}
$$
The *critical* value $\sigma_c = \text{NPSHR}/H$ marks the onset of incipient cavitation. Design practice: $\sigma \geq 1.2 \cdot \sigma_c$.

- **Suction specific speed:** *(2 marks)*
$$
N_{ss} = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(g \cdot \text{NPSHR})^{3/4}}
$$
It is a geometric index of the impeller eye. Higher $N_{ss}$ implies a smaller eye and a higher risk of cavitation.

- **Design usage:** *(3 marks)*
  1. $\sigma$ is used to scale cavitation performance between fluids of different vapour pressure.
  2. $N_{ss}$ is used to compare impeller designs irrespective of duty.
  3. Standard $N_{ss}$ range: 8000–11000 for single-suction centrifugal pumps; values above 14000 require double-suction or inducer stages.
  4. Both parameters must satisfy: $\sigma \geq 1.2 \sigma_c$ **and** $N_{ss}$ within the recommended band.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** *(1 mark)* Convert units consistently:
- $Q = 50/3600 = 0.01389 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $N = 2900 \text{ rpm}$, $H = 30 \text{ m}$, $\text{NPSHR} = 4.0 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 2:** *(1.5 marks)* Thoma parameter:
$$
\sigma_c = \frac{\text{NPSHR}}{H} = \frac{4.0}{30} = 0.1333
$$

**Step 3:** *(1.5 marks)* Suction specific speed:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{ss} &= \frac{2900 \cdot \sqrt{0.01389}}{(9.81 \cdot 4.0)^{3/4}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2900 \cdot 0.1179}{(39.24)^{0.75}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{341.91}{14.04} = 24.35 \text{ (scaled)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the standard metric form (with $N$ in rpm, $Q$ in m³/s, $g \cdot \text{NPSHR}$ in m²/s²):

$$
N_{ss} = \frac{2900 \sqrt{0.01389}}{9.81^{0.75} \cdot (4.0)^{0.75}} \approx 1920 \text{ rpm-metric}
$$

**Step 4:** *(1.5 marks)* Design comments:
- $\sigma_c = 0.1333$ is within the typical range of 0.05 to 0.3 for single-stage pumps. Acceptable.
- $N_{ss} \approx 1920$ is **well below** the recommended ceiling of 8000–11000. The pump has a conservative (low-cavitation-risk) eye design.

**Step 5:** *(1.5 marks)* Final verdict: The design is **cavitation-safe** provided NPSHA $> 4.8$ m (i.e., $\text{NPSHR} + 20\%$ margin). If the system is hot, the operator must verify $P_v$ at the actual suction temperature and re-check $\sigma$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. **Sign error in $h_s$:** Using "+ $h_s$" when the source is below the pump — instant loss of 1 to 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the vapour pressure subtraction** $P_v/(\rho g)$ — students often quote only the atmospheric head.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing kPa and metres without explicit conversion. Always state the conversion.
> 4. **Omitting the safety margin:** A 14-mark part (b) without a final "margin = NPSHA − NPSHR" comment loses at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Wrong $\sum K$:** Forgetting minor losses (foot valve, strainer, bends) in the suction line. This typically reduces NPSHA by 0.5–1.5 m.
> 6. **Mis-application of Thoma's parameter:** Using $H$ from a different operating point than the one used to compute NPSHA.
> 7. **Forgetting temperature correction:** Using $P_v$ at 20 °C when the actual liquid is at 80 °C — a classic 2-mark deduction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NPSHA** is a *system* property; **NPSHR** is a *pump* property.
- The operating rule is: $\text{NPSHA} \geq \text{NPSHR} + 0.5 \text{ m}$ (for water at moderate temperatures).
- The canonical formula is:
$$
\text{NPSHA} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f
$$
- $h_s$ is **positive** for flooded suction, **negative** for suction lift.
- $h_f$ depends on suction-pipe length, diameter, fittings, and the chosen flow velocity.
- Suction-line velocity should ideally be **$\leq 1.5$ to 2.0 m/s** to keep friction losses low.
- Hot liquids have **high $P_v$**, drastically reducing NPSHA — a major cause of cavitation in boiler-feed pumps.
- **Thoma's parameter** $\sigma = \text{NPSHA}/H$; critical value $\sigma_c = \text{NPSHR}/H$; design rule: $\sigma \geq 1.2 \sigma_c$.
- **Suction specific speed** $N_{ss}$ classifies impeller eye design; values between **8000 and 11000 (rpm-metric)** are typical for single-suction centrifugal pumps.
- **Altitude** reduces $P_{atm}$ and therefore NPSHA — approximately 1.2 kPa per 100 m of elevation.
- **Flooded suction** is always preferred over suction lift for cavation-prone services.
- Cavitation symptoms: **noise, vibration, head drop, efficiency loss, impeller pitting**.
- **Safety margins** must increase with: hot liquids, volatile hydrocarbons, altitude, and abrasive slurries.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Pump Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Pump Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pump**: A pump is a hydraulic turbomachine that converts **mechanical energy** (received from a prime mover such as an electric motor, diesel engine, or turbine) into **hydraulic energy** (a combination of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy) of a fluid, thereby enabling its transportation from a lower elevation/pressure to a higher elevation/pressure against system resistances.

A **Pump System** is a complete integrated assembly consisting of the pump, its prime mover (driver), suction and delivery piping, control valves, fittings, instrumentation, and the hydraulic network it serves. The KTU 2024 scheme treats the pump system as a holistic unit rather than just the bare pump element, emphasizing the interaction between the pump and its hydraulic environment.

**Key Performance Parameters (As per KTU PEAGT522 Module 1):**
- **Discharge (Q)**: Volume of fluid delivered per unit time, measured in **m³/s** or **L/s**.
- **Head (H)**: Energy added per unit weight of fluid, measured in **metres (m)** of fluid column.
- **Power (P)**: Energy transferred per unit time, measured in **Watts (W)** or **kilowatts (kW)**.
- **Efficiency (η)**: Ratio of useful hydraulic power output to mechanical power input (dimensionless, expressed as a percentage).
- **Speed (N)**: Rotational speed of the impeller, measured in **rpm (revolutions per minute)** or **rad/s**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Heart and Circulatory System**: Think of a pump system exactly like the human heart and blood vessels. The **heart** is the pump — it receives oxygen-poor blood (suction side) and forcefully ejects oxygen-rich blood (delivery side). The **arteries and veins** are the piping network. The **blood pressure** corresponds to the pump head, and the **blood flow rate** corresponds to the discharge. Just as a doctor evaluates a patient by checking blood pressure, pulse rate, and cardiac efficiency, a pump engineer evaluates a pump system by its head, flow rate, and efficiency. If arteries are clogged (blocked pipe), the heart has to work harder — this is analogous to a pump drawing more power when system resistance increases.

Another simple analogy: A pump is like a **bucket and pulley system** used to lift water from a well, but operating continuously and at very high speeds, transferring energy to hundreds or thousands of buckets per minute.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants Used in Pump Engineering:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Density of water at standard conditions (4°C): **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Specific weight of water: **γ = ρg = 9810 N/m³**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **P_atm = 101.325 kPa = 10.33 m of water column**
> - 1 metric horsepower: **1 HP = 745.7 W**
> - 1 kW = **102 kg·m/s** of mechanical power

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Pumps (KTU 2024 Module 1 Syllabus)

Pumps are broadly classified into two fundamental categories based on the mechanism of energy transfer:

**A. Dynamic Pumps (Rotodynamic Pumps)**
- Energy is added continuously by a rotating impeller that imparts velocity and pressure to the fluid.
- The fluid flow is continuous and smooth.
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Centrifugal Pumps** (radial flow): Fluid enters axially and is discharged radially. Used for moderate to high heads.
  - **Axial Flow Pumps**: Fluid enters and leaves axially. Used for very high discharges at low heads.
  - **Mixed Flow Pumps**: Combination of radial and axial flow characteristics.

**B. Positive Displacement (PD) Pumps**
- Energy is added by physically trapping a fixed volume of fluid and forcing it into the discharge pipe.
- The flow is inherently pulsating (in reciprocating types) or pulsed (in rotary types).
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Reciprocating Pumps**: Piston-type, plunger-type, diaphragm-type.
  - **Rotary PD Pumps**: Gear pumps, vane pumps, screw pumps, lobe pumps.

### 2.2 Components of a Typical Pump System

A complete pump installation consists of:
- **Pump unit** (impeller, casing, shaft, bearings, seals)
- **Prime mover** (electric motor, diesel engine, steam turbine)
- **Coupling and drive system** (direct drive, belt drive, gear drive)
- **Suction piping** (suction strainer, foot valve, suction pipe)
- **Delivery piping** (delivery valve, non-return valve, pressure gauge)
- **Control and instrumentation** (flow meter, pressure gauges, level sensors)
- **Foundation and baseplate**

### 2.3 Fundamental Operating Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Principle of a Centrifugal Pump (Most Common Type)**: When the impeller rotates at high speed, it creates a low-pressure region at its eye (centre). The atmospheric pressure (or upstream pressure) on the suction tank pushes the fluid up the suction pipe into the impeller eye. The rotating impeller blades throw the fluid outward by centrifugal force, adding both kinetic energy and pressure energy. The volute casing then converts a large portion of the kinetic energy into pressure energy before the fluid exits through the delivery nozzle.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol Description** | **Units** | **Application** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | Water power (hydraulic power) | W (Watts) | Power transferred to fluid |
| $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{overall}}$ | Shaft power (brake power) | W | Power required at pump shaft |
| $H = \dfrac{P}{\rho g Q}$ | Head from power | m | Head calculation from power |
| $Q = A \cdot v$ | Discharge from velocity | m³/s | Volumetric flow rate |
| $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{H_{man}}{H_{th}}$ | Manometric efficiency | dimensionless | Impeller-to-casing efficiency |
| $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless | Leakage losses assessment |
| $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | dimensionless | Bearing/friction losses |
| $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | Overall efficiency | dimensionless | Total pump performance |
| $N_{s} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed | rpm·m⁻³ᐟ⁴·s⁻¹ᐟ² | Pump type identification |
| $H_{total} = H_{static} + H_{friction} + H_{minor}$ | System head | m | Total system resistance |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pump systems are the **backbone of modern civilization**:
- **Municipal water supply**: Pumping drinking water from treatment plants to households.
- **Agriculture**: Irrigation pumps for field watering.
- **Oil and gas industry**: Crude oil transport through pipelines, refinery process pumps.
- **Power plants**: Boiler feed pumps, cooling water circulation, condensate extraction.
- **HVAC systems**: Chilled water and hot water circulation in buildings.
- **Chemical industry**: Handling corrosive, viscous, and hazardous fluids.
- **Fire protection**: High-pressure fire-fighting pumps.
- **Marine**: Bilge pumps, ballast pumps, cargo pumps on ships.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Turbomachine Equation for Pumps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Equation** is the fundamental governing equation of all turbomachines (pumps, turbines, compressors). It relates the energy transfer between a rotating impeller and the fluid to the velocity triangles at the impeller inlet and outlet.

**Step 1: Setup and Assumptions**
Consider a centrifugal pump impeller rotating with angular velocity **ω** rad/s. The fluid enters the impeller at the inner radius **r₁** with absolute velocity **V₁** and leaves at the outer radius **r₂** with absolute velocity **V₂**. The impeller blades move with blade velocity **u = ωr**.

**Step 2: Apply the Angular Momentum Theorem**
The torque on the fluid is the rate of change of angular momentum. For steady flow with no pre-whirl (V_w1 = 0 for most centrifugal pumps), the torque is:

$$
T = \rho Q (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)
$$

**Step 3: Relate Torque to Power**
Power imparted to the fluid equals torque multiplied by angular velocity:

$$
P = T \cdot \omega = \rho Q \omega (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)
$$

**Step 4: Substitute Blade Velocity (u = ωr)**
Since the blade velocity at radius r is **u = ωr**, we can rewrite the equation as:

$$
P = \rho Q (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)
$$

**Step 5: Convert Power to Head**
Dividing both sides by **ρgQ** to get energy per unit weight (head):

$$
H_{Euler} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}
$$

**Step 6: Final Euler Equation for Pumps**

$$
H_{Euler} = \frac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g} \quad \text{(metres of fluid)}
$$

For a pure radial entry (no pre-whirl, V_w1 = 0), the equation simplifies to:

$$
H_{Euler} = \frac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Specific Speed

**Step 1: Non-dimensional Analysis Foundation**
Specific speed is derived by making the pump performance equations dimensionless. The three fundamental variables governing pump performance are **flow rate Q**, **head H**, and **rotational speed N**.

**Step 2: Form the Dimensionless Group**
The specific speed N_s is defined as the speed (in rpm) at which a geometrically similar pump would operate if it were delivering unit discharge (1 m³/s) against unit head (1 m).

**Step 3: Dimensional Homogeneity**
By Buckingham's Pi theorem, the dimensionless form yields:

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}
$$

**Step 4: Significance**
- **Low N_s (below 500)**: Centrifugal pumps (radial flow)
- **Medium N_s (500 to 4000)**: Mixed flow pumps
- **High N_s (above 4000)**: Axial flow pumps

### 3.3 Derivation of Manometric Efficiency

**Step 1: Definition Setup**
Manometric efficiency is the ratio of the actual head developed by the pump (as measured by pressure gauges) to the head imparted by the impeller (theoretical Euler head).

**Step 2: Mathematical Formulation**

$$
\eta_{man} = \frac{H_{man}}{H_{Euler}} = \frac{\frac{(p_d - p_s)}{\rho g} + \frac{(V_d^2 - V_s^2)}{2g} + (z_d - z_s)}{H_{Euler}}
$$

**Step 3: Practical Significance**
A manometric efficiency of 70-90% is typical for well-designed centrifugal pumps. The remaining 10-30% loss is due to:
- Friction in the impeller passages
- Turbulence and eddy formation
- Shock losses at impeller entry
- Leakage through clearance gaps

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!TIP]
> **Problem**: A centrifugal pump delivers 0.05 m³/s of water against a head of 20 m. The pump runs at 1450 rpm with an overall efficiency of 75%. Calculate: (a) The water power, (b) The shaft power required, and (c) The specific speed.

**Solution:**

**(a) Water Power (Hydraulic Power):**

$$
P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 20
$$

$$
P_{water} = 9810 \text{ W} = 9.81 \text{ kW}
$$

**(b) Shaft Power:**

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{9810}{0.75} = 13080 \text{ W} = 13.08 \text{ kW}
$$

**(c) Specific Speed:**

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}} = \frac{1450 \times \sqrt{0.05}}{20^{3/4}}
$$

$$
N_s = \frac{1450 \times 0.2236}{20^{0.75}} = \frac{324.22}{9.457} \approx 34.28 \text{ rpm}
$$

Since N_s is very low, this is a **radial flow (centrifugal) pump**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pump System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Prime Mover: Electric Motor or Engine] --> B[Coupling and Drive System]
    B --> C[Pump Impeller and Casing]
    C --> D[Suction Side: Strain, Foot Valve, Pipe]
    C --> E[Delivery Side: NRV, Valve, Pipe]
    E --> F[Hydraulic Network: Load, Tank, Tower]
    F --> G[Control and Instrumentation]
    G --> A
    D --> H[Suction Source: Well, River, Reservoir]
    H --> C

    style A fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style E fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style F fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style G fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
    style H fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40
```

### 4.2 Classification Flow Chart of Pumps

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Pumps] --> Q[Dynamic Pumps]
    P --> R[Positive Displacement Pumps]

    Q --> Q1[Centrifugal Radial Flow]
    Q --> Q2[Mixed Flow]
    Q --> Q3[Axial Flow]

    R --> R1[Reciprocating Pumps]
    R --> R2[Rotary Pumps]

    R1 --> R1A[Piston Type]
    R1 --> R1B[Plunger Type]
    R1 --> R1C[Diaphragm Type]

    R2 --> R2A[Gear Pumps]
    R2 --> R2B[Vane Pumps]
    R2 --> R2C[Screw Pumps]
    R2 --> R2D[Lobe Pumps]

    Q1 --> Q1A[Volute Casing]
    Q1 --> Q1B[Diffuser Type]

    style P fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style Q fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
    style R fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#4527a0
```

### 4.3 Typical Centrifugal Pump System Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Suction Tank] -->|Suction Pipe| F1[Foot Valve and Strainer]
    F1 --> G1[Suction Pipe with Bend]
    G1 --> I1[Impeller Inlet Eye]
    I1 --> I2[Rotating Impeller Blades]
    I2 --> I3[Volute Casing]
    I3 -->|Delivery Nozzle| V1[Delivery Valve and NRV]
    V1 --> M1[Pressure Gauge]
    M1 --> D1[Delivery Pipe to Consumer]
    D1 --> T1[Discharge Tank or Tower]

    M2[Electric Motor] -->|Coupling| I2

    style S1 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style I2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style I3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style M2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style T1 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1. Define a pump and classify pumps based on the principle of energy transfer.**

**Model Answer:**
A pump is a mechanical device that converts mechanical energy into hydraulic energy of a fluid, enabling it to flow from a low-pressure region to a high-pressure region. Based on the principle of energy transfer, pumps are classified into two main categories:
1. **Dynamic (Rotodynamic) Pumps**: Energy is added continuously by a rotating impeller through velocity changes. Examples: centrifugal, axial flow, mixed flow pumps.
2. **Positive Displacement Pumps**: Energy is added by trapping a fixed volume of fluid and mechanically displacing it into the discharge pipe. Examples: reciprocating pumps (piston type) and rotary pumps (gear type).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Write the Euler equation for a centrifugal pump and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
The Euler equation for a centrifugal pump is:

$$
H_{Euler} = \frac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g}
$$

Where:
- **H_Euler** = Theoretical head developed by the impeller (metres of fluid)
- **u_1, u_2** = Tangential (blade) velocity of impeller at inlet and outlet respectively (m/s)
- **V_w1, V_w2** = Whirl (tangential) component of absolute fluid velocity at inlet and outlet (m/s)
- **g** = Acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)

For a pump with no pre-whirl (radial entry), V_w1 = 0, and the equation simplifies to **H_Euler = u₂V_w2 / g**.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1):** [14 Marks]

**(a) Derive the Euler equation for a centrifugal pump starting from the angular momentum principle. State clearly all assumptions made. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow.
2. Uniform velocity distribution at impeller inlet and outlet.
3. No friction losses within the impeller passages.
4. Flow enters the impeller without pre-whirl (V_w1 = 0) for a pure radial entry design.

**Derivation:**

Consider an impeller rotating with angular velocity ω. Apply the angular momentum theorem to the control volume of the impeller:

$$
T = \rho Q (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)
$$

Power transferred to fluid:

$$
P = T \omega = \rho Q \omega (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)
$$

Since blade velocity **u = ωr**, substitute:

$$
P = \rho Q (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)
$$

The Euler head (energy per unit weight) is **P / (ρgQ)**:

$$
\boxed{H_{Euler} = \frac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g}}
$$

For radial entry (V_w1 = 0):

$$
H_{Euler} = \frac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g}
$$

**(b) A centrifugal pump has an impeller diameter of 0.4 m running at 1450 rpm. The velocity of water at impeller outlet is 12 m/s at an angle of 30° with the tangent. Calculate: (i) Blade velocity at outlet, (ii) Whirl component of velocity, (iii) Work done per unit weight of water, (iv) Manometric efficiency if the actual head developed is 18 m. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** D = 0.4 m, N = 1450 rpm, V_2 = 12 m/s, β = 30° (angle with tangent), H_man = 18 m.

**(i) Blade velocity at outlet:**

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 1450}{60}
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{1822.12}{60} = 30.37 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Whirl component of velocity:**

$$
V_{w2} = V_2 \cos(30°) = 12 \times 0.866 = 10.39 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iii) Work done per unit weight (Euler head):**

$$
H_{Euler} = \frac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g} = \frac{30.37 \times 10.39}{9.81}
$$

$$
H_{Euler} = \frac{315.54}{9.81} = 32.16 \text{ m}
$$

**(iv) Manometric efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{man} = \frac{H_{man}}{H_{Euler}} = \frac{18}{32.16} = 0.5597 \approx 55.97\%
$$

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative):** [14 Marks]

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a centrifugal pump. List its main components. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A centrifugal pump consists of the following main components:
- **Impeller**: A rotating disc with curved or straight blades that imparts energy to the fluid.
- **Casing (Volute)**: A spiral-shaped stationary chamber that collects the fluid from the impeller and converts kinetic energy into pressure energy.
- **Shaft**: Connects the impeller to the prime mover (motor/engine).
- **Bearings**: Support the shaft and allow smooth rotation.
- **Shaft Sealing**: Stuffing box or mechanical seal to prevent leakage along the shaft.
- **Suction and Delivery Nozzles**: Pipe connections for inlet and outlet.

**Working Principle:**
1. The prime mover rotates the impeller at high speed.
2. The impeller creates a low-pressure region at its eye (centre).
3. Atmospheric pressure or suction tank head pushes the fluid up the suction pipe into the impeller.
4. The rotating blades throw the fluid outward by centrifugal force, adding kinetic energy and pressure energy.
5. In the volute casing, the kinetic energy is progressively converted into pressure energy.
6. The pressurized fluid leaves through the delivery nozzle into the system.

**Diagram:** (Refer to the Mermaid schematic in Section 4.3)

**(b) Explain the terms: manometric efficiency, volumetric efficiency, mechanical efficiency, and overall efficiency. How are they related? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Manometric Efficiency (η_man):** It is the ratio of the actual head developed by the pump (as measured between suction and delivery gauge points) to the head imparted by the impeller (Euler head).

$$
\eta_{man} = \frac{H_{man}}{H_{Euler}}
$$

It accounts for hydraulic losses like friction, turbulence, and shock losses within the impeller and casing.

**Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol):** It is the ratio of the actual discharge delivered by the pump to the theoretical discharge (impeller displacement).

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}
$$

It accounts for leakage losses through the clearances between the impeller and casing.

**Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech):** It is the ratio of the power imparted to the fluid (water power) to the power supplied at the pump shaft (brake power).

$$
\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}}
$$

It accounts for mechanical losses in bearings, seals, and disc friction.

**Overall Efficiency (η_overall):** It is the product of all three efficiencies and represents the total performance of the pump:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}}
$$

For a well-designed centrifugal pump, η_overall typically ranges from 60% to 85%.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Errors**: Forgetting to convert N from rpm to rad/s when using **u = πDN/60**. Always use **u = πDN/60 (m/s)** to get blade velocity directly.
> 2. **Confusing V and V_w**: Students often use the absolute velocity V₂ directly in Euler's equation instead of the whirl component V_w2 = V₂ cos(β). The angle β must be extracted carefully.
> 3. **Sign Convention**: For a pump, work done is **positive** (energy added to fluid). For a turbine, it is **negative** (energy extracted). Mixing up signs loses marks.
> 4. **Efficiency Calculation Order**: When computing overall efficiency, multiply all three component efficiencies as **fractions (not percentages)**. Convert to percentage only at the final answer.
> 5. **Specific Speed Units**: The units of specific speed are often left unspecified. Always state the units or use the dimensionless version.
> 6. **Skipping Assumptions**: In derivations, KTU examiners specifically look for a clear statement of assumptions. Skipping this step costs 1-2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction to Pump Systems**

- **Definition**: A pump converts mechanical energy → hydraulic energy of a fluid.
- **Classification**: Two main types — **Dynamic** (centrifugal, axial, mixed flow) and **Positive Displacement** (reciprocating, rotary).
- **Centrifugal Pump**: Most common; uses rotating impeller + volute casing.
- **Euler Equation**: **H = (u₂V_w2 − u₁V_w1) / g**
- **Power Equation**: **P = ρgQH** (water power); **P_shaft = P_water / η_overall**
- **Specific Speed**: **N_s = N√Q / H^(3/4)** — determines pump type.
- **Efficiencies**: η_man × η_vol × η_mech = η_overall
- **Standard Values**: g = 9.81 m/s², ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³, γ_water = 9810 N/m³
- **Applications**: Water supply, irrigation, oil & gas, power plants, HVAC, chemical, marine.
- **Remember**: For radial entry pumps, **V_w1 = 0** (simplification used in 90% of KTU problems).
- **Typical Efficiency Range**: 60-85% for centrifugal pumps; 70-95% for PD pumps.
- **Component Identification**: Impeller, casing, shaft, bearings, seals, suction/delivery nozzles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Pump Systems in Agriculture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Importance of Pump Systems in Agriculture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme** and the **NEP 2020** outcome-based syllabus for **PEAGT522**, a **Pump System** in agriculture refers to an integrated mechanical-hydraulic assembly — comprising a prime mover (electric motor, diesel engine, or tractor PTO), a pumping device (centrifugal, submersible, axial-flow, or positive-displacement), associated piping, control valves, and conveyance structures — designed to lift, transfer, pressurize, and distribute water and other agricultural fluids (slurry, liquid fertilizer, pesticides) for productive agronomic use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Module-1 Highlight):**
> *"Agricultural pump systems are the mechanical heart of irrigated agriculture, converting rotary or reciprocating mechanical energy into hydraulic energy to overcome gravitational head, friction losses, and elevation differences between the water source and the point of application."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a small hilltop farm where a farmer grows paddy. Below the hill flows a river. Without any device, water simply flows downhill — the farmer cannot use it at the top. The **pump** is essentially the "water elevator." It grabs water at the bottom, pushes it up the pipeline using rotating blades (or pistons), and delivers it to the field at the top so the crop can drink.

Think of it like a **water blood-circulation system for the farm**:
- The **river/borewell** = the heart (source of water).
- The **pump** = the heart's pumping action.
- The **pipes** = arteries and veins.
- The **field/crop** = the organ receiving blood (water).

Without this circulation, even a water-rich region can have thirsty crops.

### 1.3 Why Pumps are Central to Modern Agriculture

Agriculture consumes approximately **70% of global freshwater withdrawals**, and within that, irrigation accounts for the bulk. In India alone, the agricultural sector uses roughly **80% of total available freshwater**, and a substantial fraction of this water is delivered via pump-driven systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Remember (High-Yield for KTU Exams):**
> - **Global irrigation water use ≈ 2,800 km³/year**
> - **India's net irrigated area ≈ 73 million hectares** (≈ 50% of cultivated land)
> - **Energy consumed by agricultural pumps in India ≈ 18–20% of total national electricity** — this single fact is a favourite KTU board question.

The **importance** of pump systems in agriculture can be classified into four pillars:

| Pillar | Function |
|---|---|
| **Irrigation** | Supplementing rainfall to meet crop evapotranspiration demand |
| **Drainage** | Removing excess water to prevent waterlogging and salinization |
| **Livestock Watering** | Supplying clean drinking water to dairy/poultry units |
| **Agro-Processing** | Operating drip/micro-sprinkler systems, fertigation, aquaculture |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Role of Pumps in Agriculture — Structured Logic

A. **Overcoming Geospatial Mismatch**
   - Water sources (rivers, wells, reservoirs) and crop fields are rarely co-located at the same elevation.
   - Pumps deliver the **hydraulic head (H)** required to lift water against gravity.

B. **Matching Crop Water Requirement (CWR)**
   - Crop water demand = **ETc × Area**, where ETc is crop evapotranspiration.
   - Pumps must deliver a discharge **Q (m³/s)** that matches the peak daily requirement during critical growth stages.

C. **Pressure Generation for Modern Methods**
   - Drip and sprinkler systems need a **minimum operating pressure** (typically **1–3 bar**).
   - Pumps convert electrical/mechanical input into pressure head.

D. **Energy Conversion Chain**
   - Electrical energy → Mechanical energy (motor) → Hydraulic energy (pump) → Useful work at the field outlet.
   - Each stage has losses, captured by the overall **pump-system efficiency η_system**.

E. **Operational Flexibility**
   - Pumps allow **time-shifting** of water — abstracting water when available (e.g., night-time grid power, monsoon flow) and applying when crop needs it.

### 2.2 Classification of Agricultural Pump Applications

1. **Open-well submersible pumps** — for shallow water tables (up to 25 m).
2. **Borewell submersible pumps** — for deep tubewells (up to 300 m).
3. **Centrifugal mono-block pumps** — for surface water (river, canal, pond lifting).
4. **Axial-flow / mixed-flow pumps** — for high discharge, low head applications (e.g., delta irrigation).
5. **Solar PV pumps** — off-grid sustainable option, promoted under **PM-KUSUM** scheme.
6. **Tractor PTO-driven pumps** — mobile, deployable anywhere.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains the **must-know formulas** for any numerical question on this topic. Every symbol, unit, and boundary condition is exam-critical.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \times V$ | Discharge = cross-section × velocity | m³/s |
| 2 | $P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | Hydraulic (water) power | **Watt (W)** |
| 3 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Shaft power input to pump | **Watt (W)** |
| 4 | $P_{input} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$ | Electrical power consumed | **Watt (W)** |
| 5 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}$ | Combined system efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
