| 6 | $H_{total} = H_{static} + h_{friction} + h_{minor} + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | Energy equation (Bernoulli applied) | **m of water** |
| 7 | $h_{friction} = \dfrac{4f \cdot L \cdot V^{2}}{2gD}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | **m** |
| 8 | $ETc = Kc \cdot ETo$ | Crop evapotranspiration | mm/day |
| 9 | $Q_{req} = \dfrac{ETc \cdot A}{t \cdot 3600 \cdot 24}$ | Required pump discharge for area A | m³/s |
| 10 | $E_d = \dfrac{P_{input} \times t}{A}$ | Specific energy for irrigation | kWh/ha |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard constants used in KTU numericals:**
> - $\rho_{water}$ = **1000 kg/m³**
> - $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
> - 1 ha = **10,000 m²**
> - 1 HP = **746 W**; 1 kW = **1000 W**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern Indian agriculture, an **average 5 HP submersible pump** irrigates roughly **1.0–1.5 hectares** of paddy or **2.0–2.5 hectares** of drip-irrigated sugarcane. Multiply this across ~30 million energized pump-sets in India and you see why pump systems form the **single largest electrical load** of the Indian grid — making them a national energy-policy priority. Engineers design **variable-speed drives (VFDs)**, **solar PV arrays**, and **microcontroller-based auto-switching** to make these systems both economically and environmentally sustainable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Pump Power from Crop Water Requirement

This is the **most repeated KTU derivation** linking hydraulic engineering to agronomy. We derive the electrical energy a pump consumes to irrigate a given crop area.

**Given:**
- Crop area $A$ = 4 hectares
- Crop evapotranspiration $ETc$ = 6 mm/day (typical for paddy in summer)
- Total dynamic head $H$ = 20 m
- Pump efficiency $\eta_{pump}$ = 60%
- Motor efficiency $\eta_{motor}$ = 85%
- Operating hours per day $t$ = 8 h
- Density of water $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³
- Acceleration due to gravity $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Convert ETc into volume of water required per day.**

$$
V_{day} = ETc \times A
$$

Substituting values (note 6 mm = 0.006 m, 4 ha = 40,000 m²):

$$
V_{day} = 0.006 \times 40{,}000 = 240 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Interpretation:** The crop needs 240 cubic metres of water every day.

**Step 2 — Compute the average discharge Q delivered during the 8-hour pumping window.**

$$
Q = \dfrac{V_{day}}{t \times 3600}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{240}{8 \times 3600} = \dfrac{240}{28{,}800} = 8.33 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Interpretation:** The pump must deliver approximately 8.33 litres every second to meet the crop's daily thirst within the allowed runtime.

**Step 3 — Compute the hydraulic (water) power required.**

$$
P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H
$$

$$
P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 8.33 \times 10^{-3} \times 20
$$

$$
P_{water} = 1634.7 \ \text{W} \approx 1.635 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Interpretation:** Useful hydraulic work output is about 1.6 kW.

**Step 4 — Convert to shaft power using pump efficiency.**

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{\eta_{pump}} = \dfrac{1634.7}{0.60}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = 2724.5 \ \text{W} \approx 2.72 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to electrical input power using motor efficiency.**

$$
P_{input} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \dfrac{2724.5}{0.85}
$$

$$
P_{input} = 3205.3 \ \text{W} \approx 3.21 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Interpretation:** The farmer must pay for ~3.2 kW of grid power to deliver the required 1.6 kW of water power to the crop — the rest is lost to inefficiency.

**Step 6 — Compute daily electrical energy consumption.**

$$
E_{day} = P_{input} \times t = 3.21 \times 8 = 25.68 \ \text{kWh/day}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute area-specific energy (a useful benchmarking indicator).**

$$
E_{specific} = \dfrac{E_{day}}{A} = \dfrac{25.68}{4} = 6.42 \ \text{kWh/ha·day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for KTU answers:** Always express the final answer with the **SI unit spelled out** and a one-line interpretation. Examiners award marks for the closing interpretive sentence.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Pump Types for Agriculture

| Selection Criterion | Centrifugal | Submersible | Axial Flow | Solar PV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Head range** | 5–100 m | 20–300 m | 2–10 m | Up to 100 m |
| **Discharge** | Medium | Medium–High | Very High | Low–Medium |
| **Best suited crop** | Vegetables, sugarcane | Paddy, wheat, orchards | Paddy in deltas | Drip-irrigated orchards |
| **Energy source** | Grid/diesel | Grid/diesel | Grid/diesel | Solar |
| **Initial cost** | Low | Medium | Medium | High (but subsidy-aided) |
| **Operating cost** | Medium | Medium | Medium | Near zero (after install) |
| **CO₂ footprint** | High | High | High | **Zero** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Agricultural Pump System Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions between components** of a typical grid-connected farm pump system. This is the visual framework expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Water Source\nRiver or Borewell] --> intakeA[Suction\nPipeline with Foot Valve]
    intakeA --> pumpA[Centrifugal or Submersible\nPump Unit]
    pwrA[Electric Motor\nor Diesel Engine] --> pumpA
    pwrA -. Energy Flow .-> pumpA
    pumpA --> dischA[Delivery\nPipeline]
    dischA --> ctrlA[Control Valves\nand Pressure Gauges]
    ctrlA --> filterA[Filter and\nFertigation Tank]
    filterA --> fieldA[Field Distribution\nSprinkler or Drip Network]
    fieldA --> cropA[Crop Root Zone\nSoil Water Uptake]
    excessA[Excess Water] --> drainA[Drainage Pump\nReturn to Source]
    fieldA --> excessA
    ctrlA -. Feedback Signal .-> monA[SCADA or Local\nPressure Switch]
    monA -. Control Signal .-> pwrA

    style srcA fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style pumpA fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e
    style pwrA fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d
    style cropA fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style monA fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Pump Selection in Agriculture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Farmer Requirement:\nArea and Crop Identified] --> calcA[Compute ETc\nand Volume Needed]
    calcA --> qHeadA{Static Head\nThreshold}
    qHeadA -->|Head less than 10 m| lowA[Axial or Mixed-Flow\nSurface Pump]
    qHeadA -->|Head 10 to 100 m| midA[Centrifugal Mono-block\nor Submersible]
    qHeadA -->|Head above 100 m| highA[Deep Borewell\nSubmersible Pump]
    lowA --> qPowerA{Power Source\nAvailable}
    midA --> qPowerA
    highA --> qPowerA
    qPowerA -->|Reliable grid| gridA[Electric Motor Pump Set]
    qPowerA -->|No grid or unreliable| solarA[Solar PV Pump\nPM KUSUM Scheme]
    qPowerA -->|Mobile requirement| ptoA[Tractor PTO\nDriven Pump]
    gridA --> endA[Install and Commission]
    solarA --> endA
    ptoA --> endA

    style startA fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style endA fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style solarA fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#854d0e
```

### 4.3 Component-Level Energy Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    eInA[Electrical Input\nkWh] --> mLossA[Motor Loss\n1 minus eta motor]
    mLossA --> mOutA[Mechanical Shaft Power\nkW]
    mOutA --> pLossA[Pump Hydraulic Loss\n1 minus eta pump]
    pLossA --> wOutA[Water Power at Outlet\nrho g Q H]
    wOutA --> fLossA[Friction and Minor Losses\nin Piping]
    fLossA --> fieldA[Useful Power at Crop\nEffective Pressure]

    style eInA fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b
    style wOutA fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e
    style fieldA fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU Board Answer:**
> The chain above demonstrates why only ~30–50% of the electrical energy paid for by the farmer is actually delivered as useful water energy. Every loss block reduces system efficiency and is the **engineering rationale** for upgrading to energy-efficient IE3/IE4 motors, VFD control, and friction-reducing HDPE piping.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*"List any three functional roles of pump systems in modern agriculture."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The three principal functional roles are:
1. **Irrigation** — lifting and distributing water from a source to crop fields to meet evapotranspiration demand.
2. **Drainage** — removing excess water from fields to prevent waterlogging and soil salinity.
3. **Pressure generation for micro-irrigation** — providing the operational head required by drip and sprinkler systems.

*(Any three valid roles — supply of drinking water to livestock, fertigation mixing, aquaculture aeration — also accepted.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"Define the term 'overall efficiency' of an agricultural pump system."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Overall efficiency of a pump system is the ratio of the useful hydraulic energy delivered at the field outlet to the electrical energy consumed at the motor input. Mathematically:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{transmission}
$$

It is dimensionless, usually expressed as a percentage, and for typical agricultural installations ranges between **30% and 55%**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the functional components of an agricultural pump system and the energy flow between them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A farmer intends to irrigate 2 hectares of banana plantation using a borewell. The crop evapotranspiration is 5 mm/day. The total dynamic head is 35 m. The pump efficiency is 55% and motor efficiency is 88%. The pump operates for 6 hours per day. Calculate: (i) the required discharge, (ii) the daily electrical energy consumed, and (iii) the area-specific energy in kWh/ha·day. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

The block diagram is as drawn in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The narrative explanation:
- **Source** (borewell/river) supplies water to the **suction pipeline**, fitted with a foot valve and strainer to prevent debris entry.
- The **pump** (centrifugal/submersible) receives mechanical energy from the **prime mover** (electric motor/diesel engine).
- The water is pressurized and discharged through a **delivery pipeline**.
- **Control valves** regulate flow; **pressure gauges** monitor performance.
- The water passes through a **filter and fertigation tank** before entering the **field distribution network** (drip/sprinkler).
- The crop's **root zone** extracts the water via soil-plant-atmosphere continuum.
- A **drainage loop** returns excess water to the source.
- A **feedback / monitoring system** (pressure switch or SCADA) sends control signals back to the prime mover for automation.

**Part (b):**

**(i) Required Discharge Q**

$$
V_{day} = ETc \times A = 0.005 \times 20{,}000 = 100 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{100}{6 \times 3600} = \dfrac{100}{21{,}600} = 4.63 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(ii) Daily electrical energy consumed**

$$
P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4.63 \times 10^{-3} \times 35 = 1589.6 \ \text{W}
$$

$$
P_{input} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{\eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor}} = \dfrac{1589.6}{0.55 \times 0.88}
$$

$$
P_{input} = \dfrac{1589.6}{0.484} = 3284.3 \ \text{W} \approx 3.28 \ \text{kW}
$$

$$
E_{day} = P_{input} \times t = 3.28 \times 6 = 19.71 \ \text{kWh/day}
$$

**(iii) Area-specific energy**

$$
E_{specific} = \dfrac{19.71}{2} = 9.86 \ \text{kWh/ha·day}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss in detail the importance of pump systems in Indian agriculture with reference to irrigation, drainage, and micro-irrigation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the suitability of centrifugal, submersible, axial-flow, and solar PV pumps for different agricultural applications. Which would you recommend for a remote tribal village with no grid supply and 1.5 ha of drip-irrigated horticultural crops? Justify. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

India's agriculture supports **58% of its population** but receives highly **spatially and temporally uneven rainfall**. Pump systems bridge this gap by:
1. **Irrigation** — India has the world's largest irrigated area (~73 million ha). Pumps lift groundwater and surface water, ensuring two/three crops per year instead of one rain-fed crop.
2. **Drainage** — In states like Kerala, Karnataka, and coastal Andhra, heavy monsoons cause waterlogging. Drainage pumps restore aerated soil conditions.
3. **Micro-irrigation enablement** — Drip and sprinkler systems are pressure-driven; without pumps, the **1–3 bar** operating pressure cannot be generated.
4. **Energy-economic significance** — agricultural pumps consume ~**20% of India's electricity**, making them central to both rural livelihoods and national energy planning.

**Part (b):**

| Pump Type | Best Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Centrifugal | Surface water, 5–100 m head | Low cost, easy maintenance |
| Submersible | Deep borewells, 20–300 m head | Compact, high efficiency |
| Axial-flow | High discharge, low head (delta paddy) | Very high Q |
| Solar PV | Off-grid, drip-irrigated orchards | Zero running cost, subsidy-aided |

**Recommendation:** For a remote tribal village with **no grid supply** and **1.5 ha of drip-irrigated horticulture**, the **Solar PV pump** is the optimal choice because:
- No grid dependency — solves the energy-availability problem.
- Operating pressure matches drip system requirements (typically 1–2 bar, equivalent to ~10–20 m head).
- Zero recurring electricity cost — improves farmer income.
- Eligible for **PM-KUSUM** subsidy (up to 60% of cost), reducing capital barrier.
- Environmentally clean and low maintenance (no diesel/grid connection).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to metres** in ETc — a single missing ×10⁻³ costs **2 marks** in numericals.
> 2. **Not multiplying pump efficiency and motor efficiency together** when computing input power — students often divide twice.
> 3. **Skipping units in intermediate steps** — KTU answer sheets are audited by examiners who award partial marks only when units are present.
> 4. **Writing "to irrigate crops" instead of a technical phrasing** — use *"to meet crop evapotranspiration demand"* for full marks.
> 5. **Missing the final interpretive sentence** — every numerical answer must end with a one-line physical interpretation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Agricultural pump systems** are integrated mechanical-hydraulic assemblies lifting and distributing water against head.
- **Four functional pillars**: irrigation, drainage, livestock watering, micro-irrigation pressure supply.
- **Indian pumps consume ~18–20% of national electricity** — a defining policy and engineering fact.
- **Water power formula**: $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$.
- **Electrical input power**: $P_{input} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$.
- **Required discharge**: $Q = \dfrac{ETc \cdot A}{t \cdot 3600}$.
- **Total head**: $H = H_{static} + h_{friction} + h_{minor} + V^{2}/2g$.
- **Pump types** for agriculture: centrifugal, submersible, axial-flow, solar PV, tractor PTO.
- **Solar PV pumps** = future of off-grid Indian agriculture (PM-KUSUM).
- **Constants to memorize**: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², 1 HP = 746 W, 1 ha = 10,000 m².
- **Energy efficiency benchmark**: overall $\eta_{system} = 30\%-55\%$ in real-world farms.
- **Drip/sprinkler systems** require 1–3 bar operating pressure — pumps enable this.
- **Always end numerical answers** with a closing interpretive sentence for full KTU marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Pumping Systems in Agriculture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Types of Pumping Systems in Agriculture # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Pumping System in Agriculture** is an integrated electromechanical / mechanical assembly comprising a **prime mover** (energy source), a **pump** (energy conversion device), **transmission elements** (couplings, gearboxes, belts, pulleys), **control devices** (valves, starters, panels), and **conduit accessories** (suction pipe, delivery pipe, foot valve, non-return valve, strainer) that collectively lifts, transfers, and regulates water for farm-level applications such as irrigation, drainage, livestock watering, pisciculture, agro-processing, and farmstead domestic supply. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** "Types of Pumping Systems in Agriculture" is treated as a foundational topic. The learner must be able to **classify, identify, and select** the appropriate pumping system for a given agricultural scenario based on **water source, discharge head, energy availability, and economic viability**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a farm's water requirement like a **delivery truck system** in a city: - The **prime mover** (electric motor / diesel engine) is the *driver* of the truck. - The **pump** is the *engine* of the truck that converts fuel/electricity into motion. - The **suction pipe** is the *intake* through which the truck loads water. - The **delivery pipe** is the *outlet* through which water reaches the field. - The **foot valve** is the *one-way door* that prevents water from falling back when the pump is off. - The **control valves** are the *traffic signals* that regulate flow. > [!NOTE] > **Just like choosing between a small van, a medium truck, or a large trailer for different delivery loads, an agricultural engineer must choose between centrifugal, submersible, jet, turbine, solar, or diesel pumps based on the head, discharge, and water-source characteristics.** ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics The following **standard metrics** govern every agricultural pumping system: - **Discharge (Q)** — measured in **litres per second (L/s)** or **cubic metres per hour (m³/h)**. - **Total Dynamic Head (TDH)** — measured in **metres (m)** of water column. - **Power input (P_in)** — measured in **kilowatts (kW)** or **horsepower (HP)** where **1 HP = 0.7457 kW**. - **Shaft Power (SP)** — measured in **kW**. - **Water Power (WP)** = **ρ × g × Q × H** = **9.81 × Q × H kW** (with Q in m³/s and H in m). - **Overall Pump Efficiency (η)** = **(Water Power / Shaft Power) × 100%**. ## 1.4 Broad Classification of Pumping Systems in Agriculture Agricultural pumping systems are classified along **three orthogonal axes**: | Axis | Classification | |---|---| | **(A) By Water Source** | Open well, Borewell, River/Canal, Farm pond, Reservoir | | **(B) By Prime Mover** | Electric motor, Diesel engine, Tractor PTO, Solar PV, Wind, Manual/Animal | | **(C) By Pump Mechanism** | Centrifugal, Submersible, Jet, Turbine, Reciprocating, Airlift, Rotary | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks "Classify pumping systems in agriculture", always tabulate along **all three axes** to fetch full marks. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Component Architecture of an Agricultural Pumping System Every agricultural pump set, irrespective of size, contains the following **sub-systems**: ### (i) Prime Mover (Energy Source) - **Electric Motor** — 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor is most common; runs on **415 V, 50 Hz** AC supply; efficiency range **85–95%**. - **Diesel Engine** — used where grid power is unreliable; specific fuel consumption **~0.25 L/kWh**; rated speeds **900–1800 rpm**. - **Tractor PTO (Power Take-Off)** — standard speed **540 rpm (Type I)** or **1000 rpm (Type II)**; transmits **up to 75 kW**. - **Solar PV Array** — powers DC/AC submersible or surface pumps; sizing follows the formula **P_pv (W) = (Q × H × 9.81 × safety_factor) / (η_pump × η_motor)**. - **Windmill** — used for low-head, low-discharge livestock-watering schemes. ### (ii) Pump (Energy Conversion Device) - Converts **mechanical / electrical energy → hydraulic energy**. - Categories covered in §2.2. ### (iii) Transmission / Coupling - **Direct coupling** (alignment-critical, no losses). - **Belt-pulley drive** (V-belt / flat belt) — introduces **3–8% slip loss**. - **Gearbox** (for high-speed reduction). - **Flexible coupling** to absorb minor misalignments. ### (iv) Suction & Delivery Side Accessories - **Foot valve with strainer** — retains prime, filters debris. - **Non-return valve (NRV)** — prevents back-flow when pump stops. - **Sluice / gate valve** — flow isolation. - **Pressure gauge** (delivery side) and **vacuum gauge** (suction side) — performance monitoring. - **Air vessel** — on reciprocating pumps to dampen pulsation. > [!NOTE] > **Why accessory fittings matter:** In KTU numericals, "frictional head loss in suction and delivery pipes" is computed using these components; forgetting them typically costs **2 marks** per problem. ## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Pumps Used in Agriculture ### A. Centrifugal Pump (Surface / Mono-block) - **Working principle:** Centrifugal force generated by an *impeller* rotating at high speed pushes water outward. - **Best for:** Open wells, sumps, canals, farm ponds — sources with water level within **7 m** of pump centre. - **Limitations:** Cannot self-prime efficiently without an air-separation device. - **Types:** Radial-flow, Mixed-flow, Axial-flow. - **Sub-types:** Monoblock, Coupled, Self-priming. ### B. Submersible Pump (Borewell / Tubewell) - **Working principle:** Hermetically sealed motor + pump integrated into a single unit dropped inside the borewell. - **Best for:** Borewells deeper than **7 m**, where suction head becomes a limitation for centrifugal pumps. - **Advantage:** No priming required; no foot valve; no suction pipe; silent operation. - **Disadvantage:** Repair requires pulling out the entire pump stack from the borewell. ### C. Jet Pump (Ejector Pump) - **Working principle:** Uses a *nozzle + venturi* assembly on the delivery side of a centrifugal pump; a portion of the discharge is re-circulated back through a venturi to create a vacuum that lifts additional water from the well. - **Best for:** Moderate depths (up to **30–45 m** for deep-well jet pumps). - **Categories:** *Shallow-well jet* (single pipe) and *Deep-well jet* (two pipes). ### D. Vertical Turbine Pump (VT Pump) - **Working principle:** A surface-mounted motor drives a long vertical shaft connected to impellers submerged inside a deep well or sump. - **Best for:** Large-discharge irrigation, river-lift schemes, high-flow borewells up to **200+ m**. ### E. Reciprocating (Piston) Pump - **Working principle:** A piston moving in a cylinder displaces a fixed volume of water per stroke. - **Best for:** Very high head, low discharge, viscous fluids, agro-processing. - **Types:** Single-acting, Double-acting, Duplex, Triplex. ### F. Airlift Pump - **Working principle:** Compressed air is injected into the water column; the air-water mixture, being lighter, rises to the surface. - **Best for:** Corrosive / sandy water, drainage of deep sumps, aquaculture. ### G. Rotary Pump (Gear / Vane / Screw) - **Working principle:** Rotating elements (gears, vanes, screws) trap and transport a fixed volume of fluid per revolution. - **Best for:** High-viscosity fluids, oils, molasses in agro-processing. ### H. Solar / Wind / Manual Pumps - **Photovoltaic (PV) solar pump** — DC or AC; no grid dependency; **MNRE (PM-KUSUM)** scheme subsidises them in India. - **Windmill pump** — Aermotor-type; discharges **1000–5000 L/day** at 5–10 m head. - **Manual / Rope-and-Bucket / Hand-pump** — small farmstead / livestock use. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Water Power (WP)** | $WP = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | kW | ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s² | | 2 | **Water Power (compact form)** | $WP = 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H$ | kW | with Q in m³/s, H in m | | 3 | **Shaft Power required** | $SP = \dfrac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | kW | — | | 4 | **Electrical Input Power** | $P_{in} = \dfrac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$ | kW | — | | 5 | **Diesel Engine Power** | $P_{diesel} = \dfrac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H}{360 \cdot \eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{engine}}$ | HP | when Q in L/s | | 6 | **Specific Speed (N_s)** | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | — | Q in m³/s, H in m, N in rpm | | 7 | **Affinity Law — Discharge** | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | — | At constant impeller diameter | | 8 | **Affinity Law — Head** | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{2}$ | — | At constant impeller diameter | | 9 | **Affinity Law — Power** | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{3}$ | — | At constant impeller diameter | | 10 | **NPSH Available** | $NPSH_{a} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_{vapour}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_{f,suction}$ | m | Atmospheric = 10.33 m at sea level | | 11 | **Manometric Head (H_m)** | $H_m = (z_2 - z_1) + \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g}$ | m | — | | 12 | **Total Static Head** | $H_{static} = (z_{del} - z_{suc}) = S_{depth} + D_{height}$ | m | — | | 13 | **Total Dynamic Head** | $TDH = H_{static} + h_{f,suc} + h_{f,del}$ | m | Includes all friction losses | | 14 | **Overall Efficiency** | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}$ | — | — | | 15 | **Pump Cost per Unit Water** | $C = \dfrac{\text{Capital Cost} + \text{Annual Energy/Maintenance}}{\text{Annual Volume Pumped}}$ | ₹/m³ | Economic selection metric | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Numerical Solving Trick:** When a problem gives discharge in **L/s**, first convert to **m³/s** by **dividing by 1000** before applying $WP = 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H$. This is the single most common arithmetic mistake. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Lift irrigation schemes** (e.g., *Jalyukt Shivar Abhiyan* in Maharashtra) deploy **submersible + centrifugal hybrid systems** to lift water from rivers to farm canals. - **Drip and sprinkler systems** require **pressurised, regulated flow** of **1–3 bar**, mandating the use of **centrifugal pumps with pressure regulators**. - **Aquaculture ponds** use **aerial pumps or aerators** to maintain dissolved oxygen; *airlift pumps* serve as low-cost alternatives. - **Drainage in waterlogged fields** relies on **axial-flow submersibles** of very high discharge (Q > 1000 L/s) and low head (H < 5 m). - **Renewable-energy-based pumping** (solar PV pumps) is now subsidised under the **PM-KUSUM Scheme** of the Government of India, targeting 35 lakh solar pumps by 2026. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivative 1 — Total Dynamic Head (TDH) Expression The **Total Dynamic Head** of a pump is the *net mechanical energy per unit weight* that the pump must deliver to the water to overcome all the resistance between the source and the destination. ### Step-by-step Derivation **Step 1 — Define Static Head.** Static head is the *vertical distance* between the source water level (suction) and the highest delivery point. For a pump installed above the source, suction lift (S) is positive; for a submerged pump, S is negative (suction head). $$H_{static} = D_{height} + S_{depth}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Suction and Delivery Reservoirs).** Writing Bernoulli's equation at the suction water surface (point 1) and the delivery water surface (point 2), with velocities being essentially zero (large reservoir assumption), the energy added by the pump per unit weight is: $$H_{pump} = (z_2 - z_1) + \frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho \cdot g} + \frac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2g} + h_{f,suc} + h_{f,del}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify for open reservoirs.** For two open reservoirs, P_1 = P_2 = P_atm and V_1 ≈ V_2 ≈ 0. Therefore the third and fourth terms vanish, leaving: $$H_{pump} = (z_2 - z_1) + h_{f,suc} + h_{f,del}$$ **Step 4 — Identify the components.** - $(z_2 - z_1)$ = **Total Static Head** $H_{static}$. - $h_{f,suc}$ = Frictional head loss in the suction pipe + losses across foot valve, NRV, bends, strainer. - $h_{f,del}$ = Frictional head loss in the delivery pipe + losses across NRV, bends, valves, fittings. **Step 5 — Combine into TDH.** $$\boxed{TDH = H_{static} + h_{f,suc} + h_{f,del}}$$ This is the **master expression** for the head the pump must develop. ## 3.2 Derivative 2 — Frictional Head Loss using Darcy–Weisbach Equation The friction head loss in a pipe carrying water is given by the **Darcy–Weisbach equation**: $$h_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot V^{2}}{2 g \cdot D}$$ **Where:** - $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless). - $L$ = Length of pipe (m). - $V$ = Velocity of flow (m/s). - $D$ = Diameter of pipe (m). - $g$ = 9.81 m/s². ### Derivation from First Principles **Step 1 — Express velocity in terms of discharge.** $$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q \cdot 4}{\pi \cdot D^{2}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into Darcy–Weisbach.** $$h_f = \frac{f \cdot L}{2 g \cdot D} \cdot \left(\frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}\right)^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify.** $$h_f = \frac{8 f \cdot L \cdot Q^{2}}{g \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot D^{5}}$$ This is the most-used form in KTU numericals where Q is given in m³/s. **Step 4 — Apply for cast-iron / GI pipes in field use (f ≈ 0.02–0.03).** For a typical farm suction pipe of 50 mm diameter, 6 m length, carrying 5 L/s water, with f = 0.025: - Q = 0.005 m³/s. - V = 0.005 / (π × 0.025²) = 2.546 m/s. - h_f = (0.025 × 6 × 2.546²) / (2 × 9.81 × 0.05) = (0.025 × 6 × 6.482) / 0.981 = **0.99 m**. This is a **non-negligible loss** and must be added to TDH. ## 3.3 Derivative 3 — Power Required at the Pump Shaft **Given:** Q = 30 m³/h, H = 25 m, η_pump = 0.65, η_motor = 0.90. Compute shaft power and input power. **Step 1 — Convert units.** $$Q = \frac{30}{3600} = 0.00833 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Water Power (WP).** $$WP = 9.81 \times 0.00833 \times 25 = 2.043 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Shaft Power (SP).** $$SP = \frac{WP}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{2.043}{0.65} = 3.144 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 — Compute Electrical Input Power.** $$P_{in} = \frac{SP}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{3.144}{0.90} = 3.493 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5 — Round-up for practical motor selection.** A standard **3.7 kW (5 HP)** or **4.0 kW** motor is selected. Note: A **safety factor of 1.1–1.25** is multiplied in real design. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pump Selection Decision Tree | Water Source | Water Depth | Discharge Required | Recommended Pump | Prime Mover | |---|---|---|---|---| | Open well / Sump (depth ≤ 7 m) | 3–7 m | Low–Medium (< 30 m³/h) | **Centrifugal monoblock** | Electric (1–5 HP) | | Open well / Sump (depth ≤ 7 m) | 3–7 m | High (> 30 m³/h) | **Centrifugal coupled** | Electric / Diesel | | Borewell 7–30 m | 7–30 m | Low–Medium | **Jet pump** | Electric | | Borewell 30–150 m | 30–150 m | Low–Medium (< 50 m³/h) | **Submersible** | Electric | | Borewell 30–150 m | 30–150 m | High (> 50 m³/h) | **Vertical Turbine** | Electric / Diesel | | River / Canal | Lift 5–20 m | Very High | **Axial / Mixed flow submersible** | Electric / Diesel | | Drip / Sprinkler system | Suction head 0–3 m | Low (2–10 m³/h) | **Centrifugal (high-pressure)** | Electric | | Drainage sump | 0–5 m | Very High | **Axial-flow submersible** | Electric / Tractor PTO | | Aquaculture | 1–4 m | Low | **Airlift / Centrifugal** | Electric / Solar | | Livestock (remote) | Variable | Very Low | **Hand pump / Windmill** | Manual / Wind | | High-viscosity agro-fluid | — | Low | **Rotary / Reciprocating** | Electric | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The decision tree above is the single most "mark-rich" content for a 14-mark KTU answer on "selection of pump for a given scenario." Sketching a similar table and writing *one-line justifications* for each row fetches **10+ marks** directly. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical — KTU Style **Problem:** A farm needs 25 m³/h of water for sprinkler irrigation from an open well. The static water level is 5 m below ground. The pump is to be installed at ground level. Suction pipe length is 6 m, diameter 80 mm, friction factor 0.025. Delivery pipe length is 200 m, diameter 100 mm, with 4 standard elbows (K = 0.9 each) and a foot valve (K = 2.5). The pump centre is 1 m above the ground. Calculate the shaft power required if pump efficiency is 65% and motor efficiency is 88%. ### Solution **Step 1 — Static head.** $$H_{static} = 5 \text{ (depth)} + 1 \text{ (above ground)} = 6 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Suction friction loss.** $$V_{suc} = \frac{25/3600}{\pi \times 0.08^2/4} = 0.00694 / 0.00503 = 1.38 \text{ m/s}$$ $$h_{f,suc} = \frac{0.025 \times 6 \times 1.38^{2}}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.08} = 0.182 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Delivery friction loss (Darcy–Weisbach on pipe + minor losses on fittings).** $$V_{del} = \frac{0.00694}{\pi \times 0.10^2/4} = 0.884 \text{ m/s}$$ $$h_{f,del,pipe} = \frac{0.025 \times 200 \times 0.884^{2}}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.10} = 1.99 \text{ m}$$ $$h_{f,del,fittings} = 4 \times 0.9 \times \frac{0.884^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.143 \text{ m}$$ $$h_{f,del} = 1.99 + 0.143 = 2.13 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Total Dynamic Head.** $$TDH = 6 + 0.182 + 2.13 = 8.31 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Water Power.** $$WP = 9.81 \times 0.00694 \times 8.31 = 0.566 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 6 — Shaft Power.** $$SP = \frac{0.566}{0.65} = 0.871 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 7 — Input Power (electrical).** $$P_{in} = \frac{0.871}{0.88} = 0.99 \text{ kW} \approx 1.0 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 8 — Practical selection.** A **1.1 kW (1.5 HP) monoblock centrifugal pump** is the right choice. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistakes:** (a) Forgetting minor losses — they are ~10% of major losses and examiners specifically test this. (b) Using Q in L/s directly in $9.81 Q H$ — must convert to m³/s. (c) Adding delivery friction but missing suction friction. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Components of an Agricultural Pumping System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Power Source
Electric Motor / Diesel Engine /
Tractor PTO / Solar PV / Windmill] B[Transmission
Direct Coupling / Belt / Gearbox] C[Pump
Centrifugal / Submersible /
Jet / Turbine / Reciprocating] D[Suction Side
Foot Valve, Strainer,
Suction Pipe, NRV] E[Water Source
Open Well / Borewell /
River / Canal / Pond] F[Delivery Side
Delivery Pipe, NRV,
Gate Valve, Pressure Gauge] G[Field / Crop
Surface / Sprinkler / Drip
Irrigation / Livestock] H[Control Panel
Starter, MCB, Capacitor
VFD optional] A --> B B --> C C --> F D --> C E --> D F --> G H --> A ``` ## 4.2 Decision Tree — Pump Selection for an Agricultural Scenario ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Farmer's Water Demand] --> B1[Identify Water Source] B1 --> C1[Open Well or Sump depth 7m or less] B1 --> C2[Borewell depth 7 to 30m] B1 --> C3[Borewell depth 30 to 150m] B1 --> C4[River or Canal lift] B1 --> C5[Remote field without grid] C1 --> D1[Centrifugal Monoblock Pump] C1 --> D2[Self Priming Centrifugal Pump] C2 --> D3[Jet Pump Single or Double Pipe] C3 --> D4[Submersible Pump] C3 --> D5[Vertical Turbine Pump] C4 --> D6[Axial Flow Submersible] C4 --> D7[Centrifugal High Flow Coupled] C5 --> D8[Solar PV Pump] C5 --> D9[Windmill Pump] C5 --> D10[Manual or Hand Pump] D1 --> E1[Prime Mover: 1 to 5 HP Electric Motor] D4 --> E2[Prime Mover: 3 to 50 HP Electric Submersible Motor] D6 --> E3[Prime Mover: 10 to 100 HP Diesel or Electric] D8 --> E4[Prime Mover: Solar PV Array with MPPT] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Centrifugal Pump Installation with All Accessories ```mermaid flowchart TB SU[Water Source Sump or Well] FV[Foot Valve with Strainer] SP[Suction Pipe L1] PC[Pump Casing with Impeller] PM[Electric Motor or Diesel Engine] COU[Coupling or Belt Drive] NRV1[Non Return Valve] DP[Delivery Pipe L2] GV[Gate Valve] PG[Pressure Gauge] TK[Water Tank or Field Outlet] SU --> FV --> SP --> PC PM --> COU --> PC PC --> NRV1 --> DP --> GV --> PG --> TK ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Submersible Borewell Pump Setup ```mermaid flowchart TB BW[Borewell] SP1[Strainer Bottom] SUBP[Submersible Pump and Motor Unit Sealed in One Stack] DPR[Drop Pipe / Riser Pipe] NSB[Non Return Valve at Surface] BV[Butterfly or Gate Valve] PGS[Pressure Gauge and Flow Meter] OUT[Delivery to Farm Sprinkler or Drip] SR[Submersible Cable Connected to Starter Panel] ST[Starter Panel with MCB and Overload] BW --> SP1 --> SUBP --> DPR --> NSB --> BV --> PGS --> OUT SUBP --> SR --> ST ``` ## 4.5 Comparative Radar — Key Performance Attributes of Pumps ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Centrifugal] -- High Flow -- F1[Best for Surface Irrigation] A2[Submersible] -- High Head -- F2[Best for Deep Borewells] A3[Jet Pump] -- Moderate Head -- F3[Best for Medium Wells] A4[Vertical Turbine] -- Very High Head and Flow -- F4[Best for Lift Irrigation] A5[Solar PV] -- Off grid -- F5[Best for Remote Farms] A6[Reciprocating] -- Pulsating High Pressure -- F6[Best for Agro Processing] A7[Airlift] -- Corrosive / Sandy Water -- F7[Best for Drainage] A8[Rotary] -- Viscous Fluids -- F8[Best for Oil and Molasses] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Diagram Scoring Rule:** All schematics above are drawn using **Mermaid** (KTU-allowed tool). Each diagram is worth **2–3 marks** when included in long answers (e.g., "With a neat sketch explain a submersible pumping system"). Labels are in **clean uppercase alphanumeric text** to avoid rendering issues. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART — A (3-Mark Short-Answer Questions) > **[KTU University Exam — April 2024 Model]** **Q1.** Define the term **Pumping System** as applied in agriculture. List any **four essential components** of a typical agricultural pumping system. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A pumping system in agriculture is an integrated assembly of a prime mover, a pump, transmission devices, control accessories, and pipes used to lift and deliver water for farm use. The four essential components are: 1. **Prime mover** (electric motor / diesel engine / solar PV). 2. **Pump** (centrifugal / submersible / jet / turbine). 3. **Transmission element** (coupling / belt / gearbox). 4. **Control and safety accessories** (foot valve, NRV, gate valve, pressure gauge). > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Q2.** Distinguish clearly between a **centrifugal pump** and a **submersible pump** in two points each. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Centrifugal Pump | Submersible Pump | |---|---|---| | **Location** | Surface-mounted; pump and motor are separate | Submerged inside the borewell; hermetically sealed | | **Suction Limit** | Limited to ~7 m suction lift | No suction limit — water is pushed up the column | | **Priming** | Foot valve required; pump must be primed | Self-primed — water surrounds the pump | | **Maintenance** | Easy surface-level maintenance | Requires pulling out the entire pump stack | --- ## PART — B (14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions) > **Question A (14 Marks) — Sub-question (a): 7 marks, (b): 7 marks** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper aligned with 2024 Scheme CO1]** **(a) Classify in detail the various types of pumping systems used in agriculture, giving at least one example and one typical application for each class.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Pumping systems used in agriculture can be classified along three principal axes: **(I) Based on Prime Mover:** - **Electric motor driven** — Monoblock / coupled centrifugal pumps; e.g., 5 HP monoblock for open well irrigation. - **Diesel engine driven** — High-power coupled pumps; e.g., lift irrigation from a river. - **Tractor PTO driven** — Axial-flow pumps for drainage; e.g., 540 PTO operating an axial pump. - **Solar PV driven** — DC submersible pumps; e.g., 3 HP solar pump for drip irrigation under PM-KUSUM. - **Wind powered** — Aermotor windmill; e.g., livestock watering in arid regions. - **Manual / Animal** — Rope-and-bucket or Persian wheel; e.g., small-scale farms. **(II) Based on Water Source:** - **Open well pumping systems** — Centrifugal monoblock. - **Borewell pumping systems** — Submersible / jet. - **Surface-water pumping systems** — Axial / mixed flow. **(III) Based on Pump Mechanism:** - **Centrifugal pumps** — Most common in agriculture. - **Submersible pumps** — For deep borewells. - **Jet pumps** — Moderate depth wells. - **Vertical turbine pumps** — High-discharge lift schemes. - **Reciprocating pumps** — High head, low flow, agro-processing. - **Airlift pumps** — Drainage, sandy water, aquaculture. - **Rotary pumps** — Viscous agro-fluids. **(b) With a neat schematic, explain the working of a submersible pumping system for a borewell. State two advantages and two limitations.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A submersible pumping system consists of a **multi-stage centrifugal pump** and a **water-filled squirrel-cage induction motor** hermetically sealed into a single cylindrical stack. This entire unit is lowered into the borewell through the drop (riser) pipe. The motor is cooled by the surrounding water. A submersible cable supplies power from the surface starter panel. Water enters the strainer at the bottom, is lifted stage-by-stage by the impeller-diffuser assembly, and is pushed up the riser pipe to the surface. **Working Principle:** - Electric power → rotation of motor → rotation of impeller stack → impeller imparts kinetic energy to water → converted to pressure energy in the diffuser stages → water is pushed up the riser. **Advantages:** 1. **No priming required** — pump is always submerged in water. 2. **No suction head limitation** — works at any depth, even > 200 m. 3. **Silent operation** — fully submerged, hence no noise. 4. **Higher efficiency** for deep-well applications. **Limitations:** 1. **High repair cost** — any fault requires pulling the entire unit out of the borewell. 2. **Sensitive to voltage fluctuation** — burns out easily; needs a starter with thermal overload. 3. **Sand / silt in water damages impellers** — needs a good-quality strainer and clean water. > **Question B (14 Marks) — Sub-question (a): 7 marks, (b): 7 marks** > **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Choice]** **(a) Describe the different types of centrifugal pumps used in agriculture. With a labelled sketch, explain the working of a centrifugal monoblock pump.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Types of centrifugal pumps used in agriculture: - **Radial-flow centrifugal pump** — Head-dominant; used for high head, low discharge. - **Mixed-flow pump** — Intermediate head and discharge. - **Axial-flow pump** — Discharge-dominant; used for very high flow, low head (drainage, river lift). - **Self-priming centrifugal pump** — Re-primes itself after air enters the suction line. - **Monoblock pump** — Motor and pump impeller mounted on a common shaft in a single frame. **Working of a Centrifugal Monoblock Pump (Sketch):** ``` Delivery | [Casing] _____|_____ / \ [Impeller]---[Motor Shaft]---[Electric Motor] \___________/ | Suction (with foot valve) ``` When the electric motor rotates the impeller, water inside the casing is flung outward by centrifugal force, creating a low-pressure region at the centre (eye) of the impeller. Atmospheric pressure pushes water up the suction pipe into this low-pressure zone, and the high-velocity water at the periphery is collected in the volute casing and delivered to the field. **(b) Compare solar PV pumps with diesel engine-driven pumps for agricultural use. Mention any four parameters of comparison.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Solar PV Pump | Diesel Engine Pump | |---|---|---| | **Energy Source** | Sunlight (renewable, free) | Diesel (non-renewable, cost) | | **Operating Cost** | Very low (after installation) | High (fuel + maintenance) | | **Initial Cost** | Higher (PV array + electronics) | Lower | | **Environmental Impact** | Zero emissions, silent | CO₂, NOx emissions, noise | | **Reliability** | Affected by cloudy days | Works day-night, on demand | | **Maintenance** | Minimal | Frequent (oil change, filter) | | **Subsidy (India)** | PM-KUSUM: 30–60 % subsidy | Not subsidised | | **Suitability** | Remote, off-grid farms | Grid-connected / high-power needs | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse "suction head" with "delivery head".** Suction head is *below* the pump centre; delivery head is *above*. Mixing them causes 4-mark loss. > 2. **Do NOT forget unit conversion.** Q in L/s vs m³/s is the #1 KTU arithmetic error. > 3. **Do NOT draw pump diagrams with vague lines.** A *labelled* schematic (impeller, casing, foot valve, NRV) fetches 1.5–2 marks separately. > 4. **In classification questions, classify along all three axes** (prime mover × source × mechanism) for full marks. > 5. **Avoid generic statements** like "centrifugal pump is used for water" — instead, write "centrifugal monoblock pumps are preferred for open wells with depth ≤ 7 m and discharge < 30 m³/h". --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST - **Pumping system** = prime mover + pump + transmission + control + pipes. - **Three classification axes** — Prime Mover, Water Source, Pump Mechanism. - **Centrifugal pumps** — surface, ≤ 7 m suction, moderate head. - **Submersible pumps** — borewell, no suction limit, deep depth. - **Jet pumps** — moderate borewells (7–45 m), re-circulate water through venturi. - **Vertical turbine pumps** — high discharge + high head, large-scale lift irrigation. - **Airlift pumps** — drainage, sandy / corrosive water, aquaculture. - **Reciprocating / Rotary pumps** — agro-processing, viscous fluids. - **Solar / Wind / Manual pumps** — off-grid, livestock, remote farms. - **Water Power** $WP = 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H$ kW; convert Q to m³/s. - **Shaft Power** $SP = WP / \eta_{pump}$; **Input Power** $P_{in} = SP / \eta_{motor}$. - **TDH** = static head + suction friction + delivery friction (with minor losses). - **Darcy–Weisbach** $h_f = \dfrac{f L V^{2}}{2 g D}$ — used for pipe friction. - **Affinity laws** — $Q \propto N$; $H \propto N^2$; $P \propto N^3$ (constant impeller). - **Specific speed** $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ classifies pump type. - **NPSH** must be > $NPSH_{required}$ to avoid cavitation. - **Selection rule of thumb** — Centrifugal ≤ 7 m depth; Submersible > 7 m depth; Solar for off-grid.

topics.title: Types of Pumps and Their Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Pumps and Their Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Pump? — Formal Definition

A **pump** is a mechanical hydraulic machine that converts the **mechanical energy** (supplied by a prime mover such as an electric motor, diesel engine, or turbine) into **hydraulic energy** (pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy of a fluid). The primary function of a pump is to **move fluid** (liquids, slurries, or gases in some cases) from a lower elevation/pressure region to a higher elevation/pressure region, against the opposing forces of gravity, friction, and system resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A pump is a device that imparts energy to a fluid, causing it to flow from a point of lower energy (suction side) to a point of higher energy (delivery side). The energy transfer is achieved through either a **displacement mechanism** (positive displacement) or a **velocity/centrifugal mechanism** (dynamic action).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — How a Pump Works

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Heart:**
> Think of a pump exactly like the **human heart**. The heart does not "create" blood; it simply **pushes** existing blood through the circulatory system by repeatedly contracting and relaxing. Similarly, a pump does not manufacture fluid — it adds energy to the fluid that already exists in the system, forcing it to circulate.
>
> - When you **drink water through a straw**, your mouth acts as a low-pressure zone (suction), and atmospheric pressure pushes water up. A pump does the same — it creates a low-pressure region on its suction side, allowing atmospheric/reservoir pressure to push fluid into the pump.
> - When you **squeeze a plastic water bottle**, you are performing a positive displacement action — the water is forcibly pushed out as the bottle's volume decreases. Centrifugal pumps, in contrast, work like a **spinning fan** but for liquids — they impart velocity (kinetic energy) which then converts to pressure energy in the volute/diffuser.

## 1.3 Fundamental Classification of Pumps

All hydraulic pumps fall under **two broad categories** based on the energy transfer mechanism:

| Category | Energy Transfer Method | Operating Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **Positive Displacement (PD) Pumps** | Direct mechanical displacement | A fixed volume of fluid is trapped, mechanically displaced, and discharged. Flow is theoretically independent of pressure. |
| **Dynamic (Rotodynamic) Pumps** | Velocity/centrifugal action | Fluid velocity is increased by an impeller, and the kinetic energy is converted to pressure in a casing. Flow depends on pressure. |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Aid:** *"PD pumps Push Directly; Dynamic pumps Dance (spin) first, then push."*

## 1.4 Head, Power, and Efficiency — Foundational Metrics

Three fundamental parameters define any pump's performance, and they form the backbone of every KTU numerical problem:

- **Head (H)** — The height (in metres) to which a pump can lift a fluid column. Measured in **metres (m)** of the fluid being pumped.
- **Discharge / Flow Rate (Q)** — The volume of fluid delivered per unit time. Measured in **m³/s** or **litres per second (L/s)**.
- **Power (P)** — The energy supplied per unit time. Measured in **Watts (W)** or **kilowatts (kW)**.

The **universal constant** in pump engineering:

$$g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2 \; \text{(acceleration due to gravity)}$$

$$\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3 \; \text{(density of water at 4°C)}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification Hierarchy

### A. Positive Displacement (PD) Pumps

In PD pumps, a **fixed volume of fluid is mechanically trapped, isolated, and then forced (displaced) into the discharge pipe**. The discharge per cycle is theoretically constant, regardless of the discharge pressure (within mechanical limits).

#### A.1 Reciprocating Pumps (Linear / Back-and-Forth Motion)

- **Single-acting piston pump** — Fluid is displaced only on one side of the piston during the forward stroke.
- **Double-acting piston pump** — Fluid is displaced on both sides of the piston during forward and return strokes.
- **Diaphragm pump** — A flexible diaphragm replaces the piston; used for slurries, corrosive fluids, and sanitary applications.

#### A.2 Rotary Pumps (Continuous Rotational Motion)

| Type | Operating Element | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Gear Pump** | Two meshing gears | Lubricating oils, hydraulic oils |
| **Screw Pump** | Helical screws (2 or 3) | Viscous fluids, fuel oil |
| **Vane Pump** | Sliding vanes on a rotor | Refrigerants, low-viscosity fuels |
| **Lobe Pump** | Rotating lobed rotors | Food, dairy, pharmaceuticals |

### B. Dynamic (Rotodynamic) Pumps

In dynamic pumps, the impeller imparts **kinetic energy** to the fluid. This kinetic energy is then converted into **pressure energy** in the casing (volute or diffuser).

#### B.1 Centrifugal Pumps (Radial Flow)

The fluid enters the impeller **axially** (along the shaft axis) and exits **radially** (perpendicular to the shaft).

- **Volute Casing** — Spiral-shaped casing; common in single-stage pumps.
- **Diffuser Casing** — Stationary vanes surrounding the impeller; common in multistage pumps.
- **Open / Semi-open / Closed Impeller** — Differ by the shroud arrangement.

#### B.2 Axial Flow Pumps

The fluid flows **parallel to the shaft axis** through a propeller-like impeller. These deliver **very high discharge at low head**.

#### B.3 Mixed Flow Pumps

A hybrid of centrifugal and axial flow — fluid exits at an angle between radial and axial. Used for **medium head and medium discharge**.

## 2.2 Operating Principles — The "Why" and "How"

### Why PD pumps produce constant flow:

- A known volume `V` is trapped in a chamber.
- The chamber mechanically reduces in volume (or moves fluid through it).
- The fluid must exit via the discharge port because the suction check valve closes.
- Since the displacement per cycle is fixed, **Q = V × N** (where N is the number of cycles per second), independent of pressure.

### How centrifugal pumps produce variable flow:

- The impeller rotates at angular velocity `ω` (rad/s).
- Centrifugal force `F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r` acts on each fluid particle at radius `r`.
- This creates a pressure rise across the impeller.
- The Euler equation of turbomachinery gives the theoretical head imparted by the impeller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept — Euler's Turbomachinery Equation:**
> The fundamental equation that governs all rotodynamic pump theory is the **Euler Equation**, derived from angular momentum conservation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Discharge (Single-acting reciprocating)** | $Q = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s | A = piston area, L = stroke length, N = rpm |
| 2 | **Discharge (Double-acting reciprocating)** | $Q = \dfrac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s | a = piston rod area |
| 3 | **Theoretical Power (Water Power)** | $P_{\text{water}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | Watts | Input hydraulic energy per second |
| 4 | **Shaft Power (Brake Power)** | $P_{\text{shaft}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{\text{overall}}}$ | Watts | Power input to pump shaft |
| 5 | **Manometric Efficiency (η_man)** | $\eta_{\text{man}} = \dfrac{H_{\text{man}}}{H_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | — | Ratio of useful head to theoretical |
| 6 | **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)** | $\eta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{water}}}{P_{\text{shaft}}}$ | — | Accounts for bearing/friction losses |
| 7 | **Overall Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{man}} \cdot \eta_{\text{mech}} \cdot \eta_{\text{vol}}$ | — | Product of all three efficiencies |
| 8 | **Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol)** | $\eta_{\text{vol}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | — | Slip losses in PD pumps |
| 9 | **Specific Speed (N_s)** | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | — | For geometric similarity scaling |
| 10 | **Suction Lift / NPSH** | $\text{NPSH}_A = \dfrac{P_{\text{atm}}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} - H_s$ | m | Available NPSH at suction |
| 11 | **Velocity of Piston (Crank & Connecting Rod)** | $v = \omega r \left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta\right)$ | m/s | Simple harmonic + correction |
| 12 | **Head imparted by Impeller (Euler)** | $H_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{1}{g}(V_{w2} \cdot u_2 - V_{w1} \cdot u_1)$ | m | u = blade velocity, V_w = whirl velocity |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Trap in KTU Exams:**
> Students frequently mix up:
> - `L/s ↔ m³/s` (divide L/s by 1000 to get m³/s)
> - `rpm ↔ rad/s` (multiply rpm by `2π/60`)
> - `metres of water ↔ Pascals` (multiply m by `ρg`)
> Always state units in every line of your KTU answer.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Preferred Pump Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Municipal water supply** | Centrifugal (single-stage) | High flow, clean water, low maintenance |
| **High-rise building water systems** | Multistage centrifugal | High pressure head required |
| **Oil refineries / chemical plants** | Reciprocating, gear, screw | High pressure, viscous/corrosive fluids |
| **Irrigation / flood control** | Axial / mixed flow | Very high discharge, low head |
| **Food & pharmaceutical** | Diaphragm, lobe, peristaltic | Sanitary, no contamination, gentle handling |
| **Hydraulic systems (machine tools)** | Vane, gear, piston | Precise flow, high pressure, reliability |
| **Boiler feed (power plants)** | Multistage centrifugal | Very high pressure, continuous duty |
| **Slurry / mining** | Centrifugal with hard liners, diaphragm | Abrasion resistance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Discharge of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the theoretical discharge of a single-acting reciprocating pump.

### Given:
- Piston cross-sectional area = `A` (m²)
- Stroke length = `L` (m)
- Speed of crank = `N` (rpm)

### Step 1 — Volume displaced per stroke

During one forward stroke, the piston sweeps through the entire cylinder length.

$$
V_{\text{stroke}} = A \times L
$$

Where:
- `A` is the piston face area in m²
- `L` is the stroke length in m

### Step 2 — Number of strokes per second

Since the pump is **single-acting**, only one stroke (the forward/delivery stroke) displaces fluid per revolution.

$$
n_{\text{stroke/sec}} = \frac{N}{60}
$$

Where:
- `N` is the crank speed in revolutions per minute (rpm)
- Division by 60 converts minutes to seconds

### Step 3 — Theoretical discharge per second

Multiplying the volume per stroke by the number of strokes per second:

$$
Q_{\text{th}} = V_{\text{stroke}} \times n_{\text{stroke/sec}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
Q_{\text{th}} = A \cdot L \cdot \frac{N}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

This is the **theoretical discharge** assuming no leakage and complete filling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Discharge of a Double-Acting Reciprocating Pump

**Statement:** Derive the discharge expression when the piston has a connecting rod of area `a`.

### Step 1 — Volume during forward stroke

During the forward stroke, the fluid is displaced from the cylinder side (area `A`), while suction occurs on the rod side (area `A - a`).

$$
V_f = A \cdot L
$$

### Step 2 — Volume during return stroke

During the return stroke, the cylinder side sucks fluid in, but the rod side displaces fluid through the delivery port.

$$
V_r = (A - a) \cdot L
$$

### Step 3 — Total volume per revolution

Since both strokes deliver fluid (double-acting):

$$
V_{\text{total}} = V_f + V_r = A \cdot L + (A - a) \cdot L
$$

$$
V_{\text{total}} = (2A - a) \cdot L
$$

### Step 4 — Theoretical discharge

Multiplying by strokes per second `N/60`:

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Slip, Coefficient of Discharge, and Volumetric Efficiency

**Statement:** Define slip, derive the coefficient of discharge and volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating pump.

### Definitions:
- `Q_th` = Theoretical discharge (assuming no leakage)
- `Q_actual` = Actual discharge measured at the delivery pipe
- **Slip (S)** = `Q_th - Q_actual` (the fluid that leaks back from delivery to suction through valves/seals)

### Step 1 — Coefficient of Discharge (C_d)

$$
C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{th}}}
$$

### Step 2 — Percentage Slip

$$
\%\text{Slip} = \frac{Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} \times 100
$$

### Step 3 — Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol)

$$
\eta_{\text{vol}} = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} = C_d
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** In KTU numerical problems, if slip is given as a positive percentage, the actual discharge is **less** than theoretical, and `η_vol < 1`. If slip is negative (rare, indicates air entrainment effects), actual > theoretical.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Work Done and Power of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump (No Acceleration)

**Statement:** Derive the work done per second against friction-less delivery at constant pressure.

### Step 1 — Pressure during delivery stroke

Let `p_d` = pressure head during delivery (in metres of fluid), and `p_s` = pressure head during suction.

### Step 2 — Work done during delivery stroke

$$
W_d = p_d \cdot A \cdot L
$$

### Step 3 — Work done during suction stroke

The suction stroke does **negative work** (the fluid is being pushed into the cylinder against suction pressure):

$$
W_s = -p_s \cdot A \cdot L
$$

### Step 4 — Net work per cycle

$$
W_{\text{cycle}} = W_d + W_s = (p_d - p_s) \cdot A \cdot L
$$

### Step 5 — Work per second (Power input to fluid)

Multiplying by strokes per second `N/60`:

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{fluid}} = \frac{(p_d - p_s) \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \;\; \text{Watts}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **work done on the fluid**. To find the **shaft power** required, divide by mechanical efficiency: `P_shaft = P_fluid / η_mech`.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Pump Types

| Parameter | Reciprocating PD | Centrifugal (Dynamic) | Axial Flow (Dynamic) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flow characteristic** | Pulsating, constant per cycle | Smooth, non-pulsating | Smooth |
| **Pressure characteristic** | Almost constant flow regardless of pressure | Flow drops as head rises | Similar to centrifugal |
| **Maximum head (single stage)** | Up to **200 m** typical | Up to **100 m** typical | Up to **20 m** typical |
| **Discharge range** | Low to medium | Medium to high | Very high |
| **Self-priming?** | No (needs foot valve) | No (centrifugal); Yes (peripheral regenerative) | No |
| **Best suited for** | High pressure, viscous fluids | General-purpose, water supply | Flood water, cooling water |
| **Maintenance** | High (valves, piston) | Low (only bearings) | Moderate |
| **Efficiency curve** | Flat (almost constant) | Peaks at BEP, drops on both sides | Peaks sharply |
| **Solid handling** | Poor (piston-cylinder) | Moderate (open impeller) | Poor (clog easily) |
| **Cost** | High (per kW) | Moderate | Moderate |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** *"Recip for high-rise, Centri for compromise, Axial for aqua-flow."*

---

## 3.6 Specific Speed — Selection Criterion for Pump Type

The **specific speed `N_s`** uniquely identifies the pump impeller shape best suited for given operating conditions (`Q` and `H`):

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}
$$

Where `N` is in rpm, `Q` in m³/s, and `H` in metres (per stage, for multistage).

| Specific Speed Range | Pump Type |
|---|---|
| `N_s < 500` (low) | Centrifugal (radial flow) |
| `500 \leq N_s \leq 3000` | Mixed flow |
| `N_s > 3000` (high) | Axial flow |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the student's ability to **interpret a specific speed value** and identify the suitable pump type. This single derivation carries 7-mark weightage in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Master Classification of Pumps

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hydraulic Pumps]:::root --> B[Positive Displacement Pumps]:::pd
    A --> C[Dynamic Rotodynamic Pumps]:::dyn

    B --> B1[Reciprocating Pumps]:::pd
    B --> B2[Rotary Pumps]:::pd

    B1 --> B1a[Single Acting Piston]
    B1 --> B1b[Double Acting Piston]
    B1 --> B1c[Diaphragm Pump]

    B2 --> B2a[Gear Pump]
    B2 --> B2b[Screw Pump]
    B2 --> B2c[Vane Pump]
    B2 --> B2d[Lobe Pump]

    C --> C1[Centrifugal Radial Flow]:::dyn
    C --> C2[Mixed Flow Pump]:::dyn
    C --> C3[Axial Flow Propeller Pump]:::dyn

    C1 --> C1a[Volute Casing Type]
    C1 --> C1b[Diffuser Casing Type]
    C1 --> C1c[Open Semi-open Closed Impeller]

    classDef root fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef pd fill:#f4a261,stroke:#7a3b0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dyn fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#0a4a44,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Centrifugal Pump System

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[Sump Reservoir]:::tank --> SUC[Suction Pipe Strainer Foot Valve]:::pipe
    SUC --> IMP[Impeller Rotating Component]:::rotor
    IMP --> VOL[Volute Casing Diffuser]:::casing
    VOL --> DEL[Delivery Pipe NRV Check Valve]:::pipe
    DEL --> OUT[Discharge Tank Overhead]:::tank

    M[Electric Motor Prime Mover]:::motor -.SHAFT COUPLING.-> IMP
    IMP -.PRESSURE TRANSDUCER.-> M

    classDef tank fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pipe fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#a47e1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rotor fill:#e63946,stroke:#5a0c12,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef casing fill:#457b9d,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef motor fill:#f1faee,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Selection Logic for Pump Type

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST[Start Given Q and H Requirements]:::start --> Q1{Is pressure very high H greater than 100 m per stage}:::decision
    Q1 -- YES --> P1[Choose Positive Displacement Reciprocating or Multistage]:::pdbox
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is Q very large flood irrigation cooling}:::decision
    Q2 -- YES --> P2[Choose Axial or Mixed Flow Pump]:::axbox
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Is fluid viscous oil or slurry}:::decision
    Q3 -- YES --> P3[Choose Rotary PD Pump Gear Screw Vane]:::pdbox
    Q3 -- NO --> P4[Choose Centrifugal Pump Most General]:::cenbox
    P1 --> ENDX[Selected Pump Type]:::endx
    P2 --> ENDX
    P3 --> ENDX
    P4 --> ENDX

    classDef start fill:#264653,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef endx fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef decision fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#7a3b0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pdbox fill:#f4a261,stroke:#7a3b0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef axbox fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cenbox fill:#e63946,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use these diagrams in your KTU answer sheet:**
> 1. Draw the classification tree as shown in 4.1 (partial draw = 1 mark, complete with all types = 2 marks).
> 2. Always **label the energy transfer mode** on the arrows (e.g., "kinetic energy," "pressure energy").
> 3. Use the functional architecture (4.2) to support your explanations of working principles.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Differentiate between positive displacement pumps and rotodynamic pumps. Give two examples of each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Positive Displacement (PD) | Rotodynamic (Dynamic) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy transfer | Direct mechanical displacement | Velocity → Pressure conversion |
| Flow vs. pressure | Flow is theoretically independent of pressure | Flow decreases as head increases |
| Flow continuity | Pulsating (reciprocating) or non-pulsating (rotary) | Smooth, continuous |
| Self-priming | Generally yes (rotary) / No (reciprocating) | Generally no |
| **Examples** | Reciprocating pump, Gear pump | Centrifugal pump, Axial flow pump |

> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** PD vs. RD concept (1) + 2 differences (1) + Examples (1).

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Define specific speed of a centrifugal pump and state its significance in pump selection."**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Specific speed `N_s` is the speed (in rpm) at which a geometrically similar pump would deliver unit discharge (1 m³/s) against unit head (1 m).

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}
$$

**Significance:**
1. It identifies the **shape and type** of impeller best suited for given `Q` and `H`.
2. It enables **scaling** of pump performance between model and prototype.
3. It classifies pumps into **radial, mixed, and axial flow** categories.

> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Significance (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **single-acting reciprocating pump**. Derive the expression for its theoretical discharge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of **150 mm** and a stroke of **300 mm**. It runs at **60 rpm**. If it delivers water against a total head of **20 m**, calculate:
- (i) Theoretical discharge in L/s
- (ii) The power required to drive the pump (assume η_overall = 75%) **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Working Principle:**
A single-acting reciprocating pump consists of a piston/ plunger reciprocating inside a cylinder. The cylinder is connected to a **suction pipe** (with a foot valve and strainer) and a **delivery pipe** (with a delivery check valve).

**Suction Stroke (Piston moves backward):**
1. The piston retracts, increasing the cylinder volume.
2. Pressure inside the cylinder drops below atmospheric.
3. Atmospheric pressure on the sump water surface pushes water up the suction pipe.
4. The **foot valve opens** and water enters the cylinder.

**Delivery Stroke (Piston moves forward):**
1. The piston advances, decreasing the cylinder volume.
2. Pressure rises above atmospheric.
3. The **foot valve closes** (preventing backflow) and the **delivery valve opens**.
4. Water is forced up the delivery pipe to the required height.

**Derivation of Theoretical Discharge:**

Let:
- `A` = cross-sectional area of piston (m²) = `(\pi/4) \cdot D^2`
- `L` = stroke length (m)
- `N` = crank speed in rpm

**Step 1:** Volume of water displaced per stroke:

$$
V = A \times L
$$

**Step 2:** Number of delivery strokes per second (single-acting, so 1 stroke per revolution):

$$
n = \frac{N}{60}
$$

**Step 3:** Theoretical discharge:

$$
Q_{\text{th}} = A \cdot L \cdot \frac{N}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

**Sketch (describe in answer):** Draw a horizontal cylinder with piston, suction pipe going down to sump (with foot valve), delivery pipe going up (with delivery valve), and crank-connecting rod mechanism.

> **Marks Distribution (7):** Working explanation (3) + Sketch (2) + Derivation (2).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Bore `D = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}`
- Stroke `L = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.30 \text{ m}`
- `N = 60 \text{ rpm}`
- `H = 20 \text{ m}`
- `\eta_{\text{overall}} = 75\% = 0.75`

**Step 1: Piston cross-sectional area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.15)^2
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0225 = 0.01767 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Theoretical discharge in m³/s**

$$
Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{0.01767 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 60}{60}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{th}} = 0.01767 \cdot 0.30 = 0.005301 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Converting to L/s (multiply by 1000):

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{th}} = 5.301 \; \text{L/s}}
$$

**Step 3: Water power (theoretical hydraulic power)**

$$
P_{\text{water}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{\text{th}} \cdot H
$$

$$
P_{\text{water}} = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.005301 \cdot 20
$$

$$
P_{\text{water}} = 1040.05 \; \text{W} \approx 1.04 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4: Shaft power required (brake power)**

$$
P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{water}}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{1040.05}{0.75}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{shaft}} = 1386.7 \; \text{W} \approx 1.39 \; \text{kW}}
$$

> **Marks Distribution (7):** (i) Discharge (3) + (ii) Power (4).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Classify pumps with a neat flowchart. Explain the working principle of a **centrifugal pump** with a labeled diagram. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers **0.05 m³/s** of water against a head of **30 m** running at **1450 rpm**. Calculate:
- (i) Specific speed of the pump
- (ii) The type of pump (radial/mixed/axial) based on N_s
- (iii) Power input to the pump if overall efficiency is 70% **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Classification Flowchart:**

Pumps are classified into two main types:

1. **Positive Displacement (PD) Pumps**
   - **Reciprocating** — Single-acting, Double-acting piston; Diaphragm
   - **Rotary** — Gear, Screw, Vane, Lobe

2. **Dynamic (Rotodynamic) Pumps**
   - **Centrifugal (Radial flow)**
   - **Mixed flow**
   - **Axial flow (Propeller)**

**Centrifugal Pump Working Principle:**

1. The pump consists of a **rotating impeller** with curved vanes enclosed in a **casing** (volute).
2. Water enters the impeller **axially through the eye (suction)** at the centre.
3. As the impeller rotates at high speed, **centrifugal force** throws the water outward along the vanes.
4. The water gains **kinetic energy (velocity)** as it moves outward.
5. In the **volute casing**, the cross-sectional area gradually increases — this **converts kinetic energy into pressure energy** (by Bernoulli's principle).
6. The pressurized water exits through the **delivery port**.

**Diagram (describe in answer):** Draw a circular impeller with curved vanes, eye at centre, volute casing, suction at eye, delivery tangential to casing.

**Advantages (state 3):**
1. Simple construction, low cost
2. Smooth, non-pulsating flow
3. High discharge capacity
4. Low maintenance (no valves)
5. Can handle dirty water

**Limitations (state 3):**
1. Cannot deliver against very high head (single-stage)
2. Not self-priming (needs priming before starting)
3. Performance drops significantly with viscosity changes
4. Cavitation risk at high suction lifts

> **Marks Distribution (7):** Classification (2) + Working + Diagram (3) + Advantages/Limitations (2).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- `Q = 0.05 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}`
- `H = 30 \; \text{m}`
- `N = 1450 \; \text{rpm}`
- `\eta_{\text{overall}} = 70\% = 0.70`

**Step 1: Specific Speed**

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
N \sqrt{Q} = 1450 \cdot \sqrt{0.05} = 1450 \cdot 0.2236 = 324.22
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
H^{3/4} = 30^{0.75}
$$

Using logarithms: `\ln(30) = 3.4012`, so `0.75 \cdot 3.4012 = 2.5509`, then `e^{2.5509} = 12.82`.

$$
H^{3/4} = 12.82
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_s = \frac{324.22}{12.82} = 25.29
$$

$$
\boxed{N_s \approx 25.3}
$$

**Step 2: Type of Pump**

Since `N_s = 25.3 < 500`, this falls in the **radial flow / centrifugal pump** category.

$$
\boxed{\text{Pump Type: Centrifugal (Radial Flow)}}
$$

**Step 3: Power Input**

$$
P_{\text{water}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 30
$$

$$
P_{\text{water}} = 14715 \; \text{W} = 14.715 \; \text{kW}
$$

$$
P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{water}}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{14.715}{0.70}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{shaft}} = 21.02 \; \text{kW}}
$$

> **Marks Distribution (7):** (i) Specific speed (3) + (ii) Type identification (1) + (iii) Power (3).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert mm to m, or L/s to m³/s. *(Lose 1–2 marks per step)*
> 2. **Confusing `Q_th` with `Q_actual`:** If slip is given in the problem, the actual discharge must use `\eta_{vol}`. Most students forget this. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 3. **Specific speed formula:** Using `N\sqrt{H}/Q^{3/4}` (wrong — for turbines, not pumps). The pump formula has `Q` in numerator. *(Lose 2 marks if reversed)*
> 4. **Specific speed units:** KTU expects `N_s` as a **dimensionless ratio** using the standard formula above. Using imperial units (gpm, ft) without conversion is incorrect. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 5. **Missing the sketch in 7-mark questions:** A neat labeled diagram carries **at least 2 marks** by itself. Skipping it is a guaranteed loss. *(Lose 2 marks)*
> 6. **Showing final answers without intermediate steps:** KTU award **step marks**. Always show formula → substitution → numerical evaluation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU Module 1:**

- [x] Pumps are classified into **PD (Positive Displacement)** and **Dynamic (Rotodynamic)** categories.
- [x] **PD pumps** trap and displace a fixed volume per cycle — flow is independent of pressure.
- [x] **Dynamic pumps** impart velocity first, then convert it to pressure — flow depends on head.
- [x] **Reciprocating PD pumps** are of two main types: single-acting and double-acting.
- [x] **Single-acting reciprocating pump discharge:** `Q_th = A \cdot L \cdot N / 60`
- [x] **Double-acting reciprocating pump discharge:** `Q_th = (2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N / 60`
- [x] **Rotary PD pumps** include gear, screw, vane, and lobe types — used for viscous fluids.
- [x] **Centrifugal pumps** are the most common dynamic pump — fluid enters axially, exits radially.
- [x] **Axial flow pumps** give high discharge at low head (e.g., flood water, irrigation).
- [x] **Mixed flow pumps** are a hybrid of radial and axial — used for medium head and discharge.
- [x] **Specific speed formula (pumps):** `N_s = N \sqrt{Q} / H^{3/4}` — radial if `< 500`, axial if `> 3000`.
- [x] **Water Power:** `P_water = \rho g Q H` (Watts)
- [x] **Shaft Power:** `P_shaft = P_water / η_overall`
- [x] **Slip** = `Q_th − Q_actual`; **Volumetric efficiency** = `Q_actual / Q_th`
- [x] **Euler's turbomachinery equation** is the foundation of all rotodynamic pump theory.
- [x] **Self-priming:** Rotary PD pumps are self-priming; centrifugal pumps require external priming.
- [x] **Selection rule of thumb:** High head + low flow → Centrifugal multistage; High flow + low head → Axial.
- [x] Remember the **constant** `g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2` and `ρ_water = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3` for every numerical.
- [x] Always **draw a labeled diagram** in 7-mark theory questions — it carries 2 marks minimum.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centrifugal Pumps: Working Principles and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal Pumps: Working Principles and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Centrifugal Pump** is a **rotodynamic** (kinetic) hydraulic machine that converts the **mechanical energy** of a rotating **impeller** into **pressure energy** (and kinetic energy) of a fluid, primarily by exploiting the **centrifugal force** generated on the fluid particles as they are flung outward from the impeller eye. It belongs to the class of *positive-displacement-adjacent turbomachines* where energy transfer is continuous and rotational.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The study of centrifugal pumps covers construction, working principle, classification based on flow direction (radial, mixed, axial), head–discharge characteristics, velocity triangles, and Euler's head equation. Mastery of velocity triangles is mandatory for Part B derivations.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: "The Spinning Bucket"**
> Imagine a bucket half-filled with water, tied to a rope, and swung in a horizontal circle. The water does not fall out — it is pinned against the inner wall of the bucket. Why? Because the bucket's circular motion forces the water to continuously *change direction*, and this "change of direction" is precisely the **centrifugal force**. The water climbs the wall and wants to escape outward.
