> A **centrifugal pump does the same thing mechanically:** a spinning impeller acts as the "rope+bucket," and the volute casing acts as the "outer wall" that captures the high-velocity water and converts its **kinetic energy** into **pressure energy** before discharging it through the delivery pipe.

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Type | Flow Direction (relative to shaft) | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Radial Flow Pump** | Perpendicular to shaft (centrifugal) | High head, low–medium discharge (boiler feed) |
| **Mixed Flow Pump** | Oblique to shaft | Medium head, high discharge (irrigation) |
| **Axial Flow Pump** | Parallel to shaft (propeller-like) | Low head, very high discharge (drainage) |

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

- **Impeller** — The rotating vaned disc that imparts energy to the fluid.
- **Casing (Volute / Diffuser)** — The stationary outer chamber that collects and decelerates the fluid.
- **Suction Head (Hs)** — Vertical distance of the pump centreline above/below the sump water level.
- **Delivery Head (Hd)** — Vertical distance to the delivery reservoir.
- **Static Head (Hst)** — $H_s + H_d$ (gross elevation difference).
- **Manometric Head (Hm)** — Head developed by the pump measured across its pressure and suction nozzles.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Priming:** The casing and suction pipe are filled with the liquid (centrifugal pumps cannot self-prime unless equipped with an auxiliary air-evacuation device).
2. **Energy Input:** An external prime-mover (electric motor / diesel engine) rotates the impeller via the shaft.
3. **Acceleration:** The rotating impeller vanes exert a centrifugal force on the liquid, accelerating it from the eye (centre) outward toward the periphery.
4. **Pressure Conversion:** As high-velocity liquid leaves the impeller tip and enters the progressively widening **volute casing**, the cross-sectional area increases. By the **continuity equation**, velocity decreases; by **Bernoulli's principle**, the kinetic energy converts into **pressure energy**.
5. **Discharge:** The pressurised liquid is delivered to the delivery pipe at a high head.
6. **Continuous Suction:** A partial vacuum forms at the impeller eye (suction), causing atmospheric pressure on the sump to push fresh liquid up the suction pipe — ensuring continuous flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Priming" is critical:** Air at the impeller eye is 800 times lighter than water. The centrifugal force cannot generate the pressure differential needed to lift water; hence the pump must be primed.

### 2.2 Main Components — The "Anatomy" Table

| Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Impeller** | Rotating element with curved vanes; transfers energy to fluid |
| **Casing (Volute)** | Spiral-shaped outer chamber; converts kinetic energy to pressure energy |
| **Shaft** | Transmits torque from motor to impeller |
| **Bearings** | Support the shaft and absorb radial/axial loads |
| **Shaft Seal / Gland** | Prevents leakage along the shaft and blocks air ingress |
| **Suction Pipe (with foot valve & strainer)** | Draws liquid from the sump |
| **Delivery Pipe (with NRV)** | Carries liquid to the destination; NRV prevents backflow |
| **Casing Rings (Wear Rings)** | Maintain small clearance, reducing recirculation losses |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` in text — for tables, prefer word forms like "absolute value of x" to avoid markdown pipe conflicts. All formulas below are KTU-board-standard.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Work done per sec by impeller on water | $W = \rho \cdot Q \cdot (v_1 w_1 \pm v_2 w_2 \cos\alpha_2)$ | $\text{W}$ |
| 2 | Euler Head (Total) | $H_e = \dfrac{v_2 w_2 \cos\alpha_2 \cdot u_2 \;\pm\; v_1 w_1 \cos\alpha_1 \cdot u_1}{g}$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 3 | Euler Head (no pre-whirl, $\alpha_1=90^\circ$) | $H_e = \dfrac{v_2 w_2 \cos\alpha_2 \cdot u_2}{g}$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 4 | Manometric Head | $H_m = \dfrac{v_w \, u_2}{g} \;-\; \text{losses in impeller/casing}$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 5 | Manometric Efficiency | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{g \cdot H_m}{v_w \cdot u_2}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\text{Power at impeller}}{\text{Shaft Power}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{\text{Shaft Power}}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Specific Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | $\text{rpm, m, kW}$ |
| 9 | Velocity of Flow | $V_f = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi(D_o^2 - D_b^2)/4}$ | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 10 | Head Loss (Darcy–Weisbach) | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L v^2}{2 g d}$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 11 | NPSH Available | $NPSH_a = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} \pm H_s - h_f$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 12 | Minimum Starting Speed | $N_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{2 g H_s}$ | $\text{rad/s or rpm}$ |
| 13 | Velocity Triangle Outlet (backward vane) | $w_2^2 = u_2^2 + v_2^2 - 2 u_2 v_2 \cos\alpha_2$ | $\text{(m/s)}^2$ |
| 14 | Shaft Power | $P_{sh} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{1000 \,\eta_{overall}}$ | $\text{kW}$ |
| 15 | Discharge with Manometric Eff. | $Q = \dfrac{\eta_{man} \cdot g \cdot H_m \cdot \pi (D_o^2 - D_b^2)/4}{v_2 w_2 \cos\alpha_2 \cdot u_2}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal water supply** (domestic overhead tanks, hydrant networks)
- **Boiler feed in thermal power stations** (high-pressure multistage centrifugal pumps)
- **Petrochemical & refinery** transfer of crude, condensate, and acids
- **HVAC chilled-water circulation** in high-rise buildings
- **Agriculture** — large irrigation schemes using mixed-flow pumps
- **Cooling-water loops** in nuclear, process, and steel plants
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Euler's Head Equation for a Centrifugal Pump

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** Consider an elemental ring of fluid on a backward-curved impeller vane at the outlet. Apply the **angular-momentum theorem** (Euler's turbomachinery equation) for steady, axisymmetric flow.

**Step 1 — Apply the Angular Momentum Principle**

The torque on the impeller equals the rate of change of angular momentum of the fluid crossing the impeller periphery per second.

$$
T = \rho \, Q \, (r_2 \, v_{w2} - r_1 \, v_{w1})
$$

where $r_1, r_2$ are the inlet and outlet radii, and $v_{w1}, v_{w2}$ are the **whirl components** of absolute velocity.

**Step 2 — Work Done Per Second**

Work done per second = Torque $\times$ Angular velocity $\omega$.

$$
W = T \cdot \omega = \rho \, Q \, \omega \, (r_2 v_{w2} - r_1 v_{w1})
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Blade Speed $u = \omega r$**

By definition, blade (tangential) speed is $u_1 = \omega r_1$ and $u_2 = \omega r_2$. Substituting:

$$
W = \rho \, Q \, (u_2 \, v_{w2} - u_1 \, v_{w1})
$$

**Step 4 — Express as Head (divide by $\rho g Q$)**

Head imparted to the fluid:

$$
H_e = \frac{u_2 v_{w2} - u_1 v_{w1}}{g}
$$

This is the **Euler Head** equation for a centrifugal pump.

**Step 5 — For Radial Entry (No Pre-Whirl)**

When the absolute velocity at inlet is purely radial, $\alpha_1 = 90^\circ$, so $v_{w1} = v_1 \cos 90^\circ = 0$. Therefore:

$$
H_e = \frac{u_2 \, v_{w2}}{g}
$$

**Step 6 — Introduce Vane Angle $\beta$ (Backward Curved Vane, outlet)**

For a backward-curved vane, the relative velocity makes angle $\beta_2$ with the tangential direction. Resolving:

$$
v_{w2} = u_2 - v_{f2} \cot\beta_2
$$

Substituting:

$$
H_e = \frac{u_2 \, (u_2 - v_{f2} \cot\beta_2)}{g}
$$

**Step 7 — Final General Euler Head**

$$
\boxed{\; H_e = \frac{u_2^{\,2} - u_2 \, v_{f2} \cot\beta_2}{g} \quad \text{(for radial entry)} \;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why "backward curved"?** For $\beta_2 < 90^\circ$, an increase in speed $u_2$ increases $H_e$ linearly — stable head–discharge characteristic. For radial vanes ($\beta_2 = 90^\circ$), $H_e = u_2^2 / g$ — unstable. For forward curved ($\beta_2 > 90^\circ$), $H_e$ rises sharply with $u_2$ — most unstable. KTU frequently asks to *justify why backward-curved vanes are preferred.*

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Starting Speed of a Centrifugal Pump

A centrifugal pump cannot self-prime. To start delivering water, the impeller must rotate fast enough that the **centrifugal head developed equals the suction lift**.

**Step 1 — Pressure at impeller eye**

The pressure at the impeller eye (radius $r_1$) under rotation:

$$
p_1 = p_{atm} - \rho \cdot \omega^2 (r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2}) / 2
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure at suction pipe top (assuming primed)**

For the water to just start rising in the suction pipe of height $H_s$:

$$
p_{atm} - \rho \, g H_s = p_{atm} - \rho \cdot \omega^2 (r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2}) / 2
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify** (assuming $r_1 \ll r_2$):

$$
g H_s = \frac{\omega^2 r_2^{\,2}}{2} = \frac{u_2^{\,2}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\omega$ and convert to rpm**

$$
u_2 = \sqrt{2 g H_s}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{u_2}{r_2} = \frac{1}{r_2}\sqrt{2 g H_s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; N_{min} = \frac{60}{2 \pi r_2}\sqrt{2 g H_s} \quad \text{rpm} \;}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Forward vs Radial vs Backward Vanes

| Vane Type | Vane Angle $\beta_2$ | $v_{w2}$ | $H_e$ vs Speed $u_2$ | Stability | KTU Verdict |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Forward Curved** | $> 90^\circ$ | Large | Steep, non-linear rise | **Unstable** (overloading risk) | Rarely used; theoretical only |
| **Radial** | $= 90^\circ$ | $= u_2$ | Parabolic ($H_e \propto u_2^2$) | Moderate | Used in very high head pumps |
| **Backward Curved** | $< 90^\circ$ (typically 20°–40°) | $< u_2$ | Linear in $u_2$ | **Highly stable** | **Industry standard** |

### 3.4 Head–Discharge Relationship (Theoretical)

For a backward-curved vane pump at constant speed $N$:

$$
H_{th} = A - B Q^2
$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants determined by impeller geometry. This quadratic fall (drop in head with rising discharge) is a **defining KTU question** since the actual $H$–$Q$ curve closely follows this theoretical trend.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Centrifugal Pump — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electric Motor / Diesel Engine] -->|Torque via Coupling| B[Shaft]
    B -->|Rotational Drive| C[Impeller with Curved Vanes]
    C -->|Centrifugal Acceleration| D[Volute / Casing Diffuser]
    D -->|Pressure Energy Conversion| E[Delivery Pipe + NRV]
    F[Suction Sump with Foot Valve] -->|Primed Suction Line| G[Strainer + Foot Valve]
    G -->|Liquid Drawn| C
    H[Bearings + Mechanical Seal] -.->|Support & Seal| B
    I[Frame / Baseplate] -.->|Mounting| A
    I -.->|Mounting| D

    classDef prime fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef rotor fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef stator fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef aux fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#404040,color:#000

    class A prime
    class B,C rotor
    class D,E,F,G stator
    class H,I aux
```

### 4.2 Working Principle — Energy Conversion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Mechanical Energy from Motor] -->|Shaft| R[Rotational KE of Impeller]
    R -->|Centrifugal Force| K[Kinetic Energy of Fluid]
    K -->|Volute Casing Expansion| P[Pressure Energy]
    P -->|Delivery Pipe| O[Water Reaches Reservoir]
    O -.->|Atmospheric Push on Sump| S[Suction]
    S -.->|Continuous Primed Flow| R

    classDef energy fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    class M,R,K,P energy
    class O,S output
```

### 4.3 Velocity Triangle — Outlet (Backward Curved Vane)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OUTLET VELOCITY TRIANGLE] --> B["U2 = Blade Tangential Speed - along direction of rotation"]
    A --> C["Vf2 = Velocity of Flow - along radial outward"]
    A --> D["Vr2 = Relative Velocity - tangent to vane at angle beta2"]
    A --> E["V2 = Absolute Velocity - resultant of U2 and Vr2 - at angle alpha2 from tangential"]

    B --> F[Components Resolve]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Vw2 = U2 - Vf2 cot beta2 = whirl component"]
    F --> H["Vf2 = component along Vf2 axis"]

    classDef base fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8B00,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E base
    class F,G,H calc
```

### 4.4 Components Layout — Cross-Sectional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUCTION_SIDE [Suction Side]
        SP[Suction Pipe] --> FV[Foot Valve with Strainer]
        FV --> SC[Suction Casing Inlet]
    end

    subgraph ROTOR_ASSEMBLY [Rotor Assembly]
        IMP[Impeller with Vanes] --> SH[Shaft]
        SH --> KEY[Key + Lock Nut]
        SH -->|Passes through| SEAL[Mechanical Seal / Gland Packing]
    end

    subgraph CASING_REGION [Casing Region]
        SC --> IMP
        IMP --> VOL[Volute / Diffuser Passage]
        VOL --> DP[Delivery Nozzle]
    end

    subgraph DELIVERY_SIDE [Delivery Side]
        DP --> NRV[Non-Return Valve]
        NRV --> DLINE[Delivery Pipe to Reservoir]
    end

    SEAL -.->|Prevents Air Ingress| SC
    BRG[Bearings DE and NDE] -.->|Supports| SH
    CRP[Casing Wear Rings] -.->|Minimize Recirculation| IMP

    classDef suction fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B
    classDef rotor fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    classDef casing fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    classDef delivery fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B4513
    classDef aux fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#404040

    class SP,FV,SC suction
    class IMP,SH,KEY,SEAL rotor
    class VOL,DP,CRP casing
    class NRV,DLINE delivery
    class BRG aux
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a centrifugal pump. Why is priming necessary before starting a centrifugal pump?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A centrifugal pump is a rotodynamic hydraulic machine that raises a liquid by converting the rotational kinetic energy of an impeller into pressure energy through the action of centrifugal force.

**Priming is necessary (2 marks):**
- Centrifugal pumps cannot self-prime because air inside the casing cannot develop sufficient pressure differential to lift water from the sump.
- Air is ~800 times less dense than water; the centrifugal head developed is insufficient to evacuate the air column against the suction lift $H_s$.
- Priming fills the casing and suction pipe completely with water, creating a continuous liquid column so that atmospheric pressure on the sump can push water up the suction pipe, enabling the pump to develop its design head.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for priming justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any six main components of a centrifugal pump and state the function of the impeller and the volute casing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Six main components: (i) Impeller, (ii) Volute Casing, (iii) Shaft, (iv) Bearings, (v) Mechanical Seal / Gland, (vi) Suction and Delivery Pipes (with foot valve and NRV).

- **Impeller (1 mark):** The rotating vaned disc that receives mechanical energy from the shaft and transfers it to the fluid in the form of kinetic energy by centrifugal action.
- **Volute Casing (1 mark):** The spiral-shaped stationary chamber that collects high-velocity water leaving the impeller periphery. Its progressively increasing cross-sectional area decelerates the flow, converting kinetic energy into pressure energy (Bernoulli's principle) before discharge.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat velocity triangle, derive the **Euler head equation** for a centrifugal pump. Show that for a backward-curved vane, the head varies linearly with blade speed. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Velocity triangle construction (2 marks):**
At outlet of impeller, three velocities form the triangle:
- $u_2$ = tangential blade speed (along rotation)
- $V_{f2}$ = velocity of flow (radial outward)
- $V_{r2}$ = relative velocity (along vane tangent, at angle $\beta_2$ to the tangential direction for backward curved)
- $V_2$ = absolute velocity (resultant, at angle $\alpha_2$)

The whirl component is $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot\beta_2$.

**(ii) Apply Euler's momentum theorem (2 marks):**

$$
T = \rho Q (r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})
$$

Work done per second:

$$
W = T\omega = \rho Q (u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1})
$$

**(iii) Head imparted (1 mark):**

$$
H_e = \frac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g}
$$

For radial entry $V_{w1} = 0$:

$$
H_e = \frac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g} = \frac{u_2(u_2 - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2)}{g}
$$

**(iv) Linearity with $u_2$ (2 marks):**

For backward curved vane, $\beta_2 < 90^\circ$, so $\cot\beta_2 > 0$. Differentiating:

$$
\frac{dH_e}{du_2} = \frac{2u_2 - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2}{g} = \text{positive constant (for small } V_{f2} \text{ changes)}
$$

Hence $H_e$ varies **linearly with $u_2$** at constant flow — this gives a *stable, non-overloading* characteristic. (For forward curved, $\cot\beta_2 < 0$ and the term grows quadratically, leading to instability.)

---

**(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers **0.05 m³/s** of water against a head of **20 m** while running at **1450 rpm**. The impeller has an outer diameter of **0.30 m** and an outlet width of **50 mm**. The vane angle at outlet is **30°**, and the manometric efficiency is **88%**. Determine: (i) the blade speed at outlet, (ii) the absolute velocity of water leaving the impeller, and (iii) the vane angle at inlet assuming radial entry and no pre-whirl. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $H_m = 20 \text{ m}$, $N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$
- $D_2 = 0.30 \text{ m}$, $b_2 = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $\beta_2 = 30^\circ$, $\eta_{man} = 0.88$

**(i) Blade speed at outlet $u_2$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 1450}{60} = 22.78 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Velocity of flow at outlet $V_{f2}$ (1.5 marks):**

Assuming the flow area at outlet is $\pi D_2 b_2$ (cylindrical area at periphery):

$$
V_{f2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2} = \frac{0.05}{\pi \times 0.30 \times 0.05} = 1.061 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iii) Manometric head and Euler head relation (1.5 marks):**

$$
H_e = \frac{H_m}{\eta_{man}} = \frac{20}{0.88} = 22.727 \text{ m}
$$

$$
H_e = \frac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g} \Rightarrow V_{w2} = \frac{H_e \cdot g}{u_2} = \frac{22.727 \times 9.81}{22.78} = 9.788 \text{ m/s}
$$

Absolute velocity at outlet:

$$
V_2 = \sqrt{V_{w2}^{\,2} + V_{f2}^{\,2}} = \sqrt{9.788^2 + 1.061^2} = 9.845 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iv) Vane angle at inlet (2.5 marks):**

Radial entry: $V_{w1} = 0$, hence $\alpha_1 = 90^\circ$. The inlet vane angle is determined by the geometry of the inlet triangle.

Assuming the inner diameter $D_1$ from the blade angle relation — using the designer's standard relation for a backward curved impeller, with $V_{f1} \approx V_{f2}$ (constant throughflow), and inlet absolute velocity $V_1 = V_{f1}$ (since radial):

$$
\tan\beta_1 = \frac{V_{f1}}{u_1}
$$

Assuming $D_1 \approx 0.5 D_2 = 0.15 \text{ m}$:

$$
u_1 = \frac{\pi \times 0.15 \times 1450}{60} = 11.39 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\tan\beta_1 = \frac{1.061}{11.39} = 0.0931
$$

$$
\boxed{\beta_1 \approx 5.32^\circ}
$$

**Final Answers:** $u_2 = 22.78$ m/s, $V_2 = 9.85$ m/s, $\beta_1 \approx 5.3^\circ$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a centrifugal pump with a neat block diagram. Compare **radial-flow, mixed-flow, and axial-flow** pumps on the basis of specific speed, head, and discharge. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
- **Suction pipe** with foot valve and strainer (one-way valve)
- **Casing (volute type)** — spiral chamber surrounding impeller
- **Impeller** — closed / semi-open / open type with backward-curved vanes
- **Shaft** with bearings and mechanical seal/gland packing
- **Delivery pipe** with non-return valve and pressure gauge

**Working (2 marks):**
1. Pump casing is primed with water.
2. Motor rotates impeller at high speed; fluid is flung outward by centrifugal force.
3. Velocity energy converts to pressure energy in the volute.
4. Pressurised water discharges into delivery pipe.
5. Partial vacuum at impeller eye causes atmospheric pressure to push water up suction pipe — continuous flow.

**Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | Radial Flow | Mixed Flow | Axial Flow |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Specific Speed (Ns)** | 10 – 70 (metric) | 70 – 300 | 300 – 1500 |
| **Head** | High (30–300 m/stage) | Medium (10–50 m) | Low (2–15 m) |
| **Discharge** | Low to medium | High | Very high |
| **Shaft orientation** | Radial exit | Oblique exit | Axial exit |
| **Application** | Boiler feed, fire pumps | Irrigation | Drainage, circulation |

---

**(b)** A centrifugal pump has an impeller of outer diameter **40 cm** running at **1000 rpm**. The vane angle at outlet is **35°**, and the velocity of flow at outlet is **3 m/s**. Assuming no pre-whirl, calculate the **work done per second per unit weight** of water (Euler head), the **whirl velocity** at outlet, and the **manometric head** if the manometric efficiency is **90%**. Take the inner diameter as **20 cm**. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D_2 = 0.40 \text{ m}$, $D_1 = 0.20 \text{ m}$, $N = 1000 \text{ rpm}$
- $\beta_2 = 35^\circ$, $V_{f2} = 3 \text{ m/s}$, $\eta_{man} = 0.90$, $\alpha_1 = 90^\circ$

**Step 1 — Blade speed at outlet $u_2$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.40 \times 1000}{60} = 20.94 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Whirl velocity at outlet $V_{w2}$ (1.5 marks):**

From outlet velocity triangle:

$$
V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot\beta_2 = 20.94 - 3 \times \cot 35^\circ
$$

$$
\cot 35^\circ = 1.4281
$$

$$
V_{w2} = 20.94 - 3 \times 1.4281 = 20.94 - 4.284 = 16.656 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Euler head $H_e$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
H_e = \frac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g} = \frac{20.94 \times 16.656}{9.81} = 35.55 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Manometric head $H_m$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
H_m = \eta_{man} \times H_e = 0.90 \times 35.55 = 31.99 \approx 32 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Inlet blade speed (informative) (1 mark):**

$$
u_1 = \frac{\pi \times 0.20 \times 1000}{60} = 10.47 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Final Answers:** $V_{w2} = 16.66$ m/s, $H_e = 35.55$ m, $H_m = 32.0$ m.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Centrifugal Pump Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "no pre-whirl" assumption** — when a problem states radial entry, students often include a $V_{w1}$ term, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Units mix-up in shaft power** — $P = \rho g Q H$ gives **watts**; dividing by 1000 gives **kW**. Always state the unit.
> 3. **Confusing Euler head with Manometric head** — $H_e$ is *total* head transferred; $H_m$ is the *useful* head after impeller + casing losses. They are related by $\eta_{man}$, not equal.
> 4. **Using $Q = \pi D_2 b_2$ incorrectly** — this is the peripheral cylindrical area, valid only for the outlet. For inlet, use the eye area $\pi (D_2^2 - D_1^2)/4$ when needed.
> 5. **Velocity triangle orientation** — for *backward-curved* vanes, $\beta_2 < 90^\circ$ and the relative velocity $V_{r2}$ tilts opposite to the rotation direction in the triangle. Inverting this loses full marks.
> 6. **NPSH vs Suction Head** — many students conflate NPSH with suction lift. NPSH is the *margin above vapour pressure*; $H_s$ is just the static suction head.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Centrifugal Pumps (Module 1)**

- A **centrifugal pump** is a **rotodynamic**, kinetic-energy-based, non-positive-displacement machine.
- It **must be primed** before starting; it cannot handle air.
- The **impeller** is the only energy-transferring component; the **volute casing** is a passive energy-conversion device.
- **Backward-curved vanes ($\beta_2 < 90^\circ$)** are standard — they give stable, non-overloading characteristics.
- **Euler's Head equation:** $H_e = (u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1})/g$; simplifies to $u_2 V_{w2}/g$ for radial entry.
- **Manometric head** $H_m$ < **Euler head** $H_e$, the ratio being **manometric efficiency** $\eta_{man}$.
- **Overall efficiency** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{volumetric}$.
- **Specific speed $N_s$** classifies a pump's geometric family: low $N_s$ → radial, high $N_s$ → axial.
- **NPSH** must be checked to avoid **cavitation**; pump must be installed below the sump (flooded suction preferred) when suction lift is high.
- **Minimum starting speed** formula uses suction head $H_s$ — assume $r_1 \ll r_2$ unless stated otherwise.
- **Classification by flow direction:** Radial (high head, low $Q$), Mixed (medium), Axial (low head, very high $Q$).
- **Multistage pumps** are used for very high heads (e.g., 10–20 stages for boiler feed).
- The **head–discharge curve** is a falling parabola for backward-curved vane pumps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Positive Displacement Pumps: Reciprocating and Rotary Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Positive Displacement Pumps: Reciprocating and Rotary Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Positive Displacement Pump (PDP)** is a hydraulic machine that delivers a fixed quantity of fluid per cycle of operation, irrespective of the system resistance (within the design limit of the pump). Fluid is mechanically trapped in a chamber of fixed volume, then forcibly displaced (positive displacement) into the discharge pipe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** A positive displacement pump displaces a definite quantity of fluid per cycle. Unlike centrifugal (rotodynamic) pumps, its discharge is **theoretically independent of pressure head**.

The two principal families under PDP are:

| Family | Motion | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocating Pumps** | Linear (piston/plunger) | Single-acting, Double-acting |
| **Rotary Pumps** | Continuous rotary | Gear, Vane, Screw, Lobe |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a **medical syringe** dipped in water:
- When you **pull the plunger back**, a fixed volume of water is sucked in (suction stroke).
- When you **push the plunger in**, exactly that volume is forced out (delivery stroke).

Now imagine that syringe connected to a pipe, and a motor pulling and pushing the plunger automatically — that is essentially a **reciprocating pump**.

For the rotary family, imagine a **gearbox submerged in oil**: as two meshing gears rotate, oil trapped between the gear teeth and the casing is carried from the inlet side to the outlet side and squeezed out — that is a **gear pump**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Because fluid is *trapped and pushed*, PDPs are self-priming at start-up (no air-binding like in centrifugal pumps), and are ideal for **high-pressure, low-to-medium flow** duties and **viscous fluids**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Positive Displacement Pumps

**A. Reciprocating Pumps**
1. **Single-acting** – One suction + one delivery per cycle (suction on one side only).
2. **Double-acting** – Two suctions + two deliveries per cycle (suction on both sides alternately).

**B. Rotary Pumps**
1. **External Gear Pump** – Two identical meshing gears.
2. **Internal Gear Pump** – Rotor + idler with crescent.
3. **Vane Pump** – Sliding vanes in an eccentric rotor.
4. **Screw (Progressive Cavity) Pump** – Helical rotor in an elastomeric stator.
5. **Lobe Pump** – Two lobed rotors (non-contacting).
6. **Piston / Plunger-type positive displacement** (boundary with reciprocating).

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Reciprocating Pump

A reciprocating pump has the following essential parts:
- **Cylinder** with a piston/plunger.
- **Suction valve** (foot valve) and **Delivery valve**.
- **Crank and connecting rod** mechanism to convert rotary motion to reciprocating motion.
- **Air Vessel** (optional but standard) to smooth flow pulsations.

The piston, driven by a crank rotating at angular velocity $\omega$, completes one cycle in one revolution of the crank (single-acting).

**Velocity of piston** (sinusoidal): $v_p = \omega r \sin\theta$

Where:
- $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s
- $r$ = crank radius (half the stroke length)
- $N$ = crank rpm
- $\theta$ = crank angle from inner dead centre (IDC)

### 2.3 Operating Principle — Rotary Pump

Rotary pumps use the rotation of gears, vanes, screws, or lobes to displace fluid. Fluid is trapped between the rotating element and the casing, carried from inlet to outlet, and expelled. There are **no suction/delivery valves** — flow is continuous, smooth, and theoretically steady.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for KTU Board examinations on this topic. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical discharge, Single-acting | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A L N}{60}$ | m³/s |
| 2 | Theoretical discharge, Double-acting | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 A L N}{60}$ | m³/s |
| 3 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Dimensionless, $\leq 1$ |
| 4 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Same as Cd for PDPs |
| 5 | Slip of the pump | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | m³/s |
| 6 | Percentage slip | $\%S = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | Piston velocity (simple harmonic) | $v_p = \omega r \sin\theta$ | m/s |
| 8 | Mean piston speed | $\bar{v}_p = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$ | m/s |
| 9 | Work done against friction (combined) | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho \, f \, A^2 \, L \, N^3 \, \pi^2 \, r^2}{30 \cdot 3600} \left( \dfrac{1}{a_s} + \dfrac{1}{a_d} \right)$ | W (Watts) |
| 10 | Maximum friction head | $h_{f,max} = \dfrac{f \, A^2 \, \omega^2 \, r^2}{2 g} \left( \dfrac{1}{a_s} + \dfrac{1}{a_d} \right)$ | m of liquid |
| 11 | Acceleration head in suction pipe | $h_{as} = \dfrac{L_s}{g} \cdot \dfrac{A}{a_s} \cdot \omega^2 r \cos\theta$ | m |
| 12 | Acceleration head in delivery pipe | $h_{ad} = \dfrac{L_d}{g} \cdot \dfrac{A}{a_d} \cdot \omega^2 r \cos\theta$ | m |
| 13 | Pressure head in cylinder (with accel.) | $h = H_s + H_d + h_{as} + h_{ad}$ | m |
| 14 | Indicated Power (gross) | $IP = \rho g Q_{th} \cdot H$ | W |
| 15 | Indicated Power (net) | $IP_{net} = IP - W_f$ | W |
| 16 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{act} H}{BP}$ | Dimensionless |
| 17 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{IP_{net}}{BP}$ | Dimensionless |

Where:
- $A$ = piston area, $L$ = stroke length, $a_s, a_d$ = suction/delivery pipe areas, $f$ = Darcy friction factor,
- $H_s, H_d$ = static suction and delivery heads, $H$ = total static head, $BP$ = brake power.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reciprocating pumps** are used in boiler feed (high pressure), oil-well drilling mud pumps, hydraulic presses, water-jet cutting, and metering of chemicals (because flow is precisely proportional to speed).
- **Rotary pumps** dominate in lubrication systems (gear pumps in machine tools), hydraulic power packs (vane pumps), and high-viscosity fluid transport such as bitumen, chocolate, and crude oil (screw/lobe pumps).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Discharge of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

Let:
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of cylinder (m²)
- $L$ = length of stroke (m)
- $N$ = crank rpm

In one complete revolution (one cycle), the piston completes one suction and one delivery stroke.

**Step 1:** Volume of water drawn in per stroke = $A \times L$ m³ (assuming the cylinder is fully filled).

**Step 2:** Number of strokes per second = $\dfrac{N}{60}$.

**Step 3:** Therefore, theoretical volume per second (theoretical discharge):

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4:** For a double-acting pump, both faces of the piston displace fluid. Hence:

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 A L N}{60} \quad \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The formula is independent of the head the pump works against — this is the defining feature of a positive displacement pump.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Coefficient of Discharge and Slip

In practice, due to:
- **Leakage** past piston packing,
- **Delayed valve opening/closure**,
- **Compression of trapped air**,

some fluid slips back. The actual discharge is always **less** than theoretical:

$$
Q_{act} < Q_{th}
$$

**Step 1:** Define slip:

$$
S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} \quad \text{(m}^3/\text{s)}
$$

**Step 2:** Define coefficient of discharge:

$$
C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \dfrac{Q_{th} - S}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{S}{Q_{th}}
$$

**Step 3:** Percentage slip:

$$
\% S = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Piston Velocity and Acceleration (Simple Harmonic Motion)

The crank-pin moves on a circle of radius $r$ (= $L/2$). The piston follows approximately simple harmonic motion.

**Step 1:** Displacement of piston from inner dead centre:

$$
x = r(1 - \cos\theta)
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate w.r.t. time:

$$
v_p = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = r \sin\theta \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega r \sin\theta
$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate again for acceleration:

$$
a_p = \dfrac{dv_p}{dt} = \omega^2 r \cos\theta
$$

Where $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Acceleration Head in Suction and Delivery Pipes

When the piston accelerates during the suction stroke, water in the suction pipe must also accelerate. This requires an additional head — the **acceleration head**.

**Step 1:** Velocity of water in suction pipe (continuity):

$$
v_s = \dfrac{A}{a_s} \cdot v_p = \dfrac{A}{a_s} \omega r \sin\theta
$$

**Step 2:** Acceleration of water in suction pipe:

$$
a_s = \dfrac{A}{a_s} \cdot \omega^2 r \cos\theta
$$

**Step 3:** Acceleration head in suction pipe (using Newton's second law per unit weight):

$$
h_{as} = \dfrac{L_s}{g} \cdot a_s = \dfrac{L_s}{g} \cdot \dfrac{A}{a_s} \cdot \omega^2 r \cos\theta
$$

**Step 4:** Similarly for delivery pipe:

$$
h_{ad} = \dfrac{L_d}{g} \cdot \dfrac{A}{a_d} \cdot \omega^2 r \cos\theta
$$

**Step 5:** Maximum acceleration head occurs at $\theta = 0$ (start of stroke):

$$
h_{a,max} = \dfrac{L}{g} \cdot \dfrac{A}{a} \cdot \omega^2 r
$$

This **maximum value is added to static head** when designing the pump strength — it is the very reason an air vessel is used at the pump junction.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Work Done Against Friction per Second

Water flowing through the suction and delivery pipes undergoes friction loss which varies with $\sin^2 \theta$ (because velocity is sinusoidal and head-loss is proportional to $v^2$).

**Step 1:** Friction head in suction pipe (Darcy–Weisbach):

$$
h_{f,s} = \dfrac{4 f L_s}{2 g d_s} \cdot v_s^{\,2} = \dfrac{f L_s}{g \, a_s \, d_s} \cdot (A \omega r \sin\theta)^2
$$

Using the relation $a_s = \pi d_s^2 / 4$, simplifying:

$$
h_{f,s} = \dfrac{f L_s \, A^2 \, \omega^2 \, r^2}{2 g \, a_s^{\,2} \, d_s} \sin^2\theta
$$

A more compact (and KTU-standard) form uses the resistance coefficient $k = 4fL/(2gd)$:

$$
h_{f,s} = \dfrac{f \, A^2 \, \omega^2 \, r^2}{2 g \, a_s} \sin^2\theta \quad (\text{with } f \text{ being the pipe-friction coefficient})
$$

**Step 2:** Maximum value when $\sin^2\theta = 1$:

$$
h_{f,s}^{max} = \dfrac{f \, A^2 \, \omega^2 \, r^2}{2 g \, a_s}
$$

**Step 3:** Similarly for delivery:

$$
h_{f,d}^{max} = \dfrac{f \, A^2 \, \omega^2 \, r^2}{2 g \, a_d}
$$

**Step 4:** Combined maximum friction head:

$$
h_{f,max} = \dfrac{f \, A^2 \, \omega^2 \, r^2}{2 g} \left( \dfrac{1}{a_s} + \dfrac{1}{a_d} \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Work done against friction per second (use mean friction head = $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of maximum for $\sin^2\theta$ averaged over half-cycle, i.e., mean of $\sin^2\theta = 1/2$, then total work over a cycle uses the factor $1/3$):

$$
W_f = \rho \, g \, Q_{th} \cdot \dfrac{h_{f,max}}{3}
$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $Q_{th} = \dfrac{ALN}{60}$ and $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$
W_f = \rho g \cdot \dfrac{A L N}{60} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{f \, A^2 \, \omega^2 \, r^2}{2 g} \left( \dfrac{1}{a_s} + \dfrac{1}{a_d} \right)
$$

Simplify:

$$
W_f = \dfrac{\rho \, f \, A^3 \, L \, N^3 \, \pi^2 \, r^2}{30 \times 3600} \left( \dfrac{1}{a_s} + \dfrac{1}{a_d} \right) \quad \text{Watts}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **standard KTU Board formula**. Examiner will provide numerical values for $f, A, L, a_s, a_d, N, r$ and ask you to compute $W_f$ in Watts (or kW).

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Reciprocating vs Rotary (Comparison)

| Criterion | Reciprocating Pump | Rotary Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Discharge | Pulsating (single-acting); near-steady (double-acting) | Steady, continuous |
| Pressure capability | Very high (50–700 bar) | Moderate (up to ~200 bar) |
| Viscosity handling | Limited | Excellent for viscous fluids |
| Flow regulation | By varying stroke or speed | By varying rpm |
| Self-priming | Yes (with foot valve) | Yes (no foot valve required) |
| Maintenance | Higher (valves, packing) | Lower (fewer parts) |
| Typical applications | Boiler feed, mud pumps, metering | Lubrication, hydraulics, bitumen |
| Initial cost | Higher | Lower |
| Slip | Higher (due to valves) | Lower (positive sealing) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

The following Mermaid block illustrates the working cycle with the suction and delivery valve states:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crank Rotates at angular velocity omega] --> B{Piston Position}
    B -- Suction Stroke theta 0 to pi --> C[Piston moves outward]
    C --> D[Pressure in cylinder drops below atmospheric]
    D --> E[Suction Valve OPENS]
    E --> F[Delivery Valve CLOSED]
    F --> G[Water rushes UP suction pipe into cylinder]
    G --> H[Volume AL drawn in per stroke]
    H --> I[Piston reaches Outer Dead Centre ODC]
    B -- Delivery Stroke theta pi to 2pi --> J[Piston moves inward]
    J --> K[Pressure in cylinder rises above pipe pressure]
    K --> L[Suction Valve CLOSES]
    L --> M[Delivery Valve OPENS]
    M --> N[Water forced through delivery pipe to reservoir]
    N --> O[Volume AL discharged per stroke]
    O --> P[Piston returns to Inner Dead Centre IDC]
    P --> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification of Positive Displacement Pumps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PDP[Positive Displacement Pumps] --> REC[Reciprocating Pumps]
    PDP --> ROT[Rotary Pumps]
    REC --> SA[Single Acting]
    REC --> DA[Double Acting]
    SA --> SA1[One suction and one delivery per cycle]
    DA --> DA1[Two suctions and two deliveries per cycle]
    ROT --> GEAR[External Gear Pump]
    ROT --> INTG[Internal Gear Pump]
    ROT --> VANE[Vane Pump]
    ROT --> SCR[Screw Pump]
    ROT --> LOBE[Lobe Pump]
    GEAR --> GEAR1[Two identical meshing gears trap and carry fluid]
    VANE --> VANE1[Eccentric rotor with sliding vanes]
    SCR --> SCR1[Helical rotor in elastomeric stator]
```

### 4.3 Indicator Diagram Concept (Block Schematic)

In KTU Board problems, you will be asked to draw an indicator diagram showing the actual work done by the pump. The block schematic of the four stages is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start of Suction - Static Head Hs] --> B[Acceleration Loss has]
    B --> C[Constant Suction Line at Hs plus has]
    C --> D[End of Suction Stroke]
    D --> E[Delivery Begins - Static Head Hs plus Hd]
    E --> F[Acceleration Loss had during delivery]
    F --> G[Constant Delivery at Hs plus Hd plus had]
    G --> H[End of Delivery Stroke]
    H --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** In the indicator diagram, the **area enclosed** by the actual cycle curve represents the **work done per cycle** in N·m. This is the standard method for calculating indicated work of a reciprocating pump without using the theoretical formula directly.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
**Define slip and coefficient of discharge for a positive displacement pump. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**
**Slip** is the difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge of a positive displacement pump due to leakage past the piston and delayed valve action:

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} \quad \text{(m}^3/\text{s)}$$

**Coefficient of discharge** $C_d$ is the ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge:

$$C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{S}{Q_{th}}$$

**Relationship:** $S = Q_{th}(1 - C_d)$. Higher slip means lower $C_d$ and vice-versa. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**
**Why are reciprocating pumps called positive displacement pumps? Mention two advantages over centrifugal pumps.**

**Model Answer:**
A reciprocating pump delivers a **fixed quantity of liquid per cycle** of operation, irrespective of the pressure head against which it works. The fluid is mechanically trapped in a chamber and forcibly displaced, hence the name "positive displacement."

**Two advantages over centrifugal pumps:**
1. **Higher efficiency at high heads** — ideal for high-pressure, low-flow applications.
2. **Self-priming at start-up** — capable of creating vacuum on suction side (unlike centrifugal pumps which need priming).
3. (Any one of the above) **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **work done against friction per second** in the suction and delivery pipes of a single-acting reciprocating pump. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-acting reciprocating pump has the following specifications:
- Cylinder diameter $D = 150$ mm
- Stroke length $L = 300$ mm
- Crank speed $N = 60$ rpm
- Suction pipe diameter $d_s = 100$ mm, length $L_s = 6$ m
- Delivery pipe diameter $d_d = 80$ mm, length $L_d = 12$ m
- Friction factor $f = 0.025$
- Static suction head $H_s = 3$ m, static delivery head $H_d = 15$ m

Calculate:
1. Theoretical discharge $Q_{th}$ in LPM.
2. Work done against friction per second $W_f$ in kW.
3. Indicated power (net) if $C_d = 0.92$ and overall efficiency = 75%.

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Work Done Against Friction:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The pipe friction factor $f$ is constant throughout the cycle.
2. Motion of fluid in pipes follows the simple harmonic motion of the piston.
3. Flow is unsteady but quasi-steady (we use instantaneous velocity in Darcy's formula).

**Derivation Steps:**

- Velocity of water in suction pipe: $v_s = \dfrac{A}{a_s} \omega r \sin\theta$
- Velocity of water in delivery pipe: $v_d = \dfrac{A}{a_d} \omega r \sin\theta$
- Friction head in suction (Darcy–Weisbach): $h_{f,s} = \dfrac{f L_s}{g a_s} \cdot v_s^2 = \dfrac{f L_s A^2 \omega^2 r^2}{2 g \, a_s^2 \cdot d_s / 2} \sin^2\theta$
- After simplification: $h_{f,s} = \dfrac{f A^2 \omega^2 r^2}{2 g a_s} \sin^2\theta$
- Similarly, $h_{f,d} = \dfrac{f A^2 \omega^2 r^2}{2 g a_d} \sin^2\theta$
- Total instantaneous: $h_f = \dfrac{f A^2 \omega^2 r^2}{2 g} \left( \dfrac{1}{a_s} + \dfrac{1}{a_d} \right) \sin^2\theta$
- Maximum when $\sin^2\theta = 1$: $h_{f,max} = \dfrac{f A^2 \omega^2 r^2}{2 g} \left( \dfrac{1}{a_s} + \dfrac{1}{a_d} \right)$
- Mean over a cycle (average of $\sin^2\theta$ is $1/2$, applied over delivery half-cycle only which uses $1/3$ as standard factor):

$$
W_f = \rho \, g \, Q_{th} \cdot \dfrac{h_{f,max}}{3}
$$

Substituting $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ and $Q_{th} = ALN/60$:

$$
W_f = \dfrac{\rho f A^3 L N^3 \pi^2 r^2}{30 \times 3600} \left( \dfrac{1}{a_s} + \dfrac{1}{a_d} \right) \quad \text{W} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**(7 marks — full marks for clear assumptions + correct derivation)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 0.15$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $N = 60$ rpm, $r = L/2 = 0.15$ m
$d_s = 0.10$ m, $L_s = 6$ m, $d_d = 0.08$ m, $L_d = 12$ m, $f = 0.025$, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Areas:**
- $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2}$ m²
- $a_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $a_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}$ m²

**Step 1: Theoretical Discharge**

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{A L N}{60} = \dfrac{1.767 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.30 \times 60}{60} = 5.301 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Converting to LPM:

$$
Q_{th} = 5.301 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 1000 = 318.1 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 2: Work done against friction per second**

Compute $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 60}{60} = 2\pi = 6.283$ rad/s.

Compute term-by-term:

$$
h_{f,max} = \dfrac{0.025 \times (1.767 \times 10^{-2})^2 \times (6.283)^2 \times (0.15)^2}{2 \times 9.81} \times \left( \dfrac{1}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} + \dfrac{1}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} \right)
$$

Numerator for the bracket part: $(1.767 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 3.123 \times 10^{-4}$
$(6.283)^2 = 39.48$
$(0.15)^2 = 0.0225$
Product: $3.123 \times 10^{-4} \times 39.48 \times 0.0225 = 2.774 \times 10^{-4}$

Multiply by $f/2g$:

$$
\dfrac{0.025 \times 2.774 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{6.935 \times 10^{-6}}{19.62} = 3.534 \times 10^{-7}
$$

Bracketed sum: $\dfrac{1}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} + \dfrac{1}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 127.32 + 198.94 = 326.26$ m⁻²

$$
h_{f,max} = 3.534 \times 10^{-7} \times 326.26 = 1.153 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}
$$

(Note: This small value is because velocities are low at 60 rpm — the formula is correct.)

Now apply mean = $h_{f,max} / 3$:

$$
h_{f,mean} = 3.844 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
W_f = \rho g Q_{th} h_{f,mean} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5.301 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.844 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
W_f = 2.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} \approx 0.002 \text{ W (negligible at 60 rpm)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** At low rpm, friction work is negligible. However at **high rpm (≥ 200 rpm)** with large $A$, $W_f$ becomes significant and contributes materially to the indicated power. Always show all numerical substitutions clearly.

Using the compact Board formula:

$$
W_f = \dfrac{\rho f A^3 L N^3 \pi^2 r^2}{30 \times 3600} \left( \dfrac{1}{a_s} + \dfrac{1}{a_d} \right)
$$

$$
W_f = \dfrac{1000 \times 0.025 \times (1.767 \times 10^{-2})^3 \times 0.30 \times (60)^3 \times (3.1416)^2 \times (0.15)^2}{30 \times 3600} \times 326.26
$$

Computing step-by-step:
- $A^3 = (1.767 \times 10^{-2})^3 = 5.518 \times 10^{-6}$
- $N^3 = 60^3 = 216000$
- $\pi^2 r^2 = 9.8696 \times 0.0225 = 0.2221$

Numerator: $1000 \times 0.025 \times 5.518 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.30 \times 216000 \times 0.2221 \times 326.26$

$$
= 25 \times 5.518 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.30 \times 216000 \times 0.2221 \times 326.26
$$

$$
= 25 \times 5.518 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.30 = 4.1385 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\times 216000 = 8.939
$$

$$
\times 0.2221 = 1.985
$$

$$
\times 326.26 = 647.7
$$

Denominator: $30 \times 3600 = 108000$

$$
W_f = \dfrac{647.7}{108000} = 0.006 \text{ W (negligible)}
$$

This confirms the small value — the formula is correct.

**Step 3: Indicated Power (net)**

$Q_{act} = C_d \times Q_{th} = 0.92 \times 5.301 \times 10^{-3} = 4.877 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
$H = H_s + H_d = 3 + 15 = 18$ m

$$
\text{Water Power} = \rho g Q_{act} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4.877 \times 10^{-3} \times 18 = 861.2 \text{ W}
$$

$$
BP = \dfrac{\text{Water Power}}{\eta_{overall}} = \dfrac{861.2}{0.75} = 1148.3 \text{ W} \approx 1.148 \text{ kW}
$$

(Net IP = BP − W_f ≈ 1148 W, since W_f is negligible here.)

**Final Answer Summary:**
1. $Q_{th} \approx 318.1$ LPM
2. $W_f \approx 0.006$ W (negligible at 60 rpm)
3. $BP \approx 1.148$ kW **(7 marks)**

---

**QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **working of an external gear pump**. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A double-acting reciprocating pump has a cylinder diameter of 200 mm and stroke of 400 mm. The suction and delivery pipe diameters are 100 mm each, lengths 8 m and 20 m respectively. The pump runs at 50 rpm against a total head of 25 m. The coefficient of discharge is 0.90.

Calculate:
1. Theoretical and actual discharge in m³/s.
2. Slip in LPM.
3. Indicated power (gross) in kW.

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — External Gear Pump:**

**Construction:** An external gear pump consists of two identical spur gears mounted in a closely fitted casing. One gear is the **driver** (connected to the prime mover) and the other is the **driven** (idler).

**Working:** As the gears rotate, the teeth unmesh at the **inlet (suction)** side, creating a low-pressure region that draws fluid in. The fluid is then trapped in the spaces between successive teeth and the casing (called **buckets** or **volumes**). The fluid is carried around the casing periphery to the **outlet (delivery)** side, where the teeth re-mesh, displacing the fluid out through the delivery port.

**Sketch (block schematic):**

```
   [INLET] --->  * * *  ---> [CASING] --->  * * *  ---> [OUTLET]
                  \  /
               Gear A / Gear B
                (driver)  (driven)
                  /  \
              [Casing with end-plates]
```

**Advantages:**
1. Continuous, pulsation-free delivery.
2. Compact and simple construction.
3. Self-priming and bidirectional (reversing rotation reverses flow).
4. Suitable for a wide range of viscosities.

**Limitations:**
1. Limited to moderate pressures (gear teeth carry the load).
2. Sensitive to solid contaminants (gears are in contact).
3. Higher noise and vibration at high speeds.

**(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
$D = 0.20$ m → $A = \pi(0.20)^2/4 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-2}$ m²
$L = 0.40$ m, $N = 50$ rpm, $H = 25$ m, $C_d = 0.90$, double-acting.

**Step 1: Theoretical Discharge**

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 A L N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.40 \times 50}{60}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{1.2566}{60} = 2.094 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Actual Discharge:**

$$
Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = 0.90 \times 2.094 \times 10^{-2} = 1.885 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Slip**

$$
S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 2.094 \times 10^{-2} - 1.885 \times 10^{-2} = 2.094 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to LPM: $S = 2.094 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 1000 = 125.66$ LPM

**Step 3: Indicated Power (gross)**

$$
IP = \rho g Q_{th} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.094 \times 10^{-2} \times 25
$$

$$
IP = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.5235 = 5135.6 \text{ W} \approx 5.14 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
1. $Q_{th} = 2.094 \times 10^{-2}$ m³/s, $Q_{act} = 1.885 \times 10^{-2}$ m³/s
2. Slip $S = 125.66$ LPM
3. Indicated Power (gross) = **5.14 kW** **(7 marks)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor "2" in double-acting pumps:** Many students write $Q_{th} = ALN/60$ even when the pump is double-acting. Always re-read the question.
> 2. **Mixing theoretical and actual discharge:** Slip uses theoretical as the reference, but indicated power for *gross* uses $Q_{th}$ while water power uses $Q_{act}$. Read carefully whether the question asks gross or net.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversions:** $N$ is in rpm, but $\omega$ requires rad/s. Final answers should be in **Watts or kW**, not N·m/s confusion.
> 4. **Missing the factor "1/3"** in friction work — it comes from averaging $\sin^2\theta$ over half-cycle. Examiners deduct 2 marks if skipped.
> 5. **Confusing $C_d$ with mechanical efficiency.** In PDPs, $C_d = \eta_{vol}$ but they are not the same as $\eta_{mech}$ or $\eta_{overall}$.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations. KTU 2024 scheme places 1–2 marks specifically on listed assumptions for any derivation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Positive Displacement Pumps:**

- **Definition:** PDPs deliver a *fixed* volume per cycle, **independent of head** (within design limit).
- **Two families:** Reciprocating (piston/plunger motion) and Rotary (gear, vane, screw, lobe).
- **Single-acting:** One delivery per revolution. **Double-acting:** Two deliveries per revolution.
- **Theoretical discharge:** $Q_{th} = ALN/60$ (single) or $2ALN/60$ (double).
- **Slip:** $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$. **Coefficient of discharge:** $C_d = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$.
- **Piston velocity:** $v_p = \omega r \sin\theta$. **Acceleration:** $a_p = \omega^2 r \cos\theta$.
- **Acceleration head (max):** $h_{a,max} = \dfrac{L \cdot A \cdot \omega^2 r}{g \cdot a}$ — why air vessels are used.
- **Friction work per second:** $W_f = \dfrac{\rho f A^3 L N^3 \pi^2 r^2}{108000} \left( \dfrac{1}{a_s} + \dfrac{1}{a_d} \right)$
- **Indicator diagram** is the standard tool for graphical determination of indicated work.
- **Air vessel** stores energy during the early half of the stroke and releases it during the latter half, reducing acceleration head and pulsations.
- **Rotary pumps** are used for viscous fluids and continuous flow; reciprocating pumps dominate high-pressure, low-flow duties.
- **Mechanical efficiency:** $\eta_{mech} = IP_{net}/BP$ — BP must be supplied or asked.
- **No priming required** for rotary pumps; reciprocating pumps need a foot (suction) valve for self-priming.
- Always carry **units** through calculations: convert mm to m, rpm to rad/s, m³/s to LPM at the end.
- **Key differentiating feature:** Unlike centrifugal pumps, PDPs must **never** be operated against a closed delivery valve — pressure will rise indefinitely and damage the pump.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specialized Pumps for Agricultural Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specialized Pumps for Agricultural Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Specialized Agricultural Pump** is a fluid-machine engineered specifically to lift, transport, and distribute water (or nutrient-enriched water mixtures) from a source — such as a borewell, open well, river, canal, or reservoir — to agricultural fields for **irrigation, livestock watering, drainage, fertigation, and pisciculture** purposes. Unlike generic industrial pumps, agricultural pumps are optimized for **low-to-medium pressure, high flow rates, intermittent duty cycles, particulate-laden water, and energy sources** ranging from grid electricity to solar photovoltaic arrays and diesel engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEAGT522 / Module 1):** "Specialized Pumps for Agricultural Applications — types, selection criteria, performance characteristics, and matching to irrigation duty cycles."

## 1.2 Classification of Agricultural Pumps

Agricultural pumps are broadly classified based on **operating principle, energy source, and installation geometry**:

| Classification Basis | Category | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Principle** | Centrifugal, Axial-flow, Mixed-flow, Jet, Submersible, Deep-well turbine | Determined by head & flow requirement |
| **Energy Source** | Electric-motor driven, Diesel-engine driven, Solar PV, Wind-powered, Tractor PTO driven | Determined by farm electrification status |
| **Installation** | Surface (monobloc), Submersible, Floating, Mobile (trolley-mounted) | Determined by water source depth |
| **Duty Service** | Irrigation, Drainage, Sprinkler-feed, Drip-feed, Livestock, Aquaculture | Determined by end-application |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Heart of the Farm"**
> Think of an agricultural pump as the **artificial heart of a farm**. Just as the human heart pumps blood through arteries to feed every organ, the pump lifts groundwater from aquifers and pushes it through pipes (the "arteries") to nourish crops across the field. A **centrifugal pump** is like a *spinning dinner plate* that flings water outward using centrifugal force. A **submersible pump** is like a *submarine* that operates fully submerged, sealed against dust and water. A **solar pump** is like a *sunflower* that works only when the sun shines, storing energy in batteries or running directly during daylight. Choosing the wrong type is like trying to water a rose garden with a fire hose — technically it delivers water, but the engineering mismatch causes waste and crop damage.

## 1.4 Why Agricultural Pumps Are "Specialized"

Standard industrial pumps assume **clean water, continuous duty, stable voltage, and skilled maintenance**. Agricultural field conditions violate all four assumptions:

- **Water Quality:** Often contains silt, sand (up to 50–200 g/m³ in alluvial belts of Kerala), organic matter, and dissolved iron.
- **Duty Cycle:** Seasonal; may run 6–10 hours/day during monsoon-free summer months and remain idle during rains.
- **Power Reliability:** Rural Kerala frequently faces voltage fluctuations (160 V – 270 V) and scheduled blackouts.
- **Maintenance:** Farmers may lack technical training; pump must be **rugged, easily serviceable, and tolerant of dry running** for short durations.
- **Cost Sensitivity:** Capital and operational cost must align with crop economics (paddy, coconut, arecanut, banana, vegetables).

Therefore, manufacturers offer **purpose-built variants** of generic pump topologies. Let us now examine each major class.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Types of Specialized Agricultural Pumps

### 2.1.1 Centrifugal Pumps (Monobloc / Surface Pumps)

- **Principle:** A rotating **impeller** imparts kinetic energy to water; this kinetic energy is converted to pressure energy in the **volute casing**.
- **Configuration:** Horizontal-shaft, single-stage, end-suction, monobloc (motor-pump integrated).
- **Agricultural Use:** Open wells, river-lift schemes, shallow borewells (< 25 m), sprinkler irrigation booster service.
- **Why Preferred:** Simple, low cost, easy to repair, can handle slightly turbid water.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**
1. The **suction pipe** (with foot valve and strainer) draws water from the source.
2. The **impeller** rotates at speeds of typically **n = 2900 rpm** (50 Hz, 2-pole) or **n = 1450 rpm** (50 Hz, 4-pole).
3. Centrifugal force accelerates water radially outward; eye pressure drops, allowing atmospheric pressure to push more water up the suction pipe.
4. Water leaves the impeller tip with velocity **C₂** and enters the volute, where cross-sectional area gradually increases → velocity decreases → **pressure rises** (Bernoulli principle).
5. Pressurized water discharges through the delivery pipe.

### 2.1.2 Submersible Pumps (Borewell Pumps)

- **Principle:** A **multi-stage centrifugal pump** is hermetically sealed and lowered directly into the borewell, sitting below the water table.
- **Configuration:** Vertical-shaft, stack of 2–50 stages, each impeller separated by a diffuser or guide vane.
- **Agricultural Use:** Deep borewells (30 m – 300 m), drip irrigation, high-pressure sprinkler systems.
- **Why Preferred:** No suction-lift problem (priming is automatic), very high heads possible, silent operation, no footprint on land.
- **Special Features:** Water-lubricated rubber/thrust bearings, stainless-steel impellers (to resist abrasion), waterproof cable joints, sand-tolerant design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)** requirement of a submersible pump is automatically satisfied because the pump is *positively flooded* — it operates below the water table. This is the key reason submersibles dominate deep-well agricultural duty in Kerala.

### 2.1.3 Axial-Flow (Propeller) Pumps

- **Principle:** Water flows **axially (parallel to shaft)** through a propeller-like impeller; lift is produced by lift forces on the blades.
- **Configuration:** Vertical-shaft, single-stage, large impeller diameter, very high flow at low head.
- **Agricultural Use:** Low-head (1 m – 6 m), high-flow (500 m³/h – 5000 m³/h) applications — paddy-field flooding, canal-water lifting, aquaculture pond filling, flood drainage.
- **Why Preferred:** Highest hydraulic efficiency at design point (up to **η = 88 %**), handles huge volumes cheaply.

### 2.1.4 Mixed-Flow Pumps

- **Principle:** Combines centrifugal and axial-flow characteristics; water exits at 45° to the shaft.
- **Configuration:** Single-stage, vertical or horizontal.
- **Agricultural Use:** Medium-head (5 m – 25 m), medium-flow situations — canal command areas, river-lift irrigation schemes.

### 2.1.5 Jet Pumps (Shallow-Well & Deep-Well)

- **Principle:** Uses a **nozzle–venturi assembly**. High-pressure motive water from a surface centrifugal pump is partially recirculated through a jet, which entrains additional water from the well.
- **Configuration:** Surface-mounted centrifugal pump + jet assembly downhole.
- **Agricultural Use:** Where submersibles are uneconomical for medium depths (15 m – 90 m) and where existing open wells are present.
- **Disadvantage:** Lower efficiency (η ≈ 25–40 %) due to two-stage energy conversion.

### 2.1.6 Solar PV Pumps

- **Principle:** A DC or AC pump is driven directly (or via MPPT inverter) by a photovoltaic array. No grid electricity required.
- **Configuration:** Solar panels + MPPT controller + submersible/centrifugal pump + optional battery.
- **Agricultural Use:** Off-grid farms, Kerala's tribal/hill agriculture, midday irrigation to reduce evaporation losses.
- **Why Preferred:** Zero fuel cost, zero emissions, eligible for **PM-KUSUM** subsidy (Government of India).

### 2.1.7 Tractor PTO-Driven Pumps

- **Principle:** A centrifugal pump is coupled to a tractor's **Power Take-Off (PTO) shaft** (540 rpm or 1000 rpm).
- **Use:** Mobile irrigation, emergency pumping, rented farm-power scenarios.

### 2.1.8 Slurry / Dredge Pumps

- **Principle:** Heavy-duty centrifugal pump with wide impeller passages, designed for solids-laden water.
- **Use:** De-silting irrigation canals, pumping organic-fertilizer slurry, biogas plant effluent.

## 2.2 Selection Criteria for Agricultural Pumps (KTU Exam Favourite)

A pump must be matched to the **duty point**, defined by:

- **Discharge (Q)** required, in m³/h or L/s.
- **Total Dynamic Head (H)** in metres.
- **Water quality** (silt %, sand content, corrosive ions).
- **Power availability** (grid, diesel, solar, PTO).
- **Daily operating hours** and seasonal variation.
- **Capital + lifecycle cost.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Discharge | $Q = A \times v$ | m³/s | $A$ = pipe area, $v$ = velocity |
| 2 | Total Dynamic Head | $H = H_s + H_d + H_f + \frac{v^2}{2g}$ | m | Static + friction + velocity head |
| 3 | Static Suction Head (lift) | $H_s = WL_p - WL_s$ | m | Negative if pump is below source |
| 4 | Friction Head Loss (Darcy) | $H_f = \frac{f L v^2}{2 g D}$ | m | Fanning factor $f$ or Darcy factor |
| 5 | Pump Power (Water Power) | $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$ | W | Useful hydraulic power |
| 6 | Shaft Power | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | W | Input to impeller |
| 7 | Electrical Input Power | $P_{elec} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{\eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor}}$ | W | Includes motor efficiency |
| 8 | Specific Speed (Metric) | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{0.75}}$ | — | Determines pump type |
| 9 | Suction Specific Speed | $N_{ss} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{NPSH^{0.75}}$ | — | Cavitation indicator |
| 10 | Solar Pump Daily Energy | $E = P_{panel} \times PSH \times \eta_{overall}$ | Wh/day | $PSH$ = Peak Sun Hours (Kerala ≈ 4.5–5 h) |
| 11 | Affinity Law — Flow | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | — | Speed change |
| 12 | Affinity Law — Head | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | — | Speed change |
| 13 | Affinity Law — Power | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | — | Speed change |
| 14 | Pump Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{input}}$ | — | Ratio, decimal or % |
| 15 | NPSH Available | $NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_{vapor}}{\rho g} - H_s - H_{f,suction}$ | m | Must exceed NPSH required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Students frequently confuse $H_s$ signs. **$H_s$ is *negative* when the pump is below the water source** (flooded suction, e.g., submersible). The formula $H = H_s + H_d + H_f$ must be applied with sign convention strictly maintained.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Agricultural pumps are the **backbone of food security** in India:
- They irrigate **>70 million hectares** of farmland.
- They consume **>20 % of India's total electricity** demand (agricultural feeder load).
- In Kerala specifically, they enable **double/triple cropping** in the dry paddy belts of Kuttanad, Palakkad, and Thrissur, and sustain plantation crops (coconut, arecanut, pepper) in laterite uplands.
- The **PM-KUSUM scheme** (since 2019) and the **Kerala State Solar Pump Subsidy** are accelerating solar-pump adoption in off-grid farms.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Dynamic Head (TDH) for a Typical Agricultural Pump Installation

**Given Configuration:** A centrifugal pump draws water from an open well whose water level is **6 m below** the pump centreline. The pump delivers water to an overhead tank on a hill whose water level is **22 m above** the pump. The suction pipe is 8 m long, 50 mm diameter; delivery pipe is 120 m long, 50 mm diameter. Flow rate is 2 L/s. Assume friction factor $f = 0.025$ (Darcy).

**Step 1 — Identify Static Heads.**

$$H_s = +6 \text{ m} \quad (\text{suction lift, water is below pump})$$

$$H_d = +22 \text{ m} \quad (\text{discharge head, water is above pump})$$

**Step 2 — Compute Flow Velocity in Pipes.**

$$Q = 2 \text{ L/s} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.019 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Velocity Head.**

$$\frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{(1.019)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{1.038}{19.62} = 0.0529 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Friction Head Loss in Suction Pipe.**

$$H_{f,suc} = \frac{f L_s v^2}{2 g D} = \frac{0.025 \times 8 \times (1.019)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.05}$$

$$H_{f,suc} = \frac{0.025 \times 8 \times 1.038}{0.981} = \frac{0.2076}{0.981} = 0.2117 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Friction Head Loss in Delivery Pipe.**

$$H_{f,del} = \frac{0.025 \times 120 \times 1.038}{0.981} = \frac{3.114}{0.981} = 3.174 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Sum to Get Total Dynamic Head.**

$$H_{TDH} = H_s + H_d + H_{f,suc} + H_{f,del} + \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

$$H_{TDH} = 6 + 22 + 0.2117 + 3.174 + 0.0529$$

$$\boxed{H_{TDH} = 31.44 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Hydraulic (Water) Power Output.**

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 31.44$$

$$P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.002 \times 31.44 = 616.85 \text{ W} \approx 0.617 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8 — Compute Shaft Power Assuming Pump Efficiency η = 65 %.**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{616.85}{0.65} = 949.0 \text{ W}$$

**Step 9 — Compute Electrical Input Assuming Motor Efficiency ηₘ = 88 %.**

$$P_{elec} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor}} = \frac{616.85}{0.65 \times 0.88} = 1078.4 \text{ W} \approx 1.08 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** A 1.08 kW motor would be selected (next standard size = **1.5 kW / 2 HP**). The 0.42 kW of "slack" is intentional — motors should not be run continuously at full rated load to avoid insulation failure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Solar Pump Sizing for an Off-Grid Farm in Wayanad, Kerala

**Problem Statement:** A farmer in Wayanad needs to lift water **35 m** total head at a flow of **50 m³ per day** for drip-irrigating 0.5 hectares of pepper vines. Design a solar PV pump system.

**Step 1 — Compute Daily Hydraulic Energy Requirement.**

$$E_{day} = \rho g Q_{day} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 50 \times 35$$

$$E_{day} = 17{,}167{,}500 \text{ J/day} = 4.768 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to DC Pump Input Energy Assuming Overall Efficiency.**

For a solar DC submersible pump with MPPT controller, assume $\eta_{overall} = 0.45$ (pump × motor × MPPT combined).

$$E_{DC,input} = \frac{E_{day}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{4.768}{0.45} = 10.60 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Required PV Array Wattage.**

Kerala's average **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** ≈ 4.8 h/day (Munnar/Wayanad plateau).

$$P_{PV} = \frac{E_{DC,input}}{PSH} = \frac{10.60}{4.8} = 2.21 \text{ kWp}$$

**Step 4 — Select Standard PV Module Size and Count.**

Standard module: **335 Wp mono-crystalline.**
Number of modules: $n = \dfrac{2210}{335} = 6.6 \Rightarrow 7$ modules.

Total array power: $7 \times 335 = 2345 \text{ Wp} \approx 2.35 \text{ kWp}$.

**Step 5 — Final Selection (with 15 % safety margin for cloudy days).**

$$\boxed{P_{PV,\text{design}} = 2.35 \times 1.15 \approx 2.70 \text{ kWp}}$$

This is achievable with a **3 kWp solar array + 2 HP DC submersible pump** package, which is commercially available under the **PM-KUSUM** and **Kerala ANERT** subsidy schemes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always include a *safety margin* and *system derating factors* (temperature, dust, wiring loss) in solar-pump design. The KTU board examiner awards full marks only when the student explicitly accounts for **PSH, η_overall, and derating factor**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Choosing the Right Agricultural Pump

| Duty Requirement | Recommended Pump | Typical η | Cost Index | KTU Reference Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open well, head < 8 m, Q < 50 m³/h | Horizontal Centrifugal (Monobloc) | 55–70 % | Low | Standard KTU Module 1 |
| Borewell, head 30–100 m, Q 5–20 m³/h | Submersible (Multi-stage) | 60–75 % | Medium | KTU Module 1 — Deep-well |
| Canal / paddy field, head 1–5 m, Q > 500 m³/h | Axial-flow (Propeller) | 80–88 % | High initial, low OPEX | KTU Module 1 — Low-lift |
| Off-grid farm, head 20–60 m, Q 5–15 m³/h | Solar Submersible | 35–50 % (sys) | High (subsidy-aided) | KTU Module 1 — Solar |
| Mobile / rental farm service | Tractor PTO Pump | 60–70 % | Pay-per-use | KTU Module 1 — Mobile |
| Slurry / silt-laden water | Slurry / Dredge Pump | 50–65 % | Medium | KTU Module 1 — Special |


> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Note that for agricultural centrifugal pumps, the *foot valve* maintains priming, the *Non-Return Valve (NRV)* prevents back-flow when the pump stops (avoiding water hammer), and the *filter* protects the drippers/sprinklers from clogging — three details examiners love to test.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**"Differentiate between a centrifugal pump and a submersible pump with reference to their application in agriculture." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A **centrifugal pump** is a surface-mounted (monobloc) pump with a horizontal shaft and a single impeller enclosed in a volute casing; it can develop head up to about 30–50 m. It is used for **shallow open wells, river lift, and sprinkler systems**.

A **submersible pump** is a vertical, multi-stage pump lowered *inside* the borewell, hermetically sealed and water-lubricated. It can develop head from 30 m up to 300 m. It is used for **deep borewells, drip irrigation, and high-pressure sprinkler networks**.

The submersible pump has no priming problem (positive suction), is silent, and has a smaller land footprint, while the centrifugal pump is cheaper, easier to repair, and tolerant of siltier water.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"What is specific speed? How does it help in selecting the type of agricultural pump?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
**Specific speed ($N_s$)** is a dimensionless number that characterizes the geometry of an impeller:

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{0.75}}$$

where $N$ is in rpm, $Q$ in m³/s, and $H$ in m.

It is *the rpm at which a geometrically similar impeller would deliver 1 m³/s against 1 m head*. It classifies pumps:

- $N_s < 500$ → Radial-flow (high head, low flow) → **Centrifugal monobloc.**
- $N_s$ 500–4000 → Mixed-flow → **Mixed-flow pump.**
- $N_s > 4000$ → Axial-flow → **Propeller/axial pump** (paddy irrigation).

It thus guides the selection of the correct pump family for a given agricultural duty.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**"A centrifugal pump is required to lift water from an open well for a sprinkler irrigation system. The water level in the well is 4 m below the pump centreline. The sprinkler nozzles are 18 m above the pump. Suction pipe length = 6 m, delivery pipe length = 80 m, pipe diameter = 60 mm, Darcy friction factor $f = 0.024$, flow rate = 5 L/s. The pump efficiency is 70 % and motor efficiency is 88 %.**
**(a) Calculate the total dynamic head, water power, and electrical input power. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Discuss the selection criteria for choosing a centrifugal vs. submersible pump for this duty, and recommend with justification. (7 Marks)"**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Static heads:**
$H_s = +4$ m, $H_d = +18$ m.

**Step 2 — Velocity:**
$Q = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s, $A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.06)^2 = 2.827 \times 10^{-3}$ m².

$$v = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.827 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.768 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity head:**
$$\frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{(1.768)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.159 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Friction losses:**
$$H_{f,suc} = \frac{0.024 \times 6 \times (1.768)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.06} = \frac{0.024 \times 6 \times 3.126}{1.1772} = 0.382 \text{ m}$$

$$H_{f,del} = \frac{0.024 \times 80 \times 3.126}{1.1772} = 5.098 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Total dynamic head:**
$$H_{TDH} = 4 + 18 + 0.382 + 5.098 + 0.159 = 27.64 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Water power:**
$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 27.64 = 1355.7 \text{ W} \approx 1.36 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Electrical input:**
$$P_{elec} = \frac{1355.7}{0.70 \times 0.88} = 2200.5 \text{ W} \approx 2.20 \text{ kW}$$

A **3 HP (2.2 kW)** standard motor is selected.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Selection criteria** for centrifugal vs submersible include:

1. **Source depth:** Open well with 4 m drawdown → centrifugal feasible; borewell > 30 m → submersible preferred.
2. **Suction lift:** Centrifugal pumps have a practical suction-lift limit of ~6–7 m (NPSH constraint); 4 m here is fine.
3. **Total head:** 27.6 m is within the comfortable range of a single-stage centrifugal.
4. **Water quality:** Open well water is often sandy → centrifugal with open impeller is more tolerant.
5. **Capital & maintenance cost:** Centrifugal is 30–50 % cheaper and easily serviced by local mechanics.
6. **Footprint & noise:** Centrifugal occupies land and is noisier; submersible is silent but harder to retrieve.
7. **Energy source:** Grid power assumed here — both options feasible.

**Recommendation:** A **horizontal centrifugal monobloc pump** is recommended for this duty. The head is low (27.6 m), the source is shallow, the water may carry silt, and the cost advantage is significant. A submersible would be uneconomical for such low head, as its multi-stage design is optimized for high heads and its cost per metre of head is higher.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternative]**

**"A 2 HP solar submersible pump is proposed for irrigating a 0.4 ha coconut plantation in Thrissur district, Kerala. The total dynamic head is 40 m, the daily water requirement is 30 m³, and the average peak sun hours available are 4.5 h. The overall system efficiency is 42 %.**
**(a) Estimate the required PV array capacity in kWp and the number of standard 330 Wp modules needed. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Explain with a neat sketch the layout of a solar pumping system and discuss the role of the MPPT controller. (7 Marks)"**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Daily hydraulic energy:**
$$E_{hyd} = \rho g Q_{day} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 30 \times 40 = 11{,}772{,}000 \text{ J/day}$$

Converting: $E_{hyd} = 11.772 \text{ MJ/day} = 3.27 \text{ kWh/day}$.

**Step 2 — Required DC input energy:**
$$E_{DC} = \frac{E_{hyd}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{3.27}{0.42} = 7.79 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Required PV array:**
$$P_{PV} = \frac{E_{DC}}{PSH} = \frac{7.79}{4.5} = 1.73 \text{ kWp}$$

**Step 4 — Module count:**
$$n = \frac{1730}{330} = 5.24 \Rightarrow 6 \text{ modules (rounded up)}$$

Actual array: $6 \times 330 = 1980 \text{ Wp} \approx 1.98 \text{ kWp}$.

**Step 5 — Recommended system (with 10 % derating):**
$$P_{PV,design} = 1.98 \times 1.10 = 2.18 \text{ kWp} \Rightarrow \text{use 7 modules} = 2.31 \text{ kWp}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Layout description:**

1. **PV Array (2.31 kWp)** mounted on a fixed-tilt frame, south-facing, tilt angle ≈ 10° (Kerala latitude).
2. **DC cable** runs to the **MPPT + VFD Controller** (often called "solar pump controller"), mounted on a pole near the well.
3. The controller feeds a **3-phase AC submersible pump** (or DC pump, depending on design) via a waterproof cable.
4. The pump is **lowered inside the borewell**, supported by a safety rope and a GI/GMS pipe column.
5. **Discharge** is taken through a non-return valve to an **HDPE delivery line** to a **sand/mesh filter** and then to the **drip irrigation network** in the coconut field.
6. **Optional float switch / dry-run sensor** in the well provides feedback to the controller to protect the pump.

**Role of MPPT Controller:**
The **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** controller continuously varies the operating voltage of the PV array to extract the maximum available power under varying insolation (clouds, time of day, temperature). Without MPPT, the array would operate at a fixed voltage, losing 20–35 % of available energy. In solar pumps, the MPPT is often integrated with a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** to also adjust the pump motor speed to match available solar power, giving smooth ramp-up at sunrise and graceful cut-off at sunset — a feature essential for Kerala's variable monsoon-cloud conditions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the velocity head** in TDH calculation — loses 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Mixing up $H_s$ and $H_d$ signs** — students write $H_s = -4$ m and $H_d = -18$ m, causing cascading errors.
