# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cracking Pressure of a Direct-Acting Poppet Relief Valve

**Problem Setup:**
A direct-acting poppet pressure relief valve has a poppet diameter **d = 15 mm**, spring stiffness **k = 8 N/mm**, and pre-compression **x₀ = 4 mm**. The poppet seat angle is **45°** (so the effective pressure area is the projection on the seat plane). Determine the cracking pressure.

**Step 1 — Identify the force balance on the poppet at the instant of lift-off.**

The poppet is in equilibrium when the hydraulic force exactly equals the spring preload (the dead weight of the poppet is neglected).

$$F_{hydraulic} = F_{spring}$$

**Step 2 — Express the hydraulic force using the projected area on the seat plane.**

For a conical poppet with seat angle α, the projected area on which system pressure acts is:

$$A_{proj} = \pi \cdot d \cdot h$$

where **h = (d/2) · tan(α/2)** is the seating half-width. For α = 45°:

$$h = \frac{d}{2} \cdot \tan(22.5°) = \frac{15}{2} \cdot 0.4142 = 3.107 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$A_{proj} = \pi \cdot 15 \cdot 3.107 = 146.4 \text{ mm}^2 = 1.464 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

> **Logic note:** For a flat seat (α = 90°), the projected area reduces to the simple disc area πd²/4. We use the projected-area formulation here because KTU problems often give seat angles.

**Step 3 — Express the spring force at the cracking instant.**

The spring is pre-compressed by x₀ before any pressure acts. The cracking occurs when pressure just starts to lift the poppet, so the additional compression from poppet motion is essentially zero (lift-off condition).

$$F_{spring} = k \cdot x_0 = 8 \text{ N/mm} \cdot 4 \text{ mm} = 32 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the cracking pressure.**

$$p_{cr} = \frac{F_{spring}}{A_{proj}} = \frac{32}{1.464 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.186 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{p_{cr} \approx 2.19 \text{ bar}}$$

**Step 5 — Pressure Override Estimation.**

If at rated pump flow Q = 30 L/min, the poppet lifts by an additional **x_add = 1 mm** against the spring, the additional spring force is **8 × 1 = 8 N**, raising the pressure to:

$$p_{full} = \frac{32 + 8}{1.464 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.73 \text{ bar}$$

$$\Delta p = p_{full} - p_{cr} = 0.54 \text{ bar} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Delta p}{p_{cr}} \approx 24.7\%$$

> This is high — confirming that **direct-acting valves are inaccurate** and pilot-operated designs are preferred for precise regulation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Accumulator Precharge Sizing for Pressure Regulation

**Problem Setup:**
A hydraulic accumulator (nitrogen pre-charged) is to be used in a pressure-regulating circuit. The maximum working pressure is **p₂ = 200 bar**, minimum working pressure is **p₁ = 140 bar**, and the gas precharge pressure is **p₀ = 100 bar**. Find the usable oil volume per litre of accumulator total volume, and the energy stored.

**Step 1 — Apply Boyle's Law for the two gas states.**

At precharge (state 0): gas occupies the full accumulator volume V₀ at pressure p₀.
At minimum working pressure (state 1): gas has expanded and oil has entered; gas volume = V₁.

$$p_0 \cdot V_0 = p_1 \cdot V_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{p_0}{p_1} \cdot V_0 = \frac{100}{140} \cdot V_0 = 0.7143 \cdot V_0$$

**Step 2 — At maximum working pressure (state 2).**

$$p_0 \cdot V_0 = p_2 \cdot V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2 = \frac{p_0}{p_2} \cdot V_0 = \frac{100}{200} \cdot V_0 = 0.5 \cdot V_0$$

**Step 3 — Usable oil volume per litre of V₀.**

$$\Delta V = V_1 - V_2 = (0.7143 - 0.5) \cdot V_0 = 0.2143 \cdot V_0$$

For V₀ = 1 L:

$$\Delta V = 0.2143 \text{ L} \approx 214 \text{ mL}$$

> **Logic note:** This is why accumulators used in pressure regulation are often oversized in physical volume relative to their effective output volume.

**Step 4 — Hydraulic energy stored in the accumulator.**

The general energy formula for a gas-charged accumulator delivering from p₁ to p₂ is:

$$E = p_1 (V_1 - V_2) - \int_{V_2}^{V_1} p \, dV$$

With the gas following Boyle's law (pV = constant = p₀V₀):

$$\int_{V_2}^{V_1} p \, dV = p_0 V_0 \int_{V_2}^{V_1} \frac{dV}{V} = p_0 V_0 \ln\!\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$$

So the energy is:

$$E = p_0 V_0 \left[ \frac{p_1}{p_0} - \frac{p_1}{p_0} \cdot \frac{V_2}{V_0} - \ln\!\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) \right]$$

Substituting values for V₀ = 1 L = 10⁻³ m³, p₀ = 100 × 10⁵ Pa, p₁ = 140 × 10⁵ Pa:

$$E = (100 \times 10^5)(10^{-3})\left[1.4 - 1.4(0.5) - \ln\!\left(\frac{0.7143}{0.5}\right)\right]$$

$$E = 10^4 \left[1.4 - 0.7 - \ln(1.4286)\right] = 10^4 \left[0.7 - 0.3567\right]$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 3433 \text{ J} = 3.43 \text{ kJ per litre of accumulator}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Counterbalance Valve Setting for a Vertical Load

**Problem Setup:**
A hydraulic cylinder of piston diameter **D = 80 mm**, rod diameter **d = 45 mm**, supports a vertical load of mass **m = 1500 kg**. Determine the counterbalance valve setting (ignoring friction).

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant piston area.**

The counterbalance valve is on the **cap (blank) end return line** in a load-lowering application. The area on which the load pushes the oil out is the **cap-end area**:

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the pressure required to hold the load (no motion).**

$$p_{hold} = \frac{m \cdot g}{A_{cap}} = \frac{1500 \times 9.81}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.927 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{p_{hold} \approx 29.27 \text{ bar}}$$

**Step 3 — Add a margin (typically 10–15%) to ensure positive load retention and account for dynamic effects.**

$$p_{set} = 1.15 \cdot p_{hold} = 33.66 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the load-holding force and check the rod-side (annulus) area for full pressure equilibrium.**

$$A_{annulus} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.080^2 - 0.045^2) = 3.436 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

If the rod side is pressurised to a working pressure **p_w = 200 bar** to lift the load:

$$F_{lift} = p_w \cdot A_{annulus} - \frac{m \cdot g}{1} = 200 \times 10^5 \times 3.436 \times 10^{-3} - 1500 \times 9.81$$

$$F_{lift} = 68723 - 14715 = 54008 \text{ N} \approx 54 \text{ kN}$$

This confirms the cylinder can lift the load against gravity.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Direct-Acting PRV | Pilot-Operated PRV | Pressure Reducing Valve | Counterbalance Valve |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Cracking accuracy | Low | High | High | High |
| Pressure override Δp | 10–20% | 2–5% | 3–8% | 5–10% |
| Response time | Fast (ms) | Slower (10s of ms) | Medium | Medium |
| Cost | Low | Medium–High | Medium | Medium |
| Flow capacity (at Δp) | Low–Medium | High | Medium | Medium |
| Typical application | Low-pressure safety | Main system relief | Branch circuit clamp | Vertical load control |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Pressure Regulation Sub-System

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **interactions and signal flow** within a typical pressure regulation sub-system, mapping how the pump, relief valve, pressure compensator, and accumulator interact logically.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Reservoir Tank] --> nodeB[Variable Displacement Pump]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Pump Outlet Manifold]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Pressure Relief Valve}
    nodeD -- Excess Flow --> nodeA
    nodeC --> nodeE[Pressure Compensator LS]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Load Sensing Signal Line]
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeG[Pressure Reducing Valve]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Low Pressure Branch Circuit]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Sequence Valve]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Secondary Actuator]
    nodeC --> nodeK[Accumulator]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Check Valve]
    nodeL --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeM[System Pressure Gauge]
    nodeC --> nodeN[Primary Actuator Cylinder]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Counterbalance Valve]
    nodeO --> nodeA
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **pump** feeds the manifold at full displacement.
- The **relief valve** (node D) is a safety branch — it only opens on overpressure.
- The **pressure compensator** (node E) modulates pump swash plate via the **LS signal** (node F), achieving **constant-pressure** delivery.
- The **reducing valve** (node G) supplies the **low-pressure branch** (node H) — useful for pilot lines and clamping circuits.
- The **sequence valve** (node I) ensures the **secondary actuator** (node J) is only pressurised after the primary has completed its stroke.
- The **accumulator** (node K) absorbs shocks and maintains pressure during peak demand.
- The **counterbalance valve** (node O) controls the **vertical actuator** (node N) on the return side.

## 4.2 State Machine — Pressure Regulation Logic

The valve behaviour as a function of system pressure is captured in the following Mermaid state diagram.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateClosed
    StateClosed : Valve Closed No Flow to Tank
    StateClosed --> StateCracking : Pressure reaches p_cr
    StateCracking : Valve Begins to Lift Minimal Leakage
    StateCracking --> StateFullFlow : Pressure reaches p_full
    StateFullFlow : Valve Fully Open Excess Flow to Tank
    StateFullFlow --> StateCracking : Pressure drops below p_full
    StateCracking --> StateClosed : Pressure drops below p_cr
    StateClosed --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **hysteresis** between the cracking and reseating pressures is called **blow-down** or **reset** — a critical specification in KTU questions on relief valve selection.

## 4.3 Schematic — Pilot-Operated Pressure Relief Valve (Two-Stage)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInlet[System Pressure Inlet P] --> nodeMainSeat[Main Poppet Seat]
    nodeMainSeat --> nodeMainPop[Main Poppet]
    nodeMainPop --> nodeTankOut[Tank Return T]
    nodeDrain[Drain Port] --> nodeMainPop
    nodePilot[Pilot Orifice] --> nodeMainSeat
    nodeMainPop --> nodePilot
    nodePilot --> nodePilotSpring[Pilot Spring Chamber]
    nodePilotSpring --> nodePilotAdjust[Adjustment Screw]
    nodeMainPop --> nodeSpringMain[Main Spring Chamber]
```

**Functional interpretation:**

- High pressure enters at P and acts on the **bottom of the main poppet** (nodeMainPop).
- A small flow is bled through the **pilot orifice** (nodePilot) into the spring chamber, creating a small pressure drop.
- When the spring chamber pressure exceeds the pilot spring setting, the **pilot opens**, dumping the spring-chamber oil to tank.
- This sudden pressure drop at the top of the main poppet unbalances it, and the **main poppet lifts fully**.
- Adjustment screw (nodePilotAdjust) varies the pilot spring force → varies the system set pressure.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define Cracking Pressure and Pressure Override in a Pressure Relief Valve. Why is a low override desirable? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Cracking Pressure (p_cr):** It is the inlet pressure at which the pressure relief valve begins to open and a measurable flow of fluid starts to pass to the tank. It is determined by the spring preload acting on the poppet: **p_cr = F_spring / A_poppet**.

**Pressure Override (Δp):** It is the difference between the **full-flow pressure (p_full)** and the **cracking pressure (p_cr)**: **Δp = p_full − p_cr**. It represents the pressure rise above the set value required to pass the full rated pump flow through the relief valve.

**Why low override is desirable:** A low override means the system pressure is **closely regulated** to the set value even when excess flow is being dumped. This (i) protects downstream components from pressure surges, (ii) reduces energy loss as heat across the valve, and (iii) provides consistent actuator performance. Pilot-operated relief valves achieve Δp as low as **2–5%** of set pressure, whereas direct-acting valves may show **10–20%**.

---

### Question 2 — Differentiate between a Pressure Relief Valve and a Pressure Reducing Valve. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Pressure Relief Valve | Pressure Reducing Valve |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Function** | Limits **maximum** system pressure (safety) | Maintains **lower, constant** pressure in a branch circuit |
| **Position in circuit** | Across pump (return to tank) | In series, at inlet of secondary circuit |
| **Normal state** | Closed; opens only on overpressure | Open; throttles to control downstream |
| **Senses pressure from** | Upstream (system) | Downstream (regulated branch) |
| **Energy role** | Dissipates excess energy as heat | Drops pressure across a restriction |
| **Analogy** | Pressure cooker safety valve | Household tap with pressure regulator |
| **Failure mode if stuck closed** | System bursts / pump overload | No effect on main circuit; branch fails |
| **Failure mode if stuck open** | Pump flow lost, no pressure | Main circuit pressure drops to branch setting |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Set 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **pilot-operated pressure relief valve**. Compare its performance with a direct-acting relief valve in terms of pressure override and flow capacity.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (refer to Section 4.3 diagram):**

A pilot-operated relief valve consists of **two stages**:

1. **Main stage:** A large poppet (or spool) held on its seat by system pressure acting underneath and a small spring/drain pressure acting above. The main poppet controls the bulk flow from pump to tank.
2. **Pilot stage:** A small, adjustable, spring-loaded poppet screwed into the top of the main poppet. It bleeds a small pilot flow from the spring chamber to the tank drain.

**Working:**

1. At rest, system pressure p ≤ p_set, the main poppet stays closed. The spring chamber above the main poppet is pressurised through the **pilot orifice** (a small drilled hole in the main poppet) to nearly the same pressure as the inlet.
2. When pump pressure exceeds the **pilot spring setting**, the small pilot poppet lifts. Oil from the spring chamber escapes to drain.
3. A **pressure differential** develops across the main poppet: high pressure underneath, low pressure above. The main poppet lifts, opening a large flow passage to tank.
4. As excess flow is dumped, pump pressure stabilises near the set value. The pilot modulates the main poppet position to maintain this pressure — hence "modulating" or "pilot-operated" control.
5. The **adjustment screw** on the pilot sets the system relief pressure.

**Comparison Table (must appear in the answer):**

| Parameter | Direct-Acting | Pilot-Operated |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Pressure override | 10–20% | 2–5% |
| Flow handling | Limited by poppet size | Large, as main stage handles flow |
| Cost & complexity | Low | Higher (two stages) |
| Sensitivity to contamination | High (small orifices) | Moderate |
| Hysteresis (blow-down) | Higher | Lower |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A hydraulic system requires a flow of 25 L/min at a regulated pressure of 180 bar. The pump is fixed displacement delivering 30 L/min at 250 bar max. Design a pilot-operated relief valve for this duty. Assume the main poppet area is 600 mm² and the pilot area ratio τ = A_pilot/A_main = 0.04. Calculate the required main-stage spring force at cracking.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Required relief flow.**

The relief valve must pass the **excess flow** not used by the actuators:

$$Q_{relief} = Q_{pump} - Q_{load} = 30 - 25 = 5 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 2 — Cracking pressure = set pressure for an ideal pilot-operated valve.**

$$p_{cr} \approx p_{set} = 180 \text{ bar} = 180 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Main-stage spring force at cracking (using the small-area approximation).**

The pilot-operated main poppet cracking is determined by the pilot setting; however, the main spring force needed to keep the poppet seated when no flow is passing is:

$$F_{main} = p_{cr} \cdot A_{main} \cdot (1 - \tau)$$

where τ is the area ratio. Substituting:

$$F_{main} = (180 \times 10^5) \times (600 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1 - 0.04)$$

$$F_{main} = 180 \times 10^5 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.96$$

$$F_{main} = 10.368 \times 10^3 \text{ N} = 10368 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Estimated full-flow pressure using the override formula.**

$$p_{full} = p_{set} \cdot (1 + \tau) = 180 \times 1.04 = 187.2 \text{ bar}$$

$$\Delta p = 7.2 \text{ bar} \quad (\text{about } 4\% \text{ of set pressure — confirms pilot-operated accuracy})$$

**Step 5 — Hydraulic power dissipated at full relief flow (for thermal design).**

$$P_{heat} = Q_{relief} \cdot p_{full} = \left(\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{60}\right) \times (187.2 \times 10^5) = 1560 \text{ W} \approx 1.56 \text{ kW}$$

The reservoir and cooler must dissipate this continuously during relief.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently **omit the area ratio term (1 − τ)** in the spring-force calculation for a pilot-operated valve, or **confuse the area ratio with the full-flow override factor**. Both are valid uses of τ, but the **cracking spring force** uses **(1 − τ)** while the **full-flow pressure** uses **(1 + τ)**. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.

---

### Question B — Set 2 (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 2]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Explain the working of an **accumulator-based pressure regulation system**. Derive the usable oil volume and stored energy for an accumulator with V₀ = 5 L, precharge p₀ = 80 bar, working range 120–180 bar.

**Model Solution:**

**Working principle:**

A gas-charged (usually nitrogen) accumulator stores hydraulic energy in the form of **compressed gas**. It is connected to the hydraulic line via a check valve or directly. Its functions in pressure regulation are:

1. **Pressure maintenance** during pump-off or leak conditions.
2. **Shock absorption** (pulsation damping).
3. **Emergency power** supply for short-duration actuator strokes.
4. **Compensation** for thermal expansion of fluid.

When system pressure rises above precharge + fluid head, the **gas compresses** and oil enters. When system pressure falls, the gas **expands** and expels oil, maintaining pressure.

**Step 1 — Boyle's law at minimum working pressure.**

$$p_0 V_0 = p_1 V_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{p_0}{p_1} V_0 = \frac{80}{120} \times 5 = 3.333 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2 — Boyle's law at maximum working pressure.**

$$V_2 = \frac{p_0}{p_2} V_0 = \frac{80}{180} \times 5 = 2.222 \text{ L}$$

**Step 3 — Usable oil volume.**

$$\Delta V = V_1 - V_2 = 3.333 - 2.222 = 1.111 \text{ L}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V \approx 1.11 \text{ L (usable per 5 L of physical volume)}}$$

**Step 4 — Stored hydraulic energy.**

Using the integral formulation derived in Section 3.2:

$$E = p_0 V_0 \left[\frac{p_1}{p_0} - \frac{p_1 V_2}{p_0 V_0} - \ln\!\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)\right]$$

With p₀ = 80 × 10⁵ Pa, V₀ = 5 × 10⁻³ m³, p₁ = 120 × 10⁵ Pa:

$$E = (80 \times 10^5)(5 \times 10^{-3})\left[\frac{120}{80} - \frac{120 \times 2.222}{80 \times 5} - \ln\!\left(\frac{3.333}{2.222}\right)\right]$$

$$E = 4 \times 10^4 \left[1.5 - 0.6667 - \ln(1.5)\right]$$

$$E = 4 \times 10^4 \left[1.5 - 0.6667 - 0.4055\right]$$

$$E = 4 \times 10^4 \times 0.4278 = 17112 \text{ J} \approx 17.1 \text{ kJ}$$

> **Practical interpretation:** This 5 L accumulator can drive a small actuator (e.g., 50 cm³ volume at 100 bar) through about **22 strokes** between 120 and 180 bar, or supply **17 kJ** of useful work — enough for a short emergency operation.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A vertical hydraulic cylinder of bore 100 mm is to support a load of 2500 kg using a counterbalance valve on the cap-end return line. If the supply pressure to lift the load is 150 bar on the rod side (rod diameter 60 mm), calculate (i) the counterbalance valve setting to hold the load statically, (ii) the lifting force, and (iii) the margin of safety.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cap-end (piston) area.**

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.100)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Counterbalance valve setting (static).**

$$p_{set} = \frac{m \cdot g}{A_{cap}} = \frac{2500 \times 9.81}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.122 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{p_{set} \approx 31.2 \text{ bar}}$$

**Step 3 — Annulus (rod-side) area.**

$$A_{annulus} = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.100^2 - 0.060^2)}{4} = 5.026 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Total lifting force at 150 bar on rod side.**

The load opposes the lift, so the net lifting force is:

$$F_{lift} = p_{supply} \cdot A_{annulus} - m \cdot g$$

$$F_{lift} = (150 \times 10^5)(5.026 \times 10^{-3}) - 2500 \times 9.81$$

$$F_{lift} = 75390 - 24525 = 50865 \text{ N} \approx 50.87 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Margin of safety (as percentage of load weight).**

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{F_{lift} - m g}{m g} \times 100\% = \frac{50865 - 24525}{24525} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Margin} = 107.4\%$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The supply pressure provides more than **double** the force needed to hold the load — a healthy margin ensuring the cylinder can lift even under friction and minor back-pressure losses.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** For counterbalance questions, students often **use the rod-side (annulus) area instead of the cap-end area** when computing the static hold pressure. Always remember: **the load is being lowered out of the cap end**, so the **cap-end area** determines the back-pressure needed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Pressure Regulation:**

- ⭐ **Cracking pressure (p_cr)** is the threshold where the relief valve begins to open; set by **spring preload**.
- ⭐ **Pressure override (Δp)** = p_full − p_cr; **pilot-operated valves** minimise it (2–5%) versus direct-acting (10–20%).
- ⭐ **Blow-down / Reset** is the difference between cracking and reseating pressures — keep low for stability.
- ⭐ A **pressure reducing valve** is in **series** (throttles); a **relief valve** is in **parallel** (vents excess to tank).
- ⭐ **Sequence valves** activate a secondary circuit at a preset pressure — used for staged operations.
- ⭐ **Unloading valves** dump full pump flow to tank at low pressure during idle — saves energy and heat.
- ⭐ **Counterbalance valves** are placed in the **return line** of vertical actuators; setting = **m·g / A_cap** plus margin.
- ⭐ **Accumulators** in regulation follow **Boyle's law** (p₀V₀ = p₁V₁); precharge must be **less than minimum working pressure** (typically 80–90% of p_min).
- ⭐ **Hydraulic power dissipated** at the relief valve: **P_heat = Q_relief × p_full** — critical for thermal design.
- ⭐ Standard **KPI (regulation quality metric)**: percentage override; standard **KPI 2**: response time (dither, hysteresis).
- ⭐ Always use **cap-end area** for counterbalance setting and **annulus area** for lifting force calculations.
- ⭐ The **two-stage pilot-operated relief valve** is the workhorse of industrial hydraulics — know its construction intimately for KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pump Maintenance and Troubleshooting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pump Maintenance and Troubleshooting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pump Maintenance — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pump Maintenance** is the systematic, planned, and engineered set of activities — including inspection, servicing, testing, alignment, lubrication, and component replacement — performed on a pumping system to ensure it continues to operate at its design point of **flow (Q)**, **head (H)**, **efficiency (η)**, and **Net Positive Suction Head available (NPSHₐ)** without unscheduled downtime or catastrophic failure.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** outcome-based framework, pump maintenance is directly tied to **CO3 (Maintainability & Reliability)** and bridges into **plant availability, life-cycle costing, and energy efficiency**. The KTU syllabus frames maintenance under four primary paradigms:

1. **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** — Time-based or usage-based interventions performed *before* failure occurs.
2. **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** — Condition-based interventions triggered by real-time monitoring data (vibration, temperature, acoustic emission, oil analysis).
3. **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** — Repairs carried out *after* a fault is detected, but *before* complete failure.
4. **Breakdown Maintenance (BM)** — Reactive repair only after the equipment has failed (the most expensive strategy).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **HiP (Hi-Progressive) Strategy** — a combination of Preventive + Predictive — is the gold standard endorsed by API 610 and Hydraulic Institute standards, and is the expected KTU answer for "best practice" questions.

## 1.2 Pump Troubleshooting — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Troubleshooting** is the logical, evidence-based diagnostic methodology used to identify the **root cause** of a deviation between the pump's *actual* performance and its *rated* performance, by systematically isolating variables (suction side, discharge side, mechanical, hydraulic, electrical) using flowcharts, vibration spectra, pressure gauges, and ampere readings.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚗➡️🌀

Think of a **centrifugal pump** exactly like a **car's air-conditioning compressor driven by the engine belt**:

| Car AC Analogy | Pump Equivalent |
|---|---|
| AC blows warm air (poor cooling) | Pump delivers low flow/pressure |
| Strange whining noise from engine bay | Pump emits abnormal noise/vibration |
| Refrigerant gas leaking (HFC) | Mechanical seal leakage |
| Radiator coolant level drops | Oil leakage from bearings |
| Belt slipping (squeal) | Coupling misalignment / slippage |
| Engine RPM drops under load | Motor overload / bearing seizure |
| Fuel filter clogged | Strainer / suction line clogged |
| Thermostat stuck open | Impeller worn / casing eroded |
| Annual service (oil change, filter) | Preventive Maintenance schedule |

> Just as a car owner would never wait for the engine to seize before changing the engine oil, a process engineer should never wait for a pump to fail before scheduling maintenance. **Troubleshooting is the diagnostic interview; maintenance is the preventive healthcare.**

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations

- **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) — measured in **hours**
- **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) — measured in **hours**
- **Availability (A)** — dimensionless ratio (typically **95–99 %** for critical pumps)
- **Vibration velocity (Vᵣₘₛ)** — measured in **mm/s**
- **NPSH margin** — minimum **1.0 m** or **10 %** of NPSHₐ (API 610 standard)
- **L₁₀ bearing life** — minimum **40,000 hours** for industrial pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Maintenance Strategy

### 2.1.1 Preventive Maintenance (PM)
- **Trigger:** Time interval (e.g., every 3,000 hours) or calendar (e.g., every 6 months).
- **Activities:** Lubrication, alignment check, vibration survey, bearing inspection, seal replacement, impeller clearance check.
- **Strength:** Simple, easy to plan, predictable cost.
- **Weakness:** May replace components that still have useful life (over-maintenance).

### 2.1.2 Predictive Maintenance (PdM)
- **Trigger:** Condition-based threshold (e.g., vibration > 4.5 mm/s RMS).
- **Technologies:** Vibration analysis (FFT spectrum), infrared thermography, ultrasonic testing, oil/ferrography, motor current signature analysis (MCSA), acoustic emission.
- **Strength:** Minimizes unplanned shutdown, optimizes spare parts inventory.
- **Weakness:** Higher capital cost for sensors and software.

### 2.1.3 Corrective Maintenance (CM)
- **Trigger:** Performance deviation observed but unit not yet failed.
- **Example:** Slight drop in flow detected → scheduled impeller refurbishment during next planned shutdown.

### 2.1.4 Breakdown Maintenance (BM)
- **Trigger:** Catastrophic failure.
- **Cost Impact:** Highest — typically **3–10× the cost of preventive maintenance** (industry-standard Sinus Hexagon reliability data).

## 2.2 Reliability & Availability Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three equations appear in almost every KTU 2024 module-end numerical problem. **Memorize the formula sheet below.**

### 2.2.1 Mean Time Between Failures

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Cumulative Operating Time (hours)}}{\text{Number of Failures}}
$$

### 2.2.2 Mean Time To Repair

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Cumulative Repair Downtime (hours)}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}
$$

### 2.2.3 Steady-State Availability

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Critical: The vertical bar `|` symbol has been replaced with `\vert` to maintain markdown table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$ | MTBF, MTTR in **hours** | Critical pumps: A ≥ **95 %** |
| 2 | L₁₀ Bearing Life (ball) | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} \times 10^{6}$ rev | C = dynamic load (N), P = equivalent load (N) | ISO 281, p = **3** |
| 3 | L₁₀ Bearing Life (roller) | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} \times 10^{6}$ rev | Same as above | p = **10/3** |
| 4 | Bearing Life in Hours | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10}}{60 \cdot N}$ | N = rotational speed (rpm) | Min **40,000 h** |
| 5 | Affinity — Flow | $\dfrac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}} = \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}$ | Q in m³/s, N in rpm | Constant impeller diameter |
| 6 | Affinity — Head | $\dfrac{H_{2}}{H_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{2}$ | H in metres | Quadratic with speed |
| 7 | Affinity — Power | $\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{3}$ | P in kW | Cubic — very sensitive |
| 8 | NPSH Margin | $\text{NPSH}_{a} \geq \text{NPSH}_{r} + \text{Margin}$ | All in metres | API 610: Margin ≥ **1.0 m** |
| 9 | Specific Speed | $N_{s} = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{0.75}}$ | N in rpm, Q in m³/s, H in m | Pump type selection |
| 10 | Vibration RMS Velocity | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{\pi f A_{pk}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | f = Hz, Aₚₖ = displacement (mm) | ISO 10816-3 Class I |
| 11 | Cost Ratio (BM vs PM) | $C_{BM} \approx 3 \text{ to } 10 \times C_{PM}$ | Industrial benchmark | — |
| 12 | Best Efficiency Point Shift | $\eta \downarrow$ when $Q \neq Q_{BEP}$ | Off-design operation | Operate within **70–120 %** of BEP |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic toys — they underpin:

- **Refineries & Petrochemical plants** (API 610 compliance)
- **Power plant boiler feed pumps** (NPSH margin and L₁₀ life are contractually binding)
- **Municipal water supply** (MTBF drives O&M budgets for Kerala Water Authority)
- **HVAC and chilled water systems** (predictive vibration monitoring via IoT sensors)
- **Marine & offshore ballast pumps** (where BM is impossible at sea — PM is mandatory)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: L₁₀ Bearing Life Equation

### 3.1.1 Origin
The Lundberg–Palmgren fatigue theory (1947) states that bearing fatigue life is inversely proportional to the **third power of the load** for ball bearings, because stress at the contact ellipse is proportional to load and fatigue cycles follow a Weibull distribution with shape parameter **e = 10/9**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Step 1 — Define basic dynamic load rating C as the load at which **90 %** of a group of identical bearings reach **1 million revolutions** before fatigue.

Step 2 — Empirical Weibull scaling gives the relationship between load P and life L:

$$
L \propto \frac{1}{P^{p}}
$$

where the exponent **p = 3** for ball bearings and **p = 10/3** for roller bearings.

Step 3 — Normalizing to 1 million revolutions at load C:

$$
\frac{L}{10^{6}} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}
$$

Step 4 — Therefore the L₁₀ life in revolutions is:

$$
L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{3} \times 10^{6} \quad \text{(ball bearings)}
$$

Step 5 — Convert to hours using rotational speed N in rpm:

$$
L_{10h} = \frac{L_{10}}{60 \cdot N}
$$

> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
> A 6310 deep-groove ball bearing has C = 61 kN. Equivalent radial load P = 25 kN. Speed N = 2950 rpm. Find L₁₀ in hours.

Substitute the values:

$$
L_{10} = \left(\frac{61{,}000}{25{,}000}\right)^{3} \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
= (2.44)^{3} \times 10^{6} = 14.525 \times 10^{6} \text{ revolutions}
$$

Convert to hours:

$$
L_{10h} = \frac{14.525 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 2950} = \frac{14.525 \times 10^{6}}{177{,}000}
$$

$$
L_{10h} = 82{,}062 \text{ hours} \quad (\approx 9.4 \text{ years continuous duty})
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Availability vs Unreliability

### 3.2.1 Single-Component Series Model

For a pump with **n** independent failure modes in series, the system reliability is:

$$
R_{sys}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{i}(t)
$$

where $R_{i}(t) = e^{-\lambda_{i} t}$ is the exponential reliability of each sub-assembly (bearings, seals, coupling, motor).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Step 1 — Total hazard rate for series system:

$$
\lambda_{sys} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_{i}
$$

Step 2 — System reliability:

$$
R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda_{sys} \cdot t}
$$

Step 3 — For a constant hazard rate, MTBF is the inverse:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{sys}}
$$

Step 4 — Availability including MTTR:

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

> **Worked Numerical Example:**
> Bearing failure rate λ₁ = 5 × 10⁻⁵ /h, seal failure rate λ₂ = 8 × 10⁻⁵ /h, coupling failure rate λ₃ = 1.5 × 10⁻⁵ /h. Average MTTR = 12 hours. Find system MTBF and availability.

Step 1 — Total hazard rate:

$$
\lambda_{sys} = (5 + 8 + 1.5) \times 10^{-5} = 14.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /h}
$$

Step 2 — MTBF:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{14.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 68{,}965 \text{ hours}
$$

Step 3 — Availability:

$$
A = \frac{68{,}965}{68{,}965 + 12} = 0.99983 = 99.98\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This high availability figure is **misleading in isolation** — students should comment that the result is valid only when all three sub-systems follow exponential reliability and the MTTR of 12 h is itself consistent.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Affinity Law for Speed Reduction (Energy Saving)

### 3.3.1 Why It Matters
Throttling a discharge valve wastes energy. Reducing the impeller speed by **20 %** saves:

$$
\text{Power saved} = 1 - (0.8)^{3} = 1 - 0.512 = 0.488 = 48.8\%
$$

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Step 1 — Original operating point: N₁ = 2950 rpm, Q₁ = 100 m³/h, H₁ = 50 m, P₁ = 25 kW.

Step 2 — Reduce speed to N₂ = 2360 rpm (80 % of N₁):

$$
\frac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}} = \frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} = 0.8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{2} = 80 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/h}
$$

$$
\frac{H_{2}}{H_{1}} = (0.8)^{2} = 0.64 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_{2} = 32 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = (0.8)^{3} = 0.512 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{2} = 12.8 \text{ kW}
$$

Step 3 — Annual energy saving (assuming 8000 h/year, electricity ₹8/kWh in India):

$$
\text{Annual Saving} = (P_{1} - P_{2}) \times 8000 \times 8
$$

$$
= (25 - 12.8) \times 8000 \times 8 = ₹7,82,080 \text{ per year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the standard KTU 14-mark problem pattern: speed change → affinity law → energy/cost analysis. **Always comment on whether cavitation risk increases** when reducing speed (it usually decreases because NPSHₐ becomes a larger fraction of NPSHᵣ).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Troubles → Root Causes → Remedies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the single most important revision table for KTU Module 3.** Memorize the **Symptom → Probable Cause → Remedy** triplet structure.

| # | Symptom | Probable Causes | Recommended Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **No discharge flow** | (a) Pump not primed (b) Suction line air leak (c) Foot valve stuck (d) Wrong rotation | Prime, leak test, reverse motor phase |
| 2 | **Insufficient flow** | (a) Suction strainer partial block (b) Impeller wear ring clearance (c) Low RPM (V-belt slip) (d) NPSHₐ too low | Clean strainer, restore clearances, re-tension belts, raise suction head |
| 3 | **Insufficient pressure** | (a) Worn impeller (b) Air entrainment (c) Speed too low (d) Wrong diameter impeller | Replace/trim impeller, inspect seals |
| 4 | **Loss of suction after running** | (a) Vapor binding / cavitation (b) Hot liquid vaporization (c) Suction line blockage | Increase NPSHₐ, cool liquid, clean line |
| 5 | **Excessive power consumption** | (a) Head lower than design → flow too high (b) Mechanical binding (c) Misalignment (d) Specific gravity higher than rated | Trim impeller, re-align, check fluid SG |
| 6 | **Overheating of bearings** | (a) Under/over lubrication (b) Misalignment (c) Bearing failure (d) Cooling failure | Re-lubricate, laser align, replace bearing |
| 7 | **Mechanical seal leakage** | (a) Dry running (b) Seal face scoring (c) O-ring chemical attack (d) Shaft runout | Ensure prime, replace seal, check elastomer compatibility |
| 8 | **Excessive noise & vibration** | (a) Cavitation (b) Misalignment (c) Unbalance (d) Resonance (e) Foundation looseness | Increase NPSH margin, balance, re-align, grout baseplate |
| 9 | **Shaft deflection / coupling wear** | (a) Pipe strain on nozzles (b) Soft foot condition (c) Coupling misalignment | Re-pipe with proper supports, shim, realign |
| 10 | **Short bearing life** | (a) Contamination (b) Overload (c) Poor lubrication (d) Wrong installation | Clean environment, check load, install per SKF/FAG manual |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Comprehensive Pump Troubleshooting Flowchart

> [!NOTE]
> The mermaid diagram below is a fully syntactically-safe architecture mapping the diagnostic decision tree from the most generic symptom down to root cause isolation. Node IDs are alphanumeric prefixes (`n1`, `n2`, …) to comply with mermaid parser rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1([START: Abnormal Pump Behavior Detected]) --> n2{Does pump deliver any flow at all?}
    n2 -- NO --> n3{Pump is primed?}
    n3 -- NO --> n4[Action: Prime the pump and re-try]
    n3 -- YES --> n5{Suction line air-tight?}
    n5 -- NO --> n6[Action: Soap-bubble test all joints, retighten]
    n5 -- YES --> n7{Motor rotation correct CW/CCW?}
    n7 -- NO --> n8[Action: Reverse any two phases of 3-phase supply]
    n7 -- YES --> n9[Action: Check impeller key and obstruction inside casing]
    n2 -- YES --> n10{Flow is significantly below rated Q?}
    n10 -- YES --> n11{Suction pressure gauge reading normal?}
    n11 -- NO --> n12[Action: Clean strainer, verify foot valve, check NPSHa]
    n11 -- YES --> n13[Action: Inspect impeller wear rings, measure clearance]
    n10 -- NO --> n14{Pressure is below rated H?}
    n14 -- YES --> n15[Action: Compare actual speed to nameplate, check V-belt tension]
    n14 -- NO --> n16{Excessive vibration and noise?}
    n16 -- YES --> n17[Action: Vibration spectrum - check 1x RPM unbalance, 2x misalignment, HF cavitation]
    n16 -- NO --> n18[Action: Check motor ampere - is P2 greater than rated?]
    n18 -- YES --> n19[Action: Verify SG of pumped fluid, check for system head below curve]
    n18 -- NO --> n20[Action: Routine log and trending - no immediate fault]
    n20 --> n21([END: Document and resume monitoring])
    n19 --> n21
    n17 --> n21
    n15 --> n21
    n13 --> n21
    n12 --> n21
    n9 --> n21
    n8 --> n21
    n6 --> n21
    n4 --> n21
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Preventive Maintenance Time-Based Schedule (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Daily Checklist] --> B[Weekly Checks]
    B --> C[Monthly Inspection]
    C --> D[Quarterly Vibration Survey]
    D --> E[Semi-Annual Overhaul]
    E --> F[Annual Major Service]
    F --> G[3-5 Year Refurbishment]

    A1[Oil level, leak check, gauge reading] -.-> A
    B1[Grease replenishment, temperature log] -.-> B
    C1[Alignment re-check, belt tension, coupling wear] -.-> C
    D1[FFT vibration analysis at bearings and casing] -.-> D
    E1[Bearing replacement if L10h approaches end, seal inspection] -.-> E
    F1[Impeller clearance, wear ring measurement, rotor balance] -.-> F
    G1[Complete strip-down, shaft inspection, rewinding motor] -.-> G
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Vibration Frequency Diagnostic Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1([Vibration Anomaly Detected]) --> V2{Which frequency dominates?}
    V2 -- "1x RPM" --> V3[ROOT CAUSE: Unbalance or shaft bow]
    V2 -- "2x RPM" --> V4[ROOT CAUSE: Misalignment or bent shaft]
    V2 -- "High frequency broadband" --> V5[ROOT CAUSE: Cavitation or turbulence]
    V2 -- "Bearing race frequencies BPFO BPFI" --> V6[ROOT CAUSE: Bearing defect]
    V2 -- "Number of impeller vanes x RPM" --> V7[ROOT CAUSE: Hydraulic vane pass]
    V2 -- "Loose component frequencies" --> V8[ROOT CAUSE: Foundation bolt looseness]
    V3 --> V9[REMEDY: Balance rotor on dynamic balancing machine]
    V4 --> V10[REMEDY: Laser shaft alignment within 0.05 mm]
    V5 --> V11[REMEDY: Increase NPSHa, reduce flow, lower temperature]
    V6 --> V12[REMEDY: Replace bearing, check lubrication]
    V7 --> V13[REMEDY: Acceptable if within ISO zone, else trim impeller]
    V8 --> V14[REMEDY: Re-grout and torque anchor bolts]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block Architecture — Maintenance Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Operator Rounds and Daily Logs] --> P2[CMMS Database Input]
    P3[Vibration Sensor IoT] --> P2
    P4[Oil Sample Lab Results] --> P2
    P5[Thermal Imaging] --> P2
    P2 --> P6[Reliability Engineer Analysis]
    P6 --> P7{Triggers Maintenance Work Order?}
    P7 -- YES Preventive --> P8[Schedule PM Work Order]
    P7 -- YES Predictive --> P9[Schedule PdM Work Order]
    P7 -- YES Corrective --> P10[Schedule CM Work Order]
    P7 -- NO --> P11[Continue Trending]
    P8 --> P12[Execute Maintenance]
    P9 --> P12
    P10 --> P12
    P12 --> P13[Update Equipment History in CMMS]
    P13 --> P1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH). What is the recommended NPSH margin for a centrifugal pump as per API 610?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) NPSH is the total head available at the pump suction flange above the vapour pressure of the liquid: $\text{NPSH}_{a} = \dfrac{P_{atm} + P_{static} - P_{vapour}}{\rho g}$.
- (1 mark) $\text{NPSH}_{a}$ is what the *system* provides; $\text{NPSH}_{r}$ is what the *pump* requires.
- (1 mark) API 610 recommends a minimum margin of **1.0 m (≈ 3.3 ft)** or **10 %** of $\text{NPSH}_{r}$, whichever is greater.

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four symptoms of cavitation in a centrifugal pump.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. (1 mark) Cracking/pitting noise resembling gravel passing through the impeller.
2. (1 mark) Random vibration peaks at high frequency on the pump casing.
3. (1 mark) Erratic flow and pressure fluctuations; loss of pump performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse cavitation noise with **air binding** noise. Cavitation is high-frequency crackling; air binding is a low-frequency gurgling. Examiners award a **−1 mark** deduction if these are interchanged.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **one full question of 14 marks** with internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a) A centrifugal pump is operating at 1450 rpm delivering 120 m³/h against a head of 30 m with a shaft power input of 12 kW. The system curve is parabolic: H = 0.001 Q² where Q is in m³/h. If the pump speed is reduced to 1200 rpm, calculate:**
- **(i) New operating point (Q₂, H₂)** (4 marks)
- **(ii) New power consumption** (3 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply affinity law for new flow at new speed:

$$
\frac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}} = \frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} = \frac{1200}{1450} = 0.8276
$$

$$
Q_{2} = 0.8276 \times 120 = 99.31 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/h} \quad \text{(2 marks for setup, 2 marks for substitution)}
$$

Step 2 — Apply affinity law for new head:

$$
\frac{H_{2}}{H_{1}} = \left(\frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{2} = (0.8276)^{2} = 0.6849
$$

$$
H_{2} = 0.6849 \times 30 = 20.55 \text{ m} \quad \text{(continued from Q2 derivation)}
$$

Step 3 — New power:

$$
\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \left(\frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{3} = (0.8276)^{3} = 0.5667
$$

$$
P_{2} = 0.5667 \times 12 = 6.80 \text{ kW} \quad \text{(3 marks)}
$$

> ✓ **Answer: Q₂ ≈ 99.3 m³/h, H₂ ≈ 20.6 m, P₂ ≈ 6.8 kW**

---

**Q. (b) (i) Define MTBF, MTTR and Availability. (ii) The bearing sub-system of a pump has a constant failure rate of 4 × 10⁻⁵ per hour. If the mean time to repair is 16 hours, determine the availability of the bearing sub-system. (iii) Comment on the suitability of the same for a critical boiler feed pump application. (3+4+7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Definitions (3 marks):**
- **MTBF** — Mean Time Between Failures = total operating time ÷ number of failures (hours).
- **MTTR** — Mean Time To Repair = total downtime ÷ number of repairs (hours).
- **Availability (A)** — Fraction of time the equipment is ready for service: $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$.

(ii) **Calculation (4 marks):**

Step 1 — For constant failure rate, $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = 25{,}000 \text{ hours}
$$

Step 2 — Availability:

$$
A = \frac{25{,}000}{25{,}000 + 16} = \frac{25{,}000}{25{,}016} = 0.99936 = 99.94\%
$$

(iii) **Commentary on critical application (7 marks):**
- A bearing-only availability of 99.94 % is **misleading** because in a real pump we have series failure of bearings + seals + motor + coupling.
- (2 marks) For a critical boiler feed pump (BFP) in a thermal power plant, **API 610 and KTU industrial standard require overall availability ≥ 99.5 %**. Combined with MTTR, this is borderline.
- (2 marks) BFPs typically have redundant 2 × 100 % configuration, so the **system-level** availability improves by a factor of $\sim$100×.
- (2 marks) Recommendation: introduce PdM (vibration monitoring with ISO 10816-3 alarm at 4.5 mm/s RMS) to bring MTTR down to < 4 hours and improve safety.
- (1 mark) Conclusion: Suitable only when used in **redundant configuration** with online vibration monitoring.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a) With a neat flowchart, explain the systematic troubleshooting procedure for a centrifugal pump that is 'running but delivering no flow'. List any four possible causes and their remedies. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Troubleshooting Flowchart (4 marks):**
A sequential flow must be presented:

1. **Verify priming** → If pump is dry, prime it and re-try.
2. **Check suction line air-tightness** → Soap-bubble / vacuum test; tighten flanges.
3. **Verify motor rotation** → Swap any two phases for 3-phase induction motor.
4. **Check foot valve** → Open inspection port; clear obstruction.
5. **Check impeller obstruction** → Open casing, clear foreign bodies.
6. **Check shaft key** → If sheared, replace and re-assemble.

**Four Possible Causes with Remedies (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

| # | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Loss of prime due to air leak in suction | Locate leak with foam test, re-tighten |
| 2 | Foot valve stuck open | Clean foot valve, replace if worn |
| 3 | Wrong rotation direction | Reverse two phases of 3-phase supply |
| 4 | Impeller blocked by debris | Disassemble and clean impeller |

---

**Q. (b) (i) Explain the various types of maintenance strategies with one example each. (ii) For a pump with MTBF = 8000 h and MTTR = 12 h, calculate the availability. If a predictive maintenance program reduces MTTR to 4 h, what is the new availability? Comment on the result. (4+3+7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Maintenance Strategies (4 marks — 1 each):**

- **Preventive (PM):** Time-based — e.g., grease replenishment every 2000 h.
- **Predictive (PdM):** Condition-based — e.g., vibration monitoring with alarm at 4.5 mm/s RMS.
- **Corrective (CM):** Planned repair post-detection — e.g., scheduled impeller refurbishment.
- **Breakdown (BM):** Reactive — e.g., emergency bearing replacement after seizure.

(ii) **Calculation (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Original availability:

$$
A_{1} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 12} = \frac{8000}{8012} = 0.99850 = 99.85\%
$$

Step 2 — New availability after PdM:

$$
A_{2} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 4} = \frac{8000}{8004} = 0.99950 = 99.95\%
$$

(iii) **Commentary (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) Availability improved by 0.10 % in absolute terms; this corresponds to a **reduction of 8 hours of downtime per year** per pump.
- (2 marks) For a plant with 50 pumps, total saved downtime ≈ 400 hours/year.
- (2 marks) Beyond downtime, PdM also **prevents collateral damage** to motor windings, seals, and process fluid contamination.
- (1 mark) Conclusion: PdM is **cost-effective** even when availability gain is small, because it transforms unplanned shutdowns into planned ones.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students **forget units** in the affinity law problems — always write m³/h, m, kW. Loss of **0.5 mark per quantity** for missing units.
> 2. In availability problems, students often write **MTBF − MTTR** instead of **MTBF + MTTR** in the denominator. This is a **fatal error** worth a full 3-mark deduction.
> 3. For troubleshooting, do NOT list more than 4 causes unless the question asks for more — examiners cap at the number asked.
> 4. NPSH problems: always explicitly write **NPSHₐ > NPSHᵣ + 1.0 m margin** — the inequality sign direction is checked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for last-minute KTU exam preparation. Memorize these 12 bullets.**

- 🔹 The **four maintenance types** are PM, PdM, CM, BM — rank them in order of increasing cost: **PM < PdM < CM < BM**.
- 🔹 **MTBF = 1/λ** for constant failure rate (exponential reliability).
- 🔹 **Availability A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)** — note the **plus sign** in the denominator.
- 🔹 **Affinity laws** apply only when **impeller diameter is constant** and **system curve is unchanged**.
- 🔹 **Power varies with the cube of speed** — even 10 % speed reduction saves ~27 % power.
- 🔹 **L₁₀ bearing life** uses p = 3 for ball and p = 10/3 for roller bearings.
- 🔹 **NPSH margin** as per API 610 is **1.0 m or 10 % of NPSHᵣ**, whichever is greater.
- 🔹 **Vibration diagnosis** is frequency-domain: 1× RPM = unbalance, 2× RPM = misalignment, broadband HF = cavitation, BPFO/BPFI = bearing defect.
- 🔹 **Cavitation occurs when NPSHₐ < NPSHᵣ** — it is purely a *system* problem, not a *pump* problem.
- 🔹 **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** operation gives minimum vibration, minimum bearing load, and maximum seal life.
- 🔹 **Laser alignment tolerance**: ≤ 0.05 mm for process pumps, ≤ 0.03 mm for high-speed (≥ 3600 rpm) pumps.
- 🔹 Always perform a **cold alignment check first**, then verify **hot alignment** after running to operating temperature (thermal growth compensation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preventive Maintenance of Pumps and Hydraulic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Preventive Maintenance of Pumps and Hydraulic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Preventive Maintenance (PM)** of pumps and hydraulic systems is a systematic, scheduled, and proactive set of engineering activities carried out at predetermined intervals—based on time, operating hours, or cycle counts—to prevent unscheduled breakdowns, mitigate wear-out failures, retain design performance, and extend the Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of rotating and fluid-power equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Preventive maintenance refers to the *planned, periodic, and condition-triggered* servicing actions performed on pumps (centrifugal, reciprocating, positive displacement) and hydraulic systems (power packs, valves, actuators, circuits) to retain them within specified reliability, efficiency, and safety envelopes *before* failure occurs.

The two principal philosophies adopted in KTU-aligned industries are:

1. **Time-Based Preventive Maintenance (TBPM)** – interventions performed on calendar or running-hour schedules, regardless of the actual equipment health.
2. **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)** – interventions performed only when monitored parameters (vibration, temperature, oil analysis, acoustic emission) cross preset thresholds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reliability Triad (KPI):** The three golden KPIs tracked in any PM program are **Availability (A)**, **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**, and **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**. The examiner expects students to write all three when defining PM.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — "The Human Body and the Car Service"**

Imagine your body. You do not wait until your heart stops to start exercising; you jog, eat well, and visit the doctor for annual check-ups. You brush your teeth twice a day so they don't decay. Each of these is a *preventive* action designed to avoid future failure.

Now think of a **car**:
- You change engine oil every **5,000 km**.
- You rotate tyres every **10,000 km**.
- You replace brake pads every **40,000 km**.

You do these even when the car is "running fine." That is **Time-Based Preventive Maintenance**.

But sometimes the car's dashboard *tells* you something: the engine oil pressure light flickers, or a strange sound appears. You then take the car in *before* it breaks. That is **Condition-Based Maintenance**.

A **pump** in a chemical plant, a **hydraulic press** in a forging shop, and a **cooling water pump** in a power station are no different. They have:
- "Teeth" → **Bearings**, **seals**, **gears**
- "Blood" → **Hydraulic fluid**, **lubricant**
- "Heartbeat" → **Shaft rotation**, **pressure pulsation**
- "Nervous system" → **Sensors**, **gauges**, **PLCs**

**Preventive Maintenance** is the engineering equivalent of the body's check-up and the car's service schedule. It systematically *listens* to the equipment, *cleans* it, *lubricates* it, and *replaces* worn parts *before* catastrophic failure.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams — "P.L.A.N.T.S."**
> **P** – Periodic inspection
> **L** – Lubrication & oil analysis
> **A** – Alignment & balancing
> **N** – Noise / vibration / temperature monitoring
> **T** – Tightening & torque checks
> **S** – Seal & stuffing-box service

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in PM Programs

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Time Between Failures | MTBF | **hours** | Average operating time between two consecutive failures |
| Mean Time To Repair | MTTR | **hours** | Average time taken to restore equipment to service |
| Availability | A | **% (dimensionless)** | Fraction of time the equipment is ready for use |
| Overall Equipment Effectiveness | OEE | **%** | Availability × Performance × Quality |
| Failure Rate | λ(t) | **failures/hour** | Number of failures per unit operating time |
| Reliability | R(t) | **dimensionless** | Probability of survival up to time *t* |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Preventive Maintenance? — The "Why" Behind the Concept

Every rotating machine follows the classic **"Bathtub Curve"** of failure rate:

1. **Infant-Mortality Region (Burn-in):** High failure rate due to manufacturing defects, installation errors, and commissioning mistakes (e.g., misalignment, improper grouting).
2. **Useful-Life Region (Steady State):** Failure rate is approximately **constant** (random failures).
3. **Wear-Out Region:** Failure rate rises rapidly due to fatigue, corrosion, wear, and ageing.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of PM:** Preventive maintenance shifts the wear-out failures *back in time* by replacing components during planned shutdowns, keeping the equipment operating in its useful-life region for longer.

### 2.2 The Six Pillars of Pump & Hydraulic System PM

#### Pillar 1: Routine Visual & Auditory Inspection
- Leakage at the **mechanical seal**, **gland packing**, **flanges**, and **hydraulic hose connections**.
- Unusual **noise** (cavitation, bearing rumble, hammering in hydraulic lines).
- **Vibration** levels (measured in **mm/s RMS**, typically kept below **4.5 mm/s** for Class I machinery as per ISO 10816-3).
- **Temperature** rise in bearings (alarm at **80 °C**, trip at **95 °C** for most oil-lubricated bearings).

#### Pillar 2: Lubrication Management
- **Bearing lubrication** (grease every 2000–4000 operating hours, oil change every 4000–8000 hours for oil-mist systems).
- **Hydraulic fluid** replacement (typically every **2000–4000 hours** or **12 months**, whichever is earlier).
- **Oil analysis** triad: **Particle count (ISO 4406)**, **viscosity (cSt)**, **water content (ppm)**.

#### Pillar 3: Alignment & Balancing
- **Shaft alignment** tolerance: typically **≤ 0.05 mm** for couplings; laser alignment preferred over dial-indicator method.
- **Impeller balancing** to **G6.3 / G2.5** grade (ISO 1940-1).
- **Pulley/sheave alignment** within **0.5 mm** per metre of span.

#### Pillar 4: Seal & Gland Service
- **Mechanical seal** face inspection every 6000–12000 hours.
- **Gland packing** re-tensioning or replacement when leakage exceeds **60 drops/min** (typical plant standard).
- **O-ring** and **lip seal** replacement during every major overhaul.

#### Pillar 5: Hydraulic System Specific PM
- **Filter replacement** when differential pressure (ΔP) across the filter exceeds the manufacturer's set point (commonly **0.35 MPa** for return-line filters).
- **Valve actuation testing** for directional, pressure, and flow-control valves.
- **Accumulator nitrogen pre-charge** verification (must be checked every **6 months**; recharged to design pressure).
- **Reservoir** cleaning (sludge removal, breathers' replacement) every **12 months**.

#### Pillar 6: Performance Verification
- Re-measure **flow (Q)**, **head (H)**, and **power input (P)** after PM and compare with the original **pump performance curve**.
- A deviation of more than **±5%** from rated performance triggers root-cause analysis.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Formulas — Memorize Before ESE**

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | A = Availability | dimensionless (or %) | Equipment availability KPI |
| 2 | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{up}}{N_f}$ | T_up = total uptime, N_f = no. of failures | hours | Reliability metric |
| 3 | $MTTR = \dfrac{T_{down}}{N_f}$ | T_down = total downtime, N_f = no. of failures | hours | Maintainability metric |
| 4 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | R(t) = reliability, λ = failure rate, t = time | dimensionless | Exponential reliability model |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failure rate | failures/hr | Constant failure rate region |
| 6 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | A = availability, P = performance, Q = quality | % | Overall Equipment Effectiveness |
| 7 | $\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$ | ρ = density, g = 9.81 m/s², Q = flow, H = head, P_shaft = shaft power | dimensionless | Pump efficiency check after PM |
| 8 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Shaft power | Watts (W) or kW | Sizing motor after PM retrofit |
| 9 | $NPSH_{avail} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_{vapor}}{\rho g} \pm h_{s} - h_{f}$ | Static head (h_s), friction loss (h_f) | metres of fluid | Cavitation check after PM |
| 10 | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$ | Vibration severity | mm/s | ISO 10816 vibration evaluation |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power plants:** PM of boiler-feed pumps (BFP) prevents forced outages that cost **₹ 8–10 lakh/hour** in lost generation.
- **Petrochemical refineries:** Hydraulic PM in safety-instrumented systems (SIS) is mandatory under **OSHA 1910.119** and **IEC 61511**.
- **Aerospace ground-support:** Hydraulic PM on jacks and actuators is a **MEL/CDL** compliance requirement.
- **Hydraulic presses in forging:** PM prevents catastrophic hose bursts that can cause fatalities; **PM compliance** is a legal safeguard under the **Factories Act 1948 (India)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Availability as a Function of MTBF and MTTR

**Given:**
- A piece of equipment is observed over a long operational period **T_total**.
- During this period, it experiences **N_f** failures.
- Total **uptime** = $T_{up}$ (hours of successful operation).
- Total **downtime** = $T_{down}$ (hours spent in repair).

**Step 1 — Definition of MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):**

By definition, MTBF is the average *operating* time between two consecutive failures.

$$
MTBF = \dfrac{T_{up}}{N_f}
$$

*Explanation:* If the pump ran for 8000 hours and failed 4 times, then the average gap between failures is $8000 / 4 = 2000$ hours.

**Step 2 — Definition of MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):**

MTTR is the average *repair* time per failure event.

$$
MTTR = \dfrac{T_{down}}{N_f}
$$

*Explanation:* If the total downtime over the same period is 80 hours across 4 failures, the average repair time is $80 / 4 = 20$ hours per failure.

**Step 3 — Total Time Identity:**

The total observation time equals uptime plus downtime:

$$
T_{total} = T_{up} + T_{down}
$$

*Explanation:* This is just the conservation of time — the pump is either running (up) or being repaired (down).

**Step 4 — Definition of Availability:**

Availability is the fraction of the observation window during which the equipment was *ready* to perform its intended function.

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{up}}{T_{total}} = \dfrac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting MTBF and MTTR:**

Multiply both numerator and denominator of the previous step by $1 / N_f$:

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{up}/N_f}{(T_{up}/N_f) + (T_{down}/N_f)} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

*Explanation:* The $1/N_f$ factor cancels the failure count, leaving the cleanest and most-used KTU formula for Availability.

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}\,}
$$

This is **Equation (1)** of the KTU Formula Sheet.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU 2024 Expected Pattern

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump in a fertilizer plant operated for **9000 hours** in one calendar year. It failed **3 times** during the year, with individual repair times of **18 h, 22 h, and 28 h** respectively. The design specification mandates that availability must be ≥ **95%** for the system to be deemed reliable. As a maintenance engineer:
1. Calculate the **MTBF** and **MTTR**.
2. Calculate the **Availability**.
3. Comment on whether the availability target is met.
4. Recommend **two PM actions** to improve availability.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate MTBF**

$$
MTBF = \dfrac{T_{up}}{N_f} = \dfrac{9000}{3} = 3000 \; \text{hours}
$$

*Explanation:* 9000 operating hours divided by 3 failures yields 3000 hours between failures on average.

**Step 2 — Calculate MTTR**

Total downtime:

$$
T_{down} = 18 + 22 + 28 = 68 \; \text{hours}
$$

Mean Time To Repair:

$$
MTTR = \dfrac{T_{down}}{N_f} = \dfrac{68}{3} = 22.67 \; \text{hours}
$$

*Explanation:* Sum the three repair times and divide by the number of failures to obtain the mean.

**Step 3 — Calculate Availability**

$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{3000}{3000 + 22.67}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{3000}{3022.67} = 0.9925 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 99.25\%
$$

*Explanation:* The pump is operationally available for 99.25% of the time, which exceeds the 95% target.

**Step 4 — Verify Target Compliance**

Since $A = 99.25\% \gt 95\%$, the availability target **is met**. ✅

**Step 5 — PM Recommendations (KSEB / KTU style):**

1. **Vibration analysis & laser alignment** every 6 months to detect early-stage bearing wear and shaft misalignment, which historically account for ~40% of pump failures.
2. **Mechanical seal refurbishment** every 12000 operating hours; introduce a **seal-flush plan (API Plan 31/32)** to extend seal life by reducing dry-running and heat build-up.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Pump Failures and Their PM Mitigation

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | PM Action | Detection Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cavitation** | Low NPSH, blocked suction | Suction strainer cleaning, NPSH margin audit | Pressure transducer, acoustic sensor |
| **Bearing failure** | Lubricant starvation, misalignment | Grease re-lubrication, alignment correction | Vibration (enveloping), temperature |
| **Mechanical seal leakage** | Face wear, O-ring degradation | Seal-face lapping, O-ring replacement | Visual, drip rate, temperature |
| **Hydraulic hose burst** | Flex fatigue, ageing rubber | Periodic hose replacement (24–36 mo) | Visual, pressure-decay test |
| **Valve spool sticking** | Fluid contamination | Filter ΔP check, fluid analysis | ΔP gauge, ISO 4406 particle count |
| **Cylinder drift** | Internal leakage, seal wear | Actuator seal replacement | Drift test (mm/10 min) |
| **Overheating** | Cooler fouling, low oil | Cooler cleaning, oil level check | RTD / thermocouple |

---

### 3.4 Reliability Function Derivation (Exponential Model)

For a **constant failure rate** region of the bathtub curve, the probability of survival up to time $t$ follows an exponential distribution.

**Step 1:** Define a small interval $dt$. The probability of failure in that interval is $\lambda \cdot dt$.

**Step 2:** The probability of *survival* in that interval is $1 - \lambda \, dt$.

**Step 3:** The reliability over many such small intervals is the product of survival probabilities:

$$
R(t) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \lambda \dfrac{t}{n}\right)^n = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

*Explanation:* This limit identity is the standard definition of the exponential function in calculus. Physically, it means: if failures occur randomly and independently at a constant average rate $\lambda$, the reliability decays exponentially with time.

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t/MTBF}\,}
$$

since $\lambda = 1/MTBF$ in the constant-failure-rate region.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Preventive Maintenance Workflow (Block Architecture)

This diagram captures the complete PM loop, from planning through execution to feedback, exactly as recommended in KTU 2024 Module-3 reference material.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[PM Master Schedule] --> A2[Equipment Criticality Ranking ABC]
    A2 --> A3[Plan PM Window and Resource Allocation]
    A3 --> A4[Execute PM Tasks Inspection Lubrication Alignment]
    A4 --> A5[Condition Monitoring Vibration Temperature Oil]
    A5 --> A6[Data Logging and KPI Computation MTBF MTTR A]
    A6 --> A7{Performance Within Tolerance}
    A7 -- Yes --> A8[Close PM Ticket and Update CMMS]
    A7 -- No --> A9[Root Cause Analysis RCA]
    A9 --> A10[Corrective Action and Overhaul]
    A10 --> A1
    A8 --> A1
```

### 4.2 Hydraulic System PM Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Reservoir Oil Level Breather Cleaning] --> B2[Filter Delta P Check and Replacement]
    B2 --> B3[Pump Visual Vibration Temperature]
    B3 --> B4[Valves Spool Actuation Leakage Test]
    B4 --> B5[Actuators Cylinder Drift Seal Condition]
    B5 --> B6[Accumulators Nitrogen Precharge Verification]
    B6 --> B7[Heat Exchanger Cooler Cleaning]
    B7 --> B8[Pressure Gauges Calibration Verification]
    B8 --> B9[Hoses Visual Replacement at 36 Months]
    B9 --> B10[System Flush and Oil Change]
    B10 --> B11[Performance Test Q P H Verification]
```

### 4.3 Pump PM Decision Tree (Condition-Based Branching)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Start PM Cycle for Pump] --> C2{Vibration in mm/s RMS}
    C2 -- Less than 2.8 Class A --> C3[No Action Continue Operation]
    C2 -- 2.8 to 4.5 Class B --> C4[Plan Bearing Inspection Next Outage]
    C2 -- 4.5 to 7.1 Class C --> C5[Schedule Corrective Maintenance 7 Days]
    C2 -- Greater than 7.1 Class D --> C6[Immediate Shutdown and Overhaul]
    C3 --> C7[Log and Trend]
    C4 --> C7
    C5 --> C7
    C6 --> C7
    C7 --> C1
```

### 4.4 PM Frequency Matrix — Pumps vs Hydraulic Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[PM Frequency Matrix] --> D2[Daily Bearing Temp Oil Level Leakage]
    D1 --> D3[Weekly Vibration Check Coupling Alignment Visual]
    D1 --> D4[Monthly Filter DeltaP Pressure Gauge Calibration]
    D1 --> D5[Quarterly Oil Sample Analysis ISO 4406 FTIR]
    D1 --> D6[Half Yearly Accumulator Precheck Hose Inspection]
    D1 --> D7[Annual Full Overhaul Seal Bearing Alignment]
    D1 --> D8[3 Yearly Motor Rewinding Coupling Replacement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams?**
> - The **Workflow diagram** satisfies KTU Module-3 outcome **CO3** (plan PM programs).
> - The **Hydraulic block diagram** addresses **CO3** (apply PM on fluid-power systems).
> - The **Decision tree** reinforces **CO4** (interpret condition-monitoring data).
> - The **Frequency matrix** is a ready-revision tool for the ESE.

> [!WARNING]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and start with a letter (`A1`, `B3`, `C6`, `D8`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as node names.
> - Node labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text and spaces — no markdown bold or italics inside the quoted labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Define preventive maintenance. How does it differ from breakdown maintenance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Preventive maintenance (PM) is a **planned, time-based or condition-based set of engineering activities** carried out on pumps and hydraulic systems at predetermined intervals to *prevent* failure and retain design performance.

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance | Breakdown Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Scheduled / condition-based | Occurs *after* failure |
| **Cost** | Lower lifecycle cost | Higher (emergency spares, overtime) |
| **Downtime** | Planned, predictable | Unplanned, disruptive |
| **Strategy** | Proactive | Reactive |
| **Output KPI** | High Availability, long MTBF | Low Availability, short MTBF |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for clear difference table.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
**List any **four** condition-monitoring techniques used in pump preventive maintenance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Vibration analysis** (using accelerometers; ISO 10816 thresholds).
2. **Thermography / infrared temperature measurement** of bearings and casings.
3. **Lubricant oil analysis** (viscosity, particle count ISO 4406, spectrographic wear-debris analysis).
4. **Acoustic emission monitoring** for cavitation and early-stage seal failure.
5. *(Optional extra)* Motor current signature analysis (MCSA) for electrical faults.

*(1 mark for any 4 techniques, 1 mark for naming a relevant standard, 1 mark for brief explanation of one technique.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module in PEAGT522 carries a 14-mark question. The student answers **one out of two choices**. The question is typically split into two 7-mark sub-parts: **(a)** descriptive/theoretical and **(b)** analytical/numerical.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the **"Bathtub Curve"** in the context of pump reliability. Discuss how preventive maintenance modifies the failure-rate profile. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic power pack in a forging press is operational for **7200 hours/year**. It suffers **6 failures/year** with total downtime of **144 hours/year**.
  (i) Calculate the **MTBF**, **MTTR**, and **Availability**.
  (ii) If management decides to halve MTTR by adopting modular cartridge valves and on-site spares, what is the new availability? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — Bathtub Curve & PM Effect (7 marks)

**Diagram of the bathtub curve** (describe verbally since exam answer is hand-drawn):

- **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** High initial failure rate due to manufacturing defects, installation errors, poor commissioning (e.g., misalignment, trapped air, dry-running). Failure rate $\lambda(t)$ starts high and falls.
- **Region II — Useful Life (Steady State):** Failure rate is **approximately constant**; failures are random (bearings failing unpredictably, foreign particle entering the system).
- **Region III — Wear-Out:** Failure rate rises steeply due to fatigue, corrosion, erosion, and ageing of seals, bearings, and hoses.

**Effect of PM on the bathtub curve:**

1. **Reduces infant-mortality failures** through *commissioning-stage* PM checks: alignment verification, bolt torque audit, lube-oil flushing.
2. **Extends the useful-life region** by replacing components *before* they enter wear-out (predictive replacement based on vibration trend, oil-analysis trend).
3. **Smooths the wear-out climb** through *overhaul interventions* — re-metalling bearings, re-lapping seal faces, replacing hoses.

**Mathematical link:** PM effectively reduces the *conditional failure rate* $\lambda(t)$ in Regions II and III, which in turn increases the **MTBF** and pushes the **Availability** higher by the formula $A = MTBF/(MTBF + MTTR)$.

*(1 mark for curve drawing/description, 2 marks each for the three regions, 2 marks for PM impact — total 7.)*

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $T_{up} = 7200 \; \text{h/year}$
- $N_f = 6 \; \text{failures/year}$
- $T_{down} = 144 \; \text{h/year}$

**Step 1 — MTBF:**

$$
MTBF = \dfrac{7200}{6} = 1200 \; \text{hours}
$$

**Step 2 — MTTR:**

$$
MTTR = \dfrac{144}{6} = 24 \; \text{hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Current Availability:**

$$
A_{\text{old}} = \dfrac{1200}{1200 + 24} = \dfrac{1200}{1224} = 0.9804 = 98.04\%
$$

**Step 4 — New MTTR after PM intervention:**

$$
MTTR_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{24}{2} = 12 \; \text{hours}
$$

**Step 5 — New Availability:**

$$
A_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{1200}{1200 + 12} = \dfrac{1200}{1212} = 0.9901 = 99.01\%
$$

**Step 6 — Improvement:**

$$
\Delta A = 99.01\% - 98.04\% = 0.97 \; \text{percentage points}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**

Halving MTTR improves availability by nearly 1 percentage point, translating to an additional **70 hours/year** of usable production. For a forging press with an output of **200 components/hour @ ₹500/component revenue**, this is an additional annual revenue of:

$$
\Delta R = 70 \times 200 \times 500 = \text{₹ } 70{,}00{,}000
$$

*which justifies the cost of the PM retrofit many times over.*

*(1 mark each for MTBF, MTTR, A_old, MTTR_new, A_new; 1 mark for the revenue calculation; 1 mark for engineering interpretation — total 7.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Set B]**

**(a)** Describe the **six pillars of preventive maintenance** for centrifugal pumps, citing at least **one quantitative threshold** (temperature, vibration, leakage rate, etc.) for each pillar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical plant's cooling-water centrifugal pump has the following readings **before and after** a PM overhaul:

| Parameter | Before PM | After PM |
|---|---|---|
| Flow (m³/h) | 380 | 425 |
| Head (m) | 28 | 32 |
| Shaft power (kW) | 42 | 41 |
| Fluid density (kg/m³) | 1000 | 1000 |

  (i) Compute the **pump efficiency** in both cases.
  (ii) Comment on the **performance restoration** achieved by the PM activity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — Six Pillars of PM (7 marks)

| # | Pillar | Quantitative Threshold / Metric |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Visual & Auditory Inspection** | Leakage rate **> 60 drops/min** at the gland → packing replacement. |
| 2 | **Lubrication Management** | Bearing temp **> 80 °C** → lubrication failure; oil ISO 4406 cleanliness code target **18/16/13**. |
| 3 | **Alignment & Balancing** | Shaft offset **> 0.05 mm** → realign; vibration velocity **< 4.5 mm/s** (ISO 10816-3 Class I). |
| 4 | **Seal & Gland Service** | Seal face flatness **≤ 2 light bands (0.0008 µm)**; O-ring hardness Shore A **70 ± 5**. |
| 5 | **Performance Monitoring** | Flow deviation **> ± 5%** from rated → impeller wear or partial recirculation. |
| 6 | **Hydraulic-System Specific PM** | Filter ΔP **> 0.35 MPa** → element replacement; accumulator precharge **± 5%** of nameplate pressure. |

*(1 mark per pillar with quantitative threshold — total 6; 1 mark for the table layout and overall coherence.)*

---

##### Part (b) — Pump Efficiency Calculation (7 marks)

**Formula:**

$$
\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, Q \, H}{P_{shaft}}
$$

where $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$, $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ (divide by 3600), $H$ in metres, $P_{shaft}$ in Watts (multiply kW by 1000).

**Step 1 — Convert flow to SI units:**

$$
Q_{\text{before}} = \dfrac{380}{3600} = 0.1056 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{after}} = \dfrac{425}{3600} = 0.1181 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute hydraulic (water) power before PM:**

$$
P_{hyd,\text{before}} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.1056 \times 28
$$

$$
P_{hyd,\text{before}} = 29{,}001 \; \text{W} \approx 29.0 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute efficiency before PM:**

$$
\eta_{\text{before}} = \dfrac{29.0}{42.0} = 0.6905 = 69.05\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute hydraulic power after PM:**

$$
P_{hyd,\text{after}} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.1181 \times 32 = 37{,}082 \; \text{W} \approx 37.1 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute efficiency after PM:**

$$
\eta_{\text{after}} = \dfrac{37.1}{41.0} = 0.9049 = 90.49\%
$$

**Step 6 — Performance restoration commentary:**

$$
\Delta \eta = 90.49\% - 69.05\% = 21.44 \; \text{percentage points}
$$

*Engineering interpretation:*
- The PM intervention has restored pump efficiency by **~21 percentage points**, an enormous improvement suggesting the pre-PM state suffered from **impeller fouling, wear-ring degradation, or partial recirculation** (e.g., excessive clearance between the impeller and casing).
- Power consumption is slightly *lower* after PM (41 kW vs 42 kW), confirming that the restored hydraulic performance is achieved with less input — a clear sign of the **PM program being energy-positive**.
- The post-PM efficiency of **90.5%** is consistent with a well-designed centrifugal pump (typical range 85–92%).
