- The plant should now lock in this performance by adopting a **quarterly performance-trending** PM task.

*(1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for each efficiency value before/after, 1 mark for the conversion logic, 1 mark for the percentage-point improvement, 1 mark for engineering commentary, 1 mark for the lock-in recommendation — total 7.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in PEAGT522 Module-3 PM questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–1 to –2 marks):** Using **litres/min** instead of **m³/s**, or **kW** instead of **W** in the efficiency formula. Always convert to SI base units before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant failure-rate assumption (–1 mark):** When using $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, *explicitly state* that this is valid only in the useful-life region of the bathtub curve.
> 3. **Confusing MTBF with MTTR (–1 mark):** MTBF is *between* failures (running time), MTTR is *to* repair (downtime). They are not interchangeable.
> 4. **Skipping a labelled diagram in the bathtub-curve question (–1 mark):** KTU examiners explicitly reward a hand-sketched curve with all three regions labelled.
> 5. **Vague "preventive maintenance" definition (–0.5 mark):** Always mention **planned**, **periodic**, and **proactive** — the three keywords examiners scan for.
> 6. **Missing quantitative threshold in the "six pillars" answer (–1 mark per missing threshold):** Each pillar must carry at least one numerical benchmark (°C, mm/s, bar, mL/min, etc.).
> 7. **No engineering commentary in numerical answers (–1 mark):** A calculation alone is incomplete. Add 1–2 lines of *interpretation* — what does the number mean for the plant?

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (print-friendly):**

- ✅ **Definition:** PM is *planned*, *periodic*, *proactive* — written in that exact triad.
- ✅ **Two flavours:** Time-Based (TBPM) and Condition-Based (CBM).
- ✅ **Three KPIs:** Availability, MTBF, MTTR — always quote all three.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — derivable from first principles.
- ✅ **Reliability Function:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ with $\lambda = 1/MTBF$ in the constant-failure region.
- ✅ **OEE:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ where $A, P, Q \in [0,1]$.
- ✅ **Pump Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$ — convert $Q$ to $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ and $P_{shaft}$ to watts.
- ✅ **Six PM Pillars:** Inspection, Lubrication, Alignment, Seal Service, Performance, System-Specific.
- ✅ **Vibration limits (ISO 10816-3):** Class A < 2.8 mm/s; Class B < 4.5; Class C < 7.1; Class D > 7.1.
- ✅ **Temperature limits:** Bearing alarm 80 °C, trip 95 °C (oil-lubricated).
- ✅ **Hydraulic filter ΔP:** Replace at **0.35 MPa** (return-line typical).
- ✅ **Accumulator precharge:** Check every **6 months**.
- ✅ **Hydraulic fluid change:** Every **2000–4000 hours** or **12 months**.
- ✅ **Hose replacement:** Every **24–36 months** (manufacturer-specified).
- ✅ **PM effect on bathtub curve:** Reduces infant mortality, *extends* useful life, *flattens* wear-out climb.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** "P.L.A.N.T.S." for the six pillars (Periodic check, Lubrication, Alignment, Noise/vibration/temperature, Tightening, Seals).
- ✅ **Examiner hot-spots:** Bathtub curve, availability derivation, pump efficiency with units, six pillars with thresholds, MTBF vs MTTR distinction.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Preventive Maintenance of Pumps and Hydraulic Systems**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Troubleshooting Common Issues in Pump Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Troubleshooting Common Issues in Pump Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Troubleshooting in Pump Systems** is defined as the systematic, diagnostic process of identifying, analyzing, and rectifying faults or deviations in pump performance from its design intent. It involves interpreting symptoms (vibration, noise, temperature rise, flow loss, pressure drop) through instrumentation and engineering heuristics to isolate root causes—hydraulic, mechanical, or operational—and restore the system to its Best Efficiency Point (BEP) operating regime.

The discipline falls under the broader framework of **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** and **Root Cause Failure Analysis (RCFA)**, both of which are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework under Course Outcome CO3: *Diagnose and resolve hydraulic and mechanical anomalies in centrifugal and positive displacement pumping systems.*

### 1.2 Key Terminology — KTU Board-Ready Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Cavitation** | Vapor bubble formation and violent collapse within a liquid, occurring when local static pressure falls below the fluid's vapor pressure. |
| **NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)** | The total absolute pressure at the pump suction port over and above the fluid's vapor pressure. |
| **NPSHr (Required)** | Minimum NPSH mandated by the pump manufacturer to avoid cavitation. |
| **NPSHa (Available)** | Actual NPSH present in the installed system, calculated from suction tank conditions. |
| **BEP (Best Efficiency Point)** | The single duty point on the H-Q curve where pump efficiency reaches its maximum. |
| **AOR (Allowable Operating Region)** | The zone between 70% and 120% of BEP flow where stable, vibration-free operation is guaranteed. |
| **Vapor Pressure (Pv)** | Absolute pressure at which a liquid boils at a given temperature. |
| **Mechanical Seal** | A precision-engineered interface device used to prevent process fluid leakage along a rotating shaft. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Heart Analogy**
>
> Think of a centrifugal pump as the **human heart**, the suction pipe as the **vena cava (incoming veins)**, and the discharge pipe as the **aorta (outgoing artery)**.
>
> - **Cavitation** is like **anemia with low blood volume** — the heart pumps but cannot fill properly because the suction side pressure is too low, causing "bubbles" (vapor) to form and collapse, damaging valves (impeller vanes).
> - **Bearing failure** is analogous to **joint arthritis** — over time, lubrication (synovial fluid) breaks down, friction rises, and the joint overheats.
> - **Impeller wear** is like **arterial plaque buildup** — flow channels narrow, head drops, and efficiency plummets.
> - **Mechanical seal leakage** is akin to a **pericardial leak** — fluid escapes where it shouldn't, and immediate diagnosis is needed.
>
> Just as a cardiologist uses **stethoscopes (vibration analysis)**, **ECGs (pressure pulsations)**, and **blood tests (oil analysis)** to diagnose heart issues, a pump engineer uses **vibration analyzers, pressure gauges, flow meters, and thermography** to troubleshoot pump systems.

### 1.4 Why Troubleshooting Matters in KTU 2024 Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Relevance (KTU 2024 POs/PSOs Alignment):**
> - **PO1 (Engineering Knowledge):** Apply thermodynamic and fluid principles to diagnose faults.
> - **PO3 (Design/Development):** Re-engineer systems to operate away from failure zones.
> - **PSO1 (Hydraulic System Design):** Develop fault-tolerant pumping installations.
> - According to the **Hydraulic Institute (HI 9.6.6 Rotodynamic Pumps — Assessment of Performance)** standard, troubleshooting follows a defined **3-tier assessment methodology**.

---

### 1.5 The Three Categories of Pump System Problems

All pump anomalies fundamentally originate from three root domains:

1. **Hydraulic Problems** — Issues related to fluid flow, pressure, and energy transfer (e.g., cavitation, NPSH deficiency, recirculation).
2. **Mechanical Problems** — Issues related to rotating and stationary components (e.g., bearing wear, impeller erosion, seal damage).
3. **Operational/Process Problems** — Issues caused by deviation from design operating conditions (e.g., off-BEP operation, parallel/series pumping mismatches).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Symptom-to-Cause Diagnostic Framework

The **Systematic Troubleshooting Pyramid** (adopted from HI standards and widely tested in KTU exams) classifies pump symptoms into a hierarchical decision tree:

```
                    [OBSERVED SYMPTOM]
                           |
            +--------------+--------------+
            |              |              |
    [HYDRAULIC]      [MECHANICAL]     [OPERATIONAL]
        |                |                |
   Cavitation         Vibration         Off-BEP
   Recirculation      Bearing Fail      Overload
   Air entrainment    Seal Leak         Parallel mismatch
   Insufficient NPSH  Impeller wear     Wrong rotation
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The examiner expects a **symptom → probable cause → corrective action** triplet. Memorizing all 12–15 such triplets guarantees 7 marks in any descriptive question.

### 2.2 The Ten Most Common Pump Issues (Ranked by Frequency)

| Rank | Issue | Symptom | Primary Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cavitation** | Noise, vibration, pitting | NPSHa < NPSHr + safety margin |
| 2 | **Loss of Suction / Air Binding** | Flow drops to zero | Vapor formation in suction line |
| 3 | **Excessive Vibration** | Bearing damage | Misalignment, imbalance, cavitation |
| 4 | **Mechanical Seal Failure** | Leakage | Dry running, chemical attack, misalignment |
| 5 | **Bearing Overheating** | Temperature rise | Lubrication failure, overloading |
| 6 | **Impeller Wear/Erosion** | Head and flow drop | Abrasive particles, corrosion |
| 7 | **Motor Overload** | Tripping, high current | Operation far from BEP, binding |
| 8 | **Shaft Deflection/Breakage** | Catastrophic | Critical speed resonance, fatigue |
| 9 | **Recirculation** | Vibration at low flow | Operating below minimum continuous flow |
| 10 | **Packing Gland Issues** | Leakage, scoring | Improper tightening, misalignment |

### 2.3 The Cavitation Number — Diagnostic Gold Standard

The **Thoma Cavitation Parameter (σ)** is the dimensionless metric universally used to predict and diagnose cavitation:

$$\sigma = \frac{NPSH_a}{H}$$

where:
- $NPSH_a$ = Available Net Positive Suction Head in **meters**
- $H$ = Total pump head in **meters**

The **cavitation inception condition** occurs when:

$$\sigma \leq \sigma_{critical} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad NPSH_a \leq NPSH_r$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** Always maintain **NPSHa ≥ 1.3 × NPSHr** for cold water service. For hot water or volatile hydrocarbons, use **NPSHa ≥ 1.5 × NPSHr**.

### 2.4 NPSH Calculation — The Foundational Diagnostic Tool

The available NPSH for an open suction system is calculated as:

$$NPSH_a = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} \pm H_s - h_f$$

For a **flooded suction** (suction lift positive if below the pump):

$$NPSH_a = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$$

For a **suction lift** (pump above the source, $H_s$ is positive lift):

$$NPSH_a = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$$

where:
- $P_{atm}$ = Atmospheric pressure = **101.325 kPa** (at sea level)
- $P_v$ = Vapor pressure of fluid at operating temperature (in **Pa**)
- $H_s$ = Static suction head (positive if below pump, negative if above)
- $h_f$ = Friction head loss in suction piping (in **m**)
- $\rho$ = Fluid density (in **kg/m³**)
- $g$ = Gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**

### 2.5 Vibration Severity — ISO 10816 Standard

The **ISO 10816-3** standard categorizes vibration severity for pumps based on **RMS velocity (mm/s)**:

| Zone | RMS Velocity (mm/s) | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone A** | $v \leq 2.8$ | Good — newly commissioned equipment |
| **Zone B** | $2.8 < v \leq 4.5$ | Acceptable — long-term operation OK |
| **Zone C** | $4.5 < v \leq 7.1$ | Unsatisfactory — short-term tolerable |
| **Zone D** | $v > 7.1$ | Severe — immediate shutdown required |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| NPSHa (Flooded) | $\frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$ | Pump below source level |
| NPSHa (Suction Lift) | $\frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$ | Pump above source level |
| Thoma Cavitation Parameter | $\sigma = \frac{NPSH_a}{H}$ | Cavitation risk assessment |
| Specific Speed | $N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Pump classification |
| Affinity Law (Flow) | $\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Variable speed operation |
| Affinity Law (Head) | $\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Variable speed operation |
| Affinity Law (Power) | $\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | Variable speed operation |
| Suction Specific Speed | $S = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{NPSH_r^{3/4}}$ | Suction cavitation tendency |
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime detection |
| Vibration Severity | $v_{rms}$ in mm/s (ISO 10816) | Mechanical health assessment |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Warning:** Never write $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table — always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking the table syntax.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pump troubleshooting principles are applied across:

- **Municipal Water Supply** — Diagnosing pressure drop in distribution networks.
- **Oil & Gas Refineries** — API 610 pump monitoring in high-temperature hydrocarbon service.
- **Power Plants** — Boiler feed pump (BFP) NPSH margin verification.
- **HVAC Systems** — Chilled water pump recirculation diagnosis.
- **Hydraulic Press Systems** — High-pressure piston pump maintenance.
- **Marine Engineering** — Bilge and ballast pump failure analysis.

---

### 2.8 Cavitation Mechanisms — A Deeper Look

Cavitation is a **two-stage hydraulic phenomenon**:

1. **Bubble Nucleation (Inception):** When local static pressure drops below the vapor pressure, dissolved gases and vapor form microscopic bubbles at nucleation sites (surface imperfections, microscopic pits).
2. **Bubble Collapse (Implosion):** As the bubble travels into regions of higher pressure (toward the impeller eye or high-pressure discharge), it collapses violently.

The collapse generates:
- **Micro-jets** with velocities up to **150 m/s**
- **Shock pressures** of approximately **1000 MPa** locally
- **Localized temperatures** exceeding **5000 K**

This explains why cavitation:
- **Pits** the impeller leading edges (visible as sponge-like erosion).
- **Vibrates** the pump due to irregular pressure pulses.
- **Sounds** like gravel being shaken inside the pump casing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: NPSHa from First Principles

The **Available Net Positive Suction Head (NPSHa)** is derived from Bernoulli's equation applied between the free surface of the suction reservoir and the pump suction flange.

**Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between the Reservoir Surface (Point 1) and the Pump Suction Flange (Point 2)**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$

**Step 2: Apply Boundary Conditions**

At the reservoir surface (Point 1):
- $P_1 = P_{atm}$ (open to atmosphere, absolute pressure)
- $v_1 \approx 0$ (large reservoir, negligible velocity)
- $z_1$ = reference datum (let's set $z_1 = 0$ for simplicity)

At the pump suction flange (Point 2):
- $P_2 = P_s$ (absolute pressure at suction flange)
- $v_2 = v_s$ (velocity in suction pipe)
- $z_2 = H_s$ (static suction lift; positive if pump is above the source)

**Step 3: Substitute Boundary Conditions**

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + 0 = \frac{P_s}{\rho g} + \frac{v_s^2}{2g} + H_s + h_f$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to Isolate the Total Suction Head**

$$\frac{P_s}{\rho g} + \frac{v_s^2}{2g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$$

**Step 5: Subtract the Vapor Pressure Head (Definition of NPSH)**

By definition, the total head at the pump suction must be referenced to the vapor pressure of the fluid. Therefore, subtract $P_v / (\rho g)$ from both sides:

$$\frac{P_s - P_v}{\rho g} + \frac{v_s^2}{2g} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$$

**Step 6: Define the Velocity Head as Part of Suction Head**

The velocity head $\frac{v_s^2}{2g}$ is conventionally included in the suction head, giving:

$$\boxed{NPSH_a = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} \pm H_s - h_f + \frac{v_s^2}{2g}}$$

In practice, for flooded suction systems with negligible velocity head, this simplifies to:

$$NPSH_a \approx \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Cavitation Diagnostic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Style Problem**
>
> **Given:**
> A centrifugal pump handles water at **60°C** from a reservoir located **3.5 m below** the pump centerline. The suction pipe has a diameter of **150 mm**, length of **8 m**, with one foot valve (K = 5.0) and one 90° elbow (K = 0.9). The flow rate is **Q = 30 m³/h**. Atmospheric pressure is **101.3 kPa**.
>
> **Required:**
> 1. Calculate NPSHa.
> 2. The manufacturer's catalog states NPSHr = **2.8 m**. Determine if cavitation will occur.
> 3. If yes, suggest two remedies.

**Step 1: Identify Fluid Properties at 60°C**
- Vapor pressure of water at 60°C: $P_v$ = **19.9 kPa**
- Density of water: $\rho$ = **983 kg/m³**

**Step 2: Calculate Flow Velocity in Suction Pipe**

$$Q = 30 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} = \frac{30}{3600} = 0.00833 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.15)^2}{4} = 0.01767 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$v_s = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.00833}{0.01767} = 0.4716 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Friction Head Loss in Suction Pipe**

Total loss coefficient:
$$\sum K = K_{foot\,valve} + K_{elbow} + K_{entrance} = 5.0 + 0.9 + 0.5 = 6.4$$

Velocity head:
$$\frac{v_s^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.4716)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.01133 \, \text{m}$$

Friction loss:
$$h_f = \left(\sum K\right) \frac{v_s^2}{2g} = 6.4 \times 0.01133 = 0.0725 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Identify Static Suction Lift**

Pump is **3.5 m above** the source → $H_s = +3.5 \, \text{m}$ (suction lift, positive)

**Step 5: Calculate NPSHa**

$$NPSH_a = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$$

$$NPSH_a = \frac{(101300 - 19900)}{983 \times 9.81} - 3.5 - 0.0725$$

$$NPSH_a = \frac{81400}{9640.83} - 3.5725$$

$$NPSH_a = 8.443 - 3.5725 = 4.87 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 6: Compare with NPSHr**

- $NPSH_a = 4.87 \, \text{m}$
- $NPSH_r = 2.8 \, \text{m}$

Since $NPSH_a > NPSH_r$, cavitation **will not** occur. The margin is:

$$\text{Margin} = 4.87 - 2.8 = 2.07 \, \text{m}$$

$$\text{Percentage Margin} = \frac{2.07}{2.8} \times 100 = 73.9\%$$

This exceeds the recommended 30% safety margin, so the system is **safe**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Symptom-to-Action Lookup

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **exam-ready** and frequently appears as a 7-mark sub-question in KTU ESE.

| Symptom | Probable Cause | Diagnostic Test | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Screeching/grinding noise** | Cavitation | Measure NPSHa; check vapor pressure | Lower suction lift, reduce temperature, enlarge suction pipe |
| **Loss of flow** | Air ingress, vapor lock, impeller block | Pressure test suction line | Tighten flanges, reprime pump, clear blockage |
| **Excessive vibration** | Misalignment, imbalance, recirculation | Laser alignment, vibration spectrum | Realign, balance impeller, throttle to AOR |
| **High bearing temp (>80°C)** | Lubrication failure, over-greasing | Oil analysis, temperature mapping | Replace bearings, change oil, realign |
| **Seal leakage** | Dry running, face damage | Inspect seal faces, check fluid temp | Flush seal chamber, replace mechanical seal |
| **Motor overload (high amps)** | Off-BEP operation, binding | H-Q curve comparison, amp reading | Trim impeller, check rotation, inspect for jams |
| **High discharge pressure** | Blocked discharge, parallel mismatch | Pressure gauge at discharge | Clear blockage, adjust parallel control valves |
| **Shaft deflection** | Critical speed, excessive load | Modal analysis, runout measurement | Operate away from critical speed, replace shaft |
| **Corrosion on impeller** | Wrong material, pH imbalance | Material test, fluid analysis | Use stainless/Alloy 20, adjust pH |
| **Reduced efficiency** | Wear ring clearance increase | Borescope inspection | Replace wear rings, refurbish impeller |

### 3.4 Derivation: Affinity Laws (Speed Variation Diagnostics)

When a centrifugal pump operates at a different speed, the new performance can be predicted using the **Affinity Laws**:

**Flow Affinity Law:**

$$\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_2 = Q_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

**Head Affinity Law:**

$$\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_2 = H_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$$

**Power Affinity Law:**

$$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

**Validity Range:** These laws are **exact** only for geometrically similar pumps at similar efficiency points (i.e., homologous operating points). For a 10% speed variation, the error is typically less than 5%.

---

### 3.5 The Recirculation Phenomenon — Detailed Analysis

When a centrifugal pump operates at flow rates **below 70% of BEP**, a phenomenon called **Internal Recirculation** occurs:

- The **flow angle at the impeller inlet** deviates sharply from the blade angle.
- Fluid reverses direction at the impeller eye, creating a **vortex**.
- The vortex can be **stationary** (causing axial thrust imbalance) or **rotating** (causing pressure pulsations and vibration).
- A pre-rotating vortex of velocity $\omega_{vortex}$ creates a pressure deficit of:

$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho (\omega_{vortex} r)^2$$

This pressure deficit pulls dissolved gas out of solution, intensifying the cavitation risk even when NPSHa appears adequate.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]**

#### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**Q: Define cavitation in a centrifugal pump. State the condition under which it occurs.**

**Model Answer:**
Cavitation is the formation and subsequent violent collapse of vapor bubbles within a liquid as it passes through a pump. It occurs when the local static pressure at any point inside the pump falls **below the vapor pressure of the liquid** at the operating temperature.

The condition for cavitation inception is:

$$NPSH_a < NPSH_r \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \sigma < \sigma_{critical}$$

It manifests as noise, vibration, and erosion of impeller surfaces.

---

#### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**Q: What is the significance of NPSH in pump system design? Why is a 30% safety margin recommended?**

**Model Answer:**
NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head) represents the **total absolute pressure available at the pump suction over and above the vapor pressure** of the fluid. It is the critical parameter that prevents cavitation.

**Significance:**
- Ensures cavitation-free operation.
- Required to verify the **suction-side hydraulic design** is sound.

**Why 30% Margin:**
- To account for variations in **fluid temperature, atmospheric pressure (altitude), wear of internals, and transient surges**.
- The manufacturer's NPSHr is a **laboratory-measured value**; field conditions introduce additional uncertainties.
- A 30% margin (i.e., NPSHa ≥ 1.3 × NPSHr) ensures a **reliable, conservative design**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU ESE Pattern: Either/Or Internal Choice Compulsory]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** A centrifugal pump handles water at **45°C** from a sump located **2 m below** the pump. The suction pipe is **DN 100 mm**, **6 m long**, with one foot valve (K = 6.0) and one 90° elbow (K = 0.9). The flow rate is **20 m³/h**. The atmospheric pressure is **101 kPa**. The pump's NPSHr from the catalog is **2.5 m**. Determine:
- (i) The available NPSH
- (ii) Whether cavitation will occur
- (iii) The percentage safety margin

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches:
- (i) The various types of cavitation in centrifugal pumps
- (ii) The different zones of pump operation as per ISO 10816-3 for vibration severity

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1: Properties of water at 45°C**
- Vapor pressure: $P_v$ = **9.6 kPa**
- Density: $\rho$ = **990 kg/m³**

**Step 2: Velocity in suction pipe**
$$Q = \frac{20}{3600} = 0.00556 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$A = \frac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 0.00785 \, \text{m}^2$$
$$v_s = \frac{0.00556}{0.00785} = 0.708 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3: Friction head loss**
$$\sum K = 6.0 + 0.9 + 0.5 \text{ (entrance)} = 7.4$$
$$\frac{v_s^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.708)^2}{19.62} = 0.0255 \, \text{m}$$
$$h_f = 7.4 \times 0.0255 = 0.189 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 4: NPSHa calculation**
$$NPSH_a = \frac{101000 - 9600}{990 \times 9.81} - 2 - 0.189$$
$$NPSH_a = \frac{91400}{9711.9} - 2.189$$
$$NPSH_a = 9.412 - 2.189 = 7.22 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 5: Comparison**
- $NPSH_a$ = **7.22 m**
- $NPSH_r$ = **2.5 m**
- Since $NPSH_a > NPSH_r$, **cavitation will not occur**.
- Safety margin: $\frac{7.22 - 2.5}{2.5} \times 100 = 188.8\%$

**This margin is well above 30% and is exceptionally safe.**

---

**Solution to (b):**

**(i) Types of Cavitation:**

1. **Vaporization Cavitation (Classical Cavitation):** Occurs when NPSHa < NPSHr. Most common in hydraulic systems.

2. **Gaseous Cavitation (Air Cavitation):** Caused by dissolved gases coming out of solution due to pressure drop. Less damaging but reduces efficiency.

3. **Vortex Cavitation:** Occurs at the tip of an impeller vane where vortex cores form in regions of high local velocity. Common in axial flow pumps.

4. **Internal Recirculation Cavitation:** Happens when the pump operates at flows far below the Best Efficiency Point (BEP). A recirculating vortex forms at the impeller inlet, creating localized low pressure.

**(ii) ISO 10816-3 Vibration Zones:**

| Zone | RMS Velocity (mm/s) | Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| Zone A | $\leq 2.8$ | Newly commissioned equipment, no issues |
| Zone B | $2.8 < v \leq 4.5$ | Acceptable for long-term operation |
| Zone C | $4.5 < v \leq 7.1$ | Unsatisfactory; plan repair |
| Zone D | $> 7.1$ | Severe damage possible; immediate shutdown |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a **symptom-cause-correction table**, discuss the **ten most common problems** encountered in centrifugal pump operation. Indicate the appropriate **diagnostic instrument** for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump running at **1450 rpm** delivers **50 L/s** against a head of **30 m**. If the pump speed is changed to **1750 rpm**, calculate the new flow rate, head, and power consumption. The original power consumption was **18 kW**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Solution to (a):**

| # | Symptom | Probable Cause | Diagnostic Tool | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Screeching noise | Cavitation | Vacuum/pressure gauge | Increase NPSHa |
| 2 | Loss of flow | Air ingress, vapor lock | Pressure test | Reprime, tighten flanges |
| 3 | Excessive vibration | Misalignment | Laser aligner | Realign shaft-coupling |
| 4 | Bearing overheating | Lubrication failure | IR thermometer | Replace bearings, change oil |
| 5 | Seal leakage | Dry running, wear | Borescope | Replace mechanical seal |
| 6 | Motor overload | Off-BEP operation | Clamp meter | Trim impeller |
| 7 | Reduced efficiency | Wear ring damage | Borescope | Replace wear rings |
| 8 | High discharge pressure | Blocked line | Pressure gauge | Clear blockage |
| 9 | Shaft breakage | Critical speed fatigue | Strobe, runout gauge | Operate away from critical |
| 10 | Corrosion | Wrong material selection | Material analysis | Upgrade material grade |

**Solution to (b):**

Given:
- $N_1 = 1450$ rpm, $Q_1 = 50$ L/s, $H_1 = 30$ m, $P_1 = 18$ kW
- $N_2 = 1750$ rpm

**Step 1: New Flow Rate (Affinity Law — Flow)**
$$Q_2 = Q_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 50 \times \frac{1750}{1450} = 50 \times 1.2069 = 60.34 \, \text{L/s}$$

**Step 2: New Head (Affinity Law — Head)**
$$H_2 = H_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 30 \times (1.2069)^2 = 30 \times 1.4566 = 43.70 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3: New Power (Affinity Law — Power)**
$$P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = 18 \times (1.2069)^3 = 18 \times 1.758 = 31.64 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 4: Percentage Change**
- Flow: $+20.7\%$
- Head: $+45.7\%$
- Power: $+75.8\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Power rises with the **cube** of speed. A 20% speed increase leads to 75% more power consumption — important for energy audits and motor sizing.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Mistake:** Confusing suction lift with suction head. If the pump is **above** the sump, $H_s$ is **positive** (subtracted in the NPSHa formula). If **below** (flooded), it is **negative** (added). Examiners deduct 1 mark if signs are flipped.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert Units:** Mixing kPa and Pa, or using $g = 10$ instead of $9.81$ m/s². Always state units at every step.
>
> 3. **Vapor Pressure Omission:** Using $P_v = 0$ for cold water approximations. KTU 2024 expects the **correct $P_v$ at the given temperature**.
>
> 4. **NPSHr vs NPSHa:** Students frequently calculate NPSHa and claim "cavitation" without comparing it with the manufacturer's NPSHr. Always **state both values and compare explicitly**.
>
> 5. **Affinity Laws Used Outside Validity Range:** Don't apply affinity laws for more than ±15% speed change; the laws become approximate beyond this range.
>
> 6. **No Diagram for Descriptive Questions:** For "Explain cavitation" type questions, KTU examiners **expect a labeled diagram** of the suction side. Skipping it costs 2 marks.
>
> 7. **Confusing NPSH with Suction Head:** NPSH is **absolute** pressure head, not gauge. Use $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa absolute, not 0.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Troubleshooting Pump Systems**

- **Definition:** Troubleshooting is the systematic diagnosis of hydraulic, mechanical, or operational deviations in pump systems.
- **Cavitation** is the **#1 most common** pump problem — caused by NPSHa < NPSHr.
- **NPSH Rule:** Always maintain **NPSHa ≥ 1.3 × NPSHr** for cold water, **1.5 × NPSHr** for hot/volatile fluids.
- **NPSHa Formula (Flooded):** $\frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$
- **NPSHa Formula (Suction Lift):** $\frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$ (with $H_s$ positive when pump is above source)
- **Thoma Cavitation Parameter:** $\sigma = \frac{NPSH_a}{H}$; cavitation occurs when $\sigma \leq \sigma_{critical}$.
- **Vapor Pressure** of water at 60°C is **19.9 kPa**; at 100°C it is **101.3 kPa**.
- **Recirculation** occurs at flows **< 70% of BEP** and causes axial thrust imbalance.
- **ISO 10816-3 Vibration Zones:**
  - A: $\leq 2.8$ mm/s (Good)
  - B: $2.8$–$4.5$ mm/s (Acceptable)
  - C: $4.5$–$7.1$ mm/s (Unsatisfactory)
  - D: $> 7.1$ mm/s (Severe — Shutdown)
- **Affinity Laws** apply for **geometrically similar pumps** within ±15% speed variation:
  - Flow: $Q \propto N$
  - Head: $H \propto N^2$
  - Power: $P \propto N^3$
- **Top 10 Problems (Exam Priority Order):**
  1. Cavitation
  2. Loss of suction
  3. Excessive vibration
  4. Seal failure
  5. Bearing overheating
  6. Impeller wear
  7. Motor overload
  8. Shaft deflection
  9. Recirculation
  10. Packing gland issues
- **Diagnostic Instruments** (recall for KTU viva): Manometer, tachometer, vibration analyzer, IR thermometer, borescope, sound level meter, megger, laser alignment tool.
- **Troubleshooting Sequence:** Identify symptom → Categorize (hydraulic/mechanical/operational) → Form hypothesis → Measure → Confirm → Rectify → Verify.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — PEAGT522 Pump Engineering and Hydraulic Control**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overhauling Hydraulic Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Overhauling** in the context of hydraulic engineering refers to the systematic, scheduled, or condition-based process of **complete disassembly, inspection, repair, replacement of worn parts, cleaning, reassembly, and testing** of a hydraulic component (such as a pump, valve, actuator, or motor) to restore it to its original manufacturer's performance specifications. It is a preventive/corrective maintenance operation that goes beyond routine servicing and is performed at prescribed intervals measured in operating hours, cycles, or calendar time.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, overhauling encompasses all activities defined under ISO 4406 cleanliness standards and follows the **"teardown, inspect, repair, reassemble, and verify" (TIRRV)** methodology used in industrial hydraulic systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Overhauling is treated as a critical maintenance activity. Students must understand the *procedure*, *component-specific steps*, *tools required*, *inspection techniques*, and *reassembly precautions* for the major hydraulic components listed in the syllabus.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **hydraulic system as the human circulatory system**:
- The **hydraulic pump** = the heart (pumps fluid)
- The **valves** = the valves inside the heart and veins (regulate flow and pressure)
- The **hydraulic cylinder/motor** = the muscles (do the work)
- The **hydraulic fluid** = the blood (carries energy)
- The **filters** = the kidneys (clean the fluid)
- The **reservoir** = the body's storage of blood

Now, just as a person needs a **full medical check-up and surgery** every few years to clean plaque, replace worn valves, and check organ function — a hydraulic system needs **overhauling** at regular intervals to clean internal wear debris, replace seals, check tolerances, and restore component health.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metrics in Overhauling:**
> - **Operating pressure range:** typically **up to 350 bar (5000 psi)** for industrial hydraulics
> - **Filtration standard:** ISO 4406 cleanliness code (e.g., **18/16/13**)
> - **Surface finish of critical bores:** **0.2 – 0.8 µm Ra**
> - **Cylindricality tolerance:** typically **0.01 – 0.03 mm**

## 1.3 Why Overhauling is Necessary

Hydraulic components suffer from:
1. **Wear** due to sliding/rotating contact (e.g., gear meshing, piston-cylinder contact).
2. **Contamination** ingress causing abrasion of critical surfaces.
3. **Cavitation erosion** in pumps.
4. **Seal degradation** due to heat, aging, and fluid incompatibility.
5. **Fatigue failure** of springs, poppets, and loaded components.
6. **Fluid oxidation** producing varnish and sludge.

Without periodic overhauling, the Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) drops dramatically, leading to unplanned downtime.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TIRRV Methodology (Teardown → Inspect → Repair → Reassemble → Verify)

### Stage 1: Teardown (Disassembly)
- The component is removed from the system after depressurization and lockout-tagout (LOTO).
- External cleaning is done first to prevent contamination entry.
- Disassembly follows the manufacturer's service manual in a logical sequence.
- Parts are laid out on a clean bench in order of removal.

### Stage 2: Inspection
Each part is visually inspected, dimensionally measured, and tested (e.g., NDT methods):
- **Visual** for cracks, scoring, corrosion.
- **Dimensional** using micrometers, bore gauges, dial indicators.
- **Surface finish** using profilometers.
- **Hardness** for shafts and gears.
- **Pressure/leak testing** for valve bodies.

### Stage 3: Repair / Replacement
- Worn parts are either **remanufactured** (e.g., honed cylinder bores, reground valve seats) or **replaced** with OEM spares.
- Seals, O-rings, and gaskets are **always replaced** with new ones during an overhaul.

### Stage 4: Reassembly
- Done in a **cleanroom-like environment** (ISO 7 or better).
- All parts are cleaned with **filtered solvent** and lubricated with **system-compatible hydraulic fluid** before assembly.
- Torque wrenches are used to spec on all bolts.
- Special tools (e.g., seal drivers) are used for seal installation.

### Stage 5: Verification (Testing)
- Component is tested on a test bench for:
  - **Flow rate** (volumetric efficiency)
  - **Pressure holding** (case drain leakage, internal leakage)
  - **Noise and vibration**
  - **Response time** (for valves)

## 2.2 General Pre-Overhauling Precautions

1. **Depressurize** the system completely — residual pressure can be lethal.
2. **Lock-out, Tag-out (LOTO)** all energy sources (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic).
3. **Drain the fluid** into a clean container — it can be filtered and reused if clean.
4. **Clean the exterior** of the component thoroughly with a non-toxic solvent.
5. **Cover all open ports** with plastic plugs to prevent ingress of dirt.
6. **Use a clean, lint-free work area** and clean tools.
7. **Document** every step with photos for reassembly reference.

## 2.3 Overhauling of Major Hydraulic Components

### 2.3.1 Overhauling a Gear Pump

A **gear pump** (external or internal type) is the simplest positive-displacement pump and the most commonly encountered.

**Disassembly Steps:**
1. Remove the pump from the mounting and tag all ports.
2. Remove the bolts holding the front flange/back cover.
3. Separate the body from the cover carefully.
4. Remove the drive gear and idler gear (note the orientation and shims).
5. Remove the bearing(s) and seal(s) from the shaft.
6. Remove the wear plates (if present in internal gear pumps).

**Inspection:**
- **Gear teeth** for scuffing, pitting, or breakage.
- **Bore of pump body** for scoring.
- **Shaft** for wear at bearing journals and seal contact area.
- **Seal lips** for hardness and cracks.
- **Bearing raceways** for brinelling.

**Reassembly:**
- Install new seals and bearings (press-fit using proper tools).
- Replace shims to restore **end-float** (typically **0.025 – 0.10 mm**).
- Align the gears with timing marks (for internal gear pumps with a crescent).
- Torque bolts in a **star pattern** to avoid distortion.
- Re-check the shaft rotation smoothness by hand.

### 2.3.2 Overhauling a Vane Pump

A **vane pump** uses slotted rotors with retractable vanes.

**Disassembly:**
1. Remove the back cover bolts.
2. Extract the rotor and vanes carefully (count the vanes — usually **10, 12, or 14**).
3. Remove the cam ring (stator ring), wear plates, and pressure plate.
4. Remove the drive shaft and bearing.

**Inspection:**
- **Vane tips** for wear (most common wear point).
- **Cam ring bore** for ovality and scoring.
- **Rotor slots** for wear at the sliding vane contact.
- **Wear plates** for groove wear.

**Critical Dimensions:**
- Vane-to-slot clearance: typically **0.025 – 0.05 mm**.
- Vane-to-cam ring contact width: must be uniform.

**Reassembly:** Vanes are dipped in clean hydraulic oil before insertion. The cam ring is installed with proper orientation (inlet vs outlet ports).

### 2.3.3 Overhauling a Piston Pump (Axial / Radial)

Piston pumps are high-pressure, high-precision units.

**Disassembly:**
1. Remove the through-bolts holding the housing.
2. Separate the barrel from the port plate.
3. Remove the pistons (typically **9 in axial piston pumps** like Rexroth A10VSO).
4. Extract the swash plate / cam ring.
5. Remove the drive shaft and bearings.

**Inspection:**
- **Piston-bore clearance** in the barrel (most critical).
- **Port plate** surface for wear (must be lapped or replaced).
- **Slipper pads** on piston shoes (for swash plate pumps).
- **Swash plate** for pitting.

**Reassembly:** Requires precise setting of the **shoe plate** pre-load and **barrel-to-port-plate** contact pattern.

### 2.3.4 Overhauling Hydraulic Valves

#### a) Directional Control Valve (DCV) — Spool Type
- Remove the solenoid/tactile actuator.
- Extract the spool carefully (it can fall out — use a soft cloth).
- Inspect the **spool lands** for burrs, scoring, and wear.
- Inspect the **body bores** for ovality and scratches.
- Check **centering springs** for fatigue.
- Clean the **ports** with filtered solvent.
- Reassemble with new O-rings; torque to spec.
- Test on a manifold: spool must slide smoothly by hand with no binding.

#### b) Pressure Control Valve (Relief / Sequence / Counterbalance)
- Remove the bonnet/cover.
- Extract the **poppet or spool**, **spring**, and **adjustment screw**.
- Inspect the **seat** for pitting (use magnifying glass).
- Check the **spring** for free length and squareness.
- Reassemble with new seals; set the pressure on a test bench with a calibrated gauge.

#### c) Flow Control Valve
- Disassemble the needle or spool.
- Inspect the **orifice** for blockage and edge damage.
- Check the **flow rate** on a test rig at multiple dial positions.

### 2.3.5 Overhauling a Hydraulic Cylinder

**Disassembly:**
1. Retract the piston fully and drain the cylinder.
2. Remove the gland nut or tie-rod nuts.
3. Pull out the piston and rod assembly from the barrel.
4. Remove the piston seal, rod seal, wiper, and O-rings.

**Inspection:**
- **Barrel bore** — hone if scratched beyond **0.05 mm** depth, replace if severe.
- **Piston rod** — check for straightness (TIR < **0.05 mm/m**), chrome wear, and corrosion pits.
- **Clevis/eye** — check for cracks using **DPT (Dye Penetrant Test)**.
- **Ports** — clean and inspect threads.

**Reassembly:**
- All new seals (especially polyurethane rod seals and PTFE piston seals).
- Lubricate seals with system fluid or compatible grease.
- Torque gland nut to spec (using a hook spanner).

### 2.3.6 Overhauling a Hydraulic Motor

Similar to piston pumps (since most hydraulic motors are reversible pumps). Particular attention to:
- **Shaft seal** replacement.
- **Bearings** — always replace during motor overhaul.
- **Case drain** — must be checked for internal leakage.

### 2.3.7 Overhauling Accumulators, Filters, and Reservoirs

- **Accumulators (bladder/diaphragm/piston):** Pre-charge pressure check, bladder/diaphragm replacement, nitrogen pre-charge (typically to **0.9 × max working pressure**).
- **Filters:** Replace element; clean housing; check bypass valve spring.
- **Reservoir:** Drain and clean sludge, inspect baffles, clean breather filter, inspect level/temperature gauges.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula / Standard | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % — drops as wear increases |
| Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % — drops due to friction |
| Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \times \eta_m$ | Decimal |
| Internal Leakage (pump) | $Q_{leak} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | $\Delta P$ in Pa, $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| Shaft End-Float (gear pump) | $S = T_{shim}$ | **0.025 – 0.10 mm** typical |
| Vane Tip Clearance | $c_{vane}$ | **0.025 – 0.05 mm** |
| Surface Finish of Bores | $R_a$ | **0.2 – 0.8 µm** |
| Piston-Bore Clearance | $c_{pb}$ | **0.005 – 0.025 mm** |
| Piston Rod Straightness | TIR | $< 0.05$ mm/m |
| Nitrogen Pre-charge Pressure | $P_0 = 0.9 \times P_{max}$ | bar (for accumulators) |
| Hydraulic Power | $P = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{600}$ | kW (p in bar, Q in L/min) |
| Filter Beta Ratio | $\beta_x = \dfrac{N_{upstream}}{N_{downstream}}$ | where x = particle size in µm |
| ISO 4406 Cleanliness Code | (x / y / z) | particles per mL at 4, 6, 14 µm |
| Bolt Torque | $T = K \cdot d \cdot F$ | K = 0.2 (typical), d = nominal dia |

> [!NOTE]
> The **volumetric efficiency** is the most important indicator during an overhaul. A drop in $\eta_v$ below **90–95%** for industrial pumps signals that overhauling is required.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Overhauling is the **backbone of asset management** in:
- **Injection moulding machines** (piston pump + DCV overhauls every 10,000 hours).
- **Construction equipment** (excavators, loaders — gear pump overhauls every 5,000 hours).
- **Marine hydraulics** (deck cranes, hatch covers — frequent overhauling due to salt air).
- **Steel mills** (rolling mills — high-pressure piston pump overhauls at 20,000 hours).
- **Aerospace ground support** (hydraulic actuators for landing gear testing).
- **Power plants** (governor hydraulics, turbine control systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** The examiner often tests whether the student understands the *sequence* of operations, the *safety precautions*, and the *critical wear points* of each component. Mere listing of steps is not enough — you must justify *why* each step is done.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Overhaul Interval Based on Wear Rate

A key analytical task during overhauling planning is to **predict the next overhaul interval** based on measured wear.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A gear pump is found to have a gear-shaft journal bearing wear rate of $w$ mm per **1000 hours** of operation. The maximum allowable wear before failure is $W_{max}$ mm. The current measured wear after $T$ operating hours is $W_{obs}$ mm.

Find:
1. Remaining life $T_{remaining}$
2. Total overhaul interval $T_{OH}$
3. Volumetric efficiency drop expected

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the wear rate.**

$$
w = \dfrac{W_{obs}}{T} \quad \text{[mm per 1000 hours]}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the remaining allowable wear.**

$$
W_{remaining} = W_{max} - W_{obs}
$$

**Step 3: Compute remaining operating hours.**

$$
T_{remaining} = \dfrac{W_{remaining}}{w} \times 1000 \quad \text{[hours]}
$$

**Step 4: Total overhaul interval.**

$$
T_{OH} = T + T_{remaining}
$$

**Step 5: Estimate volumetric efficiency drop.**

For a gear pump, the volumetric efficiency is given by:

$$
\eta_v = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{leak}}{Q_{theoretical}}
$$

The internal leakage is proportional to the cube of the clearance, so:

$$
Q_{leak} \propto c^3
$$

Since clearance $c$ grows linearly with wear (assuming $c_0$ is the new-clearance):

$$
c = c_0 + W
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_v = 1 - K \cdot (c_0 + W)^3
$$

**Step 6: Numerically evaluate for a typical case.**

> **Worked Example:**
> Given: $c_0 = 0.02$ mm, $K = 500$ mm⁻³ (calibration constant for a typical 25 cc/rev gear pump), $W = 0.08$ mm after overhaul wear.

**Step 6a:** Compute the new clearance after wear.

$$
c = c_0 + W = 0.02 + 0.08 = 0.10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6b:** Compute $\eta_v$ at the new clearance.

$$
\eta_v = 1 - 500 \times (0.10)^3 = 1 - 500 \times 0.001 = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5
$$

Wait — this gives only 50% which is unrealistic. The constant $K$ depends on system pressure and pump geometry. For a more typical industrial case with $K = 0.8$ mm⁻³ at 200 bar:

$$
\eta_v = 1 - 0.8 \times (0.10)^3 = 1 - 0.8 \times 0.001 = 1 - 0.0008 = 0.9992
$$

That's about **99.92%**, which is realistic for a healthy gear pump. As wear progresses to $W = 0.5$ mm:

$$
c = 0.02 + 0.5 = 0.52 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\eta_v = 1 - 0.8 \times (0.52)^3 = 1 - 0.8 \times 0.1406 = 1 - 0.1125 = 0.8875 \approx 88.75\%
$$

This is the typical threshold where overhauling becomes mandatory.

**Step 7: Compute % drop in efficiency.**

$$
\Delta \eta_v = \eta_{v,new} - \eta_{v,worn} = 0.9992 - 0.8875 = 0.1117 \quad \text{(or 11.17%)}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Component-Wise Overhaul Summary

| Component | Critical Wear Point | Inspection Method | Replacement Criterion | Torque Spec Range (Nm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **External Gear Pump** | Gear teeth, body bore, shaft seal | Visual + micrometer | Body bore ovality > **0.05 mm** | **20 – 80** |
| **Internal Gear Pump** | Crescent, idler gear, wear plate | Visual + dial gauge | Crescent thickness wear > **0.5 mm** | **30 – 100** |
| **Vane Pump** | Vane tips, cam ring, rotor slots | Micrometer + surface finish | Vane length < **min length mark** | **25 – 70** |
| **Axial Piston Pump** | Piston-bore, port plate, slipper pad | Profilometer + leak test | Barrel leak > spec | **60 – 200** |
| **Spool-type DCV** | Spool lands, body bores | Bore gauge + visual | Spool-body clearance > **0.02 mm** | **15 – 50** |
| **Pressure Relief Valve** | Poppet seat, spring | Magnifier + pressure test | Seat pitting, spring set | **20 – 60** |
| **Hydraulic Cylinder** | Rod chrome, barrel bore, seals | TIR + bore gauge | Chrome pits, score depth > **0.05 mm** | **40 – 250** |
| **Hydraulic Motor** | Shaft seal, bearings | Dial gauge + NDT | Bearing noise, seal leak | **50 – 150** |
| **Accumulator** | Bladder/diaphragm, poppet | Pressure test + visual | Bladder leaks, low pre-charge | **30 – 90** |
| **Filter Assembly** | Element, bypass valve | Visual + ΔP gauge | ΔP > **0.5 bar** at rated flow | **15 – 40** |

## 3.3 Decision Tree: When to Overhaul (Logical Flow)

The following logical sequence is used by maintenance engineers:

1. **Monitor** $\eta_v$ continuously (online sensors) or via periodic testing.
2. If $\eta_v$ drops below **95%** → schedule inspection.
3. If $\eta_v$ drops below **90%** → schedule **overhauling within 500 hours**.
4. If $\eta_v$ drops below **80%** → **immediate shutdown and overhaul**.
5. After overhauling, re-test and re-baseline.
6. Log data in Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The examiner may ask you to *derive* the volumetric efficiency equation or *justify* the threshold values. Memorizing the numbers alone will not fetch full marks.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Overhauling Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trigger: Scheduled Hours OR Failure OR Low Efficiency] --> B[Stage 1: LOTO and System Depressurization]
    B --> C[Stage 2: External Cleaning of Component]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Disassembly Teardown]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Visual Inspection of All Parts]
    E --> F{Part Wear Status}
    F -->|Within Limit| G[Clean and Reuse]
    F -->|Beyond Limit| H[Replace with OEM Spare]
    F -->|Repairable| I[Regrind, Hone, or Rework]
    G --> J[Stage 5: Reassembly on Clean Bench]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Stage 6: Test Bench Verification]
    K --> L{Performance OK?}
    L -->|No| M[Re-Disassemble and Diagnose]
    M --> D
    L -->|Yes| N[Stage 7: Reinstall in System]
    N --> O[Stage 8: Commission and Log]
```

## 4.2 Disassembly Sequence for a Typical Gear Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Remove Pump from Mounting] --> P2[Step 2: Mark Body and Cover for Reassembly]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Remove Mounting Bolts]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Separate Cover from Body]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Remove Drive Gear and Idler Gear]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Remove Shaft Seal from Body]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Remove Bearing with Proper Puller]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Remove Back Plate and Shims]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Layout Parts on Clean Bench in Order]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Inspect, Clean, Measure Each Part]
```

## 4.3 Component-Wise Inspection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INSP[Inspection Station]
    A1[Visual Crack Check] --> A2[Dimensional Measurement]
    A2 --> A3[Surface Finish Check]
    A3 --> A4[Hardness Test for Shafts]
    A4 --> A5[NDT Dye Penetrant for Critical Parts]
    end
    INSP --> DEC{Decision}
    DEC -->|Pass| CLEAN[Ultrasonic Cleaning]
    DEC -->|Fail| REJ[Reject and Replace]
    CLEAN --> REASSY[Reassembly Bin]
    REJ --> OEMS[OEM Spare Bin]
    REASSY --> FINAL[Final Bench Test]
    OEMS --> FINAL
```

## 4.4 Hydraulic Cylinder Overhauling Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Step 1: Retract Piston and Drain] --> B2[Step 2: Remove Tie Rod Nuts]
    B2 --> B3[Step 3: Withdraw Barrel from Rod Assembly]

    B4 --> B5[Step 5: Remove Gland Nut and Seals]
    B5 --> B6[Step 6: Inspect Barrel Bore with Bore Gauge]
    B6 --> B7[Step 7: Check Rod Straightness on V Blocks]
    B7 --> B8[Step 8: Hone or Replace Barrel if Worn]

```
