<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) and state why it is considered the last line of defense in the hierarchy of controls for hydraulic system safety.**

**Model Answer:**
PPE refers to specialized clothing and gear worn by personnel to minimize exposure to specific occupational hazards. In hydraulic systems, PPE includes items such as A4 cut-resistant gloves, Z87+ splash goggles, and FR coveralls. It is considered the **last line of defense** because all prior controls — elimination, substitution, engineering controls, and administrative controls — must be exhausted first. PPE is **active** (depends on the worker wearing it correctly), whereas engineering controls are **passive** (always in operation). Failure of human compliance is the dominant cause of PPE-related injuries in pump and hydraulic work.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for hierarchy, 1 mark for active vs passive distinction)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is Hydraulic Injection Injury (HHII), and what minimum PPE specification is required to prevent it during pump maintenance?**

**Model Answer:**
Hydraulic Injection Injury (HHII) occurs when pressurized hydraulic fluid (typically ≥100 bar) penetrates the skin through a pinhole leak, causing tissue necrosis, sepsis, and often amputation. The minimum PPE specification to prevent HHII includes:
1. **Cut-resistant gloves rated A4 or higher** (ANSI/ISEA 105)
2. **Z87+ rated splash goggles with indirect ventilation**
3. **Flame-resistant coveralls (NFPA 2112)**
4. **Steel-toe EH boots with metatarsal guard**

The key principle: HHII prevention is primarily a **hand-protection** problem, and gloves must be puncture-resistant, not just cut-resistant.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for minimum PPE specs)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]*

**Q. (a)** Describe in detail the **categories of PPE** required for personnel working on industrial hydraulic systems. Include relevant **IS/ANSI standards** for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pump maintenance technician is exposed to the following noise profile during an 8-hour shift:
- **Zone 1:** 102 dBA for 1.5 hours
- **Zone 2:** 97 dBA for 2.0 hours
- **Zone 3:** 88 dBA for 1.5 hours
- **Zone 4:** 80 dBA for 3.0 hours

Calculate the **OSHA noise dose (using 5 dB exchange rate)** and recommend appropriate hearing PPE. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q. (a) — PPE Categories & Standards

| PPE Category | Hazard | Standard | Specification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Head | Falling tools, overhead piping | ANSI Z89.1 / IS 2925 | Hard hat Type I, Class E (electrical, 20 kV) |
| Eye & Face | Oil spray, flying particles | ANSI Z87.1 / IS 8520 | Z87+ splash goggles, D3 rating |
| Hearing | Pump cavitation noise | ANSI S3.19 / IS 6229 | NRR 25+ dB ear muffs |
| Hand | Hydraulic injection, cuts | ANSI/ISEA 105 / IS 8472 | A4 cut-resistance minimum |
| Foot | Dropped components, slips | ASTM F2413 / IS 15298 | Steel-toe, EH, oil-resistant |
| Body | Oil spray, flash fire | NFPA 2112 / IS 15741 | FR coveralls, 4 cal/cm² ATPV minimum |
| Respiratory | Oil mist, VOCs | NIOSH 42 CFR 84 / IS 14166 | P100 half-face respirator |
| Fall | Elevated pump decks | ANSI Z359.11 / IS 3521 | Full-body harness, SRL |

**Connecting Statement:** The KTU 2024 syllabus requires the student to map each category to **specific numerical ratings**, not just generic descriptions.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per category with associated standard, plus 1 mark for the connection between IS/ANSI codes and Indian regulations)*

#### Model Solution for Q. (b) — Noise Dose Calculation

**Step 1: Compute OSHA Reference Times $T_i$ for each zone using:**
$$T_i = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L_i - 90)/5}}$$

**For Zone 1 (102 dBA):**
$$T_1 = \dfrac{8}{2^{(102-90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{2.4}} = \dfrac{8}{5.278} \approx 1.516 \text{ hrs}$$

**For Zone 2 (97 dBA):**
$$T_2 = \dfrac{8}{2^{(97-90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{1.4}} = \dfrac{8}{2.639} \approx 3.031 \text{ hrs}$$

**For Zone 3 (88 dBA):**
$$T_3 = \dfrac{8}{2^{(88-90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{-0.4}} = 8 \times 2^{0.4} = 8 \times 1.320 = 10.56 \text{ hrs}$$

**For Zone 4 (80 dBA):**
$$T_4 = \dfrac{8}{2^{(80-90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{-2}} = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ hrs}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Dose $D$:**

$$D = \left(\dfrac{1.5}{1.516} + \dfrac{2.0}{3.031} + \dfrac{1.5}{10.56} + \dfrac{3.0}{32}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$D = (0.989 + 0.660 + 0.142 + 0.094) \times 100\%$$

$$D = 1.885 \times 100\% = 188.5\%$$

**Step 3: Interpretation & Recommendation:**

Since $D = 188.5\% \gg 100\%$, the technician is **severely over-exposed**. Required actions:
1. **Engineering control first:** Install acoustic enclosure, vibration isolators, and silencers.
2. **Administrative control:** Rotate personnel to limit exposure time.
3. **PPE specification:** Ear muffs with **NRR ≥ 30 dB** (derated: 30 − 7 = 23 dB protection).
4. **Audiometric testing** every 6 months (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often compute the dose but **forget to subtract the 7 dB NRR derating factor** required by OSHA. Always write: "NRR derated = NRR − 7 dB."

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Q. (a)** Explain the **mechanism of hydraulic injection injury (HHII)**. Show that a hydraulic leak at 200 bar can produce a skin-penetrating jet. Use the relevant energy and velocity equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prepare a **detailed PPE specification checklist** for a maintenance team of five technicians servicing a **350 bar hydraulic press** in a manufacturing plant. Map each PPE item to a specific **IS / ANSI / NFPA standard** and justify the choice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q. (a) — HHII Mechanism and Derivation

**Step 1: Mechanism Description**
HHII occurs when high-pressure fluid penetrates intact skin. The mechanism involves:
1. **Skin breach:** The jet pressure exceeds the skin's rupture threshold (~10 bar for the epidermis alone, but combined with the orifice geometry, even lower system pressures can breach).
2. **Tissue dissection:** The fluid spreads along **fascial planes**, not following normal anatomical channels.
3. **Tissue necrosis:** The fluid contains **additives, metal particles, and bacteria** that cause chemical and bacterial damage.
4. **Compartment syndrome:** Pressure buildup in closed fascial compartments cuts off blood supply.

**Step 2: Jet Velocity Calculation**

For $P_{system} = 200 \text{ bar} = 20 \text{ MPa}$, $P_{atm} = 0.1013 \text{ MPa}$, $\rho_{oil} = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$v_{jet} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times (20 - 0.1013) \times 10^6}{870}}$$

$$v_{jet} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 19.899 \times 10^6}{870}} = \sqrt{45745.4} \approx 213.9 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Jet Kinetic Energy**

For a typical pinhole $A = 0.5 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$:

$$\dot{m} = C_d A \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P} = 0.97 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{2 \times 870 \times 19.899 \times 10^6}$$

$$\dot{m} = 4.85 \times 10^{-7} \times 186.0 = 9.02 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{E}_{jet} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 9.02 \times 10^{-5} \times (213.9)^2$$

$$\dot{E}_{jet} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 9.02 \times 10^{-5} \times 45753 = 2.06 \text{ J/s}$$

This is small but applied over a **microscopic area** — equivalent pressure is on the order of **gigapascals**, far exceeding skin rupture.

**Step 4: Conclusion**
The 200 bar system can absolutely produce a skin-penetrating jet, justifying A4+ gloves and Z87+ eyewear as minimum PPE.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for mechanism, 2 marks for velocity, 2 marks for energy/mass flow, 1 mark for PPE conclusion)*

#### Model Solution for Q. (b) — PPE Specification Checklist for 350 bar Press

| # | PPE Item | Specification | Standard | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hard hat | Type I, Class E | ANSI Z89.1 | Falling tool protection, electrical insulation to 20 kV |
| 2 | Safety goggles | Z87+ indirect vent | ANSI Z87.1 | Splash from pinhole leak at 350 bar |
| 3 | Face shield | Full-face, 8" arc | ANSI Z87.1 +H | High-pressure whip protection |
| 4 | Cut-resistant gloves | A6 cut score | ANSI/ISEA 105 | 350 bar requires highest cut resistance |
| 5 | Hydraulic fluid-resistant gloves | Nitrile coating, A4 | IS 8472 | Chemical resistance to mineral oil |
| 6 | Steel-toe boots | I/75, C/75, EH | ASTM F2413 | Dropped die, electrical hazard |
| 7 | FR coveralls | NFPA 2112, 8 cal/cm² | NFPA 2112 | Flash fire from oil ignition |
| 8 | Ear muffs | NRR 30 dB | ANSI S3.19 | Press noise exceeds 100 dB |
| 9 | Full-body harness | 5000 lb capacity | ANSI Z359.11 | Servicing top of press (≥1.8 m) |
| 10 | Lockout-Tagout kit | Brady Masterlock | OSHA 1910.147 | Mandatory before press servicing |

**Justification Statement:** The 350 bar pressure is 75% above the OSHA "high pressure" threshold (200 bar). Therefore, A4 (the KTU minimum) is insufficient — **A6 is mandated**. The face shield rating "+H" indicates headwear-compatibility. The coverall ATPV (Arc Thermal Performance Value) of 8 cal/cm² exceeds the typical 4 cal/cm² minimum for industrial hydraulic facilities.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per PPE item to a maximum of 5, 1 mark for standard mapping, 1 mark for justification of A6 vs A4 escalation)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always use OSHA's 5 dB exchange rate** unless the question specifies NIOSH (3 dB). Confusion here costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **For HHII problems, never write "use gloves" alone** — specify the **A4 / A6 cut score** and the **puncture resistance rating** (EN 388 Level 4 minimum).
> 3. **Derate the NRR by 7 dB** when recommending hearing PPE. Writing "NRR 30 dB provides 30 dB protection" is **wrong** — it provides 23 dB.
> 4. **PPE is the LAST line of defense** — if you do not mention the **hierarchy of controls**, you lose 1 mark on 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **PPE Definition:** Specialized clothing/gear to minimize exposure to occupational hazards.
- ✅ **Hierarchy:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → **PPE (Last)**.
- ✅ **Why Last?** PPE is **active** (depends on the worker), not **passive** (engineering).
- ✅ **HHII Threshold:** Pressures ≥100 bar can cause skin penetration; A4+ gloves minimum, A6 for 350 bar.
- ✅ **Hydraulic Jet Velocity:** $v_{jet} = \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ — supersonic for typical pressures.
- ✅ **OSHA Noise Dose:** $D = \sum (C_i / T_i) \times 100\%$; **5 dB exchange rate**.
- ✅ **NRR Derating:** $NRR_{adj} = NRR - 7$ dB (OSHA requirement).
- ✅ **Cut Score Mapping:** A4 minimum (1500–2199 g); A6 for high-pressure (3500–4999 g).
- ✅ **Foot Protection:** ASTM F2413 with I/75, C/75, EH rating.
- ✅ **Standard Mapping (must memorize):**
  - Head → ANSI Z89.1
  - Eye → ANSI Z87.1
  - Hand → ANSI/ISEA 105
  - Foot → ASTM F2413
  - Hearing → ANSI S3.19
  - Body → NFPA 2112
  - Fall → ANSI Z359.11
- ✅ **Indian Standards (IS):** IS 2925 (head), IS 8520 (eye), IS 8472 (hand), IS 15298 (foot), IS 8518 (PPE general).
- ✅ **Energy of Stored Hydraulic System:** $E = \Delta P \cdot Q \cdot t$ — used to size relief valves and justify PPE zones.
- ✅ **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)** is a prerequisite to PPE use, not a substitute for it.
- ✅ **Always check the Safety Data Sheet (SDS)** for the specific hydraulic fluid in use before finalizing PPE.
- ✅ **Acronym Map:** PPE, HHII, SDS, LOTO, NRR, ATPV, FR, EH, NIOSH, OSHA, ANSI, IS, NFPA, ASTM.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** In any 14-mark PPE question, structure your answer as **(1) Definition → (2) Hazard Identification → (3) PPE Specification with Standards → (4) Engineering Control Precedence → (5) Justification with Calculation**. This 5-part structure maps directly to the marking scheme's 7+7 split.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategies for Energy-Efficient Pumping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strategies for Energy-Efficient Pumping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Energy-Efficient Pumping** refers to the systematic engineering strategies, design practices, and operational protocols adopted to minimize the electrical energy consumed per unit volume of fluid delivered by a pumping system, while satisfying the required flow rate and pressure head specifications. It involves holistic optimization across the *pump, motor, drive, piping network, and operating point* rather than just the pump itself.

### 1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bicycle Effort Principle"

Imagine you are cycling a loaded tricycle up a slope to deliver water bottles.

- If you **oversize the bicycle** (too big a pump for a small job), you waste enormous energy just moving the heavy frame — analogous to running a large pump at a low part-load, where efficiency collapses.
- If you **jam the brakes halfway** (a partially closed valve on the discharge), you pedal furiously but go nowhere — analogous to **throttling losses**, the single biggest silent energy waster in pumping systems.
- If you **switch to lower gears when climbing** (reducing motor speed using a **VFD**), you match effort to slope — analogous to **variable speed pumping**, which can cut energy bills by 30 % to 60 %.

In short: energy-efficient pumping means **matching the pump's supply curve precisely to the system's demand curve**, and minimizing the energy dissipated as heat, noise, vibration, and turbulence in between.

### 1.2 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Priority Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Globally, pumping systems account for **~20 % of all industrial electricity consumption** and **~25 % of municipal water utility energy bills**.
> - The **Hydraulic Institute (HI) and Europump** estimate that **30 % to 50 % of the energy used in pumping could be saved** by applying well-known efficiency strategies — making this a *direct application* of engineering judgment.
> - KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly maps this topic to **Course Outcomes CO2 and CO4** — Analyze pumping systems and design for energy-conscious operation.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Energy-Efficient Pumping

1. **Right Pump Selection** — Choose a pump whose Best Efficiency Point (BEP) coincides with the duty point.
2. **Right System Design** — Minimize pipe friction, eliminate unnecessary fittings, and right-size pipes.
3. **Right Speed Control** — Use Variable Frequency Drives (VFD) instead of throttling valves.
4. **Right Operation & Maintenance** — Monitor, align, and maintain the pump-driver-train to avoid efficiency drift.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (the vocabulary KTU expects)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd}$ | kW | Useful fluid power delivered |
| Shaft Power | $P_{sh}$ | kW | Mechanical power at pump input |
| Pump Efficiency | $\eta_p$ | % | Ratio of hydraulic to shaft power |
| Specific Energy | $E_{sp}$ | kWh/m³ | Energy consumed per m³ pumped |
| Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{ov}$ | % | Combined pump–motor–drive efficiency |
| Power Number | $N_s$ | — | Specific speed index |

> [!NOTE]
> The **specific energy** is the master metric for benchmarking pumping efficiency. Lower specific energy = more efficient system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Energy Flow Chain in a Pumping System

The path of energy from the electrical grid to the fluid in the pipe is:

$$\text{Electrical Input} \xrightarrow{\eta_{motor}} \text{Mechanical Input} \xrightarrow{\eta_{drive}} \text{Shaft Input} \xrightarrow{\eta_{pump}} \text{Hydraulic Output} \rightarrow \text{System Losses}$$

The **overall system efficiency** is therefore:

$$\eta_{system} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{drive} \cdot \eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{mechanical\,transmission}$$

> **Why this matters:** An efficient pump running on an oversized, inefficient motor wastes energy. KTU expects students to evaluate the *system*, not just the pump.

### 2.2 The Pump Performance Curve and the System Curve

A centrifugal pump's actual operating point is the **intersection** of:
- The **Pump Curve (H vs. Q)**: head produced decreases as flow increases.
- The **System Curve (H_sys vs. Q)**: head required by the system increases as flow increases (due to friction).

The system curve is governed by:

$$H_{sys} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^{2}$$

Where:
- $H_{static}$ = static head (elevation difference + pressure head) — **independent of Q**.
- $K \cdot Q^{2}$ = friction head loss — **parabolic in Q**.

> **Engineering Insight:** A flatter system curve (lower K) means the operating point shifts less with flow changes, which is desirable for stable, efficient operation. Reducing pipe friction directly **shrinks K** and moves the system curve down, saving energy.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$ | Hydraulic power delivered to fluid | $P_{hyd}$ in W, $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 2 | $P_{hyd}\,(\text{kW}) = \dfrac{Q \cdot H \cdot \rho}{102}$ | Hydraulic power in kW (metric) | $Q$ in L/s, $H$ in m, $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 3 | $P_{sh} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Shaft power input to pump | $P_{sh}$ in kW |
| 4 | $P_{el} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{drive}}$ | Electrical input power | $P_{el}$ in kW |
| 5 | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{P_{el}}{Q} = \dfrac{H}{\eta_{overall} \cdot 367}$ | Specific energy consumption | $E_{sp}$ in kWh/m³, $H$ in m |
| 6 | $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Affinity Law 1 (Flow) | $N$ = rotational speed (rpm) |
| 7 | $\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2}$ | Affinity Law 2 (Head) | — |
| 8 | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}$ | Affinity Law 3 (Power) | — |
| 9 | $C = \dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{3}$ | Impeller trim coefficient | $D$ = impeller diameter |
| 10 | $E_{saved} = P_{old} \cdot t - P_{new} \cdot t$ | Energy saved per cycle | $E_{saved}$ in kWh, $t$ in h |
| 11 | $C_{LCC} = C_{cap} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_{op,i} + C_{maint,i}}{(1+r)^{i}}$ | Life-Cycle Cost | $C_{cap}$ = capital, $r$ = discount rate, $n$ = life years |
| 12 | $NPSH_{a} = \dfrac{p_{atm} - p_{v}}{\rho g} \pm h_{s} - h_{f,suction}$ | Available NPSH | $p$ in Pa, $h$ in m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Affinity Laws** (Formulae 6, 7, 8) are the *most heavily tested equations* in KTU Module 4. Master them in all three forms — speed variation, impeller trimming, and combined applications.

### 2.4 The Seven Core Strategies for Energy-Efficient Pumping

Each strategy is mapped below with the underlying "why".

#### Strategy 1: Right-Sizing and BEP Matching
A pump operates most efficiently near its **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**. Oversized pumps operate far to the left of BEP, suffering:
- Severe recirculation losses.
- Higher vibration and noise.
- Lower $\eta_p$ (often 20 %–40 % lower).

> **Why:** A pump running at 50 % of BEP flow can lose 15 %–25 % efficiency. KTU expects students to recognize that "bigger is NOT better."

#### Strategy 2: Eliminating Throttling Losses (Use VFDs Instead)
A discharge throttle valve is the worst energy-wasting device in a pump station — it artificially creates head without doing useful work.

| Control Method | Energy Behavior |
|---|---|
| Throttle Valve | Wastes energy as heat in the fluid; $\Delta P$ dissipated |
| VFD on Motor | Recovers energy by reducing speed; **3 % speed reduction = ~10 % energy savings** |

> **Affinity Law insight:** Since $P \propto N^{3}$, a 20 % speed reduction saves $1 - (0.8)^{3} = 48.8\%$ of the power. This is a *huge* lever.

#### Strategy 3: Variable Speed Drives (VFD)
Modern VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives) adjust motor frequency to vary pump speed, which slides the pump curve down through the fixed system curve — the operating point moves along the system curve with minimal waste.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> VFDs are most effective in systems with significant **static head $\leq 30\%$ of total head**. In high-static-head systems, speed reduction is less effective because the system curve is dominated by $H_{static}$ (which is independent of Q).

#### Strategy 4: Impeller Trimming
For constant-speed applications, trimming the impeller diameter is the cheapest long-term efficiency fix.

$$D_{trimmed} = D_{full} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{H_{required}}{H_{full}}}$$

- Power reduction: $P \propto D^{3}$, so even a 10 % diameter cut yields ~27 % power saving.
- Practical limit: impeller should not be trimmed below **75 %–80 % of full diameter** (efficiency drops sharply beyond this).

#### Strategy 5: Hydraulic Network Optimization
- Increase pipe diameter — friction loss $\propto 1/D^{5}$ (Hagen-Poiseuille), so even a 10 % diameter increase can cut friction loss by **~40 %**.
- Minimize bends, valves, and sudden contractions.
- Use long-radius elbows and Y-branches.
- Eliminate redundant fittings.

#### Strategy 6: Parallel and Series Operation Optimization
- **Parallel pumps** boost flow at the same head — useful for varying demand.
- **Series pumps** boost head at the same flow — useful for high-head applications.
- Operating *one* pump near BEP is always more efficient than running *two* pumps at part load. KTU expects the student to evaluate this trade-off.

#### Strategy 7: Life-Cycle Cost (LCC) Analysis
The cheapest pump to **buy** is rarely the cheapest pump to **own**. The decision metric is:

$$\text{LCC} = C_{capital} + C_{installation} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( C_{energy,i} + C_{maintenance,i} \right)$$

> **Rule of thumb (KTU expected):** Energy cost over a 20-year pump life is **5× to 10× the initial purchase price**. A premium-efficiency pump pays for itself in 1–3 years.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal water supply:** Bengaluru's Cauvery water supply project uses VFD-driven pumps to save ~30 % energy.
- **HVAC chilled water systems:** Variable primary flow chilled water pumps (using VFDs) save 50 %–70 % over constant primary systems.
- **Industrial process plants:** API 610 pumps with VFDs and BEP-matched selection are now a *design standard* in petrochemical plants.
- **Irrigation:** Solar-powered VFD pumps are transforming rural Indian agriculture.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Affinity Laws (the most heavily tested derivation)

The **Affinity Laws** are derived from the principle of **dynamic similarity** — two geometrically similar pumps operating at different speeds will have proportional flow, head, and power.

**Step 1: Dimensional proportionality for Flow**

For a geometrically similar pump, all linear dimensions scale by a factor, and time scales by the inverse of rotational speed. Volumetric flow rate has units of [Length³ / Time]:

$$Q \propto L^{3} \cdot N$$

Since geometric similarity means $L \propto D$ (impeller diameter), we get:

$$Q \propto D^{3} \cdot N$$

**Step 2: Dimensional proportionality for Head**

Head has units of [Length]. From Euler's turbomachinery equation, head scales as $u^{2} / g$, where $u$ is the blade tip speed ($u = \pi D N / 60$). So:

$$H \propto u^{2} \propto (D \cdot N)^{2} = D^{2} \cdot N^{2}$$

**Step 3: Dimensional proportionality for Power**

Power is force × velocity, with units of [Mass · Length² / Time³]. Substituting the scaling:

$$P \propto \rho \cdot D^{2} \cdot N^{2} \cdot (D \cdot N) = \rho \cdot D^{5} \cdot N^{3}$$

**Step 4: Ratios for the same pump at different speeds (D constant)**

$$\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$$

$$\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2}$$

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}$$

**Step 5: Ratios for the same pump at different impeller diameters (N constant)**

$$\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{3}$$

$$\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{2}$$

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **cubed** power relationship is what makes variable speed pumping so energy-efficient. This is the single most important physical insight in this entire module.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: VFD Speed Reduction — Energy Savings

> **Problem (KTU Model):** A centrifugal pump delivers $Q_1 = 200$ m³/h at $H_1 = 40$ m with shaft power $P_1 = 32$ kW at $N_1 = 1450$ rpm. The flow requirement drops to $Q_2 = 160$ m³/h. A VFD is installed. Calculate (a) the new motor speed, (b) the new head, (c) the new shaft power, and (d) the energy saved per day (assuming 24-hour operation).

**Step 1: Apply Affinity Law 1 to find new speed**

$$\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$$

$$N_2 = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = 1450 \cdot \dfrac{160}{200}$$

$$N_2 = 1450 \cdot 0.8 = 1160 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2: Apply Affinity Law 2 to find new head**

$$H_2 = H_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2} = 40 \cdot (0.8)^{2}$$

$$H_2 = 40 \cdot 0.64 = 25.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Apply Affinity Law 3 to find new shaft power**

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3} = 32 \cdot (0.8)^{3}$$

$$P_2 = 32 \cdot 0.512 = 16.384 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Compute energy saved per day**

$$P_{saved} = P_1 - P_2 = 32 - 16.384 = 15.616 \text{ kW}$$

$$E_{saved/day} = 15.616 \cdot 24 = 374.78 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 5: Annual savings and monetary impact (assumed ₹8/kWh)**

$$E_{saved/year} = 374.78 \cdot 365 = 136{,}795 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$C_{saved/year} = 136{,}795 \cdot 8 = \text{₹}10{,}94{,}360 \approx \text{₹}10.94 \text{ lakhs/year}$$

> **Engineering Conclusion:** A single VFD on a single pump saves **~₹11 lakhs/year** in this example. The payback period of a ₹4–6 lakh VFD is less than 6 months.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Impeller Trimming — Sizing for a New Duty Point

> **Problem (KTU Model):** A pump with full impeller diameter $D_1 = 320$ mm delivers $Q_1 = 100$ L/s at $H_1 = 50$ m with $P_1 = 65$ kW. The new duty point requires only $H_2 = 38$ m at the same speed. Calculate (a) the trimmed impeller diameter, (b) the new flow rate, and (c) the new power.

**Step 1: Trim ratio from head**

$$\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{2}$$

$$\dfrac{D_2}{D_1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{H_2}{H_1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{38}{50}} = \sqrt{0.76}$$

$$\dfrac{D_2}{D_1} = 0.8718$$

**Step 2: Trimmed diameter**

$$D_2 = 320 \cdot 0.8718 = 278.98 \approx 279 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: New flow rate (constant speed)**

$$\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{3} = (0.8718)^{3} = 0.6626$$

$$Q_2 = 100 \cdot 0.6626 = 66.26 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 4: New power**

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{3} = 65 \cdot 0.6626 = 43.07 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Verify trim is within safe limits**

Trim percentage $= (1 - 0.8718) \cdot 100 = 12.82\%$

Since this is within the **20 % safe-trim limit**, the impeller can be safely trimmed.

> **Conclusion:** A 13 % diameter reduction yields a **33.7 % power saving** — a clear demonstration that even minor geometric modifications yield disproportionate energy benefits.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Energy-Efficient Strategies

| Strategy | Capital Cost | Energy Saving | Payback | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| VFD Installation | Medium–High | 30 %–60 % | 6–18 months | Variable demand systems |
| Impeller Trimming | Low | 10 %–30 % | < 6 months | Constant-speed, fixed duty |
| Pump Replacement (high-$\eta$) | High | 5 %–15 % | 2–5 years | Old, oversized pumps |
| Pipe Sizing Upgrade | High | 10 %–40 % | 5–10 years | Friction-dominated systems |
| Eliminating Throttling | Very Low | 5 %–20 % | Immediate | Systems with control valves |
| Parallel Pump Optimization | Medium | 15 %–35 % | 1–3 years | Highly variable demand |
| Maintenance & Alignment | Very Low | 3 %–10 % | < 3 months | All systems (low-hanging fruit) |

### 3.5 Derivation: Specific Energy (kWh per m³)

The energy consumed per unit volume pumped is the master metric of pumping efficiency:

**Step 1:** Hydraulic power is $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$ (W).

**Step 2:** Electrical input is $P_{el} = P_{hyd} / \eta_{overall}$.

**Step 3:** Energy per unit time per unit flow:

$$E_{sp} = \dfrac{P_{el}}{Q} = \dfrac{\rho g H}{\eta_{overall}}$$

**Step 4:** In convenient units (kWh per m³, with H in m, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²):

$$E_{sp} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot H}{3600 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta_{ov}} = \dfrac{H}{367 \cdot \eta_{ov}} \text{ kWh/m}^{3}$$

> **Benchmark values (KTU expects recall):**
> - World-class municipal pumping: $E_{sp} \approx 0.3$ – $0.5$ kWh/m³
> - Typical Indian municipal pumping: $E_{sp} \approx 0.6$ – $0.9$ kWh/m³
> - Poorly designed system: $E_{sp} > 1.2$ kWh/m³

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Energy-Efficient Pumping Decision Framework

This Mermaid block diagram maps the sequential decision logic a process engineer follows when designing or retrofitting a pumping system for energy efficiency. Each node represents a decision step; arrows show the flow of analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: System Demand Defined] --> A1[Establish Duty Point Q and H]
    A1 --> A2[Compute System Curve Hsys = Hstatic + KQ^2]
    A2 --> A3[Identify Candidate Pumps from Catalog]
    A3 --> A4{Candidate BEP Matches Duty?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Reject Pump and Select Next]
    A5 --> A3
    A4 -- Yes --> A6[Compute Specific Energy Esp]
    A6 --> A7{Esp Below Benchmark?}
    A7 -- No --> A8[Re-examine Pipe Sizing and Friction]
    A8 --> A2
    A7 -- Yes --> A9[Select Motor with High Efficiency Class]
    A9 --> A10[Choose Drive: VFD or DOL]
    A10 --> A11{Demand Variable?}
    A11 -- Yes --> A12[Install VFD for Variable Speed]
    A11 -- No --> A13[Use DOL and Trim Impeller if Needed]
    A12 --> A14[Compute Life Cycle Cost LCC]
    A13 --> A14
    A14 --> A15[Compare LCC Across Options]
    A15 --> A16[Select Final Pump-Motor-Drive System]
    A16 --> A17[End: Energy-Efficient System Specified]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Energy Flow and Loss Points in a Pumping System

This Mermaid block diagram traces the energy cascade from electrical input to fluid output, highlighting *where* energy is lost and *which* strategy addresses each loss.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Grid Supply 11kV / 415V] --> B1[Transformer]
    B1 --> B2[Variable Frequency Drive VFD]
    B2 --> B3[AC Induction Motor]
    B3 --> B4[Coupling and Alignment]
    B4 --> B5[Centrifugal Pump Impeller]
    B5 --> B6[Fluid at Discharge Head]
    B2 -.Loss 3 to 5 percent.-> BX1[VFD Heat Loss]
    B3 -.Loss 5 to 10 percent.-> BX2[Motor I^2R and Iron Loss]
    B4 -.Loss 1 to 2 percent.-> BX3[Mechanical Friction and Misalignment]
    B5 -.Loss 10 to 25 percent.-> BX4[Hydraulic Losses Recirculation Disk Friction]
    B6 -.Loss 5 to 30 percent.-> BX5[Pipe Friction Throttling Bends Fittings]
    BX1 --> S1[Use High Efficiency VFD]
    BX2 --> S2[Use IE3 or IE4 Premium Motor]
    BX3 --> S3[Laser Alignment and Condition Monitoring]
    BX4 --> S4[Operate Near BEP and Trim Impeller]
    BX5 --> S5[Right Size Pipes and Remove Throttling]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The five loss blocks (BX1–BX5) above correspond exactly to the five "efficiency pillars" of the European Pump Efficiency Initiative. KTU questions often ask students to identify *which* loss a given strategy mitigates.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparative Analysis of Control Strategies

This Mermaid diagram contrasts three common flow-control strategies on the same system curve, showing why VFD-based speed control is energetically superior to throttling and bypass-line control.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Duty Requirement Q 80 percent of Design] --> C1{Choose Control Method}
    C1 -- Option 1 --> C2[Throttle Valve on Discharge]
    C1 -- Option 2 --> C3[Bypass Line to Suction]
    C1 -- Option 3 --> C4[VFD on Motor]
    C2 --> C5[Pump Curve Fixed: H Drops Q Rises to Intersection]
    C5 --> C6[Head Dissipated as Heat in Throttle]
    C6 --> C7[Energy Wasted: 25 percent to 40 percent]
    C3 --> C8[Recirculate Excess Flow]
    C8 --> C9[Net Delivery Reduced but Power Same]
    C9 --> C10[Energy Wasted: 15 percent to 30 percent]
    C4 --> C11[Pump Curve Slides Down: H and Q Both Drop]
    C11 --> C12[Affinity Laws Apply: Power Proportional to N Cubed]
    C12 --> C13[Energy Saved: 40 percent to 60 percent]
    C7 --> C14[Throttling: Lowest Efficiency]
    C10 --> C15[Bypass: Moderate Efficiency]
    C13 --> C16[VFD: Highest Efficiency]
```

This Mermaid block diagram summarizes the three classical applications of the affinity laws, mapped to the engineering problem they solve.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Points**
> 1. **Confusing Affinity Law exponents** — Students often write $H \propto N$ or $P \propto N^{2}$ instead of $H \propto N^{2}$ and $P \propto N^{3}$. The exponents (1, 2, 3) are the most-failed detail in KTU scripts. **Memorize the sequence.**
> 2. **Forgetting overall efficiency** in power calculations — KTU expects $P_{el} = P_{hyd} / (\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{drive})$, not just $/ \eta_{pump}$.
> 3. **Impeller trim without verifying the safe trim limit** — KTU deducts marks if you do not check that the trim ratio is $\geq 0.75$–$0.80$.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** in $E_{sp}$ — Always express in kWh/m³ with H in metres and use the constant 367.
> 5. **Ignoring static head when justifying VFDs** — VFDs are ineffective when static head dominates. Always comment on this in design answers.

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
Define the term "specific energy consumption" of a pumping system. State its typical units and the benchmark value for a well-designed municipal water pumping system.

**Model Answer:**
Specific energy consumption ($E_{sp}$) is the electrical energy required to pump one cubic metre of fluid, expressed in **kWh/m³**. It is the master metric for comparing pumping systems.

$$E_{sp} = \dfrac{P_{el}}{Q} = \dfrac{H}{367 \cdot \eta_{ov}} \text{ kWh/m}^{3}$$

- A **well-designed municipal pumping system** has $E_{sp}$ in the range **0.3 to 0.5 kWh/m³**.
- An **average Indian municipal system** typically operates at **0.6 to 0.9 kWh/m³**.
- A **poorly designed or maintained** system exceeds **1.2 kWh/m³**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
List any three energy-saving strategies in pumping systems and explain in one sentence how each saves energy.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Use of VFD (Variable Frequency Drive):** Reduces motor speed according to the affinity law $P \propto N^{3}$, so even a 20 % speed cut yields ~49 % power saving.
2. **Impeller Trimming:** Reduces impeller diameter; since $P \propto D^{3}$, a small geometric change gives large power reduction.
3. **Eliminating Throttling Valves:** Replaces wasteful head dissipation with efficient speed control, recovering 5 %–20 % of the energy.
4. **(Optional) Pipe Sizing Optimization:** Larger pipes reduce friction loss ($\propto 1/D^{5}$), shifting the system curve down.
5. **(Optional) Premium-Efficient Motors (IE3/IE4):** Reduces motor $I^{2}R$ losses by 15 %–25 %.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — KTU Model Paper Style

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

A centrifugal pump delivers $Q_1 = 500$ m³/h at a head of $H_1 = 60$ m, with shaft power $P_1 = 110$ kW and rotational speed $N_1 = 1450$ rpm. The pump operates against a static head of $H_{static} = 20$ m.

**(a) (7 marks)** The duty requirement reduces to $Q_2 = 350$ m³/h. The plant engineer proposes two alternatives:
- **Alternative I:** Install a VFD and reduce the speed.
- **Alternative II:** Trim the impeller diameter.

Calculate for each alternative:
(i) The new head delivered.
(ii) The new shaft power.
(iii) The percentage energy saving.

**(b) (7 marks)** Recommend the better alternative with justification. Also compute the daily energy saved (in kWh) and the annual monetary savings at ₹9 per kWh for 24×7 operation. Comment on the role of static head in VFD effectiveness.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Alternative I: VFD Speed Control**

(i) New speed from Affinity Law 1:

$$N_2 = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = 1450 \cdot \dfrac{350}{500} = 1450 \cdot 0.7 = 1015 \text{ rpm}$$

(ii) New head from Affinity Law 2:

$$H_2 = H_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2} = 60 \cdot (0.7)^{2} = 60 \cdot 0.49 = 29.4 \text{ m}$$

(iii) New shaft power from Affinity Law 3:

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3} = 110 \cdot (0.7)^{3} = 110 \cdot 0.343 = 37.73 \text{ kW}$$

Energy saving:

$$\% \text{ saving} = \dfrac{110 - 37.73}{110} \cdot 100 = 65.7\%$$

**Part (a) — Alternative II: Impeller Trimming**

The new head required is determined by the system curve. For the *system*, the static head is 20 m, and the friction component is $H_f = 60 - 20 = 40$ m at $Q_1 = 500$ m³/h. At $Q_2 = 350$ m³/h:

$$H_{f,2} = H_{f,1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{2} = 40 \cdot (0.7)^{2} = 19.6 \text{ m}$$

$$H_{req} = H_{static} + H_{f,2} = 20 + 19.6 = 39.6 \text{ m}$$

(i) Trim ratio from Affinity Law 2 (diameter form):

$$\dfrac{D_2}{D_1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{H_{req}}{H_1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{39.6}{60}} = \sqrt{0.66} = 0.8124$$

(ii) New power (using $P \propto D^{3}$, since speed is constant):

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{3} = 110 \cdot (0.8124)^{3} = 110 \cdot 0.5362 = 58.98 \text{ kW}$$

$$\% \text{ saving} = \dfrac{110 - 58.98}{110} \cdot 100 = 46.4\%$$

**Part (b) — Recommendation and Daily Savings**

| Metric | VFD (Alt I) | Impeller Trim (Alt II) |
|---|---|---|
| New Power | 37.73 kW | 58.98 kW |
| Energy Saving | 65.7 % | 46.4 % |
| Power Saved | 72.27 kW | 51.02 kW |

**VFD delivers greater energy savings.**

**Daily energy saved (VFD path):**

$$E_{day} = 72.27 \cdot 24 = 1734.48 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Annual energy saved:**

$$E_{year} = 1734.48 \cdot 365 = 633{,}085 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Annual monetary savings (at ₹9/kWh):**

$$C_{saved} = 633{,}085 \cdot 9 = \text{₹}56{,}97{,}765 \approx \text{₹}57 \text{ lakhs/year}$$

**Role of Static Head:**
The static head in this system is $H_{static} = 20$ m, which is **33.3 %** of the original total head (60 m). Since static head is below the 30 %–50 % threshold, **VFD effectiveness is high**. If the static head had been $\geq 70\%$ of total head, VFD effectiveness would be poor because the system curve would be nearly flat, and even small speed reductions would fail to deliver the required head.

**Final Recommendation:** Install a VFD — it saves an additional ~19 percentage points of energy compared to impeller trimming, with an annual monetary benefit of ~₹57 lakhs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) (7 marks)** With the help of neat sketches and equations, derive the affinity laws for a centrifugal pump. State clearly the conditions under which these laws are valid.

**(b) (7 marks)** A pump station serves a municipal water supply scheme. The old pump delivers $Q = 800$ m³/h at $H = 80$ m with $\eta_{pump} = 65\%$, driven by a standard motor with $\eta_{motor} = 88\%$. The utility proposes to replace the old pump with a premium-efficiency pump ($\eta_{pump} = 82\%$) driven by an IE4 motor ($\eta_{motor} = 95\%$). The pump operates 18 hours per day, 330 days per year. Compute:
(i) The hydraulic power and the electrical input power for the **old** system.
(ii) The hydraulic power and the electrical input power for the **new** system.
(iii) The annual energy saved (kWh) and the annual monetary saving at ₹8.50/kWh.
(iv) The simple payback period if the replacement cost is ₹30 lakhs.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Affinity Laws**

Conditions of validity:
- The two operating points must be on **geometrically similar pumps** (i.e., dynamically similar flow).
- The fluid properties (viscosity, density) must remain the same.
- The flow regime must remain similar (no change in Reynolds number regime).

(Provide the derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes, using the three steps of $Q \propto L^{3}/T \Rightarrow D^{3}N$, $H \propto u^{2}/g \Rightarrow D^{2}N^{2}$, and $P \propto \rho QH \Rightarrow D^{5}N^{3}$.)

**Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation**

**Step 1: Hydraulic power (same in both cases — useful fluid power):**

$$P_{hyd} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{1000} \text{ kW} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (800/3600) \cdot 80}{1000}$$

$$P_{hyd} = \dfrac{9.81 \cdot 0.2222 \cdot 80}{1} = 174.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Old system electrical input**

$$\eta_{old} = 0.65 \cdot 0.88 = 0.572$$

$$P_{el,old} = \dfrac{174.4}{0.572} = 304.9 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: New system electrical input**

$$\eta_{new} = 0.82 \cdot 0.95 = 0.779$$

$$P_{el,new} = \dfrac{174.4}{0.779} = 223.9 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Power saved**

$$P_{saved} = 304.9 - 223.9 = 81.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Annual operating hours**

$$t_{annual} = 18 \cdot 330 = 5940 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step 6: Annual energy saved**

$$E_{saved} = 81.0 \cdot 5940 = 481{,}140 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 7: Annual monetary savings (₹8.50/kWh)**

$$C_{saved} = 481{,}140 \cdot 8.50 = \text{₹}40{,}89{,}690 \approx \text{₹}40.9 \text{ lakhs/year}$$

**Step 8: Simple Payback Period**

$$T_{payback} = \dfrac{\text{Capital Cost}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \dfrac{30{,}00{,}000}{40{,}89{,}690} \text{ years}$$

$$T_{payback} = 0.733 \text{ years} \approx 8.8 \text{ months}$$

> **Conclusion:** A high-efficiency pump + IE4 motor retrofit pays for itself in **less than 9 months**, after which the savings are pure profit. This justifies the **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** principle that energy efficiency is the most reliable cost-saving strategy in pumping.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

**Foundational Concepts:**
- ☐ Energy-efficient pumping is a *system-level* optimization, not a pump-only one.
- ☐ Overall efficiency = $\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{drive}$.
- ☐ Specific energy ($E_{sp}$) is the master benchmarking metric (kWh/m³).

**Affinity Laws (memorize the sequence 1, 2, 3):**
- ☐ $Q \propto N \cdot D^{3}$
- ☐ $H \propto N^{2} \cdot D^{2}$
- ☐ $P \propto N^{3} \cdot D^{5}$
- ☐ Valid only under **dynamic similarity** (same fluid, geometrically similar pump, similar flow regime).

**Seven Energy-Saving Strategies:**
- ☐ Right-sizing and BEP matching.
- ☐ Replacing throttling with VFD.
- ☐ VFD installation (effective when $H_{static} \leq 30\%$ of $H_{total}$).
- ☐ Impeller trimming (safe limit: $D_{trim}/D_{full} \geq 0.75$–$0.80$).
- ☐ Hydraulic network optimization (pipe sizing, fewer bends).
- ☐ Parallel/series pump optimization.
- ☐ Life-Cycle Cost (LCC) based selection.

**Numerical Benchmarks (must remember):**
- ☐ Premium efficiency improvement: 5 %–15 %.
- ☐ VFD energy saving: 30 %–60 %.
- ☐ Impeller trim energy saving: 10 %–30 %.
- ☐ Hydraulic losses in typical pump: 10 %–25 %.
- ☐ Friction loss: $\propto 1/D^{5}$ (Hagen-Poiseuille).
- ☐ Power cost over 20 years = 5×–10× capital cost.

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ☐ Do not use Affinity Laws across different pump models (geometric dissimilarity).
- ☐ Always check the static-head fraction before recommending VFDs.
- ☐ Always include all three efficiencies (pump, motor, drive) in power calculations.
- ☐ Trim limits: never trim below 75 % of full impeller diameter.

**Single Most Important Formula to Recall Under Exam Pressure:**

$$\boxed{E_{sp} = \dfrac{H}{367 \cdot \eta_{overall}} \text{ kWh/m}^{3}}$$

> **Closing Note:** The shift toward energy-efficient pumping is no longer optional — it is a regulatory and economic imperative. The Indian Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) star labelling for pumps, the ISO 14414 standard for pump system energy assessment, and the EU's Ecodesign Directive (EU 547/2012) all converge on the same principles covered in this module. A KTU engineer equipped with these tools can deliver value in *any* process industry, from municipal water to petrochemicals to HVAC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pump System Optimization for Energy Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pump System Optimization for Energy Efficiency ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Pump System Optimization for Energy Efficiency** is the systematic engineering process of analyzing, modifying, and operating a pumping system at its Best Efficiency Point (BEP) by matching the pump's hydraulic performance characteristics with the system's resistance curve, thereby minimizing the input shaft power per unit volume of fluid delivered and reducing the **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — typically expressed in **kWh/m³** of liquid pumped. According to the **Hydraulic Institute (HI) Standards**, this process involves: - Pump-system matching - Variable Speed Drive (VSD) application - Hydraulic and mechanical losses reduction - Life-Cycle Cost (LCC) analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Pump system optimization refers to the integrated approach of selecting, operating, and maintaining centrifugal pumps such that the system operates closest to the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**, the pump is correctly sized to the system curve, and energy consumption is minimized over the operational life of the equipment. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Fuel-Efficient Car** > Imagine driving a car with a 5-liter engine to do the job of a 2-liter car. The larger engine consumes far more fuel (energy) for the same trip. Similarly, an oversized pump wastes energy fighting friction in oversized pipes and valves. A second analogy is **bicycle gearing**: pedaling in the wrong gear wastes effort. Pump optimization is essentially "shifting gears" — selecting the correct pump (gear) and trimming the system resistance (terrain) so every watt of input shaft power becomes useful hydraulic energy. ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Standards) - **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** The single operating point on the H-Q curve where pump efficiency $\eta$ is maximum. - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Typically **0.2 – 0.5 kWh/m³** for water pumping. - **Wire-to-Water Efficiency ($\eta_{w/w}$):** Ratio of hydraulic power output to electrical power input. - **Hydraulic Institute (HI) Target Efficiency:** $\geq$ **75 % wire-to-water** for well-designed systems. - **Motor Service Factor:** Standard **1.15** for continuous-duty induction motors. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **wire-to-water efficiency** is the single most important KTU-recommended metric because it captures pump efficiency × motor efficiency × drive efficiency — the entire energy chain. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Pump-System Interaction Model A pump does not operate in isolation. Its operating point is the unique **intersection** of two curves: 1. **Pump Characteristic Curve ($H_p$ vs $Q$):** Supplied by manufacturer. Generally follows a quadratic form: $$H_p = H_0 - kQ^2$$ 2. **System Resistance Curve ($H_s$ vs $Q$):** Determined by static head plus friction: $$H_s = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^2$$ **Optimization Principle:** The duty point must lie on or near the BEP of the pump. If the duty point is far from BEP, three corrective actions exist: - **Trimming the impeller** (reducing diameter — most cost-effective) - **Replacing with a correctly-sized pump** - **Installing a Variable Speed Drive (VSD)** ### 2.2 Pump Affinity Laws (Critical for VSD Optimization) When impeller diameter $D$ or rotational speed $N$ changes, the pump performance scales predictably. **Affinity Law 1 — Flow Rate:** $$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{D_2}{D_1}$$ **Affinity Law 2 — Head:** $$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$ **Affinity Law 3 — Power:** $$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^3$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Cubic Law of Energy:** Since power scales with the **cube** of speed, even a small reduction in speed produces dramatic energy savings. A **20 %** speed reduction saves nearly **49 %** of shaft power. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$ | W (Watts) | $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ | | 2 | Shaft Power | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | W | $\eta_{pump}$ is pump efficiency | | 3 | Wire-to-Water Efficiency | $\eta_{w/w} = \eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{drive}$ | dimensionless | Target ≥ **0.75** | | 4 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{P_{input} \times t}{V}$ | kWh/m³ | $V$ is volume pumped | | 5 | Annual Energy Cost | $AEC = SEC \times V_{annual} \times C_{kWh}$ | ₹/year | $C_{kWh}$ is unit rate | | 6 | Affinity — Flow | $Q \propto N$ | m³/s | Linear in speed | | 7 | Affinity — Head | $H \propto N^2$ | m | Quadratic in speed | | 8 | Affinity — Power | $P \propto N^3$ | W | **Cubic** — key to VSD savings | | 9 | System Curve | $H_s = H_{static} + KQ^2$ | m | $K$ depends on pipe friction | | 10 | Pump Curve (parabolic) | $H_p = a - bQ^2$ | m | $a$ = shut-off head | | 11 | Impeller Trim Law | $\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$ | m | Only for trim, not speed | | 12 | Net Annual Savings | $NAS = (P_{before} - P_{after}) \times t_{annual} \times C_{kWh}$ | ₹ | Used in payback analysis | | 13 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{C_{investment}}{NAS}$ | years | ≤ **2 years** = excellent | | 14 | Life Cycle Cost | $LCC = C_{initial} + \sum (C_{energy} + C_{maint} + C_{downtime})$ | ₹ | Over design life | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Pump system optimization is the cornerstone of: - **Municipal water supply** (savings of 20–50 % energy) - **Industrial process plants** (HVAC, cooling towers, boiler feed) - **Irrigation pumping stations** (target: reduce diesel-electric hybrid costs) - **Building services** (chilled water circulation) The **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India**, mandates PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme coverage for pumping-intensive industries, making this a directly examinable and economically vital topic. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Saving by VSD vs Throttling **Given:** A centrifugal pump delivers $Q_1 = 100$ L/s at $H_1 = 30$ m. System resistance curve: $H_s = 10 + 0.002\,Q^2$ (with $Q$ in L/s, $H$ in m). The process requires a reduced flow $Q_2 = 60$ L/s. **Method A — Throttling (using a control valve):** The valve introduces additional friction loss, but pump speed stays the same. We must find the head the pump must produce at $Q_2$. **Step 1 — Required system head at new flow:** $$H_{s,2} = 10 + 0.002 \times (60)^2 = 10 + 7.2 = 17.2 \, m$$ **Step 2 — Pump power required (throttled):** Using $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81$, $Q_2 = 0.060 \, m^3/s$: $$P_{throttle} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.060 \times 17.2}{\eta_{pump}}$$ **Step 3 — Assuming $\eta_{pump} = 0.75$:** $$P_{throttle} = \frac{10119.7}{0.75} = 13493 \, W \approx 13.5 \, kW$$ **Method B — Variable Speed Drive (speed reduction):** **Step 4 — Use Affinity Law for flow:** $Q \propto N$ $$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.6$$ **Step 5 — Head at reduced speed (Affinity Law for head):** $$H_2 = H_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 30 \times (0.6)^2 = 10.8 \, m$$ **Step 6 — Power at reduced speed (Affinity Law for power):** $$P_{VSD} = P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = P_1 \times (0.6)^3 = 0.216 \, P_1$$ **Step 7 — Original power at $Q_1$:** $$P_1 = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.100 \times 30}{0.75} = 39240 \, W \approx 39.24 \, kW$$ **Step 8 — VSD shaft power:** $$P_{VSD} = 0.216 \times 39.24 = 8.48 \, kW$$ **Step 9 — Energy Saving Percentage:** $$\Delta P = 13.5 - 8.48 = 5.02 \, kW$$ $$\% \, Saving = \frac{5.02}{13.5} \times 100 \approx 37.2 \, \%$$ > [!NOTE] > This derivation demonstrates why **VSDs are the gold-standard KTU-recommended** efficiency improvement. Throttling wastes energy as heat in the valve; VSD eliminates that loss. ### 3.2 Derivation: Annual Energy Cost Savings Calculation **Given:** Energy saved $\Delta P = 5.02 \, kW$. Operating hours per year = 6000 h. Electricity tariff = ₹7/kWh. **Step 1 — Annual energy saved:** $$E_{saved} = 5.02 \times 6000 = 30120 \, kWh/year$$ **Step 2 — Annual monetary savings:** $$AEC_{saved} = 30120 \times 7 = \text{₹ } 210840 \, /\, year$$ **Step 3 — VSD Investment Cost:** Assume ₹ 3,00,000. **Step 4 — Simple Payback Period:** $$SPP = \frac{300000}{210840} \approx 1.42 \, years$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A payback period **less than 2 years** is considered excellent by HI/EU standards. KTU problems frequently test this calculation. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Optimization Techniques Comparison | Technique | Energy Saving | Typical Payback | Capital Cost | Suitability | |-----------|---------------|-----------------|--------------|-------------| | **Impeller Trimming** | 5–15 % | < 1 year | Very Low | Constant-speed, fixed flow | | **VSD Installation** | 20–50 % | 1–3 years | High | Variable flow duty | | **Pump Replacement** | 10–30 % | 2–5 years | Very High | Severely oversized pump | | **Pipe Sizing / Redesign** | 5–20 % | 3–8 years | High | Undersized/fouled pipework | | **Seal & Bearing Upgrade** | 1–3 % | 1–2 years | Low | Aged mechanical equipment | | **Parallel Pump Operation** | 10–25 % | 2–4 years | Moderate | Wide flow variation | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Pump System Optimization Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Step 1: Site Energy Audit"] --> nodeB["Measure Flow Q, Head H, Power P"] nodeB --> nodeC["Plot Operating Point on Pump Curve"] nodeC --> nodeD{"Is Operating Point Near BEP?"} nodeD -- "YES" --> nodeE["System Already Optimized: Routine Maintenance"] nodeD -- "NO" --> nodeF{"Identify Deviation Cause"} nodeF --> nodeG["Oversized Impeller"] nodeF --> nodeH["Excessive Throttling Loss"] nodeF --> nodeI["Variable Flow Demand"] nodeG --> nodeJ["Solution: Trim Impeller or Replace Pump"] nodeH --> nodeK["Solution: Reduce Friction Losses"] nodeI --> nodeL["Solution: Install VSD"] nodeJ --> nodeM["Compute Energy Savings and Payback"] nodeK --> nodeM nodeL --> nodeM nodeM --> nodeN["Implement Optimization Measure"] nodeN --> nodeO["Verify Performance and Re-audit"] nodeO --> nodeE ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Optimized Pumping System ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["ELECTRICAL SUPPLY
415 V 3-Phase AC"] --> nodeB["VFD DRIVE UNIT
Controls Speed N"] nodeB --> nodeC["INDUCTION MOTOR
Efficiency 92 to 96 percent"] nodeC --> nodeD["CENTRIFUGAL PUMP
Operating at Near BEP"] nodeD --> nodeE["DISCHARGE PIPING
Sized for Velocity 1 to 3 m per s"] nodeE --> nodeF["CONTROL VALVE
Minimal Throttling"] nodeF --> nodeG["DELIVERY NETWORK"] nodeD --> nodeH["PRESSURE TRANSDUCER"] nodeH --> nodeI["FLOW METER"] nodeI --> nodeJ["PLC CONTROLLER"] nodeJ --> nodeB ``` ### 4.3 Energy Flow Sankey of an Unoptimized vs Optimized Pump ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["100 units Electrical Input"] --> nodeB{"Unoptimized System
Wire-to-Water 45 percent"} nodeA --> nodeC{"Optimized System
Wire-to-Water 75 percent"} nodeB --> nodeD["Pump Losses 15"] nodeB --> nodeE["Motor Losses 25"] nodeB --> nodeF["Pipe Friction 10"] nodeB --> nodeG["Throttling Losses 5"] nodeB --> nodeH["Useful Hydraulic Power 45"] nodeC --> nodeI["Pump Losses 8"] nodeC --> nodeJ["Motor Losses 6"] nodeC --> nodeK["VFD Losses 3"] nodeC --> nodeL["Pipe Friction 8"] nodeC --> nodeM["Useful Hydraulic Power 75"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The optimized system redirects 30 units of wasted energy into useful hydraulic output, demonstrating the magnitude of achievable savings in KTU-style numerical problems. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Best Efficiency Point (BEP)" of a centrifugal pump. Why is operation at or near BEP preferred for energy efficiency?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Best Efficiency Point (BEP) is the single point on the pump characteristic curve (H-Q curve) at which the pump converts the maximum percentage of input shaft power into useful hydraulic energy (maximum $\eta_{pump}$). Operation at BEP is preferred because (i) it minimizes input power for a given duty, (ii) it minimizes radial thrust on the impeller, extending bearing and mechanical seal life, and (iii) it reduces vibration, cavitation risk, and noise — all contributing to lower Specific Energy Consumption. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** *State the pump affinity laws for variable speed operation. Explain why a small reduction in speed results in a large reduction in power consumption.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The affinity laws state: - $Q \propto N$ - $H \propto N^2$ - $P \propto N^3$ Since power varies with the cube of speed, a **20 % reduction in speed** causes $(0.8)^3 = 0.512$, i.e., shaft power drops to ~**51 %** of original — saving **49 %** of input energy. This cubic relationship is the fundamental principle behind Variable Speed Drive (VSD) energy savings. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - Students frequently forget to apply the **cubic law** for power and incorrectly use only the square-law for head. > - Confusing **trim law** (only head/flow, not power used as cubic) with **speed law**. > - Missing the **motor + VFD + pump efficiency chain** when computing wire-to-water efficiency. > - Failing to convert units: $Q$ from L/s to m³/s and $P$ from W to kW. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** A centrifugal pump delivers $Q = 50 \, L/s$ against a head of $H = 25 \, m$ at $N = 1450 \, rpm$ with pump efficiency $\eta_p = 78 \,\%$. The motor efficiency is $\eta_m = 90 \,\%$ and the drive efficiency is $\eta_d = 95 \,\%$. **(a)** Calculate the (i) hydraulic power, (ii) shaft power, and (iii) wire-to-water efficiency of the system. (7 marks) **(b)** If the process requirement changes to $Q_{new} = 30 \, L/s$ at the same system static head, calculate the energy saved per hour by (i) installing a VSD versus (ii) throttling the existing valve. Comment on which method is more efficient. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** **Part (a) — Step-by-step (7 marks):** **Given:** $Q = 50 \, L/s = 0.050 \, m^3/s$, $H = 25 \, m$, $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$, $\eta_p = 0.78$, $\eta_m = 0.90$, $\eta_d = 0.95$. **(i) Hydraulic Power Output:** $$P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.050 \times 25$$ $$P_{hyd} = 12262.5 \, W \approx 12.26 \, kW$$ **(ii) Shaft Power Input to Pump:** $$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_p} = \frac{12.26}{0.78} = 15.72 \, kW$$ **(iii) Electrical Input Power (Wire-Side):** $$P_{elec} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_m \cdot \eta_d} = \frac{15.72}{0.90 \times 0.95} = \frac{15.72}{0.855} = 18.39 \, kW$$ **Wire-to-Water Efficiency:** $$\eta_{w/w} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{P_{elec}} = \frac{12.26}{18.39} = 0.666 \approx 66.6 \,\%$$ --- **Part (b) — Step-by-step (7 marks):** **Method 1: Throttling (valve closure)** At $Q_{new} = 30 \, L/s$, pump speed remains $1450 \, rpm$. Assume the system curve is parabolic: $H_s = H_{static} + kQ^2$. From original duty: $25 = H_{static} + k(0.050)^2$. Assuming purely frictional system: $H_{static} = 0$, so $k = 25/(0.05)^2 = 10000$. At $Q_{new} = 0.030 \, m^3/s$: $$H_{throttle} = 10000 \times (0.030)^2 = 9.0 \, m$$ Throttled shaft power (using new $Q$ and $H$, same $\eta_p = 0.78$): $$P_{throttle} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.030 \times 9.0}{0.78} = 3396.9 \, W \approx 3.40 \, kW$$ **Method 2: Variable Speed Drive** **Step 1 — Speed ratio:** $$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.6$$ **Step 2 — VSD shaft power:** $$P_{VSD} = P_{shaft,1} \times (0.6)^3 = 15.72 \times 0.216 = 3.396 \, kW$$ **Step 3 — Energy saved per hour:** $$\Delta E = (P_{throttle} - P_{VSD}) \times 1 \, hr = (3.40 - 3.40) \times 1 \approx 0 \, kWh$$ **Comment:** With zero static head, both methods yield nearly identical shaft power. However, in systems with significant static head (e.g., $H_{static} = 15 \, m$), VSD is markedly superior because throttling cannot reduce the static component while VSD reduces the total head via the affinity law. **VSD is the universally preferred method** for variable flow systems with appreciable static head. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]** **(a)** With the help of affinity laws, derive the expression relating shaft power to rotational speed for a centrifugal pump. A pump operating at $N_1 = 2900 \, rpm$ consumes $P_1 = 22 \, kW$. Determine the speed $N_2$ and new power $P_2$ if the flow is to be reduced by 30 %. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain the concept of wire-to-water efficiency with a neat block diagram. A pump has $\eta_{pump} = 75 \,\%$, motor $\eta_{motor} = 92 \,\%$, and VFD efficiency $= 96 \,\%$. Calculate the wire-to-water efficiency and the cost of pumping $1000 \, m^3$ of water at a head of $40 \, m$. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Define dimensionless coefficients (using Euler's turbomachinery equation):** For geometrically similar pumps, the head coefficient $\psi$, flow coefficient $\phi$, and power coefficient $\lambda$ are constants: $$\phi = \frac{Q}{ND^3}, \quad \psi = \frac{gH}{N^2 D^2}, \quad \lambda = \frac{P}{\rho N^3 D^5}$$ **Step 2 — From $\phi$ = constant:** $Q \propto ND^3$. For constant $D$, $Q \propto N$. **Step 3 — From $\psi$ = constant:** $H \propto N^2 D^2$. For constant $D$, $H \propto N^2$. **Step 4 — From $\lambda$ = constant:** $P \propto \rho N^3 D^5$. For constant $D$ and $\rho$: $$P \propto N^3$$ **Numerical Application:** **Step 5 — Speed for 30 % flow reduction:** $$Q_2 = 0.7 Q_1 \implies \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = 0.7$$ $$N_2 = 0.7 \times 2900 = 2030 \, rpm$$ **Step 6 — New shaft power:** $$P_2 = P_1 \times (0.7)^3 = 22 \times 0.343 = 7.546 \, kW$$ --- **Part (b) — Wire-to-Water Efficiency (7 marks):** **Definition:** Wire-to-water efficiency is the ratio of useful hydraulic energy delivered to the fluid to the total electrical energy drawn from the supply line ("wires"). **Block Diagram:** ``` [Electric Supply] → [VFD] → [Motor] → [Coupling] → [Pump] → [Hydraulic Output] (100%) (96%) (92%) (98%) (75%) (= η_w/w) ``` **Step 1 — Compute wire-to-water efficiency:** $$\eta_{w/w} = \eta_{VFD} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{pump} = 0.96 \times 0.92 \times 0.75$$ $$\eta_{w/w} = 0.6624 \approx 66.24 \,\%$$ **Step 2 — Hydraulic energy required for $1000 \, m^3$ at $H = 40 \, m$:** $$E_{hyd} = \rho g V H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1000 \times 40 = 3.924 \times 10^8 \, J$$ $$E_{hyd} = \frac{3.924 \times 10^8}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 109.0 \, kWh$$ **Step 3 — Electrical energy required (from wire):** $$E_{elec} = \frac{E_{hyd}}{\eta_{w/w}} = \frac{109.0}{0.6624} = 164.55 \, kWh$$ **Step 4 — Cost at ₹ 8/kWh:** $$Cost = 164.55 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 1316.4$$ --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Pump System Optimization** - **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** is where $\eta_{pump}$ is maximum — always design duty to be at/near BEP. - **Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, **$P \propto N^3$** — remember the **cube**. - **Wire-to-water efficiency** = $\eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{drive}$ — target ≥ **75 %** per HI standards. - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** in **kWh/m³** is the KPI for benchmarking pumping plants. - **VSD vs Throttling:** VSD saves more energy, especially in systems with high static head. Throttling is cheap but wasteful. - **Impeller trimming** follows $H_2/H_1 = (D_2/D_1)^2$ and $Q_2/Q_1 = D_2/D_1$ (square law, NOT cubic, for power). - **Payback Period** = Investment ÷ Annual Savings; aim for **≤ 2 years** for HI-classified excellent retrofit. - **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** includes initial cost + energy + maintenance + downtime over the design life (typically 15–20 years for pumps). - **Hydraulic Institute Standards** (HI 9.6.6, HI 40.5) govern rotodynamic pump testing and net positive suction head (NPSH) margin (typically **+0.5 m to +1.0 m** above required NPSH). - **Energy Audit Sequence:** Measure → Plot operating point → Compare with BEP → Identify deviation → Apply correction → Verify. - **Common KTU Pitfalls:** Unit conversion errors (L/s to m³/s), missing the cubic law, ignoring drive efficiency in wire-to-water calculation.

topics.title: Energy Audit Techniques for Pump Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Audit Techniques for Pump Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Energy Audit of a Pump System** is a systematic, engineered process of quantifying the energy flows, identifying losses, evaluating operating efficiencies, and recommending cost-effective modifications in a pumping installation to reduce specific energy consumption (SEC) while maintaining the required hydraulic performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Bureau of Energy Efficiency – BEE aligned):**
> A pump system energy audit is the **systematic documentation, measurement, and verification** of electrical input power, hydraulic output power, system head, flow rate, and ancillary losses to evaluate the **Wire-to-Water Efficiency (η<sub>W-W</sub>)** of the pump against the design (BEP) efficiency.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\eta_{W\text{-}W} \;=\; \frac{P_{hydraulic,output}}{P_{electrical,input}} \;=\; \frac{\rho \, g \, Q \, H}{P_{in}} \;\times\; 100\%$$

where $\rho$ is fluid density (kg/m³), $g$ is gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**), $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate (m³/s), $H$ is the total developed head (m), and $P_{in}$ is the electrical power drawn at the motor terminals (W).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Heart-Circulation Checkup"**
> Think of a pump system like the human circulatory system. An energy audit is a **full cardiac stress test** for your pump:
> - **Blood flow** = water flow rate (Q)
> - **Blood pressure** = pump head (H)
> - **Heartbeat strength** = efficiency
> - **Calories burnt** = electrical kWh consumed
> 
> Just as a cardiologist measures how much oxygen your body actually *uses* versus how much your heart *pumps*, a pump auditor measures how much hydraulic *useful work* your pump delivers versus how much *electrical energy* it eats from the grid. The gap between the two is **wasted money in the form of heat, vibration, throttling, leaks, and over-sizing**.

## 1.3 Why Pump Audits Matter in KTU / Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Industrial pumps consume **~27% of all industrial electricity worldwide** (Hydraulic Institute / IEA data).
> - In Indian industry, **~30–60% of pumps operate away from their Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** with η<sub>W-W</sub> below **40%**.
> - A typical well-executed energy audit recovers **5–25% of pump energy** with payback under 18 months.

## 1.4 Salient Metrics to Remember

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Wire-to-Water Efficiency | $\eta_{W\text{-}W}$ | % |
| Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC$ | kWh / m³ |
| Pump Efficiency | $\eta_{pump}$ | % |
| Motor Efficiency | $\eta_{motor}$ | % |
| Flow Rate | $Q$ | m³/hr or LPS |
| Total Dynamic Head | $TDH$ | m |
| Power Input | $P_{in}$ | kW |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Energy Audits (BEE / ISO 14414 Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO 14414:2015** is the *de facto* global benchmark specifically for **pump system energy evaluation**. KTU examiners frequently quote its three audit tiers.

The energy audit of a pump system is classified into three hierarchical levels, each progressively deeper in instrumentation and analytical rigor.

### 2.1.1 Level 1 — Walk-Through Audit (Preliminary Audit)
- **Goal:** Rapid screening; identify "low-hanging fruit."
- **Instruments:** Visual inspection, nameplate reading, logbook review.
- **Duration:** 1–3 days.
- **Output:** Qualitative recommendations (e.g., trimming impeller, replacing worn seals).
- **Investment:** Minimal; often internal manpower only.

### 2.1.2 Level 2 — Standard Energy Audit (Targeted Audit)
- **Goal:** Quantify baseline performance; benchmark against design.
- **Instruments:** Portable clamp-on flowmeter, digital manometer, tachometer, power analyzer (e.g., Fluke 435), ultrasonic thickness gauge.
- **Duration:** 2–6 weeks.
- **Output:** kWh saved, payback periods, ECMs (Energy Conservation Measures).
- **Investment:** Moderate; engineering consultancy engagement.

### 2.1.3 Level 3 — Detailed / Investment-Grade Audit
- **Goal:** Pinpoint design-level modifications; M&V (Measurement & Verification) ready.
- **Instruments:** Permanent flowmeters (magnetic, vortex), pressure transmitters on suction and discharge, RTDs, calibrated power meters, vibration analyzers.
- **Duration:** 3–6 months; multi-stage.
- **Output:** Detailed M&V plan, CAPEX-grade feasibility, ISO 50001 action plan.
- **Investment:** High; specialist ESCO engagement.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "What type of audit would you recommend for a ₹50 lakh retrofit project?", justify Level 3. The auditor must explain *why* the deeper instrumentation is justified (e.g., M&V accuracy ±2%, IPMVP compliance).

## 2.2 Audit Methodology — The Seven-Stage Procedure

The KTU syllabus follows the seven-stage protocol derived from **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) India — Pumps & Pumping Systems Audit Manual**:

### Stage 1: Pre-Audit Planning
- Collect plant single-line diagrams, P&IDs, pump data sheets.
- Identify system boundary (Scope 1, 2, 3 emissions relevance).
- Verify operating hours per year ($t_{op}$) — base load, peak, idle.

### Stage 2: Field Survey & Inventory
- Tabulate every pump with: tag number, duty, rated Q/H/P, age, manufacturer, retrofit history.
- Categorize as **Continuous**, **Intermittent**, or **Standby**.

### Stage 3: Baseline Performance Mapping
Plot the existing operating point on the **H-Q curve** and the **system resistance curve** (parabolic, $H_{sys} = H_{static} + K Q^2$). Mismatches reveal over-design.

### Stage 4: Field Measurements
- Suction and discharge gauge pressure ($P_s$, $P_d$).
- Flow ($Q$) via clamp-on ultrasonic or inline meter.
- Voltage and current → compute $P_{in}$.
- Speed ($N$) via optical tachometer.

### Stage 5: Efficiency Computation
Compute wire-to-water efficiency using measurements:

$$\eta_{W\text{-}W} \;=\; \frac{\rho g Q (H_d - H_s + \Delta z)}{P_{in}}$$

where $H_d$, $H_s$ are discharge and suction head in metres of fluid, and $\Delta z$ is the elevation difference between gauge tapping points.

### Stage 6: Loss Decomposition
- **Hydraulic losses** (impeller surface roughness, recirculation): via pump curve deviation.
- **Mechanical losses** (bearing friction, mechanical seal): via torque measurement.
- **Volumetric losses** (recirculation past wear rings): via differential pressure analysis.
- **Motor losses** (copper, iron, stray): via no-load and locked-rotor tests.

### Stage 7: Recommendation & Reporting
Build an **Energy Conservation Measure (ECM)** matrix ranked by:
- Annual energy saving (kWh)
- Annual monetary saving (₹)
- Simple Payback Period (SPP) = CAPEX / Annual Savings
- Return on Investment (ROI)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Energy Audit of Pumps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No KTU exam on Pump Audits is solvable without these 12 equations.** Memorize units and conditions.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$ | Hydraulic power (output) | W | Always |
| 2 | $\eta_{W\text{-}W} = P_{hyd}/P_{in}$ | Wire-to-water efficiency | — | 0 to 1 |
| 3 | $\eta_{pump} = P_{hyd}/P_{shaft}$ | Pump hydraulic efficiency | — | ISO 9906 |
| 4 | $\eta_{motor} = P_{shaft}/P_{in}$ | Motor efficiency | — | IE3/IE4 |
| 5 | $SEC = P_{in}/(Q \cdot 1000)$ | Specific energy consumption | kWh/m³ | Standard audit KPI |
| 6 | $H = (P_d - P_s)/(\rho g) + \Delta z$ | Total developed head | m | Steady flow |
| 7 | $H_{sys} = H_{static} + K Q^2$ | System resistance curve | m | Turbulent flow |
| 8 | $E_{saved} = P_{saved} \cdot t_{op}$ | Annual energy savings | kWh/yr | 8760 h max |
| 9 | $SPP = CAPEX / A_{saving}$ | Simple payback period | years | SPP < 3 yr is high |
| 10 | $\Delta P = (1 - \eta_{old}/\eta_{new}) \cdot t_{op}$ | Power-saving ratio | — | ECM ranking |
| 11 | $Q_{2} = Q_{1}(N_{2}/N_{1})$ | Affinity Law — Flow | m³/s | Geometrically similar |
| 12 | $H_{2} = H_{1}(N_{2}/N_{1})^2$ | Affinity Law — Head | m | Geometrically similar |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical sign convention:** Head is **positive when added to the fluid** (suction lift = **negative** at gauge). Forgetting the sign of $\Delta z$ leads to a systematic 5–10% error and examiners will *deduct 2 full marks* for it.

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where pump audits are non-negotiable:**
> - ISO 50001 EnMS compliance certification (mandatory for large energy consumers).
> - Perform-Achieve-Trade (PAT) scheme cycle under BEE India — mandatory for Designated Consumers.
> - ESCO (Energy Service Company) M&V under IPMVP Protocol.
> - ESG reporting — Scope 2 emissions reduction.
> - Hydraulic Institute & Europump joint efforts (HI 40.6) — sealing efficiency baselines.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From First Principles to Wire-to-Water Efficiency

We derive the Wire-to-Water Efficiency from fundamental thermodynamics of the pump-motor-fluid triad.

### Step 1: Energy Balance Across the Pump-Motor Assembly
The electrical energy entering the motor terminals is progressively degraded through each subsystem:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{electrical,input} &\rightarrow \eta_{motor} \rightarrow P_{shaft} \\
&\rightarrow \eta_{pump,mech} \rightarrow P_{impeller,mech} \\
&\rightarrow \eta_{pump,hyd} \rightarrow P_{hydraulic,fluid}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Define Each Efficiency Block
- $\eta_{motor} = P_{shaft}/P_{input}$
- $\eta_{mech} = P_{rotor-net}/P_{shaft}$ (accounts for bearing + seal friction)
- $\eta_{vol} = Q/(Q + Q_{leak})$ (accounts for internal recirculation)
- $\eta_{hyd} = (gH)/(u_2 \cdot V_{u2})$ from Euler's turbomachinery equation.

### Step 3: Combine in Cascade
The combined (wire-to-water) efficiency is the product of all intermediate stages:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{W\text{-}W} &= \frac{P_{hyd}}{P_{el,in}} \\
&= \frac{P_{hyd}}{P_{rotor}} \cdot \frac{P_{rotor}}{P_{shaft}} \cdot \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{el,in}} \\
&= \eta_{hyd} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{motor}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Expand Hydraulic Power
The hydraulic output power delivered to the fluid stream is, by definition of work done per unit time:

$$P_{hyd} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H$$

### Step 5: Combine Both Halves
Substituting, the closed-form **audit-grade** Wire-to-Water Efficiency equation:

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{W\text{-}W} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, g \, Q \, H}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{L} \, I_{L} \, \cos\phi \, \eta_{step\,up}} \;\times\; 100\%\;}$$

where $V_L$, $I_L$ are line voltage and line current; $\cos\phi$ is the power factor; $\eta_{step\,up}$ is the transformer efficiency (taken as **0.98** in plant-side audits).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — System Resistance Curve (Parabolic Law)

### Step 1: Darcy–Weisbach Friction in Piping
For each straight pipe segment of length $L$ and diameter $D$, the head loss is:

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

with $V = Q/A$ and $A = \pi D^2/4$.

### Step 2: Substitute V in terms of Q

$$\begin{aligned}
h_f &= f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{Q^2}{2g \, A^2} \\
    &= \underbrace{\left(f \cdot \frac{8 L}{\pi^2 g D^5}\right)}_{K_{pipe}} \cdot Q^2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Sum All Components
The system resistance curve is the sum of static head plus all quadratic losses (fittings, valves, exchangers):

$$H_{sys} = H_{static} + \sum_{i} K_i \, Q^2$$

For a single straight pipe with one fully-open valve and several fittings, this is typically written as:

$$H_{sys} = H_{static} + K_{eq} \cdot Q^2$$

where $K_{eq}$ is the equivalent system resistance coefficient (units: **m / (m³/s)²**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Audit Implication:** During energy audit, if the actual operating point lies far to the right of the BEP on the system curve, it indicates **excessive throttling** — one of the highest-ROI ECMs (replace control valve with VFD).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

The SEC is the **single most important KPI** in any pump audit report. It normalizes energy use against the *useful product* delivered.

### Step 1: Define SEC
SEC is the electrical energy consumed per unit volume of fluid delivered:

$$SEC = \frac{E_{electrical}}{V_{pumped}} = \frac{P_{input} \cdot t}{Q \cdot t} = \frac{P_{input}}{Q}$$

with $SEC$ in **kWh per m³**, $P_{input}$ in **kW**, and $Q$ in **m³/h**.

### Step 2: Substitute Wire-to-Water Efficiency
Since $P_{input} = P_{hyd}/\eta_{W\text{-}W} = \rho g Q H / \eta_{W\text{-}W}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
SEC &= \frac{\rho \, g \, Q \, H}{\eta_{W\text{-}W} \cdot Q} \\
    &= \frac{\rho \, g \, H}{\eta_{W\text{-}W}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Numerical Sanity Check for Water
For water at $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$, $H = 50$ m, $\eta_{W\text{-}W} = 60\%$:

$$SEC = \frac{1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 50}{0.60 \cdot 3600 \cdot 1000} = \frac{490{,}500}{2{,}160{,}000} \cdot 1000$$

$$\boxed{\;SEC \approx 0.227 \;\text{kWh/m}^3\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU bench-mark:** For typical HVAC chilled-water pumps, acceptable $SEC$ lies between **0.15 and 0.30 kWh/m³**. Anything above 0.35 kWh/m³ should trigger an immediate ECM study.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Annual Energy Saving from ECM

### Step 1: Baseline Power
Baseline pump electrical input (before ECM):

$$P_{old} = \frac{\rho g Q H_{old}}{\eta_{W\text{-}W,\,old}}$$

### Step 2: Post-ECM Power
After an ECM (e.g., trimming impeller, installing VFD, replacing pump):

$$P_{new} = \frac{\rho g Q_{new} H_{new}}{\eta_{W\text{-}W,\,new}}$$

### Step 3: Net Energy Saved per Annum
Multiplying the differential power by the actual operating hours $t_{op}$:

$$E_{saved} = (P_{old} - P_{new}) \cdot t_{op}$$

### Step 4: Cost Saving
At an electricity tariff $C_e$ (₹/kWh):

$$A_{saving} = E_{saved} \cdot C_e$$

### Step 5: Simple Payback Period
SPP is the breakeven time of the capital investment $I_0$:

$$SPP = \frac{I_0}{A_{saving}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
