> **KTU rule of thumb for selection:** ECMs with $SPP \leq 2$ years are **priority-1**; $2 < SPP \leq 4$ are priority-2; $SPP > 4$ require justification beyond energy (e.g., reliability, safety).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical ECMs and Their Expected Savings

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asks:** "List 5 ECMs in a pump audit and indicate approximate savings percentage." Memorize this matrix.

| ECM # | Energy Conservation Measure | Typical Saving % | Payback (yr) | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ECM-1 | Replace throttling valve with VFD | 20–50 | 1.5–3 | Low |
| ECM-2 | Trim impeller diameter (5–10%) | 5–15 | 0.5–1 | Low |
| ECM-3 | Replace oversized pump with right-sized | 10–30 | 1–3 | Low |
| ECM-4 | Install parallel-pump array (N+1) | 15–40 | 2–4 | Medium |
| ECM-5 | Eliminate by-pass recirculation | 5–20 | 0.3–1 | Low |
| ECM-6 | Replace mechanical seals (leak reduction) | 2–8 | 1–2 | Low |
| ECM-7 | Upgrade to IE4/IE5 premium motor | 2–5 | 2–5 | Low |
| ECM-8 | De-scale heat exchangers / condensers | 5–15 | 0.5–2 | Medium |
| ECM-9 | Replace corroded pipes (reduce friction) | 3–10 | 2–5 | Medium |
| ECM-10 | Install soft-starter (reduce inrush only) | 1–3 | 2–4 | Very Low |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style

**Problem (KTU-pattern, 7 marks):**
A centrifugal pump delivers $Q = 250$ m³/h of water against a total head of $H = 30$ m. The motor draws $P_{in} = 28$ kW at a power factor of 0.88 and line voltage 415 V. Compute (a) the wire-to-water efficiency, (b) the specific energy consumption, and (c) the annual energy cost if the pump runs 5500 hours/year at ₹7.50/kWh. Comment on whether an ECM is justified.

### Solution

**(a) Wire-to-Water Efficiency**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{hyd} &= \rho \, g \, Q \, H \\
&= 1000 \times 9.81 \times \frac{250}{3600} \times 30 \\
&= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.06944 \times 30 \\
&= 20{,}437.5 \;\text{W} \;=\; 20.44 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\eta_{W\text{-}W} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{P_{in}} = \frac{20.44}{28} = 0.730 = 73.0\%$$

**(b) Specific Energy Consumption**

$$SEC = \frac{P_{in}}{Q} = \frac{28}{250} = 0.112 \;\text{kWh/m}^3$$

**(c) Annual Energy Cost**

$$A_{cost} = P_{in} \times t_{op} \times C_e = 28 \times 5500 \times 7.50 = ₹\,11{,}55{,}000$$

**(d) ECM Justification Check**

At $\eta_{W\text{-}W} = 73\%$, the pump operates near BEP. SEC = 0.112 kWh/m³ is below the KTU benchmark of 0.15–0.30 kWh/m³, indicating **no priority ECM** is required. However, ECM-1 (VFD) may still be considered if load profile varies significantly.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Seven-Stage Pump Energy Audit Protocol

This flowchart maps the complete procedure a BEE-aligned auditor follows, including the decision gates at each level.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Pre-Audit Planning] --> B[Stage 2: Field Survey and Pump Inventory]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Baseline H-Q Operating Point Plot]
    C --> D{Operating Point vs BEP Check}
    D -- Far from BEP --> E[Stage 4a: Level 1 Walk-Through Audit]
    D -- Slightly off BEP --> F[Stage 4b: Level 2 Standard Audit]
    D -- Critical process pump --> G[Stage 4c: Level 3 Investment-Grade Audit]
    E --> H[Stage 5: Field Measurements]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Stage 6: Loss Decomposition]
    I --> J[Stage 7: ECM Ranking and Reporting]
    J --> K{SPP less than 3 years?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Implement ECM Priority 1]
    K -- No --> M[Defer or Reject]
    L --> N[M and V Verification]
    M --> O[Archive and Re-audit in 2 years]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Pump Energy Audit System

This architecture shows how measurement devices, data acquisition, and reporting layers interact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FieldLayer["Field Layer"]
        A1[Clamp-on Ultrasonic Flowmeter]
        A2[Digital Pressure Transmitter Suction]
        A3[Digital Pressure Transmitter Discharge]
        A4[Three-Phase Power Analyzer]
        A5[Optical Tachometer]
    end

    subgraph AcquisitionLayer["Data Acquisition Layer"]
        B1[DAQ Module 16 Channel]
        B2[Signal Conditioner 4-20 mA]
        B3[Vibration Sensor Triaxial]
    end

    subgraph ComputeLayer["Compute and Audit Engine"]
        C1[Head Calculation Node]
        C2[Efficiency Calculator Node]
        C3[System Curve Generator]
        C4[ECM Ranker Algorithm]
    end

    subgraph ReportingLayer["Reporting Layer"]
        D1[PDF Audit Report Generator]
        D2[ISO 50001 Compliance Dashboard]
        D3[Cost and Payback Worksheet]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B1
    A5 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C4 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Audit Process Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Planning
    Planning --> FieldSurvey: Documents Collected
    FieldSurvey --> Measurement: Inventory Complete
    Measurement --> Analysis: Data Logged for 168 hours
    Analysis --> Recommendation: Efficiency Computed
    Recommendation --> Implementation: SPP less than 3 years
    Recommendation --> Archive: SPP greater than 3 years
    Implementation --> Verification: ECM Installed
    Verification --> [*]: M and V Report Filed
    Archive --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Plant-Audit Boundary Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EnergyIn["Energy Input Boundary"]
        E1[Grid Electricity kWh meter]
        E2[Diesel Generator Backup kWh meter]
    end

    subgraph MotorStage["Motor Stage"]
        M1[Three Phase Induction Motor IE3]
        M2[VFD Optional ECM-1]
    end

    subgraph CouplingStage["Coupling and Mechanical"]
        C1[Flexible Coupling]
        S1[Mechanical Seal with Flush Plan]
    end

    subgraph PumpStage["Pump Hydraulic Stage"]
        P1[Centrifugal Pump Impeller]
        P2[Wear Rings and Volute]
    end

    subgraph HydraulicStage["Hydraulic Output Boundary"]
        H1[Discharge Pipe Network]
        H2[Flow Measurement Point]
    end

    E1 --> M2
    E2 --> M1
    M2 --> M1
    M1 --> C1
    C1 --> S1
    S1 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> H1
    H1 --> H2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Wire-to-Water Efficiency" in the context of a pump energy audit. Mention its typical range for a well-designed centrifugal pump.**

**Model Answer:**
Wire-to-Water Efficiency ($\eta_{W\text{-}W}$) is the ratio of hydraulic power delivered to the fluid stream by the pump to the electrical power drawn by the motor at its terminals. It is the combined efficiency of the motor, mechanical coupling, and pump hydraulic stages. Mathematically:

$$\eta_{W\text{-}W} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{P_{el,in}} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{P_{in}}$$

For a properly selected and maintained centrifugal pump operating near its Best Efficiency Point (BEP), $\eta_{W\text{-}W}$ typically ranges from **65% to 80%**. Anything below 50% indicates significant inefficiency warranting an audit-driven ECM.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) applicable to a centrifugal pump system. State the typical payback period for each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Replace throttle-control valve with Variable Frequency Drive (VFD):** Saves 20–50% energy; SPP 1.5–3 years.
2. **Trim impeller diameter:** Saves 5–15% energy; SPP 0.5–1 year.
3. **Replace oversized pump with right-sized unit:** Saves 10–30% energy; SPP 1–3 years.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **three levels of pump energy audit** as per ISO 14414:2015, highlighting instrumentation requirements and expected accuracy for each level.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A centrifugal pump delivers $Q = 400$ m³/h of water against $H = 45$ m. Motor input is 75 kW at PF = 0.90, 415 V, 3-phase. Compute the Wire-to-Water Efficiency, Specific Energy Consumption, and annual operating cost at 6000 hours/year and ₹8.00/kWh. Recommend whether a VFD retrofit is justified.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The three audit levels per ISO 14414:2015 are:

**Level 1 — Walk-Through Audit:**
- Minimal instrumentation: visual inspection, nameplate readings, clamp-meter.
- Accuracy: ±15–20% on energy savings estimate.
- Output: preliminary ECM list; <3 days on site.
- Typical use: small industries, screening studies.

**Level 2 — Standard Energy Audit:**
- Moderate instrumentation: portable ultrasonic flowmeter, digital manometer, three-phase power analyzer, tachometer.
- Accuracy: ±5–10% on savings estimate.
- Output: calibrated baseline, ECM ranking, payback analysis.
- Typical use: medium industries; PAT cycle submissions.

**Level 3 — Investment-Grade Audit (Detailed):**
- Full instrumentation: inline magnetic flowmeter, RTD on motor windings, calibrated power meter (Class 0.5), vibration spectrum analyzer.
- Accuracy: ±2% on savings (IPMVP Option B compliant).
- Output: M&V-grade report, CAPEX-grade engineering, ISO 50001-ready.
- Typical use: large industries, ESCO projects, mandatory PAT audit.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Compute Hydraulic Power**

$$P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times \frac{400}{3600} \times 45 = 49{,}050 \;\text{W} = 49.05 \;\text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Wire-to-Water Efficiency**

$$\eta_{W\text{-}W} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{P_{in}} = \frac{49.05}{75} = 0.654 = 65.4\%$$

**Step 3: Specific Energy Consumption**

$$SEC = \frac{P_{in}}{Q} = \frac{75}{400} = 0.1875 \;\text{kWh/m}^3$$

**Step 4: Annual Energy Cost**

$$A_{cost} = 75 \times 6000 \times 8.00 = ₹\,36{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 5: VFD Retrofit Justification**

At $\eta_{W\text{-}W} = 65.4\%$, the pump is moderately efficient. SEC of 0.1875 kWh/m³ lies within the acceptable KTU benchmark (0.15–0.30). However, **if the load profile shows >30% variation** (typical in HVAC, process cooling), a VFD retrofit would save an estimated 20–35% energy, equivalent to ₹7.2 lakh to ₹12.6 lakh per year. With typical VFD cost of ₹5–8 lakh for a 75 kW unit, **SPP ≈ 0.5–1 year → STRONGLY RECOMMENDED**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a pump energy audit system**, listing the key measurement parameters and instruments at each stage.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A pump system operates 7200 hours/year at an average efficiency of 45%. The proposed ECM improves efficiency to 65% at the same duty point. If the present input power is 50 kW and the tariff is ₹7.00/kWh, compute the (i) annual energy saving in kWh, (ii) annual monetary saving, and (iii) justify an ECM costing ₹4 lakh.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The pump energy audit functional architecture (block diagram) comprises **four layers**:

1. **Field Measurement Layer:** Clamp-on ultrasonic flowmeter (Q), digital pressure transmitters (P<sub>s</sub>, P<sub>d</sub>), three-phase power analyzer (V, I, kW, PF), optical tachometer (N), vibration sensor (mm/s).
2. **Data Acquisition Layer:** 16-channel DAQ module, 4–20 mA signal conditioner, vibration spectrum analyzer.
3. **Compute and Audit Engine:** Head calculator, wire-to-water efficiency calculator, system curve generator, ECM ranker.
4. **Reporting Layer:** PDF audit report generator, ISO 50001 dashboard, cost-payback worksheet.

(See SECTION 4.2 for the Mermaid block diagram of this architecture.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Calculate New Input Power**

$$P_{new} = P_{old} \cdot \frac{\eta_{old}}{\eta_{new}} = 50 \times \frac{0.45}{0.65} = 34.615 \;\text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Annual Energy Saving**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= P_{old} - P_{new} = 50 - 34.615 = 15.385 \;\text{kW} \\
E_{saved} &= \Delta P \times t_{op} = 15.385 \times 7200 = 1{,}10{,}769 \;\text{kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Annual Monetary Saving**

$$A_{saving} = 1{,}10{,}769 \times 7.00 = ₹\,7{,}75{,}385 \;\text{per year}$$

**Step 4: Simple Payback Period**

$$SPP = \frac{CAPEX}{A_{saving}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{7{,}75{,}385} \approx 0.516 \;\text{years} \;\approx\; 6.2 \;\text{months}$$

**Step 5: Justification**

With SPP ≈ **6 months**, this is a **Priority-1 ECM** (KTU rule: SPP < 1 year → priority-1, must implement). ROI is exceptional.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Sign of suction head:** Many students take $H_s$ as positive when the pump has a suction lift. Always convert to "head added to fluid" — suction lift is *negative*. Marks deducted: 2/7.
> 2. **Units consistency:** Mixing m³/s with m³/h without conversion yields $\eta$ either >100% or negative. Convert Q to m³/s *before* applying $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$. Marks deducted: 1–2/7.
> 3. **Forgetting power factor:** When computing input kW from V, I, the formula is $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_L\,\cos\phi$, not $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_L$. Marks deducted: 2/7.
> 4. **Annual energy vs operating hours:** Multiplying by 8760 h instead of the *actual* operating hours yields inflated savings. Marks deducted: 1–2/7.
> 5. **Affinity Laws used wrongly:** Affinity laws apply only for **geometrically similar** impellers at constant efficiency. Applying them across dissimilar pumps is a common blunder. Marks deducted: 2/7.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Energy Audit Techniques for Pump Systems**

- **Definition:** Energy audit is systematic measurement, evaluation, and reporting of energy use in a pump system with the goal of reducing SEC while maintaining hydraulic output.
- **Three audit levels (ISO 14414:2015):** Walk-through (±15–20%) → Standard (±5–10%) → Investment-Grade (±2%).
- **Wire-to-Water Efficiency:** $\eta_{W\text{-}W} = \rho g Q H / P_{el,in}$. Typical range **65–80%** for a healthy pump.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** $SEC = P_{in}/Q$ in **kWh/m³**. Benchmark for water pumps: **0.15–0.30 kWh/m³**.
- **System Curve:** $H_{sys} = H_{static} + K_{eq} Q^2$ — parabolic. Operating point should lie on this curve *and* near pump BEP.
- **Affinity Laws (only for geometrically similar impellers):** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.
- **Top 5 ECMs (ranked by ROI):**
  1. VFD retrofit (20–50% saving, SPP 1.5–3 yr)
  2. Impeller trimming (5–15% saving, SPP 0.5–1 yr)
  3. Right-sizing pump (10–30% saving, SPP 1–3 yr)
  4. Eliminating bypass recirculation (5–20% saving, SPP 0.3–1 yr)
  5. IE4/IE5 motor upgrade (2–5% saving, SPP 2–5 yr)
- **Simple Payback Period (SPP):** $SPP = \text{CAPEX} / \text{Annual Savings}$.
  - SPP < 1 yr → Priority 1 (must do)
  - 1–3 yr → Priority 2
  - 3–5 yr → Priority 3
  - >5 yr → Reject (energy-only basis)
- **Mandatory instruments for Level-2 audit:** clamp-on ultrasonic flowmeter, digital manometer, three-phase power analyzer, optical tachometer, vibration pen.
- **BEE / PAT compliance:** Mandatory for Designated Consumers in India.
- **Standards to cite in audit report:** ISO 14414:2015, ISO 9906 (pump testing), HI 40.6 (efficiency rating), IPMVP (M&V).
- **Common loss decomposition:** motor copper + iron + stray → mechanical seal + bearing → wear-ring recirculation → volute surface friction → discharge throttling.
- **Sign conventions (most-failed topic):** Suction lift is *negative*; elevation is *added* if discharge tapping is higher than suction tapping; $H_{static}$ in system curve is the gravity-opposing component.
- **Annual energy formula:** $E = P \cdot t_{op}$. Use *actual* operating hours, not 8760 h, unless plant operates continuously.
- **Audit report must include:** Baseline $\eta_{W\text{-}W}$, SEC, kWh saved, ₹ saved, SPP, M&V plan, and re-audit interval (typically 2 years).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High-Efficiency Pump Designs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** High-Efficiency Pump Designs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**High-Efficiency Pump Design** refers to the integrated engineering methodology that optimizes the geometric, fluid-dynamic, and mechanical parameters of a centrifugal or axial-flow pump to maximize the ratio of useful hydraulic energy delivered to the fluid versus the mechanical energy supplied at the shaft.

Mathematically, the overall (wire-to-water) efficiency is expressed as:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{hydraulic} \times \eta_{volumetric} \times \eta_{mechanical}
$$

Where:
- $\eta_{hydraulic}$ governs friction, shock, and circulation losses inside the impeller and volute (typically **85–95%** in modern designs).
- $\eta_{volumetric}$ accounts for internal recirculation through the wear-ring clearances (typically **95–98%**).
- $\eta_{mechanical}$ represents disk friction, bearing, and seal losses (typically **96–99%**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Modern high-efficiency design is no longer treated as a single-component optimization. It is a **system-level synthesis** involving specific speed selection, CFD-based impeller profiling, surface finish control, tight running clearances, and proper matching of the pump to the system resistance curve.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a ceiling fan pushing air in a closed room:
- A **poorly designed fan** has wide, flat blades that whip the air chaotically. Much of the motor's energy is wasted as turbulence, noise, and heat in the bearings.
- A **high-efficiency fan** has curved, aerofoil-shaped blades that hand off the air smoothly to a shaped housing (volute), recovering kinetic energy as pressure with minimum swirl, friction, and back-leakage.

A pump operates on the exact same principle, but with water (which is **800 times denser than air**). Because of this density, the consequences of poor design — recirculation, cavitation, vibration — are **far more punishing** than in air-moving machines. That is why KTU emphasises high-efficiency design as a non-negotiable industrial requirement.

## 1.3 Why Efficiency Matters in Engineering

- **Energy cost dominates Life Cycle Cost (LCC):** For a 100 kW pump running 8000 hours/year, every **1% gain in efficiency saves roughly ₹4–6 lakh/year** in Indian industrial tariff slabs.
- **Environmental compliance:** Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) star labelling and IS 15310 mandate minimum efficiencies for industrial pumps.
- **Reduced heat generation:** Higher efficiency means less wasted energy dumped into the pumped fluid, lowering thermal stress on seals and bearings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Efficiency Metric:** ISO 9906:2012 / HI 40.7-2015 define the reference tolerances for pump performance testing. Any "high-efficiency" claim must be backed by **Grade 1B or better** test certification.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of High-Efficiency Design

### Pillar 1 — Selection of Optimal Specific Speed ($N_s$)

Specific speed is the single most important dimensionless grouping in pump selection. It tells the designer **what geometry to choose** before any drawing is made.

$$
N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = rotational speed in **rpm**
- $Q$ = flow rate in **m³/s**
- $H$ = head per stage in **m**

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule:** Pumps with $N_s$ between **30 and 80 (metric units)** typically deliver the **peak hydraulic efficiency**, often exceeding **88–92%**. Outside this band, geometry is forced toward either pure radial (low $N_s$) or pure axial (very high $N_s$), and efficiency drops.

### Pillar 2 — Minimization of Internal Losses

Losses are decomposed into five well-defined sources:

1. **Incidence (Shock) Loss** — Flow angle does not match blade inlet angle.
2. **Friction Loss** — Skin friction along blade and casing surfaces.
3. **Diffusion Loss** — Unrecovered kinetic energy in the volute/diffuser.
4. **Recirculation Loss** — Back-flow at off-design conditions.
5. **Disk Friction Loss** — Fluid spinning on the outer shroud of the impeller.

### Pillar 3 — Volumetric & Mechanical Containment

- **Wear-ring (stuffing-box) clearances** must be minimized while preventing thermal binding. Typical radial clearance: **0.3–0.5 mm** for small pumps, scaling up to **1.5–2.0 mm** for large pumps.
- **Mechanical seals** vs packed stuffing: seals are preferred for high-efficiency service because they eliminate the leakage-driven volumetric loss entirely.

## 2.2 Euler Head and Slip Factor (Foundation Theory)

The theoretical head imparted by an impeller follows the **Euler Turbomachinery Equation**:

$$
H_{th} = \frac{u_2 V_{2} \cos\alpha_2 - u_1 V_{1} \cos\alpha_1}{g}
$$

For a pure radial outlet (no pre-whirl, $\alpha_1 = 90°$):

$$
H_{th} = \frac{u_2 V_{2} \cos\alpha_2}{g}
$$

However, real flow inside the impeller rotates slower than the blade due to **slip**. The **Stodola Slip Factor** is widely used:

$$
\sigma = 1 - \frac{0.63 \, \pi}{n_s}
$$

More accurate **Wiesner Slip Factor**:

$$
\sigma = 1 - \frac{\sqrt{\cos\beta_2}}{Z^{0.7}}
$$

Where $Z$ is the number of blades and $\beta_2$ is the outlet blade angle.

The actual head becomes:

$$
H_{actual} = \sigma \cdot H_{th}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|-----------|----------|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$ | ρ = density, P = power | **70–92 %** |
| 2 | Specific Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | N in rpm, Q in m³/s, H in m | **30–80** (high eff. zone) |
| 3 | Suction Specific Speed | $S = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{NPSH^{3/4}}$ | NPSH available | **< 8500** (no cavitation) |
| 4 | Euler Head (radial outlet) | $H_{th} = \dfrac{u_2 V_2 \cos\alpha_2}{g}$ | u, V are blade & absolute velocities | metres |
| 5 | Stodola Slip Factor | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{0.63\pi}{n_s}$ | empirical | 0.85 – 0.99 |
| 6 | Wiesner Slip Factor | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{\sqrt{\cos\beta_2}}{Z^{0.7}}$ | Z = blade count | 0.85 – 0.99 |
| 7 | Disk Friction Loss | $P_{disk} = K_{disk}\,\rho\,\omega^3\,r_2^5$ | $K_{disk}$ ≈ 0.0011–0.0044 | Watts |
| 8 | Reynolds Number (impeller) | $Re = \dfrac{\omega r_2^2}{\nu}$ | ν = kinematic viscosity | $10^6$ – $10^7$ |
| 9 | Head Coefficient | $\psi = \dfrac{gH}{u_2^2}$ | dimensionless | 0.4 – 1.2 |
| 10 | Flow Coefficient | $\phi = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2 u_2}$ | $b_2$ = outlet width | 0.02 – 0.15 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These ten equations form the **closed-loop design cycle**. Given a target (Q, H), the designer iterates through specific speed → geometry → slip-corrected head → efficiency check → re-iterate until peak efficiency is achieved. Modern CAE tools automate this, but a KTU examiner expects you to demonstrate the **manual iteration** in derivations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Head from Velocity Triangles (Radial-Flow Impeller)

### Step 1 — Draw the Inlet Velocity Triangle

At the impeller inlet (radius $r_1$), the fluid enters with absolute velocity $V_1$ at angle $\alpha_1$ relative to the tangential direction. The blade moves at $u_1 = \omega r_1$, and the relative velocity $V_{r1}$ is along the blade angle $\beta_1$.

$$
V_{r1}^2 = u_1^2 + V_1^2 - 2 u_1 V_1 \cos\alpha_1
$$

### Step 2 — Draw the Outlet Velocity Triangle

At radius $r_2$, with outlet blade angle $\beta_2$:

$$
u_2 = \omega r_2
$$

$$
V_{r2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2 \, \psi_2}
$$

Where $\psi_2$ is the **flow area coefficient** (accounts for blockage by blade thickness).

### Step 3 — Apply the Euler Equation

The tangential component of absolute velocity at outlet is:

$$
V_{2u} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2
$$

Substituting into Euler head:

$$
H_{th} = \frac{u_2 V_{2u}}{g}
$$

### Step 4 — Introduce the Slip Factor

The real outlet whirl component is reduced by slip $\sigma < 1$:

$$
H_{real} = \frac{\sigma \, u_2 V_{2u}}{g}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Hydraulic Efficiency

$$
\eta_{hyd} = \frac{H_{real}}{H_{th}}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A centrifugal pump delivers **$Q = 0.05 \, m^3/s$** against a head of **$H = 30 \, m$** at **$N = 1450 \, rpm$**. The impeller outlet diameter is **$D_2 = 300 \, mm$**, outlet width **$b_2 = 25 \, mm$**, blade angle at outlet **$\beta_2 = 30°$**, and number of blades **$Z = 6$**. Determine:
1. Specific speed and identify the impeller type.
2. Slip factor using Wiesner's formula.
3. Theoretical and actual heads developed.
4. The wire-to-water efficiency if shaft input is **$25 \, kW$**.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Specific Speed**

$$
N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}
$$

$$
N_s = \frac{1450 \times \sqrt{0.05}}{30^{0.75}}
$$

First compute $30^{0.75}$:
$30^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \ln 30} = e^{0.75 \times 3.401} = e^{2.551} \approx 12.82$

Then:

$$
N_s = \frac{1450 \times 0.2236}{12.82} = \frac{324.2}{12.82} \approx 25.3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> $N_s = 25.3$ lies in the **radial-flow / Francis-impeller** regime. Peak-efficiency geometry is appropriate.

**Step 2 — Wiesner Slip Factor**

$$
\sigma = 1 - \frac{\sqrt{\cos 30°}}{Z^{0.7}}
$$

$\cos 30° = 0.866$, $\sqrt{0.866} = 0.931$
$Z^{0.7} = 6^{0.7} \approx 3.51$

$$
\sigma = 1 - \frac{0.931}{3.51} = 1 - 0.265 = 0.735
$$

**Step 3 — Blade (Tangential) Speed at Outlet**

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.3 \times 1450}{60}
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{1366.9}{60} = 22.78 \, m/s
$$

**Step 4 — Radial Velocity at Outlet**

$$
V_{r2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2} = \frac{0.05}{\pi \times 0.3 \times 0.025}
$$

$$
V_{r2} = \frac{0.05}{0.02356} = 2.122 \, m/s
$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical Head**

$$
V_{2u} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 = 22.78 - 2.122 \times \cos 30°
$$

$$
V_{2u} = 22.78 - 2.122 \times 0.866 = 22.78 - 1.838 = 20.94 \, m/s
$$

$$
H_{th} = \frac{u_2 V_{2u}}{g} = \frac{22.78 \times 20.94}{9.81}
$$

$$
H_{th} = \frac{476.95}{9.81} = 48.62 \, m
$$

**Step 6 — Actual Head (with slip)**

$$
H_{actual} = \sigma \times H_{th} = 0.735 \times 48.62 = 35.74 \, m
$$

This is the head generated by the impeller. The system head is 30 m, so the pump is appropriately sized (margin ≈ 19 % for off-design cushion).

**Step 7 — Wire-to-Water Efficiency**

Water power:
$$
P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 30 = 14{,}715 \, W = 14.72 \, kW
$$

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{14.72}{25} = 0.589 = 58.9 \, \%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 58.9 % overall efficiency reveals that **this is NOT a high-efficiency pump**. It is a marginal industrial design. A high-efficiency redesign would target:
> - Increase $N_s$ to 50–60 by raising speed or modifying impeller.
> - Reduce disk friction by streamlining the back shroud.
> - Apply CFD-optimized volute to recover exit kinetic energy.

## 3.3 Analytical Loss Matrix

| Loss Component | Typical Magnitude | Mitigation Strategy |
|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Incidence shock | 1–3 % of $H$ | Match inlet blade angle to flow angle at BEP |
| Friction in passages | 2–5 % | Smooth surface (Ra < 0.8 µm), streamlined blades |
| Diffusion (exit kinetic energy) | 3–8 % | Volute or vane diffuser with area ratio > 2.5 |
| Recirculation at part-load | 1–4 % | Induce controlled back-flow via splitter blades |
| Disk friction | 1–3 % | Lightweight impeller, polished back shroud |
| Volumetric (leakage) | 1–5 % | Minimize wear-ring clearance; use non-contacting seals |
| Mechanical (bearing/seal) | 1–4 % | Premium bearings, mechanical seals |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: High-Efficiency Pump Design Loop

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **iterative design methodology** that KTU expects students to describe when answering "explain the design procedure of a high-efficiency centrifugal pump."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define Q and H Requirements] --> A1[Compute Specific Speed Ns]
    A1 --> A2{Ns in High-Efficiency Band 30 to 80?}
    A2 -->|Yes| A3[Select Francis-Mixed Flow Impeller Type]
    A2 -->|No| A4[Adjust Speed or Geometry to Enter Band]
    A4 --> A1
    A3 --> B1[Choose Rotational Speed N and Outlet Diameter D2]
    B1 --> B2[Compute Blade Speed u2 equals pi D2 N over 60]
    B2 --> C1[Draw Inlet Velocity Triangle]
    C1 --> C2[Draw Outlet Velocity Triangle beta2]
    C2 --> C3[Compute Euler Head Hth]
    C3 --> D1[Apply Wiesner Slip Factor sigma]
    D1 --> D2[Compute Actual Head H actual]
    D2 --> E1[Evaluate Hydraulic Efficiency eta h]
    E1 --> F1{Eta h exceeds 88 percent?}
    F1 -->|No| G1[Refine Blade Angles beta1 beta2]
    G1 --> C2
    F1 -->|Yes| H1[Design Volute Casing Using Area Ratio 2 to 3]
    H1 --> I1[Set Wear Ring Clearance 0.3 to 2.0 mm]
    I1 --> J1[Select Bearings and Mechanical Seal]
    J1 --> K1[Run CFD Validation and Prototype Test]
    K1 --> L1[Final High Efficiency Pump Design]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Loss-Mitigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Hydraulic Loss Block] --> L1a[Shock Loss]
    L1 --> L1b[Friction Loss]
    L1 --> L1c[Diffusion Loss]
    L2[Volumetric Loss Block] --> L2a[Wear Ring Leakage]
    L2 --> L2b[Balance Hole Leakage]
    L3[Mechanical Loss Block] --> L3a[Disk Friction]
    L3 --> L3b[Bearing Friction]
    L3 --> L3c[Seal Friction]
    L1a --> M1[High Efficiency Design Mitigations]
    L1b --> M1
    L1c --> M1
    L2a --> M1
    L2b --> M1
    L3a --> M1
    L3b --> M1
    L3c --> M1
    M1 --> M1a[CFD Optimized Blades]
    M1 --> M1b[Polished Surfaces Ra less than 0.8 micrometer]
    M1 --> M1c[Shaped Volute or Vane Diffuser]
    M1 --> M1d[Mechanical Seal Replacement]
    M1 --> M1e[Minimized Wear Ring Clearance]
    M1 --> M1f[Premium Class Bearings]
    M1 --> M1g[Splitter Blades for Recirculation Control]
```

## 4.3 Decision-Tree for Selecting Efficiency-Optimized Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Compute Ns] --> P1{Ns less than 30?}
    P1 -->|Yes| P2[Use Radial Vane Impeller]
    P2 --> P2a[Accept Moderate Efficiency 70 to 80 percent]
    P1 -->|No| P3{Ns between 30 and 80?}
    P3 -->|Yes| P4[Use Francis or Mixed Flow Impeller]
    P4 --> P4a[Target Peak Efficiency 88 to 92 percent]
    P3 -->|No| P5{Ns between 80 and 300?}
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Use Mixed or Axial Flow Impeller]
    P6 --> P6a[Efficiency 80 to 87 percent]
    P5 -->|No| P7[Use Pure Axial Propeller Pump]
    P7 --> P7a[Accept Lower Efficiency 75 to 85 percent]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** A common mistake students make is selecting an axial-flow pump for high-head, low-flow service (where $N_s$ is low). This gives a structurally dangerous, inefficient design. **Always match $N_s$ first, geometry second.**

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define overall efficiency of a centrifugal pump and list the three sub-efficiencies that constitute it.**

**Model Answer:**
Overall efficiency is the ratio of useful hydraulic power delivered by the pump to the mechanical power supplied at the shaft.

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}
$$

It is the product of three sub-efficiencies:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{hydraulic} \times \eta_{volumetric} \times \eta_{mechanical}
$$

Where:
- $\eta_{hydraulic}$ accounts for friction, shock, and diffusion losses inside the impeller and casing (typically **85–95%**).
- $\eta_{volumetric}$ accounts for internal leakage past wear rings and balance holes (typically **95–98%**).
- $\eta_{mechanical}$ accounts for disk friction, bearing friction, and seal friction (typically **96–99%**).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is specific speed, and why is it considered the most important parameter in high-efficiency pump selection?**

**Model Answer:**
Specific speed is a dimensionless grouping that combines rotational speed, flow rate, and head into a single index:

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}
$$

It is critical because it directly determines the **optimum impeller geometry** — radial, Francis (mixed), or axial — without which no further design decision is meaningful. Pumps with $N_s$ in the **30–80** range (metric) consistently achieve **peak hydraulic efficiency (88–92%)**, while pumps outside this band are structurally constrained to lower efficiencies.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer ONE of the two Part B choices below.**

---

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** A centrifugal pump has the following data:
- Flow rate $Q = 0.08 \, m^3/s$
- Head $H = 25 \, m$
- Speed $N = 2900 \, rpm$
- Outlet diameter $D_2 = 250 \, mm$
- Outlet width $b_2 = 20 \, mm$
- Outlet blade angle $\beta_2 = 25°$
- Number of blades $Z = 7$

Calculate:
1. Specific speed and identify impeller type.
2. Wiesner slip factor.
3. Theoretical and actual heads.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **five major loss mechanisms** in a centrifugal pump and explain how each is mitigated in high-efficiency designs. **Use a labeled block diagram or flowchart in your answer.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Specific Speed**

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}} = \frac{2900 \times \sqrt{0.08}}{25^{0.75}}
$$

$\sqrt{0.08} = 0.2828$
$25^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \ln 25} = e^{0.75 \times 3.2189} = e^{2.414} \approx 11.18$

$$
N_s = \frac{2900 \times 0.2828}{11.18} = \frac{820.2}{11.18} \approx 73.4
$$

**Identification:** $N_s = 73.4$ lies near the upper end of the high-efficiency band (30–80), suggesting a **Francis / mixed-flow** impeller.

**Step 2: Wiesner Slip Factor**

$$
\sigma = 1 - \frac{\sqrt{\cos 25°}}{7^{0.7}}
$$

$\cos 25° = 0.9063$, $\sqrt{0.9063} = 0.9520$
$7^{0.7} \approx 3.91$

$$
\sigma = 1 - \frac{0.9520}{3.91} = 1 - 0.2435 = 0.7565
$$

**Step 3: Blade Tip Speed**

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi \times 0.25 \times 2900}{60} = \frac{2277.7}{60} \approx 37.96 \, m/s
$$

**Step 4: Radial Velocity at Outlet**

$$
V_{r2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2} = \frac{0.08}{\pi \times 0.25 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.08}{0.01571} \approx 5.092 \, m/s
$$

**Step 5: Tangential Component of Absolute Velocity**

$$
V_{2u} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2 = 37.96 - 5.092 \times \cos 25°
$$

$$
V_{2u} = 37.96 - 5.092 \times 0.9063 = 37.96 - 4.616 = 33.34 \, m/s
$$

**Step 6: Theoretical Head**

$$
H_{th} = \frac{u_2 V_{2u}}{g} = \frac{37.96 \times 33.34}{9.81} = \frac{1265.6}{9.81} \approx 129.0 \, m
$$

**Step 7: Actual Head**

$$
H_{actual} = \sigma \times H_{th} = 0.7565 \times 129.0 \approx 97.6 \, m
$$

This is the **head generated by the impeller**. Since the system head is only 25 m, the pump is significantly oversized — a redesign with smaller $D_2$ or lower $N$ would yield a much higher operating efficiency.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Five Major Loss Mechanisms & Their Mitigation in High-Efficiency Pumps:**

1. **Incidence (Shock) Loss** — Occurs when inlet flow angle ≠ blade inlet angle. Mitigated by **matching the blade inlet angle to the flow angle at the Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.

2. **Friction Loss in Passages** — Caused by boundary layer shear on blade and casing walls. Mitigated by **polishing all wetted surfaces to Ra < 0.8 µm**, using aerofoil-shaped blades, and selecting **low-roughness materials** (stainless steel over cast iron for high-efficiency service).

3. **Diffusion Loss (Exit Kinetic Energy)** — Kinetic energy at impeller exit not fully converted to pressure. Mitigated by **shaped volute casing** or **vane diffuser** with area ratio of 2.5–3.0.

4. **Disk Friction Loss** — Fluid spinning on the back shroud dissipates energy. Mitigated by **lightweight impellers**, polished back shrouds, and (in some designs) **balance holes** to reduce side-thrust pressure.

5. **Volumetric Loss (Leakage)** — Flow returning from high-pressure to low-pressure side through wear-ring clearances. Mitigated by **tight running clearances (0.3–2.0 mm)** and **non-contacting mechanical seals** instead of packed stuffing boxes.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the **Euler head equation** for a centrifugal pump from first principles, clearly stating all assumptions. Discuss the effect of **slip factor** on the actual head developed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A multistage centrifugal pump is required to deliver **$0.04 \, m^3/s$** against **$120 \, m$** head at **$1450 \, rpm$**. The pump has **4 stages**. Suggest an appropriate impeller type based on specific speed, and outline the **step-by-step design procedure** for achieving high overall efficiency (target > 85 %). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation of Euler Head Equation:**

Consider an impeller with radial vanes. At inlet (radius $r_1$) and outlet (radius $r_2$), let the absolute velocities be $V_1$ and $V_2$ making angles $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ with the tangential direction. The blade speeds are $u_1 = \omega r_1$ and $u_2 = \omega r_2$.

By the **angular momentum theorem** (Euler's turbomachinery equation):

$$
H_{th} = \frac{1}{g}\left(u_2 V_2 \cos\alpha_2 - u_1 V_1 \cos\alpha_1\right)
$$

**Assumptions:**

1. Flow is steady, incompressible, and one-dimensional at inlet and outlet.
2. Fluid enters and leaves the impeller in a plane perpendicular to the axis.
3. The blade is infinitely thin (no blockage).
4. The number of blades is infinite (no slip).

**Effect of Slip:**

In reality, the fluid leaves the impeller at a velocity whose tangential component is **less than predicted** by the infinite-blade assumption because of finite blade count and viscous effects within the impeller passages.

This is quantified by the slip factor $\sigma < 1$:

$$
H_{actual} = \sigma \, H_{th}
$$

**Stodola's Empirical Formula:**

$$
\sigma = 1 - \frac{0.63\pi}{n_s}
$$

**Wiesner Formula (more accurate):**

$$
\sigma = 1 - \frac{\sqrt{\cos\beta_2}}{Z^{0.7}}
$$

Higher blade count and larger outlet blade angles reduce slip. Slip reduces head by **5–25%** in typical industrial pumps; neglecting it is a major source of design error.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Specific Speed per Stage**

$$
H_{stage} = \frac{120}{4} = 30 \, m
$$

$$
N_s = \frac{1450 \times \sqrt{0.04}}{30^{0.75}} = \frac{1450 \times 0.2}{12.82} = \frac{290}{12.82} \approx 22.6
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **$N_s = 22.6$ is BELOW the high-efficiency band (30–80).** This is a **radial-flow impeller** requirement. A pure radial impeller can deliver this duty but is structurally inefficient.

**Step 2: Recommendations to Enter the High-Efficiency Band**

Two options:
- **Increase speed** to ~1900 rpm so that $N_s \approx 30$ — entry into the high-efficiency zone.
- **Use mixed-flow impeller** with redesigned blade angles — keeps speed but changes geometry.

**Step 3: Step-by-Step Design Procedure**

1. Fix $N_s$ in 30–80 band by adjusting speed or geometry.
2. Choose $D_2$ such that tip speed $u_2$ keeps the head coefficient $\psi = gH/u_2^2$ in 0.4–1.2.
3. Set $\beta_2$ between **20° and 35°** for high efficiency (backward-curved blades).
4. Determine blade number $Z$ using the Cordier-type chart for optimal blockage.
5. Apply Wiesner slip factor to get actual head.
6. Iteratively refine until $\eta_h > 88\%$.
7. Match the volute throat area to $Q/V_{throat}$ for non-overloading performance.
8. Specify wear-ring clearance as **0.3–0.5 mm** for a pump of this scale.
9. Select **mechanical seals** (John Crane Type 2 / Burgmann equivalent) for low leakage.
10. Validate using CFD; perform field testing per **ISO 9906 Grade 1B**.

**Expected Outcome:** With these measures, overall wire-to-water efficiency of **86–90 %** is achievable for the stated duty.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse specific speed ($N_s$) with rotational speed ($N$).** Students frequently interchange them, costing 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Always report slip factor to THREE decimals.** Sloppy 2-decimal answers get partial credit only.
> 3. **Units matter:** $Q$ must be in m³/s (not L/s) and $H$ in metres (not bar) when using the metric specific-speed formula.
> 4. **For Part B, never skip the assumption list in derivations.** Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for stating assumptions clearly.
> 5. **Numerical answers must include units at every step.** A unit-less final answer receives half-marks at best.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Overall efficiency** = $\eta_{hyd} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$. Target high-efficiency pumps exceed **85 %**.
- **Specific speed** is the master key: aim for **30–80 (metric)** to access peak efficiency.
- **Euler head** $H_{th} = u_2 V_{2u}/g$ must be corrected by the **slip factor** ($\sigma \approx 0.85$ for backward-curved blades).
- **Five big losses** to attack: shock, friction, diffusion, recirculation, and disk friction.
- **Volumetric loss** is killed by tight wear-ring clearances and mechanical seals.
- **High-efficiency design is iterative**: $N_s$ → geometry → velocity triangles → slip correction → efficiency check → refine.
- **Backward-curved blades** ($\beta_2 < 90°$) give non-overloading characteristic and higher efficiency.
- **CFD validation** and **ISO 9906 testing** are non-negotiable for industrial-grade high-efficiency pumps.
- **Volute or vane diffuser** is mandatory for recovering exit kinetic energy into pressure head.
- **Disk friction** is reduced by polished back shrouds and minimum impeller weight.
- **Material and surface finish** matter: stainless-steel wetted parts polished to Ra < 0.8 µm can lift efficiency by 1–2 %.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Pump Systems and Their Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Smart Pump Systems and Their Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Smart Pump System** is an integrated, cyber-physical fluid-handling assembly in which a conventional centrifugal or positive-displacement pump is augmented with **embedded sensors, microprocessor-based controllers, Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), bidirectional communication interfaces, and cloud/edge analytics platforms**, enabling real-time autonomous regulation of flow, pressure, efficiency, and health in accordance with dynamic demand profiles.

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme glossary for Module 4 of PEAGT522, a smart pump is classified under the broader umbrella of **Industry 4.0-enabled Hydraulic Machinery**, where the pump ceases to be a passive mechanical device and becomes an *intelligent node* in a wider Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4.6):**
> Smart pumps are characterized by the fusion of three engineering domains:
> 1. **Mechatronic Actuation** (VFD + Servo + PLC)
> 2. **Instrumentation & Data Acquisition** (Multi-modal sensors)
> 3. **Computational Intelligence** (Edge AI + Cloud Analytics)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smartphone" of Fluid Systems

Imagine a basic **dumb pump** as a *dumb Nokia 1100 phone* — it does one job (make calls / move water), and nothing more. You press "start" and it runs at 100% speed regardless of whether you need a trickle or a torrent.

Now picture a **Smart Pump System** as a *modern smartphone*. It senses the environment (ambient light, GPS, voice), adjusts its own brightness, conserves battery, runs background apps that predict your next action, syncs to the cloud, and alerts you if anything is about to fail — **all automatically**.

The "screen" of a smart pump is its **Human-Machine Interface (HMI) / SCADA dashboard**, the "processor" is its **PLC / Edge Controller**, and the "apps" are its **predictive maintenance algorithms** and **demand-based speed control loops**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Standard signal range for industrial sensors: **4–20 mA** (current loop) or **0–10 V DC** (voltage loop)
- Communication baud rates commonly used: **9600 bps (Modbus RTU)** to **1 Gbps (Industrial Ethernet)**
- Hydraulic efficiency benchmark for IE5-class smart pumps: **≥ 89.5 %**
- Sampling rate for vibration-based Condition Monitoring: **≥ 10 kHz**

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that students understand the difference between **"Instrumented Pump"** (sensors added externally) and **"Smart Pump"** (intelligence integrated at design stage). This distinction is a high-frequency Part-A question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Pillars of a Smart Pump System

A smart pump system is engineered around **five operational pillars**, each of which is independently testable and contributes to the overall system intelligence:

1. **Sensing Layer (Data Acquisition)**
   - Pressure transducers (piezoresistive / strain-gauge type)
   - Flow meters (electromagnetic, ultrasonic, Coriolis)
   - Temperature sensors (RTD Pt100 / Pt1000, thermocouples)
   - Vibration sensors (tri-axial MEMS accelerometers, IEPE)
   - Current/power transducers (Hall-effect CTs)
   - Level sensors (hydrostatic, ultrasonic, radar)

2. **Actuation & Control Layer**
   - Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) for speed modulation
   - Soft starters for inrush current control
   - Modulating control valves for throttling
   - PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) – IEC 61131-3 languages
   - DCS (Distributed Control System) for plant-wide integration

3. **Communication Layer**
   - Fieldbus protocols: **Modbus RTU/TCP, Profibus DP, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, HART, FOUNDATION Fieldbus**
   - Wireless: **LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, 5G URLLC, WirelessHART**
   - OPC UA for semantic interoperability

4. **Edge & Cloud Computing Layer**
   - Edge gateways (ARM Cortex-A class processors)
   - Cloud platforms (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT)
   - Time-series databases (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB)
   - Digital twin engine (Ansys Twin Builder, Siemens Xcelerator)

5. **Intelligence & Analytics Layer**
   - Pump performance curves modelling using **affinity laws**
   - Anomaly detection (statistical, ML-based)
   - Predictive maintenance (RUL — Remaining Useful Life estimation)
   - Energy optimization algorithms (greedy, MPC — Model Predictive Control)

### 2.2 Operational Logic Flow (Why & How)

- **Why is speed control preferred over valve throttling?**
  Throttling dissipates energy across a valve (irreversible entropy rise). Speed control via VFD reduces the *input* power directly, yielding up to **30–50 % energy savings** at part-load — a primary justification for smart-pump retrofits in HVAC and water distribution.

- **How does closed-loop control function?**
  A **PID controller** continuously compares the measured process variable (PV) — say, discharge pressure — with the **setpoint (SP)**. The error $e(t) = SP - PV$ is fed through Proportional, Integral, and Derivative paths to generate a VFD frequency command $f(t)$, which alters pump speed $N$ in real time.

- **How does predictive maintenance work?**
  Vibration spectral data (FFT of accelerometer signal) is analyzed for characteristic fault frequencies: impeller vane-pass frequency, bearing race frequencies, cavitation sidebands. A deviation beyond statistical control limits (e.g., ±3σ from baseline) triggers a maintenance alert *before* catastrophic failure.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Units / Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Affinity Law — Flow vs Speed | $Q$ in m³/h, $N$ in rpm |
| 2 | $\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2}$ | Affinity Law — Head vs Speed | $H$ in m |
| 3 | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}$ | Affinity Law — Power vs Speed (cube law) | $P$ in kW |
| 4 | $\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{P_{shaft}}$ | Hydraulic Efficiency | dimensionless, 0–1 |
| 5 | $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ | Control error signal | bar, m³/h, °C |
| 6 | $u(t) = K_{p}e + K_{i}\int e\,dt + K_{d}\dfrac{de}{dt}$ | PID control law | Hz (VFD command) |
| 7 | $SSE = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left( Q_{m,k} - Q_{p,k} \right)^{2}$ | Curve-fit error for digital twin | (m³/h)² |
| 8 | $C_{payback} = \dfrac{C_{capex}}{\Delta E \cdot C_{unit}}$ | Simple payback period (years) | years |
| 9 | $RUL = \dfrac{T_{end} - t_{now}}{f_{vib}(t) - f_{vib,0}} \cdot f_{vib,0}$ | Remaining Useful Life (vibration) | hours |
| 10 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Sensor Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 11 | $\eta_{wire-to-water} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{VFD} \cdot \eta_{pump}$ | Overall system efficiency | dimensionless |
| 12 | $P_{saved} = P_{rated}\!\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{N_{part}}{N_{rated}}\right)^{3}\right]$ | Power saved by VFD at part load | kW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU board exams, **Formula #3 (cube law) and #12 (VFD power saving)** are the two most frequently asked derivations. Memorize the cube relationship — it is the *physical reason* VFDs save energy, not just a textbook number.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Smart pump systems are deployed across:
- **Municipal water supply** (Bengaluru, Singapore PUB — NEWater) for leak detection and DMA (District Metered Area) pressure management
- **Building HVAC** (chilled-water and condenser-water variable primary flow systems)
- **Oil & Gas upstream** (artificial lift, ESP — Electric Submersible Pumps with downhole sensors)
- **Process industries** (chemical, pharma, food & beverage — hygienic CIP pumps with CIP recipe tracking)
- **Agriculture** (solar-powered smart drip irrigation with soil-moisture feedback)
- **Power generation** (boiler feed pumps, cooling-water circulation, FGD slurry pumps)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Savings from VFD Speed Modulation (Affinity Cube Law Application)

We start from the dimensional relationship between shaft power and the impeller tip speed.

**Step 1 — Express shaft power as the product of torque and angular velocity:**

$$
P = T \cdot \omega
$$

where $T$ is the shaft torque in N·m and $\omega$ is the angular velocity in rad/s.

**Step 2 — Relate torque to head through Euler's turbomachinery equation (for a pump with radial exit blades):**

$$
T = \rho \cdot Q \cdot r \cdot v_{tan}
$$

For geometrically similar operation, $v_{tan} \propto N \cdot D$ and $r \propto D$, so torque scales as $T \propto \rho \cdot Q \cdot D^{2} \cdot N$.

**Step 3 — Apply the similarity (affinity) relationships:**

Since $Q \propto N \cdot D^{3}$ and $H \propto N^{2} \cdot D^{2}$ for geometrically similar impellers, and combining with Step 1, we obtain:

$$
P \propto \rho \cdot g \cdot H \cdot Q \propto \rho \cdot g \cdot (N^{2}D^{2}) \cdot (ND^{3}) = \rho \cdot g \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}
$$

**Step 4 — Hold the impeller diameter $D$ constant (same physical pump) and vary $N$:**

$$
\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \left(\frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute fractional power saved when pump is reduced from 100 % to 75 % speed:**

$$
\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = (0.75)^{3} = 0.421875
$$

$$
P_{saved} = P_{1} \left(1 - 0.421875\right) = 0.578125 \, P_{1}
$$

So a **25 % speed reduction cuts power consumption by ~57.8 %** — this is the foundational economic argument for smart-pump retrofits.

> **Logic Note:** The cube law arises because *head* is squared and *flow* is linear in speed, so their product (hydraulic power) is cubic. This is the most important quantitative relationship in VFD-pump selection.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Wire-to-Water Efficiency for a Smart Pump System

The overall system efficiency must account for **every** conversion step from electrical input to hydraulic output.

**Step 1 — Electrical input to motor:**

$$
P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L} \cdot I_{L} \cdot \cos\phi
$$

**Step 2 — Motor efficiency converts electrical to mechanical:**

$$
P_{shaft} = \eta_{motor} \cdot P_{in}
$$

**Step 3 — VFD efficiency (modern IGBT-based drives):**

$$
P_{motor,term} = \eta_{VFD} \cdot P_{in,term}
$$

Typical $\eta_{VFD} \in [0.96, 0.98]$ at rated load.

**Step 4 — Pump hydraulic power output:**

$$
P_{hyd} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H
$$

**Step 5 — Combine all stages to get the wire-to-water efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{w2w} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{P_{in}} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{VFD} \cdot \eta_{pump}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example:**

A 15 kW rated system with $\eta_{motor} = 0.94$, $\eta_{VFD} = 0.97$, $\eta_{pump} = 0.82$:

$$
\eta_{w2w} = 0.94 \times 0.97 \times 0.82 = 0.7476 \approx 74.76\,\%
$$

A loss of ~25 % from grid to fluid is normal; smart-pump analytics target *which* of the three stages is underperforming.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Digital Twin Curve-Fitting using Least-Squares Regression

The digital twin represents the pump's $H$–$Q$ curve analytically as a second-order polynomial:

$$
H(Q) = a_{0} + a_{1}Q + a_{2}Q^{2}
$$

The coefficients $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$ are obtained by minimizing the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**:

$$
SSE(a_0, a_1, a_2) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left[ H_{m,k} - (a_0 + a_1 Q_k + a_2 Q_k^{2}) \right]^{2}
$$

Taking partial derivatives and setting to zero yields the **normal equations**:

$$
\frac{\partial SSE}{\partial a_0} = -2 \sum H_{m,k} + 2 \sum (a_0 + a_1 Q_k + a_2 Q_k^{2}) = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial SSE}{\partial a_1} = -2 \sum H_{m,k} Q_k + 2 \sum (a_0 Q_k + a_1 Q_k^{2} + a_2 Q_k^{3}) = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial SSE}{\partial a_2} = -2 \sum H_{m,k} Q_k^{2} + 2 \sum (a_0 Q_k^{2} + a_1 Q_k^{3} + a_2 Q_k^{4}) = 0
$$

Solving the resulting $3 \times 3$ linear system gives the twin's coefficients. Any deviation of live data from this curve flags degradation (wearing of impeller, increase in clearance, gas entrainment).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Smart Pump vs Conventional Pump

| Performance Criterion | Conventional Pump (Throttled) | Smart Pump (VFD + Sensors) | Improvement Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Part-load energy consumption | 85–100 % of rated | 25–60 % of rated (via cube law) | 2× to 4× |
| Specific Energy (kWh/m³) | 0.55 – 0.80 | 0.25 – 0.40 | ~2× better |
| Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) | 18 000 h | 35 000 h (with predictive maintenance) | ~2× |
| Maintenance cost / year | High (reactive) | Low (planned) | 30 – 50 % saving |
| Leakage detection latency | Weeks (manual) | Hours (DMA + analytics) | 50× faster |
| Operator intervention frequency | Daily | Weekly / Remote | 5–10× reduction |
| CO₂ footprint per m³ pumped | High | 40 – 60 % lower | Significant ESG gain |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Smart Pump System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Process Demand Input] --> B[PLC Controller]
    B --> C[VFD Drive Unit]
    C --> D[Smart Pump Assembly]
    D --> E[Pressure Transducer]
    D --> F[Flow Meter]
    D --> G[Vibration Sensor]
    D --> H[Temperature Sensor]
    E --> I[Edge Gateway DAQ]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Cloud Analytics Platform]
    J --> K[Digital Twin Engine]
    J --> L[Predictive Maintenance Module]
    J --> M[Energy Optimization Module]
    J --> N[SCADA HMI Dashboard]
    L --> O[Maintenance Alert to Operator]
    K --> P[Performance Report]
    M --> Q[VFD Setpoint Update]
    Q --> B
```

### 4.2 Data Flow Diagram — Sensor-to-Cloud Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Vibration Sensor] --> S1a[Anti Aliasing Filter]
    S1a --> S1b[ADC 16 bit]
    S2[Pressure Sensor] --> S2a[4 to 20 mA Loop]
    S2a --> S2b[Signal Conditioner]
    S2b --> S1b
    S3[Flow Sensor EM] --> S3a[Modbus RTU Output]
    S3a --> S1b
    S1b --> G1[Edge Gateway Processor]
    G1 --> G2[OPC UA Server]
    G2 --> G3[Cloud MQTT Broker]
    G3 --> G4[Time Series Database]
    G4 --> G5[Machine Learning Model]
    G5 --> G6[Anomaly Detection Output]
    G6 --> HMI1[Operator Dashboard]
    G6 --> ALM1[SMS Email Alert]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Control Loop Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Level 4 Enterprise Planning] --> L2[Level 3 SCADA Supervision]
    L2 --> L3[Level 2 PLC Control Loop]
    L3 --> L4[Level 1 VFD and Field Actuators]
    L4 --> L5[Level 0 Pump Motor and Sensors]
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Mapping:** Because detailed schematics of hydraulic piping, impeller geometry, and VFD power circuits are beyond text-based Mermaid rendering, the diagrams above are presented as *functional* and *signal-flow* architectures — the exact format used in KTU board-exam answers when a circuit or piping diagram is requested.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
*Define a Smart Pump System. List any four intelligent features that distinguish it from a conventional pump.*

**Model Answer:**
A Smart Pump System is an integrated fluid-handling assembly equipped with **sensors, controllers, VFDs, and communication interfaces** that allow it to autonomously regulate its operating point, monitor its health, and communicate with higher-level systems.

Four distinguishing features:
1. **Variable speed operation** via integrated VFD
2. **Real-time condition monitoring** (vibration, temperature, power)
3. **Remote connectivity** through industrial IoT protocols (Modbus, OPC UA)
4. **Predictive maintenance capability** using analytics and digital twins

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 0.5 each for the four features)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*State the Affinity Laws for a centrifugal pump. Why is the power–speed relationship cubic?*

**Model Answer:**
For a pump with constant impeller diameter, operating at speeds $N_1$ and $N_2$:

$$
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \quad ; \quad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2} \quad ; \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}
$$

The power relationship is **cubic** because hydraulic power $P = \rho g Q H$. With $Q \propto N$ and $H \propto N^{2}$, their product is $\propto N^{3}$.

(3 marks: 1 for stating laws, 2 for cubic explanation)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Smart Pump System. Identify and describe the functions of at least six distinct components.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A centrifugal pump rated at 30 kW operates 5000 hours/year. It is proposed to replace the existing throttling control with a VFD-based speed control. The average operating load is estimated at 70 % of the rated flow. Electricity cost is ₹ 7.50 per kWh. Compute:
(i) Annual energy consumption with throttling.
(ii) Annual energy consumption with VFD.
(iii) Annual monetary savings, assuming pump and motor efficiencies remain unchanged.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

A Smart Pump System consists of the following layered architecture:

| Layer | Components | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing** | Pressure, flow, vibration, temperature, level sensors | Acquire real-time process data |
| **Actuation** | VFD, modulating valve, soft starter | Regulate pump speed and discharge |
| **Control** | PLC / DCS / Edge controller | Execute PID, MPC, and logic algorithms |
| **Communication** | Modbus, Profinet, OPC UA, HART | Transmit data to SCADA / cloud |
| **Analytics** | Cloud platform, ML models, digital twin | Anomaly detection, RUL estimation |
| **HMI** | SCADA dashboard, mobile app | Visualization and operator interface |

(Mark distribution: 1 mark block diagram, 1 mark layer identification, 5 marks for component description and function — allocate 0.5–1 mark per component.)

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Throttled operation power estimate:**

Assume throttling at part load dissipates energy; shaft power scales approximately linearly with flow for fixed head. Hence at 70 % flow, $P_{throttle} \approx 0.85 \times P_{rated}$ (loss factor due to throttling dissipation).

$$
P_{throttle} = 0.85 \times 30 = 25.5 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — VFD-based power at 70 % speed (using affinity cube law):**

If the flow ratio $Q_{2}/Q_{1} = 0.70$, the speed ratio is also $0.70$ (assuming $D$ is constant).

$$
P_{VFD} = P_{rated} \times (0.70)^{3} = 30 \times 0.343 = 10.29 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy consumption:**

$$
E_{throttle} = 25.5 \times 5000 = 1{,}27{,}500 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
E_{VFD} = 10.29 \times 5000 = 51{,}450 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary savings:**

$$
\Delta E = 1{,}27{,}500 - 51{,}450 = 76{,}050 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\text{Savings} = 76{,}050 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ } 5{,}70{,}375 \text{ per year}
$$

(7 marks: 2 marks step 1–2 derivation, 2 marks energy calculation, 2 marks monetary calculation, 1 mark for final answer with units.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a **Digital Twin** for a pump. How does it enable predictive maintenance? Discuss any two machine-learning approaches used for fault detection.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pump delivers 100 m³/h at 30 m head. The motor draws 11 kW. The discharge pressure transducer outputs 4–20 mA corresponding to 0–10 bar. If the measured current is 12 mA, compute:
(i) The measured pressure in bar.
(ii) The wire-to-water efficiency.
(iii) Comment on whether the pump is operating in its best-efficiency-point (BEP) range, assuming the rated BEP efficiency is 75 %.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Digital Twin (7 marks):**

A **Digital Twin** is a real-time, dynamic, virtual replica of a physical pump that uses sensor data streams to mirror the pump's actual operating state. It is constructed using:
- Geometric CAD model
- Physics-based performance model (RANS-CFD or analytical $H$–$Q$ polynomial)
- Live data ingestion via OPC UA / MQTT

**Predictive Maintenance Enablement:**
- Continuously compares live sensor data with twin-predicted values
- Anomaly score = statistical deviation between measured and twin-predicted
- Trend extrapolation forecasts RUL using exponential degradation models

**Two ML approaches:**
1. **Autoencoders (unsupervised):** Reconstruct normal sensor patterns; high reconstruction error signals anomaly.
2. **Random Forest / Gradient Boosting (supervised):** Classify fault type (cavitation, impeller wear, bearing failure) from labelled vibration spectra.

(7 marks: 2 marks for digital twin definition, 2 marks for predictive maintenance link, 3 marks for ML methods with examples.)

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Pressure from 4–20 mA signal:**

Span = 20 − 4 = 16 mA corresponds to 0–10 bar.

$$
P = \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times 10 = \dfrac{12 - 4}{16} \times 10 = \dfrac{8}{16} \times 10 = 5 \text{ bar}
$$

Convert to head: $H = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{5}}{1000 \times 9.81} = 50.97 \text{ m}$

**Step 2 — Wire-to-water efficiency:**

$$
P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times \left(\dfrac{100}{3600}\right) \times 50.97 = 13{,}880 \text{ W} \approx 13.88 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\eta_{w2w} = \dfrac{13.88}{11.0} = 1.262
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The calculated efficiency exceeds 100 %, which is **physically impossible**. This indicates an error in the problem statement or the *transducer output represents suction-side pressure*, not discharge pressure. Alternatively, the rated head of 30 m and measured pressure head of 50.97 m are inconsistent — students should explicitly state the inconsistency in their KTU answer to earn full marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Conflating *pump hydraulic efficiency* with *wire-to-water efficiency*. Wire-to-water includes motor + VFD + pump; pump efficiency alone is only the last term.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Reporting a calculated efficiency > 100 % without flagging the physical inconsistency — KTU examiners *deduct 1 mark* for not questioning the input.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** In VFD savings questions, students often forget the cube law and use $P \propto N$ (linear), arriving at 30 % savings instead of 65.7 % savings. This is the single most frequent error in the 14-mark numerical.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** In digital-twin questions, students describe *what* a digital twin is but fail to explain *how* sensor data is integrated — for 7 marks, the data-pipeline mechanism (OPC UA / MQTT) is mandatory.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Smart Pump = Sensors + VFD + PLC + Cloud + Analytics** (5-layer architecture)
- [x] **Affinity Cube Law** is the energy-saving core: $P \propto N^{3}$ for constant $D$
- [x] **Wire-to-water efficiency** = product of motor × VFD × pump efficiencies
- [x] **Digital Twin** enables RUL estimation and anomaly detection
- [x] **VFD vs Throttling:** VFD saves input power; throttling wastes it as heat
- [x] **Communication Protocols:** Modbus (simple), Profinet (high-speed), OPC UA (semantic), HART (legacy)
- [x] **Sensors used:** Pressure (4–20 mA), Flow (EM/ultrasonic), Vibration (IEPE), Temperature (RTD)
- [x] **PID Law:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d\,de/dt$
- [x] **Condition Monitoring:** Vibration spectral analysis (FFT) detects bearing/impeller faults
- [x] **Payback period** for VFD retrofit: typically 1.5 to 3 years
- [x] **Key distinction for KTU:** *Instrumented Pump ≠ Smart Pump* — intelligence must be **integrated**, not retrofitted
- [x] **IE5 motor + VFD + smart pump** can push wire-to-water efficiency above 80 %
- [x] **Applications:** Water supply, HVAC, Oil & Gas, Pharma, Agriculture, Power plants
- [x] **Memory Aid — "SCALE":** **S**ensors, **C**ontrol (PLC), **A**ctuators (VFD), **L**ink (Communication), **E**xpertise (Analytics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always pair a *block diagram* (1 mark) with a *layered tabular description* (3 marks) and a *quantitative numerical* (3 marks). This 1-3-3 structure mirrors the KTU board examiner's marking rubric for Module 4 questions on Smart Pump Systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automation in Pump Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automation in Pump Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automation in Pump Control Systems** refers to the integrated use of sensing elements, signal processing units, feedback controllers, and actuating devices to regulate the operating parameters of centrifugal, positive displacement, and special-purpose pumps *without continuous human intervention*. The principal objective is to maintain desired process variables — such as **discharge pressure**, **flow rate**, **liquid level**, **temperature**, or **pump speed** — within prescribed tolerance bands by automatically modulating the input energy delivered to the pump.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, automation in pump control encompasses four functional layers:

1. **Sensing Layer** — Transducers that convert physical quantities (pressure, flow, level) into electrical/pneumatic signals.
2. **Control Layer** — PLCs, PID controllers, or microcontrollers executing control algorithms.
3. **Actuation Layer** — Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), solenoid valves, modulating valves, and contactors.
4. **Supervisory Layer** — SCADA, HMIs, and IoT-based remote monitoring platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module specifically emphasizes **closed-loop feedback control**, **PID-based pump speed regulation**, and the application of **pump affinity laws** to compute energy savings in VFD-driven systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Cruise Control of a Car**
> Imagine driving a car on a highway with cruise control. You set a target speed of 80 km/h. The car automatically adjusts the fuel (throttle) based on the slope of the road. Going uphill, it gives more fuel; downhill, it reduces. You (the driver) don't touch the accelerator continuously — the system does it for you using speed sensors and a feedback controller.
>
> **An automated pump control system works identically:**
> - **Target speed (80 km/h)** → Desired pressure/flow setpoint.
> - **Speed sensor** → Pressure/flow transmitter on the discharge line.
> - **ECU of the car** → PID controller or PLC.
> - **Throttle** → Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) controlling the motor speed.
> - **Fuel consumption** → Electrical power drawn by the pump motor.

In simple terms, automation transforms a **manually regulated pump** (where an operator opens/closes valves to control flow) into a **self-regulating intelligent system** that responds instantly to load variations in the hydraulic network.

### 1.3 Why Automate Pumps? — Engineering Motivation

- **Energy Efficiency:** Pumps account for nearly **20%** of global industrial electricity consumption. VFD-based automation can yield **30–60%** energy savings under variable load conditions.
- **Process Stability:** Maintains constant pressure/flow irrespective of demand fluctuations.
- **Equipment Protection:** Automatic shutdown on dry running, cavitation, overcurrent, or phase failure.
- **Reduced Maintenance:** Soft-start via VFDs eliminates water hammer and mechanical shock.
- **Remote Operation:** SCADA/IoT integration enables unmanned pump stations.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Density of water at 25°C:** $\rho = 997 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (commonly approximated as **1000 kg/m³** in KTU problems).
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (often taken as **9.8 m/s²**).
- **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma = \rho g \approx 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$.
- **Standard process control tolerance band:** $\pm 2\%$ to $\pm 5\%$ of setpoint.
- **VFD switching frequency:** typically **2 kHz to 16 kHz**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of an Automated Pump Control Loop

A standard **closed-loop feedback system** for a centrifugal pump comprises the following sequential elements:

1. **Setpoint Input ($SP$):** The desired value of the controlled variable (e.g., 4 bar discharge pressure).
2. **Sensor / Transmitter:** Measures the actual process variable ($PV$) and converts it to a standardized signal (4–20 mA current loop, 0–10 V, or digital HART/Modbus).
3. **Error Detector:** Computes the error signal as
$$e(t) = SP - PV(t)$$
4. **Controller (PID Algorithm):** Processes the error and generates a control output $u(t)$.
5. **Final Control Element (VFD / Control Valve):** Modulates pump speed or valve opening.
6. **Process (Pump + Pipeline):** Responds to the manipulated variable.
7. **Disturbance Inputs:** Variations in demand, suction head changes, pipe leaks, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed-Loop Justification (KTU 2024):** Without feedback, the pump operates in *open-loop* mode where the output is purely a function of input. In *closed-loop*, the output is continuously measured and compared — providing **self-correcting action** against disturbances.

### 2.2 The PID Controller — Working Principle

The **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller** is the industry-standard algorithm for pump automation. Its time-domain output equation is:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where:
- $K_p$ = Proportional gain (instantaneous correction)
- $K_i$ = Integral gain (eliminates steady-state offset)
- $K_d$ = Derivative gain (anticipates future error based on rate of change)
- $e(t)$ = Error signal at time $t$
- $u(t)$ = Controller output (sent to VFD frequency reference)

**Practical Interpretation of Each Term:**

- **Proportional (P):** Acts *now* — like turning a tap based on how far the pressure is from setpoint. Too high $K_p$ causes oscillations.
- **Integral (I):** Acts on *accumulated past errors* — ensures the final pressure exactly matches the setpoint, eliminating *offset*.
- **Derivative (D):** Acts on the *rate of change* — damps overshoots; useful for systems with inertia like large pump-motor sets.

### 2.3 Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) — Speed Control Foundation

A VFD controls pump motor speed by varying the **supply frequency** $f$. The synchronous speed of an induction motor is:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$

Where:
- $N_s$ = Synchronous speed in **RPM**
- $f$ = Supply frequency in **Hz**
- $P$ = Number of poles

The actual rotor speed is slightly less than $N_s$ due to slip $s$:

$$N = N_s (1 - s) = \frac{120 f (1 - s)}{P}$$

For a 4-pole motor at 50 Hz, $N_s = 1500$ RPM; with typical slip of 4%, $N \approx 1440$ RPM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why VFDs are preferred over throttling valves for flow control:**
> - Throttling wastes energy as heat across the valve.
> - VFD reduces pump input power **cubically** with speed (Affinity Law), yielding massive energy savings.
> - VFD provides soft-start, eliminating inrush current (6–8× rated) and water hammer.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables & Units** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous motor speed | $N_s$: RPM; $f$: Hz; $P$: poles | VFD frequency-to-speed conversion |
| $N = N_s(1-s)$ | Actual rotor speed | $s$: slip (dimensionless, 0.02–0.06) | Speed regulation calculation |
| $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Affinity Law 1 (Flow) | $Q$: m³/h; $N$: RPM | Predicting flow at reduced speed |
| $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Affinity Law 2 (Head) | $H$: m | Predicting head at reduced speed |
| $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | Affinity Law 3 (Power) | $P$: kW | Energy savings estimation |
| $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$ | Hydraulic power | $P_{hyd}$: W; $\rho$: kg/m³; $Q$: m³/s; $H$: m | Power delivered to fluid |
| $P_{brake} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Brake (shaft) power | $\eta_{pump}$: efficiency (0–1) | Motor power selection |
| $P_{input} = \dfrac{P_{brake}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{VFD}}$ | Electrical input power | $\eta_{motor}$, $\eta_{VFD}$: efficiencies | Energy cost calculation |
| $e(t) = SP - PV(t)$ | Error signal | $e$: same units as PV | PID input |
| $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | PID output | $K_p, K_i, K_d$: controller gains | Control signal generation |
| $C_s = \dfrac{P_{rated} - P_{VFD}}{P_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | Energy saving % | $P$: kW | VFD retrofitting savings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Affinity Laws are *only valid for* **dynamic similarity** — meaning the pump operates within its **recommended operating range**, impeller geometry is unchanged, and the fluid properties remain constant. They are **not** valid across vastly different operating points or when the impeller diameter is altered.

### 2.5 Real-World Application: Booster Pump Skid in a High-Rise Building

Consider a 30-floor residential complex with a variable water demand. A traditional system uses a fixed-speed pump + pressure reducing valves, wasting energy during low-demand hours (night). The **automated alternative** is:

- A **pressure transmitter** on the riser sends a 4–20 mA signal to a **PID controller**.
- The PID compares actual pressure $PV$ with setpoint (e.g., **3.5 bar**).
- The controller output $u(t)$ is sent as a frequency reference (e.g., 35–50 Hz) to the **VFD**.
- The VFD adjusts motor speed, which via affinity laws reduces $Q$ and $H$ proportionally.
- The **net result**: power consumption drops to ~30% during off-peak hours (from the cube law: $0.7^3 = 0.343$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Savings Using Pump Affinity Laws in VFD-Controlled Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question):** A centrifugal pump operates at $N_1 = 1450$ RPM delivering $Q_1 = 100$ m³/h against a head $H_1 = 30$ m, requiring $P_1 = 12$ kW. To match a reduced system demand, the speed is lowered to $N_2 = 1100$ RPM. Calculate the new flow, head, power, and percentage energy saving.

**Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities**

$$N_1 = 1450 \text{ RPM}, \quad Q_1 = 100 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}, \quad H_1 = 30 \text{ m}, \quad P_1 = 12 \text{ kW}$$

$$N_2 = 1100 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Speed Ratio**

The speed ratio is the foundational parameter upon which all affinity relationships depend.

$$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{1450}{1100} = \frac{145}{110} = \frac{29}{22} \approx 1.3182$$

**Step 3 — Apply Affinity Law 1 (Flow Proportional to Speed)**

By Affinity Law 1, the new flow $Q_2$ is computed as:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} &= \frac{N_1}{N_2} \\
Q_2 &= Q_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} \\
Q_2 &= 100 \times \frac{1100}{1450} \\
Q_2 &= 100 \times 0.7586 \\
Q_2 &\approx 75.86 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A 24.14% reduction in speed produces an equal 24.14% reduction in flow.

**Step 4 — Apply Affinity Law 2 (Head Proportional to Speed Squared)**

The new head $H_2$ is computed using the square relationship:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{H_1}{H_2} &= \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 \\
H_2 &= H_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \\
H_2 &= 30 \times \left(\frac{1100}{1450}\right)^2 \\
H_2 &= 30 \times (0.7586)^2 \\
H_2 &= 30 \times 0.5755 \\
H_2 &\approx 17.26 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A 24.14% speed reduction produces a 42.45% head reduction — much larger than the flow reduction.

**Step 5 — Apply Affinity Law 3 (Power Proportional to Speed Cubed)**

The new power $P_2$ is computed using the cube relationship:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_1}{P_2} &= \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3 \\
P_2 &= P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \\
P_2 &= 12 \times \left(\frac{1100}{1450}\right)^3 \\
P_2 &= 12 \times (0.7586)^3 \\
P_2 &= 12 \times 0.4365 \\
P_2 &\approx 5.238 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A 24.14% speed reduction produces a **56.35% power reduction** — a massive energy saving.

**Step 6 — Calculate Percentage Energy Saving**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_s &= \frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1} \times 100\% \\
C_s &= \frac{12 - 5.238}{12} \times 100\% \\
C_s &= \frac{6.762}{12} \times 100\% \\
C_s &\approx 56.35\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Summary Result Table**

| **Parameter** | **At $N_1$ = 1450 RPM** | **At $N_2$ = 1100 RPM** | **% Reduction** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed (RPM) | 1450 | 1100 | 24.14% |
| Flow (m³/h) | 100 | 75.86 | 24.14% |
| Head (m) | 30 | 17.26 | 42.45% |
| Power (kW) | 12 | 5.238 | **56.35%** |
