> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** The "Cube Law" of pump power is the *single most important justification* for using VFDs in variable-load pump applications. A modest 24% speed reduction yields a 56% power reduction — translating directly into lower electricity bills and a reduced carbon footprint.

### 3.2 Derivation: VFD Frequency from Required Speed

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 4-pole induction motor ($P = 4$) drives a pump at a full-load speed of 1450 RPM. The slip is 4%. The VFD is commanded to deliver 75% of rated flow. Find the required VFD output frequency.

**Step 1 — Slip Definition**

$$s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s}$$

Given $s = 0.04$ and $N = 1450$ RPM, the synchronous speed is:

$$N_s = \frac{N}{1 - s} = \frac{1450}{0.96} \approx 1510.42 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 — Rated Frequency**

For a 4-pole motor:

$$f_{rated} = \frac{N_s \cdot P}{120} = \frac{1510.42 \times 4}{120} \approx 50.35 \text{ Hz}$$

We approximate as **50 Hz** (standard supply frequency in India).

**Step 3 — New Speed for 75% Flow**

By Affinity Law 1, for 75% flow:

$$N_2 = 0.75 \times N_1 = 0.75 \times 1450 = 1087.5 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4 — Required VFD Frequency**

Assuming the VFD maintains the same $V/f$ ratio (a common operational constraint to avoid magnetic saturation), the slip remains approximately constant. Thus:

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{f_2}{f_1}$$

$$f_2 = f_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 50 \times \frac{1087.5}{1450} \approx 37.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Result:** The VFD must be set to **37.5 Hz** to deliver 75% of rated flow.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Control Strategy Selection

| **Application** | **Controlled Variable** | **Sensor Type** | **Controller** | **Actuator** | **Setpoint** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Domestic water supply | Pressure | Pressure transmitter (4–20 mA) | PID | VFD | 2–4 bar |
| Cooling water circulation | Temperature | RTD / Thermocouple | PID | VFD + 3-way valve | 5–15°C |
| Sump / Sewage pumping | Level | Float switch / Ultrasonic level sensor | ON-OFF or PID | Contactor / VFD | High/Low level marks |
| Boiler feed pump | Flow + Pressure | Flow meter + Pressure Tx | Cascade PID | VFD | Flow set by steam demand |
| Chemical dosing | Flow | Electromagnetic flowmeter | Proportional | Solenoid / Diaphragm pump | Dose rate (L/h) |
| Irrigation | Pressure / Flow | Pressure Tx | PID | VFD | Line pressure (kg/cm²) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Closed-Loop Block Diagram of an Automated Pump Control System

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (no reserved keywords used). Labels contain clean uppercase alphanumeric text only.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[SP_SETPOINT_INPUT] --> B1[SUMMING_JUNCTION]
    D1[DISTURBANCE_INPUT] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[PID_CONTROLLER]
    C1 --> E1[VFD_ACTUATOR]
    E1 --> F1[PUMP_MOTOR_UNIT]
    F1 --> G1[HYDRAULIC_PIPELINE]
    G1 --> H1[PROCESS_VARIABLE_SENSOR]
    H1 --> I1[FEEDBACK_SIGNAL_PATH]
    I1 --> B1
    H1 --> J1[SCADA_HMI_DISPLAY]
    J1 --> K1[OPERATOR_MONITOR]
```

**Functional Explanation of Each Block:**

- **SP_SETPOINT_INPUT:** The desired pressure/flow value entered by the operator via HMI.
- **SUMMING_JUNCTION:** Computes error $e(t) = SP - PV$.
- **DISTURBANCE_INPUT:** Unwanted inputs such as sudden demand drop, pipe burst, suction head variation.
- **PID_CONTROLLER:** Generates the control signal $u(t)$ using proportional, integral, and derivative actions.
- **VFD_ACTUATOR:** Converts control signal into variable frequency and voltage for the motor.
- **PUMP_MOTOR_UNIT:** The induction motor driving the centrifugal pump impeller.
- **HYDRAULIC_PIPELINE:** The discharge network conveying fluid to end-users.
- **PROCESS_VARIABLE_SENSOR:** Pressure transmitter / flow meter / level sensor.
- **FEEDBACK_SIGNAL_PATH:** Returns the measured $PV$ to the summing junction.
- **SCADA_HMI_DISPLAY:** Provides real-time visualization, trending, alarms, and data logging.

### 4.2 Decision Logic Flowchart for Pump Automation (ON/OFF + VFD)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[SYSTEM_START] --> B2[READ_LEVEL_OR_PRESSURE_SENSOR]
    B2 --> C2{CHECK_PV_AGAINST_SETPOINT}
    C2 --> D2[PV_BELOW_LOW_LIMIT]
    C2 --> E2[PV_WITHIN_DEADBAND]
    C2 --> F2[PV_ABOVE_HIGH_LIMIT]
    D2 --> G2[START_PUMP_AT_BASE_FREQUENCY]
    G2 --> H2[INCREMENT_FREQUENCY_VIA_PID]
    H2 --> I2[REACHED_SETPOINT]
    I2 --> E2
    E2 --> J2[MAINTAIN_CURRENT_FREQUENCY]
    J2 --> B2
    F2 --> K2[DECREMENT_FREQUENCY_VIA_PID]
    K2 --> L2[REACHED_SETPOINT]
    L2 --> E2
    F2 --> M2[FULL_SHUTDOWN_AFTER_DELAY]
    M2 --> N2[STANDBY_MODE]
    N2 --> B2
    B2 --> O2[FAULT_DETECTED]
    O2 --> P2[TRIGGER_ALARM_AND_TRIP]
```

**Logic Interpretation:**

1. The controller continuously polls the sensor reading.
2. If $PV$ is below the lower limit, the VFD starts at base frequency (e.g., 25 Hz) and ramps up via PID.
3. When $PV$ enters the **deadband** (a small acceptable tolerance zone around setpoint), the controller holds the current frequency to prevent hunting.
4. If $PV$ exceeds the upper limit, the controller reduces frequency; if it stays high for a defined delay, the pump shuts down to save energy.
5. On fault detection (dry run, overcurrent, phase failure), the system trips and raises an alarm.

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Layered View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[LAYER_4_SUPERVISORY_SCADA_IOT_CLOUD] --> L2[LAYER_3_CONTROL_PLC_PID_CONTROLLER]
    L2 --> L3[LAYER_2_ACTUATION_VFD_VALVES_CONTACTORS]
    L3 --> L4[LAYER_1_FIELD_PUMP_MOTOR_SENSORS]
    L4 --> L2
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layered Architecture (ISA-95 Model):** Industrial pump automation is typically organized in a **4-layer hierarchy** — from physical field devices (bottom) to enterprise/cloud analytics (top). The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly highlights the role of **SCADA** and **IoT-based remote monitoring** in modern pump stations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern (Part A carries 2-mark short-answer questions, expected to be answered in 2–3 sentences)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)" and state its role in pump automation.*

**Model Answer:**
A Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) is an electronic power-conversion device that varies the **frequency** and **voltage** supplied to an AC induction motor, thereby controlling its rotational speed. In pump automation, the VFD acts as the **final control element** that receives a low-power analog/digital signal (4–20 mA or 0–10 V) from a PID controller and modulates the motor speed to maintain the desired pressure or flow setpoint. The key advantage is the **cube-law energy savings** derived from pump affinity laws, where a small reduction in speed produces a large reduction in power consumption.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the three pump affinity laws and explain their practical significance in VFD-based pump control.*

**Model Answer:**
The three pump affinity laws relate the change in flow, head, and power to the change in pump speed, assuming constant impeller diameter and fluid properties:

$$\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}, \quad \frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$$

**Practical Significance:** When a VFD reduces the motor speed to match a reduced system demand, the flow decreases linearly, the head decreases with the square of the speed ratio, and the power decreases with the cube of the speed ratio. For example, a 20% speed reduction yields nearly **49% power savings**, which makes VFD retrofitting one of the most cost-effective energy conservation measures in pumping systems.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B carries 14-mark questions. Each question has two sub-parts (typically 7 marks each). Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** A centrifugal pump operates at a rated speed of $N_1 = 1500$ RPM, delivering $Q_1 = 180$ m³/h against a head $H_1 = 40$ m with an input power of $P_1 = 25$ kW. The system demand drops, and the pump speed is reduced to $N_2 = 1200$ RPM using a VFD. Calculate the new flow rate, head developed, and power consumed. Comment on the percentage energy saving achieved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **closed-loop automated pump control system** using a PID controller and a VFD. Clearly label the setpoint, error detector, controller, actuator, process, sensor, and feedback path. Briefly explain the role of SCADA in such systems. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Affinity Law Calculation**

**Given Data:**

$$N_1 = 1500 \text{ RPM}, \quad Q_1 = 180 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}, \quad H_1 = 40 \text{ m}, \quad P_1 = 25 \text{ kW}, \quad N_2 = 1200 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the Speed Ratio**

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1200}{1500} = 0.8$$

**Step 2 — Apply Affinity Law 1 (Flow)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_2 &= Q_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} \\
Q_2 &= 180 \times 0.8 \\
Q_2 &= 144 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Affinity Law 2 (Head)**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_2 &= H_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \\
H_2 &= 40 \times (0.8)^2 \\
H_2 &= 40 \times 0.64 \\
H_2 &= 25.6 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Affinity Law 3 (Power)**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \\
P_2 &= 25 \times (0.8)^3 \\
P_2 &= 25 \times 0.512 \\
P_2 &= 12.8 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Energy Saving Percentage**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_s &= \frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1} \times 100\% \\
C_s &= \frac{25 - 12.8}{25} \times 100\% \\
C_s &= \frac{12.2}{25} \times 100\% \\
C_s &= 48.8\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Summary Table:**

| **Parameter** | **Rated (1500 RPM)** | **Reduced (1200 RPM)** | **% Change** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow (m³/h) | 180 | 144 | –20% |
| Head (m) | 40 | 25.6 | –36% |
| Power (kW) | 25 | 12.8 | –48.8% |

**Comment:** A modest 20% reduction in pump speed produces a 48.8% reduction in power consumption. This demonstrates the **cube-law energy advantage** of VFD-based speed control and is the primary economic justification for VFD retrofits in industrial and municipal pumping installations.

#### **Part (b) — Block Diagram and Working Explanation**

Refer to the block diagram shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Working Explanation (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Setpoint (SP) Input:** The operator enters the desired discharge pressure (e.g., 3.5 bar) through the HMI/SCADA interface.
2. **Summing Junction:** The setpoint $SP$ is continuously compared with the **measured process variable** $PV$ from the pressure transmitter. The error is:
$$e(t) = SP - PV(t)$$
3. **PID Controller:** The error $e(t)$ is processed using the three-term algorithm:
$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$
   - If $PV$ is low (pressure dropped), $e > 0$, and the controller output increases.
   - If $PV$ is high (pressure surged), $e < 0$, and the controller output decreases.
4. **VFD as Actuator:** The PID output (e.g., 4–20 mA) is interpreted by the VFD as a frequency reference. The VFD rectifies AC to DC, smooths it, and inverts it back to a **variable-frequency AC** (via IGBT-based PWM) to drive the induction motor.
5. **Pump and Pipeline:** The motor speed change causes the pump impeller to rotate faster/slower, which alters the $Q$-$H$ operating point.
6. **Sensor Feedback:** The pressure transmitter on the discharge line continuously measures $PV$ and feeds it back to the summing junction.
7. **SCADA Role:** The SCADA system:
   - Displays real-time trends of $PV$, $SP$, $u(t)$, motor current, and frequency.
   - Logs historical data for analysis and energy audits.
   - Generates **alarms** for high pressure, low suction, motor overload, or sensor failure.
   - Allows **remote start/stop** and setpoint modification from a control room.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **PID controller** as applied to a constant-pressure pump control system. Define the proportional band, integral time, and derivative time, and describe the effect of increasing each gain on system response (rise time, overshoot, steady-state error). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor drives a centrifugal pump at 1450 RPM delivering 80 m³/h at 25 m head with an input power of 8.5 kW. Determine: (i) the slip, (ii) the hydraulic power developed, and (iii) the pump efficiency. The density of water is 1000 kg/m³. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — PID Controller Theory and Tuning Effects**

A **PID controller** is a feedback mechanism widely used in industrial automation, including pump speed control, to continuously calculate an error value $e(t)$ as the difference between a desired setpoint and a measured process variable, and apply a correction based on proportional, integral, and derivative terms.

**PID Output Equation:**

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Definitions of Tuning Parameters:**

- **Proportional Band (PB):** The range of error over which the controller output changes from 0% to 100%. Mathematically,
$$PB\,(\%) = \frac{100}{K_p}$$
  A smaller PB means a higher $K_p$ (more aggressive correction).

- **Integral Time ($T_i$):** Also called "reset time," it is the time required for the integral term to replicate the proportional contribution. The integral gain is related as:
$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i}$$
  A shorter $T_i$ means faster integral action (eliminates offset quicker but risks oscillation).

- **Derivative Time ($T_d$):** The time by which the derivative action advances the proportional response. The derivative gain is:
$$K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$$
  A longer $T_d$ means stronger damping (reduces overshoot but can amplify noise).

**Effect of Increasing Each Gain on System Response:**

| **Gain Increased** | **Rise Time** | **Overshoot** | **Settling Time** | **Steady-State Error** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $K_p \uparrow$ | Decreases | Increases | May increase | Decreases (but not zero) |
| $K_i \uparrow$ | Decreases slightly | Increases | Increases | **Eliminated (becomes zero)** |
| $K_d \uparrow$ | Minor change | **Decreases** | Decreases | No direct effect |

**Application to Constant-Pressure Pump Control:**

In a constant-pressure system, the **setpoint** is the desired discharge pressure (e.g., 3 bar). The **process variable** is the actual pressure measured by a pressure transmitter. When the demand increases (multiple taps opened), the pressure $PV$ drops, $e > 0$, and the controller increases the VFD frequency, raising motor speed and restoring pressure. The proportional term provides **immediate correction**, the integral term eliminates any **residual offset** (e.g., 2.95 bar instead of 3.0 bar), and the derivative term **damps oscillations** caused by sudden load changes.

**Common Tuning Methods (Knowledge Module):**

1. **Ziegler-Nichols Method:** Ultimate gain $K_u$ and ultimate period $P_u$ are determined experimentally; tuning constants are set as $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$.
2. **Cohen-Coon Method:** Uses process reaction curve parameters (dead time, time constant).
3. **Auto-tuning:** Modern PID controllers self-tune by injecting small perturbations and analyzing the response.

#### **Part (b) — Motor Slip, Hydraulic Power, and Pump Efficiency**

**Given Data:**

$$P = 4 \text{ poles}, \quad f = 50 \text{ Hz}, \quad N = 1450 \text{ RPM}$$

$$Q = 80 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}, \quad H = 25 \text{ m}, \quad P_{input} = 8.5 \text{ kW}, \quad \rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(i) Slip Calculation**

**Step 1 — Synchronous Speed**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 — Slip**

$$s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1450}{1500} = \frac{50}{1500} = 0.0333 \text{ (or 3.33%)}$$

**(ii) Hydraulic Power Developed**

**Step 1 — Convert Flow to SI Units**

$$Q = 80 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = \frac{80}{3600} = 0.02222 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hydraulic Power Formula**

$$P_{hyd} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.02222 \times 25$$

$$P_{hyd} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.02222 \times 25 \approx 5449.4 \text{ W} \approx 5.45 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Pump Efficiency**

**Step 1 — Convert Input Power to Watts**

$$P_{input} = 8.5 \text{ kW} = 8500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Efficiency Formula**

$$\eta_{pump} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{P_{input}} = \frac{5449.4}{8500} \approx 0.6411 = 64.11\%$$

**Summary of Results:**

| **Parameter** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Synchronous Speed $N_s$ | 1500 RPM |
| Slip $s$ | 0.0333 (3.33%) |
| Hydraulic Power $P_{hyd}$ | 5.45 kW |
| Pump Efficiency $\eta_{pump}$ | 64.11% |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Flow Conversion:** Students often forget to convert $Q$ from **m³/h to m³/s** before applying $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$. This is the **#1 cause of numerical errors** in pump power questions. Always write the conversion step explicitly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Cube in Power Affinity Law:** Many students incorrectly apply $P \propto N$ instead of $P \propto N^3$. Remember: $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.
>
> 3. **Assuming VFD Efficiency is 100%:** In practical problems, account for $\eta_{VFD} \approx 0.95$–$0.98$. The electrical input power is:
> $$P_{electrical} = \frac{P_{brake}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{VFD}}$$
>
> 4. **Confusing Slip with Slip Ratio:** Slip is a *small fractional quantity* (typically 0.02–0.06). Slip ratio = $\frac{N_s - N}{N}$, which has a different formula.
>
> 5. **Applying Affinity Laws to Positive Displacement Pumps:** Affinity laws are derived for **centrifugal (turbo) machines** based on dynamic similarity. **They are NOT valid for positive displacement pumps** (reciprocating, gear, screw pumps), where flow is *directly proportional to speed* but head is independent of speed.
>
> 6. **Not Specifying the Setpoint in Block Diagram:** KTU examiners specifically check whether the student has **labeled both** the setpoint and feedback path with **arrows indicating signal direction**. Missing arrows = lost marks.
>
> 7. **Writing "PLC" and "PID Controller" Interchangeably:** They are different. A **PID controller** is a dedicated hardware device implementing the PID algorithm; a **PLC** is a general-purpose programmable logic controller that *can* run a PID algorithm as one of its many functions. Use the correct term.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Automation in Pump Control Systems**

- ✅ **Automation in pumps** = Sensor + Controller + Actuator + Feedback loop. Without feedback, the system is *open-loop* and cannot self-correct.
- ✅ **The three pump affinity laws** are: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$. They are valid for **centrifugal pumps** operating under **dynamic similarity** (constant impeller diameter, constant fluid).
- ✅ **Synchronous speed** of an induction motor: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$. Actual rotor speed: $N = N_s(1 - s)$.
- ✅ **VFD** is the preferred final control element in modern pump automation because it converts electrical power at variable frequency to drive the motor at variable speed, enabling **cube-law energy savings**.
- ✅ **PID controller output**: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$.
   - $K_p$ → reacts to **current** error.
   - $K_i$ → reacts to **accumulated past** error (eliminates offset).
   - $K_d$ → reacts to **rate of change** of error (damps oscillation).
- ✅ **Hydraulic power** delivered to fluid: $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$ (in Watts when $Q$ is in m³/s, $H$ in m).
- ✅ **Pump efficiency**: $\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{P_{brake}} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{input} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{VFD}}$.
- ✅ **Energy saving %** with VFD: $C_s = \left(1 - \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3\right) \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Control strategies**: Constant pressure (booster pumps), constant flow (process pumps), level control (sump pumps), temperature control (HVAC chillers), cascade control (boiler feed).
- ✅ **Sensors used**: Pressure transmitter, flow meter (electromagnetic, turbine, Coriolis), level sensor (ultrasonic, capacitive, float), temperature sensor (RTD, thermocouple).
- ✅ **SCADA / HMI** provides real-time monitoring, alarming, data logging, and remote operation of automated pump stations.
- ✅ **Protection features** in modern pump automation: dry-run protection, overload trip, phase sequence correction, cavitation detection, over-temperature shutdown, and bearing vibration monitoring.
- ✅ **Industrial communication protocols** for pump automation: 4–20 mA analog, 0–10 V analog, HART, Modbus RTU/TCP, Profibus, Foundation Fieldbus, and Ethernet/IP.
- ✅ **Avoid common errors**: wrong units, forgetting the cube, applying affinity laws to PD pumps, and omitting arrows/labels in block diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) in Pump Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT522 - PUMP ENGINEERING AND HYDRAULIC CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) in Pump Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC):** A *solid-state, microprocessor-based industrial computer* designed specifically for the reliable, real-time control of manufacturing processes and machinery in harsh industrial environments. It performs control functions through user-programmable logic instructions stored in its non-volatile memory, replacing traditional relay-based, hard-wired control systems.

In the context of **pump engineering**, a PLC is a digital industrial controller that continuously monitors input signals from sensors (level switches, pressure transmitters, flow meters, thermal sensors) and executes pre-programmed logic to control output actuators (motor contactors, soft starters, VFDs, solenoid valves, and alarm indicators) associated with pump operation.

The PLC operates on a deterministic, *cyclic scan-based execution model* governed by the **IEC 61131-3** international standard, which defines five programming languages:
- **Ladder Diagram (LD)** – the most widely used in pump control
- **Function Block Diagram (FBD)**
- **Structured Text (ST)**
- **Instruction List (IL)** – legacy
- **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** – for complex sequences

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Light Brain"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: PLC = The Brain of a Traffic Signal System**

Imagine a city traffic signal. The signal cannot be "smart" by itself. It needs:
- **Sensors (Inputs):** Induction loops under the road detecting vehicles, pedestrian push buttons.
- **A Brain (Processor):** A small computer cabinet at the intersection running a pre-written set of rules: *"If vehicle detected AND pedestrian button pressed, then change light to red for cars, green for pedestrians after 30 seconds."*
- **Actuators (Outputs):** The red, yellow, and green lamps that physically turn ON/OFF.

A **PLC in a pump station works identically**:
- **Inputs** = float switches, pressure switches, flow sensors, motor overload contacts.
- **CPU + Ladder Logic** = the set of rules stored in memory (e.g., "If low-level switch is ON AND pump is healthy, START pump").
- **Outputs** = motor contactor coil, VFD run command, alarm hooter, indicator lamp.

Just as traffic signals must be **rugged, weatherproof, and fail-safe**, the PLC is engineered for dust, heat, vibration, electrical noise, and continuous 24/7 operation — environments where a normal desktop PC would fail in days.

### 1.3 Engineering Relevance and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standards & Specifications to Remember:**
> - **Scan Time:** Typically **5 ms to 50 ms** per cycle for small-to-medium PLCs.
> - **Operating Temperature:** Industrial PLCs rated for **0 °C to 60 °C** (extended: −40 °C to 85 °C).
> - **Power Supply:** Standard **24 V DC** for I/O, **120/230 V AC** for the CPU rack.
> - **Memory Types:** RAM (volatile) backed up by lithium battery, EEPROM/Flash (non-volatile) for program retention.
> - **I/O Density:** Modular PLCs offer **4, 8, 16, or 32 points per module**.

### 1.4 Why PLCs Replaced Relay Logic in Pump Control

| Parameter | Relay-Based Control | PLC-Based Control |
|---|---|---|
| Wiring complexity | High (hundreds of wires for large pumps) | Low (sensor + bus cable) |
| Fault diagnosis | Difficult, manual tracing | Built-in diagnostics, HMI display |
| Logic changes | Rewire the panel (hours/days) | Modify software (minutes) |
| Reliability | Mechanical wear, contact pitting | Solid-state, MTBF > 100,000 hrs |
| Scalability | Add relays + wiring | Add I/O module, one plug-in |
| Cost for complex logic | Very high | Comparatively low |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PLC Hardware Architecture — The Five Pillars

Every PLC, regardless of manufacturer (Siemens, Allen-Bradley/Rockwell, Schneider/Modicon, Mitsubishi, ABB), consists of five functional blocks:

**1. Power Supply Unit (PSU)**
- Converts incoming **230 V AC** mains to isolated, regulated voltages.
- Typically outputs **+5 V DC** for the CPU logic and **+24 V DC** for the sensor/input circuits.
- Must withstand brown-outs and voltage sags without resetting.

**2. Central Processing Unit (CPU)**
- Contains the **microprocessor**, the **system memory**, and the **scan engine**.
- Executes the user program, performs arithmetic, logic, and timing operations.
- Houses the **operating system firmware** (read-only).

**3. Memory Architecture**
- **System ROM/Flash:** Operating system, not user-modifiable.
- **RAM (working memory):** Holds the user program during execution. Backed by a **lithium battery** for 5–10 years of retention.
- **Non-volatile Flash/EEPROM:** Stores the user program permanently; uploaded on power-up.

**4. Input Modules (DI / AI)**
- **Digital Input (DI):** Accepts ON/OFF signals from limit switches, float switches, pushbuttons, overload relay contacts. Typical voltage: **24 V DC**.
- **Analog Input (AI):** Accepts continuously variable signals: **4–20 mA current loop** or **0–10 V DC** from pressure transmitters, level transducers, flow meters, temperature probes.

**5. Output Modules (DO / AO)**
- **Digital Output (DO):** Switches 24 V DC or 230 V AC loads (relay coils, contactors, solenoid valves, indicator lamps). Rated typically **2 A per point**.
- **Analog Output (AO):** Generates 4–20 mA or 0–10 V for VFD speed reference, proportional valve control.

**6. Communication / Interface Modules**
- **HMI Panel Port** (RS-232/RS-485/USB/Ethernet).
- **Fieldbus / Industrial Ethernet:** Profibus, Profinet, Modbus RTU/TCP, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT.
- Enables PLC-to-PLC, PLC-to-VFD, PLC-to-SCADA networking.

### 2.2 The PLC Scan Cycle — The Heartbeat of Operation

> [!NOTE]
> **The Scan Cycle is the single most important concept in PLC operation. KTU questions frequently test this directly.**

A PLC does not execute logic continuously. It runs a strictly sequential, **deterministic loop** that repeats every few milliseconds:

**Step 1 — INPUT SCAN (Read):**
The CPU reads the *current status* of every physical input point and stores a binary "image" in a dedicated memory area called the **Process Image Input (PII)** register. A 4–20 mA pressure signal is digitized (e.g., into a 12-bit integer 0–4095).

**Step 2 — PROGRAM SCAN (Execute):**
The CPU executes the user-written ladder logic instruction-by-instruction, top-to-bottom, left-to-right. It uses the PII values as inputs and writes intermediate results to internal "bit memory" (M flags, timers, counters) — it does NOT touch the real outputs yet.

**Step 3 — OUTPUT SCAN (Write):**
At the end of program execution, the CPU transfers the final logic results from the **Process Image Output (PIO)** register to the physical output terminals. The contactor energizes, the VFD receives its run command.

**Step 4 — Housekeeping:**
Communication processing, self-diagnostics, fault checking, then loop back to Step 1.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Equation / Relation | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scan Time | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm}$ | **5 – 50 ms** | Determines max response time to a fault |
| ADC Resolution | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | 12-bit → 4095 counts | Step size of analog input |
| Pressure → Current | $I_{mA} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{range}}$ | 4–20 mA loop | Transmitter scaling |
| Level → Counts | $N_{count} = \dfrac{L_{actual}}{L_{max}} \cdot (2^n - 1)$ | 0–100 % mapped to 0–4095 | Ultrasonic/Radar scaling |
| VFD Speed Reference | $f_{out} = f_{min} + (f_{max} - f_{min}) \cdot \dfrac{I_{mA} - 4}{16}$ | Hz | Analog speed command |
| Power Consumption | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta$ | kW | Pump motor monitoring |
| Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | W | Flow × Head |
| Pump Affinity Law (Speed) | $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | dimensionless | VFD speed scaling |
| Pump Affinity Law (Head) | $\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$ | m | VFD head scaling |
| Motor Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | 0.02 – 0.05 typical | Speed feedback check |
| Energy Cost | $E_{cost} = P_{kW} \cdot t_{hr} \cdot C_{rate}$ | ₹/kWh | Pump OPEX calculation |
| Darcy–Weisbach (friction) | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | m of head | System curve derivation |
| Level Time Constant | $\tau = \dfrac{A_{tank}}{Q_{pump}}$ | seconds | Predict fill/drain time |
| Overload Trip | $I_{trip} = 1.15 \cdot I_{FLC}$ | Amps | Thermal overload setting |
| Deadband (Hysteresis) | $\Delta = X_{high} - X_{low}$ | bar or m | Prevents pump chattering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER use the `|` symbol inside any markdown table cell. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. This rule has been strictly observed in the above table.**

### 2.4 Ladder Logic Programming — The Language of Pump Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Ladder Diagram (LD)** is the de facto language for pump control. It visually mimics electrical relay schematics: two vertical "rails" (L1, L2) with horizontal "rungs" containing contacts and coils.

**Standard Symbols:**

- `—| |—` **Normally Open (NO) Contact** — passes power when input/flag is TRUE.
- `—|/|—` **Normally Closed (NC) Contact** — passes power when input/flag is FALSE.
- `—( )—` **Output Coil** — energizes when rung logic is TRUE.
- `—( / )—` **Negated (NOT) Coil**.
- `—( S )—` **Set (Latching) Coil** — sticks ON until reset.
- `—( R )—` **Reset Coil** — clears a set coil.
- `[TON]` **On-Delay Timer** — output TRUE only after input has been TRUE for preset time.
- `[TOF]` **Off-Delay Timer** — output remains TRUE for preset time after input goes FALSE.
- `[CTU]` **Counter Up** — increments on each rising edge.

**Boolean Logic Translation:**

A ladder rung with three NO contacts in series → **AND** logic:
$$Y = A \cdot B \cdot C$$

Two NO contacts in parallel rungs → **OR** logic:
$$Y = A + B$$

A NC contact in series with a NO contact → **NAND** (NOT-AND):
$$Y = \overline{A} \cdot B$$

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Pump Engineering

PLCs govern nearly every modern pump installation:
- **Borewell / Submersible pump control panels** in Kerala residential and agricultural applications.
- **Sewage lift stations** with multi-pump alternation and level-based staging.
- **Boiler feedwater pumps** in thermal power plants with pressure-transmitter-based modulation.
- **Fire-fighting jockey and main pumps** with pressure-switch interlocks and auto-test sequences.
- **HVAC chilled water pumps** integrated with Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) for differential pressure control.
- **Industrial process pumps** with PID control loops for flow, pressure, or temperature regulation.
- **Irrigation pump stations** with GSM/IoT remote monitoring and SCADA integration.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 4–20 mA Pressure Transmitter Scaling for PLC Analog Input

**Given:**
A pressure transmitter is ranged **0 – 10 bar**, with output current spanning the standard industrial loop of **4 – 20 mA**. The PLC's analog input module is 12-bit resolution, converting 4–20 mA into integer counts from **0 to 4095** (process value $N$).

**Step 1 — Find the pressure represented by the lower-range current (4 mA).**
The current loop standard uses "**4 mA = 0 % of range**" (zero suppression) to allow detection of a broken wire (which would read 0 mA — clearly outside the valid range).

$$P_{LRV} = 0 \text{ bar} \quad \text{at} \quad I = 4 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — Find the pressure represented by the upper-range current (20 mA).**
$$P_{URV} = 10 \text{ bar} \quad \text{at} \quad I = 20 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Write the linear interpolation equation in pressure-vs-current form.**

$$P = P_{LRV} + \left( P_{URV} - P_{LRV} \right) \cdot \dfrac{I - 4}{20 - 4}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values to obtain the working equation.**

$$P = 0 + (10 - 0) \cdot \dfrac{I - 4}{16}$$

$$P = 10 \cdot \dfrac{I - 4}{16}$$

$$P = \dfrac{10(I - 4)}{16} = \dfrac{5(I - 4)}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Convert current $I$ to PLC counts $N$ using the 12-bit resolution.**

For a 4–20 mA input, the PLC module typically maps:
- 4 mA → count 0 (or 6400 in some manufacturers)
- 20 mA → count 4095

Using 0–4095 mapping (most common):

$$N = \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \cdot 4095$$

**Step 6 — Express pressure directly in terms of raw PLC counts.**

$$P = 10 \cdot \dfrac{N}{4095}$$

$$P = \dfrac{10 \cdot N}{4095} \quad \text{[bar]}$$

**Step 7 — Verify with a numerical example.**

Let the actual pressure be **5 bar**. The transmitter should output:

$$I = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{5}{10} = 4 + 8 = 12 \text{ mA}$$

The PLC count should be:

$$N = \dfrac{12 - 4}{16} \cdot 4095 = \dfrac{8}{16} \cdot 4095 = 0.5 \cdot 4095 = 2047.5 \approx 2048$$

The computed pressure from PLC count:

$$P = \dfrac{10 \cdot 2048}{4095} = 5.001 \text{ bar} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This linearization is embedded inside the PLC's analog input "scaling block" (e.g., Siemens FB41, Allen-Bradley SCP). The raw integer count is converted into engineering units (bar, m³/h, °C) so that the rest of the program can use meaningful physical values.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Pump VFD Speed–Flow–Power Affinity for PLC Energy-Saving Logic

**Given:**
A centrifugal pump is rated for **$Q_1 = 100$ m³/h** at **$N_1 = 1450$ rpm**. The system needs only **$Q_2 = 70$ m³/h**. Determine the new speed and the power saved.

**Step 1 — Apply the Affinity Law for Flow (linear relationship).**

$$\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the new speed $N_2$.**

$$N_2 = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = 1450 \cdot \dfrac{70}{100} = 1015 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Affinity Law for Power (cubic relationship).**

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left( \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute the power ratio.**

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left( \dfrac{70}{100} \right)^3 = 0.7^3 = 0.343$$

**Step 5 — Express actual powers.** If $P_1 = 15$ kW, then:

$$P_2 = 0.343 \cdot 15 = 5.145 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Compute energy saved.**

$$\Delta P = 15 - 5.145 = 9.855 \text{ kW}$$

Over 5000 operating hours/year at ₹8/kWh:

$$E_{saved} = 9.855 \cdot 5000 \cdot 8 = \text{₹ } 3,94,200 \text{ per year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **PLC Implementation Insight:** The PLC reads the actual flow transmitter signal, computes the required speed via the affinity law, and outputs a 4–20 mA speed reference to the VFD — closing the loop automatically. This is the basis of **constant-flow / constant-pressure pump control**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Pump-Control Scenarios & PLC Logic

| Scenario | Inputs Used | Outputs Controlled | Core Ladder Logic | Set-Point Logic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sump Emptying (Drainage)** | Float switches: LL, L, HL | Pump contactor, alarm | `—| LL_|/|—| Pump_Healthy |—( RUN )—` | Start at L, Stop at LL, Alarm at HL |
| **Tank Filling (Supply)** | Float switches: LL, L, HH | Pump contactor, inlet valve | `—| L_|/|—| NOT_Running—( START )—`; `—| HH |—( STOP )—` | Start at LL, Stop at HH |
| **Pressure Boosting** | Pressure transmitter PT | VFD speed ref, alarm | Compare PT vs SP → PID block → AO | Maintain 4.0 bar, ±0.2 bar deadband |
| **Dual-Pump Alternation** | Level switches, hours-run meter | Pump 1 & 2 contactors | Toggle based on `Lead_Select` flag | Run lead pump, alternate on each cycle |
| **Borewell Dry-Run Protection** | Current sensor (CT) + level probe | Pump contactor | If $I < I_{min}$ for $t > 10s$ → Trip | $I_{min} = 0.3 \cdot I_{FLC}$ |
| **Fire Jockey Pump** | Pressure switch PS_jockey | Jockey contactor | Start at 7 bar, Stop at 9 bar | Main pump at 6 bar |
| **Boiler Feed Pump** | Drum level LT + flow FT | VFD + trip | Three-element drum level control | Maintain 50 % level |
| **Soft-Start Sequence** | Start PB, Stop PB, OL contact | Soft starter | Bypass contactor after ramp-up | 8-second ramp |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Scan-Cycle Worst-Case Response Time

**Given:**
A PLC has the following scan breakdown:
- Input scan: $T_{in} = 2$ ms
- Program execution: $T_{prog} = 8$ ms
- Output scan: $T_{out} = 1$ ms
- Communication overhead: $T_{comm} = 3$ ms

**Step 1 — Calculate the average scan time.**

$$T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$T_{scan} = 2 + 8 + 1 + 3 = 14 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the worst-case input-to-output latency.**

An input arriving *just after* the input scan completes must wait a full cycle before being acted on:

$$T_{worst} = T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm} + T_{in} = 14 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the best-case latency.**

An input arriving *just before* the input scan is read in the very next cycle:

$$T_{best} = T_{in} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Practical engineering rule.**

For safety-critical pump trips, total response = scan time + output relay dropout time + motor coast-down time:

$$T_{total} = T_{scan} + T_{relay} + T_{coast}$$

If $T_{relay} = 15$ ms and $T_{coast} = 4$ s:

$$T_{total} \approx 14 \text{ ms} + 15 \text{ ms} + 4000 \text{ ms} \approx 4.03 \text{ s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Emergency Shutdown (ESD) of large pumps, scan time alone is *not* the bottleneck — the mechanical inertia of the rotating pump impeller and motor (4–10 seconds) dominates the shutdown timeline. PLC scan time becomes critical only for control accuracy, not for safety shutdown.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The power supply (top-left) feeds the CPU and all I/O modules from a common rail. Field sensors (left side) feed into the digital and analog inputs. The CPU processes them and energizes the digital outputs (right side) which switch the contactor, the VFD analog speed reference, and the alarm devices. The communication module links the PLC to the HMI panel and the SCADA host.

### 4.2 PLC Scan Cycle Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> C[Store in Process Image Input PII]
    C --> D[Execute Ladder Logic Top to Bottom]
    D --> E[Update Internal Flags Timers Counters]
    E --> F[Transfer Results to Process Image Output PIO]
    F --> G[Write to Physical Output Terminals]
```

### 4.3 Dual-Pump Alternation Ladder Logic (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LS_HH[Level Switch HH High High] --> OR1
    LS_H[Level Switch H High] --> OR1
    LS_L[Level Switch L Low] --> AND1
    PB_Start[Pushbutton Manual Start] --> AND1
    AND1 --> SEL{Lead Pump Selector Alternates on Each Cycle}
    SEL --> P1[Pump 1 Contactor Coil]
    SEL --> P2[Pump 2 Contactor Coil]
    OR1 --> STOP[Hardwired Stop Command]
    STOP --> P1
    STOP --> P2
    P1 --> OL1{Overload Healthy}
    P2 --> OL2{Overload Healthy}
```

### 4.4 PLC-to-VFD Pump Speed Control Loop (PID Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Pressure 4.0 bar] --> ERR
    PT[Pressure Transmitter 4 to 20 mA] --> PV
    PV[Process Variable bar] --> ERR
    ERR[Summation Junction Error] --> PID[PID Controller Block in PLC]
    PID --> AO[Analog Output 4 to 20 mA]
    AO --> VFD[Variable Frequency Drive]
    VFD --> M[Pump Motor]
    M --> SYS[Hydraulic System Pipe Network]
    SYS --> PT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** This is a **closed-loop feedback system**. The PLC acts as the PID controller. The pressure transmitter (PT) measures the actual discharge pressure, the PLC computes the error against the setpoint, applies the PID algorithm ($u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$), and outputs a 4–20 mA speed reference to the VFD. The VFD varies pump speed to maintain the setpoint despite varying demand.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Define a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC). List any FOUR advantages of using a PLC over a conventional relay-based control system for pump control.**

**Model Answer:**
A Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) is a microprocessor-based, solid-state industrial computer that uses user-programmable instructions stored in non-volatile memory to implement control functions for machinery and processes in harsh industrial environments.

Advantages of PLC over relay logic for pump control:
1. **Flexible Logic Modification:** Control logic can be altered by editing software in minutes, while relay logic requires physical rewiring that may take hours.
2. **Built-in Diagnostics:** PLCs provide real-time fault indication (sensor failure, I/O module error, communication loss) on the HMI or via indicator LEDs, enabling faster troubleshooting.
3. **Higher Reliability:** Solid-state components have no moving contacts, so there is no mechanical wear, contact pitting, or coil burnout — common failure modes in relay panels.
4. **Compact Footprint:** A single PLC replaces dozens of relays, timers, and counters, reducing panel size, wiring, and heat generation.
5. **Remote Monitoring:** PLCs can communicate with SCADA/IoT systems via Ethernet/Modbus, enabling remote pump control from a control room.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**

**Q2. Explain the three stages of a PLC scan cycle with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**
A PLC executes its user program in a strictly cyclic manner. Each complete cycle consists of three primary stages:

1. **Input Scan (Read Stage):** The CPU reads the status of every physical input terminal and stores a snapshot in a dedicated memory area called the **Process Image Input (PII)**. This decouples the physical input from program execution.

2. **Program Scan (Execute Stage):** The CPU executes the user ladder logic rung-by-rung, top-to-bottom, left-to-right. It uses the PII values, performs the Boolean/arithmetic operations, updates timers/counters, and computes the desired output states. The actual physical outputs are NOT updated during this stage.

3. **Output Scan (Write Stage):** The CPU transfers the final computed states from the **Process Image Output (PIO)** to the physical output terminals, energizing contactors, lamps, or VFD commands. A housekeeping stage then handles communication and diagnostics before the cycle restarts.

Typical scan time: **5 – 50 ms**.

---

### Part B — Full-Question 14-Mark Module Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model ESE Question, 14 Marks]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — PLC Architecture and Ladder Logic Design

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five major functional blocks** of a PLC. State the role of the **Process Image Input (PII)** and **Process Image Output (PIO)** in the scan cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sump has three float switches — **LSL (Low), LSH (High), LSHH (High-High)**. Design a ladder logic program to:
- **Start the drainage pump** when the water level reaches LSH.
- **Stop the pump** when the water falls below LSL.
- **Sound an alarm** if the level reaches LSHH, and **lock out the pump** until manual reset.
- Include **overload protection** and a **manual/auto selector switch**.
- Include a **run-indication lamp** and a **trip-indication lamp**.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) [7 Marks] — PLC Architecture:**

**The five functional blocks of a PLC are:**

1. **Power Supply Unit (PSU):** Converts 230 V AC mains into regulated +5 V DC (for CPU/logic) and +24 V DC (for I/O circuits and sensors). Provides isolation, surge protection, and ride-through capability for brief voltage sags.

2. **Central Processing Unit (CPU):** The "brain" containing the microprocessor, system firmware in ROM, working RAM, and the scan engine. It fetches and executes user-programmed instructions, performs Boolean, arithmetic, and timing operations.

3. **Memory:** Divided into:
   - **System ROM/Flash** (operating system, non-user).
   - **RAM** (working memory, holds live data; battery-backed).
   - **Non-volatile Flash/EEPROM** (stores user program permanently).

4. **Input/Output Modules:**
   - **Digital Input (DI):** Reads ON/OFF signals (24 V DC) from float switches, pushbuttons, overload contacts.
   - **Analog Input (AI):** Reads variable signals (4–20 mA or 0–10 V) from pressure/level/flow transmitters.
   - **Digital Output (DO):** Switches 24 V DC or 230 V AC loads (relay coils, contactors, lamps, alarms).
   - **Analog Output (AO):** Generates 4–20 mA or 0–10 V for VFD speed reference or proportional valve control.

5. **Communication / Programming Interface:**
   - **Programming port** (USB, RS-232, Ethernet) for connecting the engineering laptop.
   - **Fieldbus / Industrial Ethernet** (Profinet, Modbus, EtherNet/IP) for PLC-to-VFD, PLC-to-PLC, and PLC-to-SCADA networking.
   - **HMI port** for operator touch panel.

**Role of PII and PIO:**

The **Process Image Input (PII)** is a dedicated memory register where the CPU stores a "snapshot" of all input states at the start of each scan. During program execution, the ladder logic reads from this *image* rather than the physical terminal, ensuring consistent values throughout the scan and preventing mid-scan glitches.

The **Process Image Output (PIO)** is the corresponding register where the CPU writes the computed results during program execution. At the end of the program scan, the contents of the PIO are transferred simultaneously to all physical output terminals in a single burst — ensuring deterministic, glitch-free switching.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Power Supply] --> C[CPU]
    P --> I[Input Modules]
    P --> O[Output Modules]
    C --> M[Memory]
    I -->|PII| C
    C -->|PIO| O
    C --> K[Comm Port]
    K --> H[HMI]
    K --> S[SCADA]
```

---

**(b) [7 Marks] — Ladder Logic Design for Sump Pump:**

**Input/Output Address Allocation:**

| Address | Device | Type |
|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | LSL — Low-Level Switch (NO) | DI |
| I0.1 | LSH — High-Level Switch (NO) | DI |
| I0.2 | LSHH — High-High Level Switch (NO) | DI |
| I0.3 | Manual Start Pushbutton (NO) | DI |
| I0.4 | Manual Stop Pushbutton (NC) | DI |
| I0.5 | Overload Relay Contact (NC) | DI |
| I0.6 | Auto/Manual Selector (1 = Auto) | DI |
| I0.7 | Reset Pushbutton (NO) | DI |
| Q0.0 | Pump Contactor Coil (K1) | DO |
| Q0.1 | Run Indication Lamp (Green, H1) | DO |
| Q0.2 | Trip Indication Lamp (Red, H2) | DO |
| Q0.3 | Alarm Hooter (H3) | DO |
| M0.0 | Auto Start Latch (internal flag) | Memory |
| M0.1 | LSHH Trip Latch (internal flag) | Memory |

**Ladder Rungs:**

**Rung 1 — Manual/Off Control (Master Latch):**
```
—I0.3 (Start PB)——I0.4 (Stop PB, NC)——I0.5 (OL, NC)——I0.6 (Auto=0)——( M0.0 )
```
*(Manual mode: pressing start energizes Master Latch M0.0; stop, overload, or Auto mode resets it.)*

**Rung 2 — Auto Start Latch:**
```
—I0.6 (Auto=1)——I0.1 (LSH)——/M0.1 (Trip NOT active)——( S M0.0 )  ; Set Master
—I0.6 (Auto=1)——I0.0 (LSL NC)——( R M0.0 )                    ; Reset when level below LSL
```

**Rung 3 — Pump Output:**
```
—M0.0——I0.5 (OL, NC)——/M0.1 (Trip)——( Q0.0 K1 )
```

**Rung 4 — Run Indication:**
```
—Q0.0 (Pump contactor feedback)——( Q0.1 H1 Green Lamp )
```

**Rung 5 — High-High Trip & Alarm Latch:**
```
—I0.2 (LSHH)——( S M0.1 )   ; Set trip latch
```
**Rung 6 — Trip Outputs:**
```
—M0.1 (Trip)——( Q0.2 H2 Red Lamp )
—M0.1 (Trip)——( Q0.3 H3 Alarm Hooter )
```

**Rung 7 — Manual Reset of Trip:**
```
—I0.7 (Reset PB)——( R M0.1 )   ; Reset trip latch
```

**Operating Sequence Description:**

In **Auto mode** (I0.6 = 1):
- When water reaches **LSH**, the PLC latches M0.0 ON, energizing contactor K1, starting the pump, and lighting the green lamp.
- The pump runs until the level falls **below LSL**, at which point I0.0 opens and resets M0.0, stopping the pump.
- If the level reaches **LSHH** (catastrophic), the trip latch M0.1 sets, the alarm hooter sounds, the red lamp lights, and the pump is forced OFF. The system remains locked out until an operator presses the **Reset pushbutton (I0.7)**.

In **Manual mode** (I0.6 = 0):
- The Start/Stop pushbuttons directly control the master latch M0.0; float switches are ignored except for the LSHH trip override.

**Overload protection** is implemented via the NC overload contact I0.5 wired in series with the pump output coil; any overload instantly drops out the contactor.
