## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Savings from Variable Speed Drives Using the Affinity Laws

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A centrifugal pump delivers $Q_1 = 0.10 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ at $H_1 = 30 \text{ m}$ when running at $N_1 = 1450 \text{ rpm}$. The system requires only $Q_2 = 0.07 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ during night-time low-demand. Using a VSD, the speed is reduced to $N_2$. Calculate: (a) the new speed $N_2$, (b) the new head $H_2$, and (c) the **percentage power saving** achieved.

**Assumptions:** Flow is proportional to speed (Affinity Law valid in the homologous zone); water density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$; pump efficiency assumed constant at $\eta = 75\%$ for the comparison.

#### Step (a) — Calculate the Reduced Speed $N_2$

From the Affinity Law for flow:

$$
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}
$$

Rearranging for $N_2$:

$$
N_2 = N_1 \cdot \frac{Q_2}{Q_1}
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
N_2 = 1450 \cdot \frac{0.07}{0.10}
$$

$$
N_2 = 1450 \cdot 0.7
$$

$$
\boxed{N_2 = 1015 \text{ rpm}}
$$

> **Logic:** Because flow is linearly proportional to speed, a 30 % reduction in required flow translates directly to a 30 % reduction in rotational speed.

#### Step (b) — Calculate the New Head $H_2$

From the Affinity Law for head:

$$
\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
H_2 = H_1 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2
$$

Substitute:

$$
H_2 = 30 \cdot \left(\frac{1015}{1450}\right)^2
$$

$$
H_2 = 30 \cdot (0.7)^2
$$

$$
H_2 = 30 \cdot 0.49
$$

$$
\boxed{H_2 = 14.7 \text{ m}}
$$

> **Logic:** Even though we only needed to reduce flow by 30 %, the head capability drops by $1 - 0.49 = 51\%$. The system must still have at least $14.7 \text{ m}$ of head available; otherwise, the VSD will drive the pump into **deep part-load** and the efficiency assumption collapses.

#### Step (c) — Percentage Power Saving

Hydraulic power at the original operating point:

$$
P_{hyd,1} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_1 \cdot H_1
$$

$$
P_{hyd,1} = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 30
$$

$$
P_{hyd,1} = 29430 \text{ W} = 29.43 \text{ kW}
$$

Shaft input power at original point (assuming $\eta_1 = 0.75$):

$$
P_{shaft,1} = \frac{P_{hyd,1}}{\eta_1} = \frac{29.43}{0.75}
$$

$$
P_{shaft,1} = 39.24 \text{ kW}
$$

Hydraulic power at the new operating point:

$$
P_{hyd,2} = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.07 \cdot 14.7
$$

$$
P_{hyd,2} = 10095.69 \text{ W} = 10.10 \text{ kW}
$$

Shaft input power at the new point (assuming $\eta_2 \approx 0.75$):

$$
P_{shaft,2} = \frac{10.10}{0.75} = 13.47 \text{ kW}
$$

**Percentage Power Saving:**

$$
\%\text{Saving} = \frac{P_{shaft,1} - P_{shaft,2}}{P_{shaft,1}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{Saving} = \frac{39.24 - 13.47}{39.24} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{Saving} = \frac{25.77}{39.24} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\%\text{Saving} \approx 65.7\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 30 % flow reduction through a 30 % speed reduction yields nearly **65.7 % power savings** — confirming the **cube-law** behavior of the Affinity relationship.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Anticipated Future Technologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU comparison tool**. Examiners frequently ask students to "compare two approaches" worth 7 marks. Use this format.

| Evaluation Criterion | Conventional Fixed-Speed Pump | Smart VSD + IIoT Pump |
|----------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Energy Efficiency** | Low; throttled via valves | High; follows load curve |
| **Capital Cost (CAPEX)** | ₹ 4–6 lakh (low) | ₹ 7–10 lakh (moderate) |
| **Operating Cost (OPEX)** | High (constant full-speed) | Low (cube-law savings) |
| **Maintenance Philosophy** | Reactive / scheduled | Predictive (AI/ML) |
| **Data Availability** | Manual logs only | Continuous cloud telemetry |
| **Compliance with MEI ≥ 0.7** | Often fails | Easily meets |
| **CO₂ Footprint** | High | Reduced by **40–60 %** |
| **Skill Requirement** | Basic electrician | PLC + Data Engineer |
| **Cyber-Security Risk** | Negligible | High (requires firewalls) |
| **Payback Period** | — | **2–4 years** typical |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Minimum Efficiency Index (MEI)" as per the EU Ecodesign Directive. Why is it critical for future pump selection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Minimum Efficiency Index (MEI)** is a dimensionless metric defined as:

$$
\text{MEI} = \frac{\eta_{pump,actual\,at\,BEP}}{\eta_{pump,reference}}
$$

It is specified in the **EU Regulation 2019/1781** and applies to rotodynamic water pumps with a rated hydraulic power between **1 kW and 150 kW**. From **January 2025**, all such pumps sold in the EU must achieve an **MEI ≥ 0.70**.

**Why it matters (1 mark each):**
- It pushes manufacturers toward **higher hydraulic efficiency**, reducing global electricity demand.
- It standardizes **global procurement** — engineers can compare pumps objectively across vendors.
- It supports **carbon neutrality goals** under the Paris Agreement and India's **Panchamrit** commitment.

> **Distribution:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for numerical threshold, 1 mark for engineering significance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
*List any **three** anticipated future developments in pump engineering and briefly explain each in one line.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Digital Twin Integration** — A virtual replica of the pump simulates performance and predicts failures before they occur in the physical asset. *(1 mark)*
2. **AI/ML-Based Predictive Maintenance** — Machine learning models analyze vibration and current signatures to estimate the **Remaining Useful Life (RUL)** of bearings and impellers. *(1 mark)*
3. **Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing) of Impellers** — Enables complex internal flow passages that are not castable, improving hydraulic efficiency by **15–25 %**. *(1 mark)*

> **Examiner's note:** Any three well-explained future trends earn full marks. Avoid vague answers like "automation" — be specific.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]*

**A.** *(a)* Explain the concept of **"Smart Pump"** with a labeled block diagram, describing the role of **IIoT sensors, edge gateways, and cloud analytics** in achieving **predictive maintenance**.
*(7 marks)*

*(b)* A centrifugal pump operating at $N_1 = 2900 \text{ rpm}$ delivers $Q_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ against a head of $H_1 = 50 \text{ m}$ with a shaft power input of $P_1 = 35 \text{ kW}$. If a **VSD** is installed and the speed is reduced to $N_2 = 2320 \text{ rpm}$, calculate the **new flow, new head, new shaft power**, and the **percentage energy saving**. Assume pump efficiency remains constant. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Smart Pump Architecture (7 marks):**

A **Smart Pump** is a pumping system in which **physical sensors, communication modules, and intelligent software** are integrated to enable **autonomous, data-driven operation**. Its architecture has four layers:

1. **Physical Layer (1 mark):** Includes the pump, motor, VSD, and embedded sensors — vibration (accelerometer), temperature (RTD), flow (EM flowmeter), pressure (strain-gauge transducer), and current (CT).
2. **Edge Layer (1.5 marks):** A PLC or edge gateway collects data via **Modbus/Profibus**, runs local control logic, and publishes to the cloud over **MQTT/HTTP** with **TLS encryption**.
3. **Cloud Layer (2 marks):** A time-series database (e.g., **InfluxDB, AWS IoT Core**) feeds an **AI/ML anomaly detection engine**. The ML model — typically an **Isolation Forest** or **LSTM** — classifies vibration spectra and predicts **Remaining Useful Life (RUL)**.
4. **Decision Layer (1.5 marks):** A dashboard visualizes **KPI trends** (flow, head, efficiency, SEC). When the model flags a fault, it auto-generates a **work order in the CMMS** (e.g., SAP PM).

**Predictive maintenance outcome:** Downtime reduced by up to **70 %**; unplanned failures cut by **50 %**; maintenance cost reduction of **25–30 %**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — New Flow $Q_2$:** Using Affinity Law $Q \propto N$:

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 0.05 \cdot \frac{2320}{2900} = 0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — New Head $H_2$:** Using $H \propto N^2$:

$$
H_2 = H_1 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 50 \cdot (0.8)^2 = 50 \cdot 0.64 = 32 \text{ m}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Original Efficiency $\eta_1$:**

$$
P_{hyd,1} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_1 \cdot H_1 = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 50 = 24525 \text{ W} = 24.525 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{P_{hyd,1}}{P_1} = \frac{24.525}{35} = 0.7007 \approx 70.07\%
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — New Hydraulic Power $P_{hyd,2}$:**

$$
P_{hyd,2} = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 32 = 12556.8 \text{ W} = 12.557 \text{ kW}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — New Shaft Power $P_2$** (assuming $\eta$ constant at 70.07 %):

$$
P_2 = \frac{P_{hyd,2}}{\eta_1} = \frac{12.557}{0.7007} = 17.92 \text{ kW}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 6 — Percentage Energy Saving:**

$$
\%\text{Saving} = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1} \times 100 = \frac{35 - 17.92}{35} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\%\text{Saving} \approx 48.8\%}
$$

*(2 marks: 1 for setup, 1 for final answer with correct units)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Many students apply the cube law $P \propto N^3$ directly **without first verifying** whether the Affinity Law conditions (homologous operation, negligible viscosity change) hold. Always state the **limitation** explicitly. *(−1 mark if omitted)*
> - A common error is to use $P_2 = P_1 \cdot (N_2/N_1)^3 = 35 \cdot (0.8)^3 = 17.92 \text{ kW}$ — this is **numerically equivalent** here, but in problems where the system curve and pump curve intersect differently, the two methods diverge. Examiners test this distinction deliberately. *(−1 to −2 marks for blindly using the cube law without justification)*
> - Forgetting to convert $P_{hyd}$ from W to kW costs **0.5 mark** consistently.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

**B.** *(a)* Discuss **digital twin technology** in pump engineering. Compare it with conventional physical testing in terms of **cost, time, accuracy, and risk**. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Estimate the **annual energy cost saving (in ₹)** when replacing a **throttled fixed-speed pump** with a **VSD-controlled** version in a process plant. Given: Original operation — $Q = 0.08 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $H = 25 \text{ m}$, $\eta_{fixed} = 65\%$, plant operates **6000 hours/year**, and electricity tariff is **₹ 8 per kWh**. The VSD operates at **80 % of rated speed** for **70 % of the year** and full speed for the remaining **30 %**. Use $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Digital Twin Technology (7 marks):**

A **Digital Twin** is a **continuously updated, multi-physics virtual replica** of a physical pump that mirrors its real-time state through sensor data. It integrates **CFD (computational fluid dynamics)**, **FEA (finite element analysis)**, and **thermal-electrical models**.

**Comparison Table (to be drawn in exam):**

| Parameter | Physical Testing | Digital Twin |
|-----------|------------------|--------------|
| **Cost** | ₹ 15–25 lakhs per test rig | ₹ 2–5 lakhs software license |
| **Time** | Weeks to set up rig | Hours to run simulation |
| **Accuracy** | Real boundary conditions | Approximate (depends on mesh) |
| **Risk to Asset** | High (cavitation damage possible) | Zero (virtual) |
| **What-if Analysis** | Costly per scenario | Unlimited scenarios |
| **Real-time Sync** | No | Yes (via IIoT) |

*(Award 1 mark per comparison row, capped at 5 marks; remaining 2 marks for definition + flow explanation.)*

**Part (b) — Annual Energy Cost Saving (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Fixed-Speed Annual Energy Consumption (1.5 marks):**

Hydraulic power required:

$$
P_{hyd} = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 25 = 19620 \text{ W} = 19.62 \text{ kW}
$$

Shaft input power (fixed-speed):

$$
P_{in,fixed} = \frac{19.62}{0.65} = 30.18 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual Energy with Fixed-Speed Pump (1 mark):**

$$
E_{fixed} = 30.18 \cdot 6000 = 181080 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — VSD Operation: 80 % Speed for 70 % of Year (2 marks):**

Using Affinity Laws at $N_2 = 0.8 N_1$:

$$
Q_{VSD,80} = 0.08 \cdot 0.8 = 0.064 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
H_{VSD,80} = 25 \cdot (0.8)^2 = 16 \text{ m}
$$

$$
P_{hyd,VSD,80} = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.064 \cdot 16 = 10046 \text{ W} = 10.05 \text{ kW}
$$

Assuming the VSD efficiency itself is **95 %** and pump efficiency is constant at **65 %**:

$$
P_{in,VSD,80} = \frac{10.05}{0.65 \cdot 0.95} = 16.27 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — VSD Operation: 100 % Speed for 30 % of Year (1 mark):**

$$
P_{in,VSD,100} = \frac{19.62}{0.65 \cdot 0.95} = 31.78 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Weighted Annual Energy with VSD (1 mark):**

$$
E_{VSD} = (16.27 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 6000) + (31.78 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 6000)
$$

$$
E_{VSD} = 68334 + 57204 = 125538 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 6 — Annual Energy Saving & Cost Saving (1.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta E = 181080 - 125538 = 55542 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\text{Cost Saving} = 55542 \cdot 8 = \boxed{₹\ 4{,}44{,}336 \text{ per year}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout — Part B**
> - **Forgetting VSD inverter efficiency** (typically 95–98 %) is the most common error. It costs **1 full mark**.
> - Students often apply the cube law on **total time** instead of segmenting into **80 % and 100 % operating hours**. KTU explicitly tests the **weighted average** approach.
> - Currency units must be in **₹** with proper grouping (Indian numbering system) — `₹ 4,44,336` not `₹ 444336`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- ✅ The **EU MEI ≥ 0.70** regulation is the **single most important benchmark** for future-ready pump procurement.
- ✅ **Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$ — these are valid only in the **homologous operating zone** near **BEP**.
- ✅ **Digital Twin** ≠ **Simulation**. A digital twin is **synchronized in real time** with the physical asset.
- ✅ **Predictive Maintenance** uses **RUL (Remaining Useful Life)** estimation — not just threshold alarms.
- ✅ **Industry 4.0 in pumps** = IIoT sensors + Edge gateway + Cloud historian + AI/ML + Automated work orders.
- ✅ **SEC (Specific Energy Consumption)** is measured in **kWh/m³** — lower is better. It is the **key KPI** for benchmarking.
- ✅ **3D-printed impellers** can improve efficiency by **15–25 %** but require **post-machining** and **CT-scanning** for quality.
- ✅ **VSD energy savings** follow the **cube law** — a 20 % speed reduction saves **~49 %** energy.
- ✅ **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** includes **CAPEX + OPEX + Downtime + Environmental cost** — not just purchase price.
- ✅ **Cybersecurity** is a non-negotiable future concern — IIoT pumps need **network segmentation, TLS, and OTA-signed firmware**.
- ✅ KTU Module 4 questions frequently test **comparison tables** and **affinity-law numerics** — practice at least **3 numerical problems** before exams.
- ✅ Always quote **units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners **deduct 0.5 marks** for missing units.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — PEAGT522**
> *Anticipating and Adapting to Future Developments: A graduate engineer who masters this topic is equipped not just to maintain today's pumps, but to engineer tomorrow's intelligent fluid systems.*
